topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Arc Welding and Cutting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Arc Welding

> [!NOTE]
> **Arc Welding** is a fusion welding process wherein coalescence of metals is produced by heating them to the welding temperature (above the melting point of the base metal) using an **electric arc** struck between an electrode and the workpiece. The arc serves as a sustained, high-intensity heat source, while a filler metal (from a consumable electrode or a separate rod) is added to form a strong metallurgical bond upon solidification.

The **electric arc** itself is a sustained electrical discharge occurring between two conductors (the electrode and the workpiece) through a gaseous medium (usually ionised shielding gas or vapour). Temperatures at the arc core typically range between **6,000 °C and 20,000 °C**, vastly exceeding the melting point of any commercial metal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 OBE framework**, the primary safety focus in arc welding is the *triad of hazards*: **(1) Electrical hazards**, **(2) Radiation hazards (UV/IR)**, and **(3) Chemical/Fume hazards**. Mastery of these three categories forms the core of Module 3's Course Outcome (CO3).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lightning Bolt" Model

Imagine trying to join two steel plates together using a tiny, controlled bolt of lightning. That is essentially what arc welding does:

- When you bring a welding electrode close to the workpiece without touching it, the air gap becomes a **high-resistance electrical pathway**.
- A high voltage from the welding power source **ionises the air molecules**, creating a plasma channel — this is the "lightning."
- The plasma channel conducts enormous current, generating intense heat that **melts both the base metal and the electrode** simultaneously.
- The molten pool cools and solidifies, **fusing** the two pieces into a single, monolithic joint — just as two wax candles pressed together while molten become one piece.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the arc as a "controlled lightning bolt"** — invisible from a distance, blindingly bright up close, and capable of melting steel in milliseconds. This mental model helps you remember *why* the safety protocols are so strict: arc welding is, quite literally, domesticated electrical discharge engineering.

## 1.3 Arc Cutting — The Companion Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Arc Cutting** refers to a group of thermal cutting processes in which the **severing of metals is accomplished by melting** (with or without oxidation) using the heat of an electric arc. The arc here is used to remove, rather than to join, material.

Common arc cutting methods include:
- **Plasma Arc Cutting (PAC)** — uses a constricted, high-velocity plasma jet.
- **Carbon Arc Cutting (CAC-A)** — uses a carbon electrode and an oxidising flame.
- **Air Carbon Arc Cutting (CAC-A)** — molten metal is blown away by a compressed air jet.
- **Metal Arc Cutting** — uses a coated electrode similar to welding but with deeper penetration.

## 1.4 Standard Classification Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Arc Temperature | **6,000 °C – 20,000 °C** |
| Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) | **50 V – 90 V** (AC/DC) |
| Arc Voltage (working) | **18 V – 40 V** |
| Welding Current Range | **10 A – 1,500 A** |
| Typical Power Consumption | **3 kW – 50 kW** per station |
| Filler Metal Deposition Rate | **0.5 – 15 kg/hour** |
| Standard Reference (Safety) | **IS 818 (Code of Practice for Arc Welding Safety)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Physics of Arc Formation — Step-by-Step

The creation of a stable welding arc is governed by the following sequence of physical events:

1. **Initiation (Arc Striking):** The electrode is momentarily touched to the workpiece (a "short circuit") and then withdrawn by 2–4 mm. This creates a high-voltage gradient across the gap.
2. **Ionisation:** The intense electric field strips electrons from gas molecules (electron emission — thermionic for non-consumable electrodes, field emission for consumable ones), creating a **conductive plasma** of ions and free electrons.
3. **Sustained Discharge:** The ionised gas provides a low-resistance path, allowing massive current flow at a relatively low voltage.
4. **Heat Generation:** The arc power is dissipated as heat at three distinct zones — the **anode** (workpiece), the **cathode** (electrode), and the **arc column** itself.
5. **Metal Transfer:** Molten droplets from the electrode (if consumable) are transferred across the arc by electromagnetic force, gravity, or aerodynamic drag, depending on the process.

## 2.2 The "Why & How" of Each Welding Stage

| Stage | Why it matters | How it is controlled |
|---|---|---|
| **Arc Striking** | Poor striking = arc outages, spatter, defects | Controlled by **open-circuit voltage** setting |
| **Arc Stability** | Unstable arc = inconsistent penetration, porosity | Influenced by electrode coating, current type (AC vs DC) |
| **Heat Input** | Excessive heat = distortion, HAZ embrittlement | Controlled by voltage, current, and travel speed |
| **Solidification** | Cooling rate determines grain structure | Influenced by preheat, interpass temperature |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **board-exam favourites** for KTU ESE. Memorise the symbols, units, and the typical numerical magnitude of each.

### A. Arc Power Output

$$P_{arc} = V \times I$$

where $P_{arc}$ is in **watts (W)**, $V$ is the **arc voltage in volts (V)**, and $I$ is the **welding current in amperes (A)**.

### B. Heat Input per Unit Length (H)

This is the **single most important formula** in welding safety and quality control, because it directly determines the **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ)** size and the risk of thermal distortion or burn-through.

$$H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

where:
- $V$ = arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = welding current (A)
- $S$ = travel speed (mm/min)
- 60 = conversion factor (seconds per minute)
- 1000 = conversion factor (W to kW)

### C. Heat Input (with Thermal Efficiency $\eta$)

For more precise engineering calculations, the **process thermal efficiency** $\eta$ is included:

$$H = \eta \times \frac{V \times I}{S \times 1000 / 60} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

| Process | Thermal Efficiency $\eta$ |
|---|---|
| Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) | **0.75 – 0.85** |
| Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG) | **0.65 – 0.85** |
| Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG) | **0.40 – 0.60** |
| Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) | **0.85 – 0.99** |
| Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) | **0.55 – 0.70** |

### D. Melting Rate of Electrode (Deposition Rate)

$$R_{melt} = \frac{I \times \eta_{m}}}{1000} \quad \text{(kg/hour)}$$

For cellulosic-coated mild steel electrodes, an empirical formula is:

$$R_{melt} \approx 0.0008 \times I \quad \text{(kg/hour per 100 A)}$$

### E. Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

$$SEC = \frac{P_{arc}}}{W_{deposited}} \quad \text{(kWh/kg)}$$

Typical values range from **1.5 to 3.0 kWh/kg** for SMAW, used in estimating electrical costs and energy audits.

### F. Fume Generation Rate (Safety-Relevant)

$$F_{fume} = 0.0006 \times I \quad \text{(g/min per 100 A)}$$

This empirical relation is critical for **ventilation design** in confined-space welding operations.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Safety

- **Heat Input (H)** is the cornerstone of **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** in pressure vessel, bridge, and offshore structure fabrication. Excessive H means poor impact toughness of the HAZ.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is used for **carbon footprint calculations** in shipbuilding and heavy engineering.
- **Fume Generation Rate** drives the design of **local exhaust ventilation (LEV)** in shipyards, ensuring compliance with **OSHA PEL** and **Indian Factories Act** thresholds for manganese, hexavalent chromium, and ozone.
- **Arc Power** governs the **rating of electrical distribution systems** in welding bays, including cable sizing, breaker selection, and protective earthing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Heat Input Equation

> [!NOTE]
> The Heat Input equation is *not* arbitrary — it emerges from a fundamental energy balance. Below is the full derivation that KTU examiners expect for **14-mark derivations**.

### Step 1: Define Heat Input Conceptually

Heat input is the **total thermal energy delivered to the workpiece per unit length of weld**. Since welding is essentially an energy transfer process, we start from the basic electrical power equation.

### Step 2: Electrical Power in the Arc

The instantaneous electrical power dissipated in the arc is:

$$P_{arc} = V \times I \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

Here, $V$ is the **arc voltage** (not the open-circuit voltage), and $I$ is the current flowing during the active welding time.

### Step 3: Energy per Unit Time

Power is energy per unit time. Therefore, in time $t$ seconds, the energy delivered is:

$$E = P_{arc} \times t = V \times I \times t \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

### Step 4: Length Travelled in Time t

The welding torch moves at a travel speed $S$, expressed in **mm/min**. To convert to mm/s, we divide by 60:

$$S_{mm/s} = \frac{S}{60}$$

In time $t$ seconds, the length of weld produced is:

$$L = S_{mm/s} \times t = \frac{S \times t}{60} \quad \text{(mm)}$$

### Step 5: Heat Input Definition

By definition, heat input is energy per unit length:

$$H = \frac{E}{L} = \frac{V \times I \times t}{\dfrac{S \times t}{60}}$$

### Step 6: Simplification (The t Cancels)

The time variable $t$ cancels out beautifully:

$$H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S} \quad \text{(J/mm)}$$

### Step 7: Convert to Standard kJ/mm Unit

To express in kilojoules (the KTU-preferred unit), divide by 1000:

$$H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

This is the final, **board-ready equation**. $\blacksquare$

### Step 8: Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> A welder uses SMAW with the following parameters: $V = 25$ V, $I = 200$ A, $S = 300$ mm/min. Given the thermal efficiency of SMAW as $\eta = 0.80$, compute the **effective heat input**.

**Solution:**

$$H = \eta \times \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$$

Substituting values:

$$H = 0.80 \times \frac{25 \times 200 \times 60}{300 \times 1000}$$

Compute the numerator:
$$25 \times 200 = 5{,}000$$
$$5{,}000 \times 60 = 300{,}000$$

Compute the denominator:
$$300 \times 1000 = 300{,}000$$

Therefore:
$$H = 0.80 \times \frac{300{,}000}{300{,}000} = 0.80 \times 1.0$$

$$\boxed{H = 0.80 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **For low-carbon steel**, the recommended maximum heat input to avoid HAZ embrittlement is typically **1.5 – 2.0 kJ/mm**. The above value of 0.80 kJ/mm is comfortably within safe limits.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Hazard, Consequence, and Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **Hazard-Consequence-Control Matrix** is the most exam-relevant table in Module 3. Memorise the structure, not just the content.

| S.No | Hazard Category | Specific Hazard | Consequence (Health/Safety) | Primary Control Measure | Secondary Safeguard |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Electrical** | Primary voltage shock (415 V) | Cardiac arrest, fatal burns | Double insulation, ELCB | Lockout-tagout, dry flooring |
| 2 | **Electrical** | Secondary (open-circuit) shock | Mild shock, startle injury | Voltage Reduction Device (VRD) | Auto-darkening helmets |
| 3 | **Radiation** | UV-C (200–280 nm) | Photokeratitis ("arc eye") | Welding helmet with shade 10–13 | Curtains, paint darkening |
| 4 | **Radiation** | UV-B (280–315 nm) | Skin erythema, DNA damage | Flame-resistant clothing (FRC) | Sleeve covers, neck flaps |
| 5 | **Radiation** | IR radiation | Cataracts, retinal burns | IR-filtering lens | Side-shielded goggles |
| 6 | **Chemical** | Hexavalent chromium fumes | Lung cancer, nasal ulceration | Local exhaust ventilation | Powered air-purifying respirator (PAPR) |
| 7 | **Chemical** | Manganese fumes | Neurotoxicity (Parkinsonism) | LEV with 100 ft/min face velocity | Half-mask P100 respirator |
| 8 | **Chemical** | Ozone & NOx | Pulmonary oedema | Cross-flow ventilation | Continuous gas monitoring |
| 9 | **Thermal** | Spatter and molten metal | 2nd/3rd degree burns | Leather apron, spatter-resistant gloves | Fire-resistant booth dividers |
| 10 | **Fire** | Sparks igniting combustibles | Structural fire, explosion | Hot work permit system | Fire watcher + extinguisher within 5 m |
| 11 | **Mechanical** | Cylinder mishandling (gas) | Valve rupture, projectile injury | Trolley + chain securing | Cap-on-when-not-in-use |
| 12 | **Ergonomic** | Prolonged posture | Musculoskeletal disorders | Adjustable worktable | Job rotation, micro-breaks |

## 3.3 Derivation — Fume Generation Rate Justification

The empirical formula $F_{fume} = 0.0006 \times I$ (g/min per 100 A) is derived from the following reasoning:

- During welding, approximately **0.1 % to 2 %** of the electrode mass is vaporised as fume.
- For a mild steel electrode of mass $m$ (kg), mass vaporised = $0.01 m$ (using a mid-range 1 %).
- Electrode consumption time at current $I$ is inversely proportional to $I$.

Mass vaporised per minute (for 100 A baseline):

$$F_{fume} = k \times I$$

Empirically fitted constant $k$ for mild steel electrodes yields:

$$F_{fume} = 0.0006 \times I \quad \text{(g/min at 100 A current)}$$

This value scales linearly with current, and is the basis for **ACGIH TLV** compliance checks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Arc Welding Process Flow — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[Power Source 415V AC Mains] -->|Step Down| TRX[Transformer Rectifier]
    TRX -->|Low Voltage High Current| ELE[Electrode Holder]
    ELE -->|Via Cable| ARC[Electric Arc 6000-20000 C]
    WP[Workpiece Clamped] -->|Return Path| GND[Earth Clamp Cable]
    TRX -->|OCV| GND
    ARC --> MELT[Melting of Base Metal and Filler]
    MELT --> POOL[Molten Weld Pool]
    POOL -->|Solidification| JOINT[Metallurgical Joint]
    SHLD[Shielding Gas or Flux Cover] --> POOL
    SHLD -->|Protects from| ATMOS[Atmospheric Oxygen and Nitrogen]
    JOINT --> HAZ[Heat Affected Zone Forms]
    HAZ --> QC[NDT Inspection]
```

## 4.2 Safety Hazard Control Hierarchy

```mermaid
    ROOT[Arc Welding Operation] --> H1[Hazard Identification]
    H1 --> E1[Electrical Shock Risk]
    H1 --> R1[UV IR Radiation]
    H1 --> C1[Toxic Fumes]
    H1 --> F1[Fire and Explosion]
    H1 --> T1[Thermal Burns]
    E1 --> CTRL1[Elimination and Substitution]
    CTRL1 --> ENG1[Engineering Controls ELCB VRD Double Insulation]
    ENG1 --> ADMIN1[Administrative Controls Permit to Work Training SOPs]
    ADMIN1 --> PPE1[PPE Helmet Gloves Apron Respirator]
    R1 --> CTRL2[Engineering Weld Curtains Shade 13 Helmet]
    CTRL2 --> ADMIN2[Admin Access Control Warning Signs]
    ADMIN2 --> PPE2[PPE Auto Darkening Helmet FRC Clothing]
    C1 --> LEV[Local Exhaust Ventilation]
    LEV --> MON[Air Quality Monitoring]
    MON --> RESP[PAPR or Half Mask Respirator]
    F1 --> PER[Hot Work Permit]
    PER --> WATCH[Designated Fire Watcher]
    WATCH --> EXT[CO2 or DCP Extinguisher Standby]
    T1 --> GLOV[Heat Resistant Leather Gloves]
    GLOV --> APR[Fire Resistant Apron and Leg Guards]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Common Arc Welding Processes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SMAW[Shielded Metal Arc Welding Manual Stick] --> C1[Consumable Coated Electrode]
    GMAW[Gas Metal Arc Welding MIG MAG] --> C2[Continuous Wire with Shielding Gas]
    GTAW[Gas Tungsten Arc Welding TIG] --> C3[Non Consumable Tungsten Electrode]
    SAW[Submerged Arc Welding SAW] --> C4[Wire under Granular Flux Blanket]
    PAW[Plasma Arc Welding PAW] --> C5[Constricted Arc Through Nozzle]
    FCAW[Flux Cored Arc Welding] --> C6[Tubular Wire with Flux Core]

    C3 --> APP
    C4 --> APP
    C5 --> APP
    C6 --> APP
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{What is the material to be cut} -->|Stainless Steel| A1[Plasma Arc Cutting PAC]
    Q -->|Mild Steel Thick Section| A2[Carbon Arc Air Cutting CAC A]
    Q -->|Aluminium Copper| A3[Plasma or TIG Adapted Cutting]
    Q -->|All Ferrous Metals| A4[Oxygen Arc Cutting]
    A1 --> P1[High Precision Clean Cut Thin to Medium Thickness]
    A2 --> P2[Heavy Section Cutting Gouging Bevel Preparation]
    A3 --> P3[Non Ferrous Specialist Application]
    A4 --> P4[Scrap Demolition Heavy Plate Edge Trimming]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]
**Define arc welding. State the typical temperature range of an electric arc used in arc welding.**

**Model Answer:**

Arc welding is a fusion welding process in which the heat required for coalescence of metals is obtained from an electric arc struck between an electrode and the workpiece. The arc is a sustained electrical discharge through a gas, which acts as a high-intensity heat source melting the base metal and filler.

The typical temperature range of an electric arc in welding is between **6,000 °C and 20,000 °C**, with the central core of the arc reaching the higher end of this range. This temperature is several times higher than the melting point of any commercial metal (iron melts at ~1,538 °C), ensuring rapid fusion and melting of the joint region. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any four safety hazards associated with arc welding and mention the corresponding PPE.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No | Hazard | Recommended PPE |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | UV/IR radiation causing "arc eye" | Welding helmet with shade **10–13** filter lens |
| 2 | Hot spatter and molten metal | **Leather aprons**, spatter-resistant gloves, leggings |
| 3 | Toxic fumes (manganese, Cr⁶⁺) | **Half-mask respirator with P100 filter** or PAPR |
| 4 | Electric shock | **Dry, insulating leather gloves** (in addition to welding gloves) plus insulated footwear |

**(3 marks — ½ mark per hazard, ½ mark for matching PPE)**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]

**a)** Explain the principle of arc welding with a neat labelled diagram of the welding circuit. List the major components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Discuss in detail the safety measures to be adopted during arc welding to protect the welder from **(i) electrical shock**, **(ii) arc radiation**, and **(iii) fumes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Principle and Components (7 marks)**

**Principle:** Arc welding is based on the principle of generating an electric arc between an **electrode** (held in an electrode holder) and the **workpiece**. The intense heat of the arc (6,000–20,000 °C) melts the base metal and a consumable electrode to form a molten weld pool. Upon cooling, this pool solidifies into a continuous, metallurgically bonded joint.

**Major Components and Functions:**

1. **Power Source (Welding Transformer/Rectifier/Inverter):** Converts 415 V AC mains into low-voltage, high-current DC or AC suitable for welding. Open-circuit voltage is **50–90 V**; working arc voltage is **18–40 V**.
2. **Electrode Holder (Stinger):** Mechanically grips the welding electrode and conducts current to it. Insulated handle prevents shock.
3. **Electrode:** Acts as one terminal of the arc and supplies filler metal (if consumable).
4. **Workpiece & Earth Clamp:** Completes the electrical circuit back to the power source.
5. **Welding Cables:** Heavy-duty, flexible, double-insulated copper cables to carry high current.
6. **Shielding Device:** Either a **flux coating** (SMAW) or **shielding gas cylinder** (GMAW/GTAW) to protect the molten pool from atmospheric contamination.
7. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Helmet, gloves, apron, respirator, and boots. **(7 marks — 1 mark for principle + 6 marks for component description, ½ mark each)**

**Part (b) — Safety Measures (7 marks)**

**(i) Protection from Electrical Shock (2 marks):**
- Use **ELCB (Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker)** of 30 mA sensitivity on the welding circuit.
- Maintain dry working conditions; never weld in rain or on wet floors.
- Inspect cables and holders daily for damaged insulation.
- Use **Voltage Reduction Devices (VRD)** that limit open-circuit voltage to below 24 V.
- Wear dry, insulating inner gloves beneath welding gloves.

**(ii) Protection from Arc Radiation (2.5 marks):**
- Use **welding helmet with auto-darkening filter**, shade number 10–13 depending on process current.
- For bystanders, install **weld curtains** of flame-resistant, UV-opaque material (typically green or gold-tinted).
- Wear **flame-resistant clothing (FRC)** — preferably wool or treated cotton, never synthetic.
- Apply **sunscreen** on exposed neck and face skin.
- Conduct **regular eye examinations** for welders (every 6–12 months).

**(iii) Protection from Fumes (2.5 marks):**
- Install **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** with capture velocity of at least **100 ft/min (0.5 m/s)** at the source.
- Provide **mechanical general ventilation** of at least **2,000 ft³/min per welder** for non-toxic conditions.
- Use **respiratory protection** — half-mask with P100 filter for routine work; **PAPR** for stainless steel (Cr⁶⁺ exposure) or confined spaces.
- Rotate welders to limit cumulative exposure (ACGIH TLV for Mn = **0.02 mg/m³**).
- Conduct periodic **air monitoring** as per IS 818 and OSHA standards. **(7 marks — distributed as above)**

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]

**a)** What is heat input in arc welding? Derive the formula for heat input per unit length. Compute the heat input for a weld made at 30 V, 250 A with a travel speed of 250 mm/min, assuming a thermal efficiency of 0.85. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Describe the **Plasma Arc Cutting (PAC)** process with a diagram. Compare PAC with **Oxy-Fuel Gas Cutting** based on speed, material versatility, and safety hazards. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Heat Input Derivation and Calculation (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Heat input is the total quantity of heat energy transferred to the workpiece per unit length of the weld, measured in **kJ/mm**. It is a critical parameter controlling weld quality, HAZ size, and mechanical properties.

**Derivation (4 marks):** [See full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes — key steps: $P = VI$, $E = P \times t$, $L = S \times t / 60$, $H = E/L$, simplify and convert to kJ/mm.]

The final formula:

$$H = \eta \times \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

**Calculation (2 marks):** Given $V = 30$ V, $I = 250$ A, $S = 250$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.85$.

$$H = 0.85 \times \frac{30 \times 250 \times 60}{250 \times 1000}$$

Compute numerator:
$$30 \times 250 = 7{,}500$$
$$7{,}500 \times 60 = 450{,}000$$

Compute denominator:
$$250 \times 1000 = 250{,}000$$

$$H = 0.85 \times \frac{450{,}000}{250{,}000} = 0.85 \times 1.8$$

$$\boxed{H = 1.53 \text{ kJ/mm}}$$

This is well within the safe range for low-carbon steel (≤ 2.0 kJ/mm), indicating acceptable procedure parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Plasma Arc Cutting and Comparison (7 marks)**

**Plasma Arc Cutting (PAC) Process (3.5 marks):**

Plasma Arc Cutting uses a **constricted electric arc** forced through a small-diameter copper nozzle. The constriction increases the arc's energy density and velocity. A **secondary gas** (usually air, nitrogen, or argon-hydrogen) is introduced at high pressure to **expel the molten metal** from the kerf (cut) as a high-velocity plasma jet.

**Key components:** Power source (200–400 V DC), torch with tungsten electrode, constricting nozzle, pilot arc circuit, cutting gas supply.

**Working principle:**
1. A high-frequency **pilot arc** is initiated between the electrode and nozzle.
2. The pilot arc transfers to the workpiece once contact is made.
3. The constricted arc melts a narrow strip of metal.
4. High-velocity plasma gas blows the molten metal out, creating a clean cut.

**Comparison Table (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Plasma Arc Cutting (PAC) | Oxy-Fuel Gas Cutting |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat Source** | Constricted electric arc (plasma jet) | Oxy-acetylene flame (~3,200 °C) |
| **Max Temperature** | **20,000 – 30,000 °C** | ~3,200 °C |
| **Material Versatility** | All metals — steel, SS, Al, Cu, alloys | **Only ferrous metals** (oxide-forming) |
| **Cutting Speed** | **2–5 times faster** on thin to medium plates | Slower; better on thick plates (>25 mm) |
| **Cut Quality** | Narrow kerf, minimal slag, smooth edge | Wide kerf, slag formation, rougher edge |
| **Plate Thickness Range** | 0.5 mm to ~50 mm | 6 mm to >300 mm |
| **Primary Safety Hazard** | UV radiation, electrical shock, fume (Cr⁶⁺) | Fire risk, flashback, oxygen enrichment explosion |
| **Setup Cost** | Higher (power source, gas system) | Lower (cylinders + torch) |
| **Operator Skill** | Moderate to high | Moderate |

**(7 marks — 3.5 + 3.5 distributed as above)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Open-Circuit Voltage with Arc Voltage:** Students frequently use OCV (50–90 V) in the heat input formula instead of arc voltage (18–40 V). This **doubles the answer** and leads to **2–3 marks deduction** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Writing the heat input answer in J/mm instead of kJ/mm (or forgetting to divide by 1000) is a **1-mark standard deduction**.
> 3. **Missing Thermal Efficiency:** For SMAW, GMAW, and SAW problems, examiners expect $\eta$ to be applied. Omitting it loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **PPE Incomplete List:** Writing only "helmet and gloves" without specifying **shade number** or **glove material** loses marks. Always quantify.
> 5. **Arc Cutting Confusion:** Students often describe **Oxy-Fuel cutting** when asked about **arc cutting**. Remember: arc cutting uses **electricity as the heat source**, not combustion of fuel gas.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Arc Welding and Cutting (Module 3):**

- **Definition:** Arc welding = fusion joining using heat of an **electric arc** between electrode and workpiece. Temperature: **6,000–20,000 °C**.
- **Arc Striking Mechanism:** Touch-and-lift method, ionisation, sustained plasma discharge.
- **Three Key Equations:**
  - $P = V \times I$ (Arc power, W)
  - $H = \eta \times \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ (Heat input, kJ/mm) — **MOST IMPORTANT**
  - $F_{fume} = 0.0006 \times I$ (Fume generation, g/min)
- **Major Processes:** SMAW (manual stick), GMAW (MIG/MAG), GTAW (TIG), SAW, PAW, FCAW — know electrode type and shielding method for each.
- **Arc Cutting Methods:** PAC (Plasma Arc), CAC-A (Carbon Arc Air), Metal Arc Cutting — all are **electricity-based**, not combustion-based.
- **Three Pillars of Arc Welding Safety:**
  1. **Electrical** → ELCB (30 mA), VRD, dry conditions, double insulation.
  2. **Radiation** → Shade 10–13 helmet, weld curtains, FRC clothing.
  3. **Chemical/Fume** → LEV (100 ft/min), respirators (P100/PAPR), air monitoring.
- **Indian Standards:** **IS 818** (Arc Welding Safety Code), **Indian Factories Act Section 39** (Fume control), **IS 1852** (Rolling/shutting of welding screens).
- **Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL):** Mn = **0.02 mg/m³** (ACGIH); Cr⁶⁺ = **0.005 mg/m³**; Ozone = **0.05 ppm**.
- **Hot Work Permit System:** Mandatory for any welding outside a designated booth — requires fire watcher, extinguisher standby, and area clearance of all combustibles within **11 m (35 ft)**.
- **Gas Cylinder Safety:** Chain upright, cap on when not in use, separate fuel and oxygen by **6 m (20 ft)** or fire-resistant barrier.
- **Standard Formula Conversions:** 1 kJ/mm = 1,000 J/mm; 1 ft/min = 0.00508 m/s; thermal efficiency values must be **memorised** for SMAW, GMAW, GTAW, SAW.
- **Common Exam Traps:** OCV vs arc voltage; units of heat input; $\eta$ value per process; arc cutting vs oxy-fuel cutting distinction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Common Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Common Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Hazards (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):**
> A **hazard** is any *source, situation, or act* with the potential to cause harm in terms of human injury or ill-health, damage to property, damage to the environment, or a combination of these. **Common Hazards** refer to the frequently encountered categories of workplace hazards classified under international safety standards such as **OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration)**, **ILO (International Labour Organization)**, and the **Indian Factories Act 1948**, which apply across engineering, manufacturing, construction, and process industries.

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** categorizes workplace hazards into **six universally accepted classifications**:

1. **Physical Hazards** — noise, vibration, radiation, temperature extremes, pressure
2. **Chemical Hazards** — dusts, fumes, vapours, liquids, gases
3. **Biological Hazards** — bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites
4. **Ergonomic / Musculoskeletal Hazards** — repetitive motion, awkward posture, manual handling
5. **Psychosocial Hazards** — workplace stress, harassment, violence, fatigue
6. **Mechanical / Safety Hazards** — slips/trips/falls, caught-in/between, struck-by, electrical, fire

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Six Spiders" of a Workshop**
> Imagine a manufacturing workshop as a room with **six invisible spiders**, each representing a hazard category. Just as spiders spin webs of different types (silk, sticky, electric), each hazard type "spins" a unique risk web. A worker crossing through all six webs accumulates compounded danger. **Hazard Identification** is the flashlight you use to see each spider before it bites. The bite (incident) is prevented when you see the web (hazard) and either remove the spider (elimination) or wear protective gear (PPE).

### 1.3 Critical Safety Metrics and Standards

The following standard metrics are used in KTU 2024 Scheme evaluations for hazard assessment:

- **TLV (Threshold Limit Value):** $\mathbf{25\text{ ppm}}$ (typical for many solvents)
- **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL):** $\mathbf{90\text{ dBA}}$ for **8-hour TWA (Time-Weighted Average)**
- **Lifting Weight Limit (NIOSH):** $\mathbf{23\text{ kg}}$ under ideal conditions
- **Fatality Risk Threshold:** $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-3}}$ per year (acceptable for workers)
- **Standard Safety Factor:** $\mathbf{SF} = \mathbf{4.0}$ for structural and lifting equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Common Hazards — Structured Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Physical Hazards
**Why they matter:** These are the most prevalent causes of occupational diseases in engineering industries.

| Sub-Category | Source | Typical Threshold | Health Effect |
|--------------|--------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Noise** | Machinery, generators, forging | 90 dBA (8-hr TWA) | Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL) |
| **Vibration** | Hand tools, heavy vehicles | Hand-Arm $\geq 5 \text{ m/s}^2$ | Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS) |
| **Radiation** | Welding arc, X-ray, lasers | Ionizing dose limit 20 mSv/yr | Burns, cancer, cataracts |
| **Temperature** | Furnaces, foundries, cold storage | WBGT index limits | Heat stroke, hypothermia |
| **Pressure** | Boilers, compressed gas cylinders | ASME Sec VIII limits | Barotrauma, explosion |

#### 2.1.2 Chemical Hazards
**Classification by physical form (KTU 2024 Scheme critical concept):**
- **Dusts** — solid particles (silica, coal, wood)
- **Fumes** — fine metallic oxides (welding fumes)
- **Vapours** — liquid evaporating at room temp (solvents)
- **Gases** — flow to fill container (CO, H₂S, Cl₂)
- **Mists** — liquid droplets in air (acid mist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Route of Entry Mnemonic — "SEAS":**
> **S**kin (absorption) → **E**yes (contact) → **A**irways (inhalation) → **S**tomach (ingestion)
> This is a KTU-favourite 3-mark question.

#### 2.1.3 Biological Hazards
Encountered in pharmaceutical, food processing, water treatment, and healthcare engineering.
- **Bacteria** (Legionella in cooling towers)
- **Viruses** (blood-borne pathogens)
- **Fungi** (moulds in damp buildings)
- **Bioaerosols** in waste-water plants

#### 2.1.4 Ergonomic Hazards
**Why** — Poor workstation design + repetitive tasks + awkward posture = **Work-Related Musculoskeletal Disorders (WRMSDs)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The NIOSH Lifting Equation (1981, revised 1991):**
> Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) is the load that nearly all workers can lift safely.
> Below 23 kg baseline = safe under ideal conditions.

#### 2.1.5 Psychosocial Hazards
Linked to **workplace stress, bullying, long hours, job insecurity, shift work**. Effects include cardiovascular disease, mental health issues, and reduced productivity.

#### 2.1.6 Mechanical / Safety Hazards
The "big four" in construction & manufacturing:
- **Struck-by** (falling objects, vehicles)
- **Caught-in / between** (machinery, collapses)
- **Slips, Trips, and Falls (STF)** — accounts for **~25%** of all workplace injuries
- **Electrical** — shock, arc flash, electrocution

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| Permissible Noise Exposure (8-hr TWA) | $TWA = 16.61 \log_{10}\left(\frac{D}{100}\right) + 90$ | $D$ = dose in % |
| Noise Dose Formula | $D = 100 \times \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | $C_i$ = actual time, $T_i$ = allowed time |
| Permissible Exposure Time $T$ | $T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$ | $L$ = measured level in dBA |
| NIOSH Lifting Equation (RWL) | $RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ | $LC = 23$ kg (Load Constant) |
| Lifting Index (LI) | $LI = \frac{\text{Load Weight}}{RWL}$ | $LI > 1$ indicates increased risk |
| Whole-Body Vibration Dose | $VDV = \left[\int_{0}^{T} a_w^4(t)\,dt\right]^{1/4}$ | $a_w$ = weighted acceleration, $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| Body Weight % to Carry | Safe load = $\frac{\text{1.5} \times \text{Body Weight}}{3}$ | For symmetrical two-handed lift |
| Probit Equation (Toxicity) | $Pr = a + b \ln C$ | $C$ = concentration, $a, b$ = constants |
| Fire Triangle | Fuel + Oxygen + Heat = Fire | All three needed |
| TLV-TWA | 8-hr time-weighted average | ACGIH standard |
| TLV-STEL | 15-min short-term exposure limit | $\leq 4$ excursions/day |
| TLV-Ceiling | Absolute limit not to be exceeded | Instantaneous |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing plants** use hazard audits to comply with **ISO 45001:2018**.
- **Construction industry** applies **HIRA (Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment)** before every task.
- **Process industries** use **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** for chemical plants.
- **Auditors** use the **Hazard Register** as a living document for compliance with the **Factories Act 1948** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Permissible Noise Exposure Time

**Statement:** For every **5 dBA increase** in noise level above 90 dBA, the permissible exposure time is **halved**.

**Step 1 — Define the OSHA Standard Formula:**

$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$$

where $T$ = permissible exposure time in hours, $L$ = measured sound level in dBA.

**Step 2 — Substitute $L = 95$ dBA:**

$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(95-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{5/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{1}} = \frac{8}{2} = 4 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $L = 100$ dBA:**

$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(100-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{10/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{2}} = \frac{8}{4} = 2 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $L = 110$ dBA:**

$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(110-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{20/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{4}} = \frac{8}{16} = 0.5 \text{ hours} = 30 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 5 — Logical Conclusion:** Doubling of noise exposure severity cuts exposure time in half — a classic **inverse exponential relationship** used by KTU examiners for 7-mark derivation questions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — NIOSH Lifting Equation (RWL Computation)

**Statement:** The Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) considers six multipliers on the base Load Constant of 23 kg.

**Step 1 — Initial Formula:**

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

**Step 2 — Define Each Multiplier:**

$$LC = 23 \text{ kg (Load Constant)}$$

$$HM = \frac{25}{H} \quad \text{where } H = \text{horizontal distance (cm)}$$

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert \quad \text{where } V = \text{vertical height (cm)}$$

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{D} \quad \text{where } D = \text{travel distance (cm)}$$

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 A \quad \text{where } A = \text{asymmetry angle (degrees)}$$

$$FM = 1 - 0.0032 F \quad \text{where } F = \text{frequency (lifts/min)}$$

$$CM = 1 \text{ (good coupling) or } 0.95 \text{ (fair) or } 0.9 \text{ (poor)}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example:**
A worker lifts a **15 kg** box with:
- $H = 40$ cm
- $V = 80$ cm
- $D = 100$ cm
- $A = 30°$
- $F = 2$ lifts/min
- $CM = 1$ (good coupling)

**Step 3a — Horizontal Multiplier:**

$$HM = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625$$

**Step 3b — Vertical Multiplier:**

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \vert 80 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \times 5 = 1 - 0.015 = 0.985$$

**Step 3c — Distance Multiplier:**

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{100} = 0.82 + 0.018 = 0.838$$

**Step 3d — Asymmetry Multiplier:**

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \times 30 = 1 - 0.096 = 0.904$$

**Step 3e — Frequency Multiplier:**

$$FM = 1 - 0.0032 \times 2 = 1 - 0.0064 = 0.9936$$

**Step 4 — Compute RWL:**

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.625 \times 0.985 \times 0.838 \times 0.904 \times 0.9936 \times 1$$

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.625 = 14.375$$

$$14.375 \times 0.985 = 14.159$$

$$14.159 \times 0.838 = 11.865$$

$$11.865 \times 0.904 = 10.726$$

$$10.726 \times 0.9936 = 10.658 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Lifting Index:**

$$LI = \frac{\text{Actual Load}}{RWL} = \frac{15}{10.658} = 1.408$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation:**
Since $LI > 1.0$, the lifting task poses an **increased risk of musculoskeletal injury**. Engineering controls (mechanical lifting aid) or administrative controls (job rotation) must be implemented.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Hazard Matrix (Risk Assessment Grid)

The **3×3 Risk Matrix** is mandated by ISO 45001 and is a KTU 2024 high-yield topic.

| Severity → ↓ Likelihood | **S1 — Minor** (First aid) | **S2 — Moderate** (Lost time) | **S3 — Major** (Fatal/Disability) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L3 — Likely** (frequent) | Medium (M) | High (H) | **Extreme (E)** |
| **L2 — Possible** (occasional) | Low (L) | Medium (M) | High (H) |
| **L1 — Rare** (seldom) | Low (L) | Low (L) | Medium (M) |

**Risk Score Formula:**

$$R = L \times S$$

where $L \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $S \in \{1, 2, 3\}$. Maximum $R = 9$.

**Decision Rules (KTU Examiner Favourite):**
- $R \leq 2$ → Accept, monitor
- $3 \leq R \leq 4$ → Mitigation needed within 30 days
- $R \geq 6$ → Stop work immediately

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hazard Classification Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    rootA[Hazard Classification]

```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Controls Block Diagram (Most Effective → Least Effective)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ctrlA[Hazard Control Hierarchy]
    ctrlA --> ctrlB[Elimination]
    ctrlA --> ctrlC[Substitution]
    ctrlA --> ctrlD[Engineering Controls]
    ctrlA --> ctrlE[Administrative Controls]
    ctrlA --> ctrlF[PPE]

    ctrlB[Elimination - Remove hazard entirely]
    ctrlC[Substitution - Replace with less hazardous material]
    ctrlD[Engineering Controls - Guards, ventilation, isolation]
    ctrlE[Administrative Controls - Training, procedures, rotation]

```

```mermaid

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define the term **"Hazard"** and distinguish it from **"Risk"** with one engineering industry example each.

**Model Answer:**

> **Hazard** is a source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm in terms of human injury or ill-health, damage to property, damage to the environment, or a combination of these. **Risk** is the combination of the **likelihood** of an occurrence of a hazardous event or exposure and the **severity** of injury or ill-health that can be caused by the event or exposure.
>
> **Example:** *Unguarded machinery in a workshop* is a **hazard**; the *probability that a worker's hand gets crushed and the severity of that injury* constitutes the **risk**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
List the **six major categories of common workplace hazards** and give one example for each.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Hazard Category | Engineering Example |
|---|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Physical | Noise from forging hammer |
| 2 | Chemical | Solvent vapour in paint shop |
| 3 | Biological | Legionella in cooling tower water |
| 4 | Ergonomic | Repetitive wrist motion in assembly line |
| 5 | Psychosocial | Shift-work stress in power plants |
| 6 | Mechanical/Safety | Live overhead electrical cable |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [a) 7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Explain the **classification of physical hazards** in engineering industries. Discuss in detail the effects of **noise** and **vibration** on workers, with permissible exposure limits.

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Classification of Physical Hazards:**
Physical hazards are environmental factors that can cause harm without physical contact. They include:
- **Noise** — unwanted sound above 80 dBA
- **Vibration** — mechanical oscillations from tools/machinery
- **Radiation** — ionizing (X-rays, gamma) and non-ionizing (UV, IR, lasers)
- **Temperature extremes** — heat (foundries) and cold (cold storage)
- **Pressure** — boilers, compressed gas, vacuum systems
- **Lighting** — inadequate or excessive illumination

**(2) Noise — Effects & Limits:**
- **Audiometric effects:** Temporary Threshold Shift (TTS) → Permanent Threshold Shift (PTS) → Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL)
- **Extra-auditory effects:** Hypertension, increased stress, sleep disturbance
- **Permissible Limit:** **90 dBA for 8-hour TWA** (OSHA) and **85 dBA for 8-hour TWA** (NIOSH/ACGIH)
- **Doubling Rule:** For every 5 dBA increase, exposure time is halved.
- **Engineering Control:** Acoustic enclosures, dampers, isolation mounts, PPE = ear plugs/muffs.

**(3) Vibration — Effects & Limits:**
- **Hand-Arm Vibration (HAV):** From power tools, jackhammers → leads to **Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS)** including Vibration White Finger (VWF)
- **Whole-Body Vibration (WBV):** From heavy vehicles, ships → spinal disorders, fatigue
- **EU Limit Value:** $5.0 \text{ m/s}^2$ (8-hr A8) for HAV; $1.15 \text{ m/s}^2$ for WBV
- **Control:** Anti-vibration gloves, job rotation, tool maintenance, vibration-isolated seats.

---

**Q.A. [b) 7 Marks]**
With a neat **risk assessment matrix**, explain how severity and likelihood are combined to determine risk score. Illustrate with one industry case study.

**Model Solution:**

**Risk Matrix (3×3 L × S):**

| Likelihood \ Severity | Minor (1) | Moderate (2) | Major (3) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Likely (3) | 3 (M) | 6 (H) | 9 (E) |
| Possible (2) | 2 (L) | 4 (M) | 6 (H) |
| Rare (1) | 1 (L) | 2 (L) | 3 (M) |

**Risk Score Formula:**

$$R = L \times S$$

**Risk Band Decision:**
- $R \in [1, 2]$ → **Low (L)** — Accept with routine procedures
- $R \in [3, 4]$ → **Medium (M)** — Mitigation within 30 days
- $R \in [6, 9]$ → **High (H) / Extreme (E)** — Immediate action / stop work

**Industry Case Study — Chemical Reactor Overpressure:**
*Likelihood:* Pressure build-up in a batch reactor = Possible (L=2, occasional operator error).
*Severity:* Reactor rupture in a populated plant = Major (S=3, fatality possible).
*Risk Score:* $R = 2 \times 3 = 6$ → **High Risk**
*Controls Applied:* (1) Rupture disc + relief valve (engineering), (2) SOP + training (administrative), (3) PPE Level C (last resort).
*Residual Risk after controls:* L=1, S=3 → $R = 3$ → **Medium** → Acceptable with monitoring.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. [a) 7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
Describe in detail the **classification of chemical hazards** and explain the **routes of entry** of hazardous chemicals into the human body. What are the **TLV** values?

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Classification by Physical Form:**

| Form | Definition | Industry Example |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **Dusts** | Solid particles produced by mechanical action | Silica in quarrying, wood dust in carpentry |
| **Fumes** | Fine metallic oxide particles from welding/smelting | ZnO from galvanizing |
| **Vapours** | Gaseous form of substances normally liquid | Benzene, acetone evaporation |
| **Gases** | Flow to fill container at room conditions | CO, H₂S, Cl₂ |
| **Mists** | Suspended liquid droplets | Acid mist in electroplating |
| **Fibres** | Solid elongated particles | Asbestos |

**(2) Routes of Entry — "SEAS" Mnemonic:**
- **Skin (Absorption):** Solvents penetrate intact skin (e.g., phenol, aniline).
- **Eyes (Contact):** Splash injury, mists, fumes.
- **Airways (Inhalation):** Most common route — dusts, fumes, vapours, gases enter lungs.
- **Stomach (Ingestion):** Eating with contaminated hands in workshops.

**(3) Threshold Limit Values (TLV) — ACGIH:**
- **TLV-TWA** (Time-Weighted Average): 8-hour exposure average.
- **TLV-STEL** (Short-Term Exposure Limit): 15-min, max 4 excursions/day, ≥60 min apart.
- **TLV-Ceiling (C):** Absolute limit not to be exceeded at any moment.
*Example:* Benzene TLV-TWA = 0.5 ppm; TLV-STEL = 2.5 ppm.

**(4) Health Effects:**
- **Acute** — Headache, narcosis (immediate, reversible)
- **Chronic** — Cancer (asbestos, benzene), organ damage (liver, kidney), CNS disorders

---

**Q.B. [b) 7 Marks]**
Discuss the **ergonomic hazards** in engineering workplaces. Explain the **NIOSH Lifting Equation** with its multipliers. A worker lifts a load of **18 kg** under the following conditions: $H = 50$ cm, $V = 90$ cm, $D = 110$ cm, $A = 45°$, $F = 3$ lifts/min, $CM = 0.95$. Determine the **Lifting Index (LI)** and comment.

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Ergonomic Hazards — Definition:**
Hazards arising from mismatch between worker physical capacity and workplace design → causing WRMSDs (Work-Related Musculoskeletal Disorders) such as low-back pain, carpal tunnel syndrome, tendonitis.

Common ergonomic stressors:
- Repetitive motion
- Awkward posture (twisting, bending)
- Forceful exertion
- Static posture
- Vibration coupling
- Manual material handling

**(2) NIOSH Lifting Equation:**

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

with $LC = 23 \text{ kg}$ baseline.

**(3) Numerical Computation:**

$$HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{50} = 0.500$$

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \times \vert 90 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \times 15 = 1 - 0.045 = 0.955$$

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{110} = 0.82 + 0.01636 = 0.83636$$

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \times A = 1 - 0.0032 \times 45 = 1 - 0.144 = 0.856$$

$$FM = 1 - 0.0032 \times F = 1 - 0.0032 \times 3 = 1 - 0.0096 = 0.9904$$

$$CM = 0.95 \text{ (given)}$$

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.500 \times 0.955 \times 0.83636 \times 0.856 \times 0.9904 \times 0.95$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$23 \times 0.500 = 11.500$$

$$11.500 \times 0.955 = 10.9825$$

$$10.9825 \times 0.83636 = 9.187$$

$$9.187 \times 0.856 = 7.864$$

$$7.864 \times 0.9904 = 7.789$$

$$7.789 \times 0.95 = 7.400 \text{ kg}$$

**Lifting Index:**

$$LI = \frac{\text{Actual Load}}{RWL} = \frac{18}{7.400} = 2.43$$

**(4) Comment:**
Since $LI = 2.43 > 1.0$, the task imposes a **moderate to high risk** of back injury. Recommended actions:
- Introduce a **mechanical lifting aid** (trolley, hoist) — primary engineering control.
- **Job rotation** and reduced frequency (administrative control).
- **Training** in correct lifting posture (knees bent, back straight).
- Redesign the workstation to bring load closer to body (reduce $H$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Hazard" with "Risk"** — Hazard is the *source*; Risk is *likelihood × severity*. Marks deducted in 7-mark definition questions.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — Always mention dBA, m/s², ppm with numerical values.
> 3. **Wrong NIOSH constant** — Always start with $LC = 23$ kg. Using 25 kg is an instant zero in derivation steps.
> 4. **Missing the L × S matrix explanation** — Examiners expect *both* the matrix AND the formula $R = L \times S$.
> 5. **PPE in wrong order** — Hierarchy is Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. Reversing this order costs 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — Common Hazards**

- [x] **Hazard vs Risk** — Hazard = potential; Risk = probability × consequence
- [x] **Six categories** — Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial, Mechanical
- [x] **Noise limit** — 90 dBA / 8 hr; 5 dBA doubling rule
- [x] **Routes of entry (Chemicals)** — SEAS: Skin, Eyes, Airways, Stomach
- [x] **TLV types** — TWA (8 hr), STEL (15 min), Ceiling (instant)
- [x] **NIOSH Lifting** — $LC = 23$ kg; LI > 1 = increased risk
- [x] **Hierarchy of Controls** — Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Admin → PPE
- [x] **Risk Matrix** — $R = L \times S$; max 9; >6 = Stop work
- [x] **Fire Triangle** — Fuel + O₂ + Heat (all three required)
- [x] **Vibration limits** — HAV 5 m/s²; WBV 1.15 m/s²
- [x] **Standards** — ISO 45001 (modern), Factories Act 1948 (India), OSHA 29 CFR 1910 (USA), ILO-OSH 2001
- [x] **5 dBA = Half exposure time** (most-tested derivation)
- [x] **Asymmetry angle** in NIOSH: $AM = 1 - 0.0032A$
- [x] **Penalties** — Indian Factories Act: imprisonment + fine for non-compliance
- [x] **HIRA** — Hazard Identification & Risk Assessment — first step in any project

> **Golden Rule for KTU 2024 Exams:** *Always present the formula first, then state the values, then show the substitution and final answer. Examiners reward step-by-step logical flow, not just the final numerical answer.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Personal Protective Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Personal Protective Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** is defined as any equipment, device, or apparatus worn, held, or used by an employee at work for the purpose of providing protection against one or more occupational hazards, thereby minimizing the risk of injury, ill-health, or fatality arising from exposure to physical, chemical, mechanical, electrical, radiological, or biological agents in the workplace.

According to the hierarchy of controls established under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.132** and **Indian Factories Act 1948 (Section 25-A & Rule 77)**, PPE is recognized as the **last line of defense** when engineering and administrative controls are insufficient to eliminate the hazard entirely. It is a complementary, not substitutive, control measure.

**Synonyms / Related Terminology in KTU Curriculum:**
- *Personlig verneutstyr* (Norwegian equivalent used in maritime PPE standards)
- *Last Line of Defense Equipment*
- *Personal Protective Devices (PPD)*
- *Worker Safety Apparel*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Knight's Armor:**
> Imagine a medieval knight riding into battle. His chainmail does not stop the enemy from existing; it does not remove the battlefield; it does not prevent arrows from being fired. What it *does* is provide a **personal shield** around the knight's body so that IF an arrow reaches him, it cannot penetrate his skin.

PPE works exactly like this:

| Knight's Armor Component | Equivalent PPE | Hazard Protected Against |
|---|---|---|
| Helmet | Hard Hat | Falling objects, head impact |
| Gauntlets | Safety Gloves | Cuts, abrasions, chemicals |
| Steel Boots | Safety Shoes | Crush injuries, punctures, electric shock |
| Face Plate | Face Shield / Goggles | Splashes, flying debris |
| Body Plate | Coveralls / Hi-Vis Vest | Heat, sparks, visibility |

The key lesson from the analogy: **just as a knight still needs strategy and fortifications, PPE must always be combined with engineering and administrative controls** — armor alone never wins a war.

### 1.3 Why PPE Matters — Core Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to **Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) data** and the **International Labour Organization (ILO)** global workplace fatality reports, approximately **2.3 million workers globally** die annually from occupational accidents and work-related diseases. PPE, when correctly specified, fitted, and worn, has been proven to reduce workplace injury severity by **60–80%** in industrial sectors.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Alignment

| CO Code | Course Outcome Statement | This Topic Addresses |
|---|---|---|
| **CO3** | Identify and select appropriate PPE for various industrial hazards | ✅ Directly |
| **CO4** | Demonstrate knowledge of occupational health & safety legislation | ✅ Factories Act, OSHA, BIS standards |
| **CO5** | Apply safety practices in real engineering environments | ✅ Practical selection logic |

### 1.5 Legal Foundation in India

> [!NOTE]
> **Legal Basis for PPE Provision in India:**
> 1. **Indian Factories Act, 1948 — Section 25-A**: Mandates the use of PPE in hazardous operations.
> 2. **Model Rules under Factories Act — Rule 77**: Specifies PPE for eyes, feet, head, and respiratory protection.
> 3. **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** — IS 2925 (Industrial Safety Helmets), IS 4770 (Rubber Gloves), IS 15298 (Safety Footwear), IS 8521 (Respiratory Protective Devices).
> 4. **OSHA Standards (29 CFR 1910 Subpart I)** — Global benchmark referenced by multinational industries in Kerala.

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Classification Framework

### 2.1 The Hierarchy of Controls — Where PPE Fits

This is the **single most important conceptual framework** for any KTU exam question on PPE. Always start your answer with this diagram in mind:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  MOST EFFECTIVE                         │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ 1. ELIMINATION  → Remove the hazard entirely     │  │
│  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│  │ 2. SUBSTITUTION → Replace with less hazardous     │  │
│  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│  │ 3. ENGINEERING  → Guards, ventilation, isolation  │  │
│  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│  │ 4. ADMINISTRATIVE → Training, signage, SOPs       │  │
│  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────┤  │
│  │ 5. PPE          → Personal shield (LAST RESORT)   │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                   LEAST EFFECTIVE                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: Students frequently lose marks by treating PPE as the *primary* safety control. You must always remember that **PPE is the LAST resort**, applied when all higher-order controls are impractical.

### 2.2 Systematic Classification of PPE

PPE is broadly classified into **eight anatomical-protection categories**, each addressing a specific zone of the human body and a specific hazard class.

#### 2.2.1 Head Protection — Industrial Safety Helmets

- **Hazard Class**: Falling objects, impact from fixed objects, electrical shock (up to **440 V** for Class G helmets, **1000 V** for Class E).
- **Standard Reference**: **IS 2925:1984** (Indian), **ANSI Z89.1-2014** (American).
- **Types Based on Impact Resistance**:

| Type (ANSI Class) | Voltage Protection | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Class G (General)** | Up to **2,200 V** | General construction, manufacturing |
| **Class E (Electrical)** | Up to **20,000 V** | Live electrical work, power utilities |
| **Class C (Conductive)** | None | Where static electricity is a hazard (e.g., explosives handling) |

- **Components**: Outer shell (HDPE or ABS plastic), suspension harness (webbing straps), sweatband, chin strap.
- **Color Coding in India (KTU commonly tested)**:

| Color | Designated Role |
|---|---|
| **White** | Managers, Engineers, Supervisors |
| **Yellow** | General Laborers, Earth-moving operators |
| **Blue** | Technical Operators, Electricians |
| **Green** | Safety Officers, New Trainees |
| **Red** | Fire Marshals, Emergency Response |
| **Orange** | Road crews, New visitors |

#### 2.2.2 Eye and Face Protection

- **Hazard Class**: Flying particles, chemical splashes, optical radiation (UV, IR, laser), molten metal splash.
- **Types**:

| Device | Protection Level | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Safety Spectacles | Front impact only | Light chipping, grinding |
| Safety Goggles | Sealed around eyes | Chemical handling, dust |
| Face Shield | Full face | Welding, furnace operations |
| Welding Helmet | Auto-darkening filter | Arc welding, gas welding |
| Laser Safety Goggles | Wavelength-specific | Laser cutting laboratories |

- **Standard**: **IS 5983** (Indian), **ANSI Z87.1** (American), **EN 166** (European).

#### 2.2.3 Hearing Protection

- **Hazard Class**: Occupational Noise Exposure above **85 dB(A)** Time-Weighted Average (TWA) over 8 hours, per **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95**.
- **Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL)**:

$$ L_{eq,\,8h} = 85 \text{ dB(A)} $$

- **Types**:

| Device | Noise Reduction Rating (NRR) | Suitable For |
|---|---|---|
| Ear Plugs (Foam) | **29–33 dB** | Continuous high noise |
| Ear Muffs (Cups) | **20–30 dB** | Intermittent exposure |
| Canal Caps | **20–25 dB** | Quick insertion/removal |

- **Standard**: **IS 6229** (Indian), **EN 352** (European).

#### 2.2.4 Respiratory Protection

- **Hazard Class**: Airborne particulates, fumes, vapors, gases, oxygen-deficient atmospheres.
- **Hygienist's Classification (Air-Purifying Devices)**:

| Respirator Type | Filter Class | Protection Factor (APF) |
|---|---|---|
| Filtering Facepiece (N95) | Particulate only | **10** |
| Half-Face Elastomeric | Gas/vapor cartridges | **10** |
| Full-Face Elastomeric | Gas/vapor/particulate | **50** |
| PAPR (Powered) | HEPA/Chemical | **25–1000** |
| SCBA (Self-Contained) | Supplied air | **10,000** |

- **Standard**: **IS 8521**, **NIOSH 42 CFR Part 84**, **EN 143/14387**.

#### 2.2.5 Hand Protection (Gloves)

- **Material Selection Matrix**:

| Glove Material | Chemical Resistance | Mechanical Resistance | Heat Resistance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Rubber (Latex)** | Acids, alkalis (poor for oils) | Good | Poor |
| **Nitrile** | Oils, solvents, hydrocarbons | Excellent | Moderate |
| **PVC (Vinyl)** | Acids, bases | Moderate | Poor |
| **Leather** | Sparks, heat, abrasion | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Kevlar/HPPE** | Cuts | Excellent | Good |
| **Butyl Rubber** | Ketones, esters | Good | Excellent |

- **Standard**: **IS 4770**, **EN 374** (chemical), **EN 388** (mechanical, scored 1–4 on cut/abrasion).

#### 2.2.6 Foot Protection (Safety Footwear)

- **Components**: Steel toe cap (impact resistance **200 J**, compression **15 kN**), steel mid-sole (puncture resistance **1100 N**), anti-slip outsole.
- **Standards**: **IS 15298 (Part 2)**, **EN ISO 20345** (rated SB, S1, S2, S3, S5).

#### 2.2.7 Body Protection

- Categories: Coveralls, aprons, high-visibility vests (Class 1, 2, 3 per **ANSI/ISEA 107**), flame-resistant (FR) garments, chemical suits (Levels A, B, C, D per **OSHA HAZWOPER**).

#### 2.2.8 Fall Protection (Height Safety)

- **Trigger Height**: OSHA mandates fall protection at heights **≥ 1.8 m (6 feet)** in construction; in general industry at **≥ 1.2 m (4 feet)**.
- **Components**: Full-body harness (Class III per **ANSI Z359.11**), lanyard, shock absorber, anchorage connector rated for **22.2 kN (5,000 lbs)** per worker.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Threshold | Unit | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Permissible Noise Exposure (TWA) | $L_{eq} = 85$ | dB(A) | OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95 |
| 2 | Doubling Rule (Noise) | Every $+5$ dB → exposure time halves | dB / hour | OSHA Table G-16a |
| 3 | Helmet Impact Energy | $E = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | Joules (J) | IS 2925 |
| 4 | Safety Shoe Impact Load | $F \leq 15$ | kN | EN ISO 20345 |
| 5 | Mid-sole Puncture Resistance | $F \geq 1100$ | N | EN ISO 20345 |
| 6 | Fall Arrest Anchorage Force | $F_{anchor} \geq 22.2$ | kN (per worker) | ANSI Z359 |
| 7 | Assigned Protection Factor | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{C_{outside}}{C_{inside}}$ | dimensionless | OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134 |
| 8 | Oxygen Threshold (IDLH) | $O_2 \geq 19.5\%$ | volume % | OSHA |
| 9 | Head Injury Criterion (HIC) | $\text{HIC} = \left(t_2 - t_1\right) \left[\dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5}$ | dimensionless | IS 2925 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: The APF formula is the most exam-relevant. It is the **ratio of contaminant concentration outside the respirator to that inside**, and directly governs which class of respirator is permissible for a given exposure scenario.

### 2.4 Limitations of PPE — Must Be Mentioned in Any KTU Answer

> [!WARNING]
> **Why PPE is the WEAKEST Control:**
> 1. **Does not eliminate** the hazard — only reduces contact.
> 2. **Worker-dependent** — failure occurs if not worn, fitted incorrectly, or removed.
> 3. **Heat stress burden** — heavy PPE causes physiological strain.
> 4. **Communication interference** — muffles speech, limits vision.
> 5. **Maintenance burden** — cleaning, inspection, replacement required.
> 6. **Right PPE must be selected** — wrong PPE may itself become a hazard (e.g., conductive helmet near live wires).

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Decision Matrices

### 3.1 The 8-Step PPE Selection Procedure (KTU High-Yield Procedural Logic)

When asked *"How do you select PPE for a given task?"* in a 7 or 14 mark question, you **must** walk through this exact sequence:

**Step 1 — Hazard Identification**
Conduct a Job Safety Analysis (JSA) to list all physical, chemical, biological, and radiological agents present.

**Step 2 — Hazard Evaluation**
Quantify each hazard: noise level (dB), chemical concentration (ppm), temperature (°C), fall height (m).

**Step 3 — Compare Against Threshold Limits**
Check whether measured values exceed OSHA PEL, ACGIH TLV, or Indian Factory Rule limits.

**Step 4 — Apply Hierarchy of Controls**
Confirm that engineering / administrative controls are infeasible before resorting to PPE.

**Step 5 — Select PPE Type**
Match the body part at risk with the appropriate PPE category from the eight classifications.

**Step 6 — Verify Compliance with Standards**
Cross-check the selected PPE with BIS / ANSI / EN standards.

**Step 7 — Fit Testing & Training**
Conduct individual fit testing for respirators and provide training on use, cleaning, and limitations.

**Step 8 — Inspection, Maintenance & Replacement**
Establish a documented program for periodic inspection, cleaning, and end-of-life replacement.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Selecting a Respirator

**Problem Statement:**
A worker is exposed to a solvent vapor concentration of **850 ppm** in a paint mixing room. The OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for the solvent is **100 ppm (8-hour TWA)**. The available respirators have the following Assigned Protection Factors (APFs):

| Respirator | APF |
|---|---|
| Half-face air-purifying | 10 |
| Full-face air-purifying | 50 |
| PAPR (loose-fitting hood) | 25 |
| SCBA (positive pressure) | 10,000 |

**Step 1 — Calculate Hazard Ratio:**

$$
HR = \frac{C_{measured}}{PEL} = \frac{850 \text{ ppm}}{100 \text{ ppm}} = 8.5
$$

**Step 2 — Select Required APF:**
The respirator's APF must be **≥ Hazard Ratio**.

$$
APF_{required} = 8.5
$$

**Step 3 — Match with Available Respirators:**

| Respirator | APF | Meets Requirement? |
|---|---|---|
| Half-face air-purifying | 10 | ✅ Yes ($10 \geq 8.5$) |
| Full-face air-purifying | 50 | ✅ Yes (over-spec) |
| PAPR | 25 | ✅ Yes (over-spec) |
| SCBA | 10,000 | ✅ Yes (over-spec, not needed) |

**Step 4 — Decision:**
Select the **Half-face air-purifying respirator** with appropriate organic vapor cartridge — it is the **minimum adequate protection** while preserving worker comfort and visibility.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Noise Exposure Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A boiler room worker is exposed to noise levels of **95 dB(A)** for 4 hours and **88 dB(A)** for 4 hours in an 8-hour shift. Determine whether the exposure exceeds the OSHA PEL of **85 dB(A) TWA** using the **Time-Weighted Average (TWA)** formula.

**Step 1 — Reference Formula (OSHA Method):**

$$
TWA = 16.61 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{T} \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i \cdot 10^{L_i / 16.61}\right) + 90
$$

But in KTU exam context, the simplified **Noise Dose** method is preferred:

$$
D(\%) = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \cdots
$$

where $C_i$ = actual exposure time at level $L_i$, and $T_i$ = OSHA reference duration at that level.

**Step 2 — OSHA Reference Table (Table G-16a):**

| Noise Level dB(A) | Reference Duration (hours) |
|---|---|
| 85 | 16.0 |
| 88 | 6.06 |
| 90 | 4.0 |
| 95 | 1.78 |
| 100 | 0.50 |

**Step 3 — Calculate Dose:**

$$
D = \frac{4}{1.78} + \frac{4}{6.06}
$$

$$
D = 2.247 + 0.660 = 2.907
$$

Converting to percentage:

$$
D(\%) = 2.907 \times 100 = 290.7\%
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
Since $D = 290.7\% \gg 100\%$, the **OSHA PEL is exceeded by ~2.9 times**. Required controls:

1. Engineering controls (sound-dampening enclosures, isolation barriers).
2. If still excessive, **mandatory hearing protection** with NRR $\geq$ 15 dB.

**Step 5 — Compute Required NRR (OSHA derating formula):**

$$
NRR_{required} = \frac{TWA_{measured} - 85 - 7}{0.5}
$$

$$
NRR_{required} = \frac{90.7 - 85 - 7}{0.5} = \frac{-1.3}{0.5}
$$

> A negative result indicates that even maximum hearing protection is insufficient — engineering controls become **mandatory**.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Body Part vs. Hazard

| Body Part | Hazard Type | Recommended PPE | Key Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Head | Falling objects, impact | Hard hat (Type II, Class E) | IS 2925 |
| Eyes | Flying chips, splash | Safety spectacles (Z87+) | IS 5983 |
| Ears | > 85 dB(A) TWA | Ear plugs / muffs | IS 6229 |
| Lungs | Dust / vapor / gas | Air-purifying respirator | IS 8521 |
| Hands | Chemicals, cuts, heat | Nitrile / Kevlar gloves | IS 4770 / EN 388 |
| Feet | Falling loads, puncture | Steel-toe boots (S3) | IS 15298 |
| Body | Arc flash, chemicals | FR coveralls, chemical suit | NFPA 70E / OSHA |
| Fall | Height > 1.8 m | Full-body harness + lanyard | ANSI Z359 |

### 3.5 PPE Program Effectiveness — Quantitative Logic

The **Failure Mode and Effect Analysis (FMEA)** can be applied to PPE programs:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

where $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence probability (1–10), $D$ = Detection difficulty (1–10).

**Interpretation**:
- $RPN < 50$: PPE program adequate.
- $50 \leq RPN < 150$: Improvement required.
- $RPN \geq 150$: Critical — engineering controls must replace PPE.

> [!NOTE]
> This quantitative scoring is rarely a direct exam question but appears in KTU **Case Study** and **Module-Internal Choice** questions for engineering disciplines.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define PPE. State the hierarchy of controls and explain why PPE is placed at the bottom.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

Personal Protective Equipment refers to any device or appliance worn or used by a worker to provide protection against one or more occupational hazards.

The hierarchy of controls, in descending order of effectiveness, is:

1. Elimination
2. Substitution
3. Engineering Controls
4. Administrative Controls
5. **Personal Protective Equipment**

PPE is placed at the bottom because it does **not eliminate** the hazard but merely creates a barrier between the hazard and the worker. It is **worker-dependent**, prone to failure due to incorrect fitting, removal, or non-compliance, and provides **no protection to others** in the vicinity. Hence it is used only when higher-order controls are impractical.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for hierarchy + 1 for the reasoning of PPE's position)*

#### **Question 2: List any four types of respiratory protective equipment with their assigned protection factors (APFs).** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| # | Respirator Type | Assigned Protection Factor |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Filtering Facepiece (N95) | 10 |
| 2 | Half-face Elastomeric APR | 10 |
| 3 | Full-face Elastomeric APR | 50 |
| 4 | Powered Air-Purifying Respirator (PAPR) | 25–1000 |
| 5 | Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA) | 10,000 |

*(3 marks: 0.5 for each correct entry + 0.5 for the APF column)*

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Classify Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) based on the body part protected. Discuss in detail the selection criteria for head protection and respiratory protection, citing relevant Indian Standards.** `(7 Marks)`

**Model Answer:**

**Classification Based on Body Part:**

PPE is classified into **eight** categories based on the body part it protects:

1. **Head Protection** — Hard hats / safety helmets
2. **Eye and Face Protection** — Goggles, spectacles, face shields
3. **Hearing Protection** — Ear plugs and ear muffs
4. **Respiratory Protection** — Air-purifying respirators, SCBA
5. **Hand Protection** — Gloves of various materials
6. **Foot Protection** — Safety shoes and gumboots
7. **Body Protection** — Coveralls, aprons, high-visibility vests
8. **Fall Protection** — Full-body harness, lanyard, lifeline

**Selection Criteria for Head Protection:**

1. **Type of Hazard**: Falling objects, impact from fixed objects, electrical shock.
2. **Voltage Class**: Class G (up to 2200 V) for general use; Class E (up to 20,000 V) for electrical work; Class C for static-sensitive environments.
3. **Suspension Harness Quality**: Adjustable webbing (6-point or 8-point suspension preferred).
4. **Comfort and Weight**: Should not exceed **400 g** for prolonged use.
5. **Color Coding**: As per site convention (white for supervisors, yellow for laborers).
6. **Compliance Standard**: **IS 2925:1984** and **ANSI Z89.1-2014**.
7. **Accessories**: Chin straps for working at height, slots for face shield mounting.

**Selection Criteria for Respiratory Protection:**

1. **Oxygen Concentration**: If $O_2 < 19.5\%$, SCBA is mandatory.
2. **Nature of Contaminant**: Particulate, gas, vapor, or combination.
3. **Concentration of Contaminant**: Determines the required **Assigned Protection Factor (APF)**.

$$
\text{Required APF} = \frac{C_{measured}}{PEL}
$$

4. **Type of Operation**: Routine (APR), IDLH (SCBA), or emergency response.
5. **Worker Comfort and Fit**: Quantitative or qualitative fit testing mandatory.
6. **Cartridge Change Schedule**: Based on breakthrough time and OSHA guidance.
7. **Compliance Standard**: **IS 8521** and **NIOSH 42 CFR Part 84**.

*(Marks: 2 for classification + 2.5 for head protection + 2.5 for respiratory protection)*

---

**(b) An industrial worker is exposed to noise levels of 92 dB(A) for 6 hours and 86 dB(A) for 2 hours in an 8-hour shift. Calculate the percentage noise dose and determine whether OSHA's Permissible Exposure Limit is exceeded. If exceeded, recommend an appropriate hearing protection device.** `(7 Marks)`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Reference OSHA Table G-16a:**

| Noise Level dB(A) | Reference Duration $T_i$ (hours) |
|---|---|
| 90 | 4.0 |
| 85 | 16.0 |

We interpolate for **92 dB(A)** and **86 dB(A)**.

For 92 dB(A):

$$
T_{92} = T_{90} \times 10^{(90-92)/10} = 4.0 \times 10^{-0.2} = 4.0 \times 0.631 = 2.524 \text{ hours}
$$

For 86 dB(A):

$$
T_{86} = T_{85} \times 10^{(85-86)/10} = 16.0 \times 10^{-0.1} = 16.0 \times 0.794 = 12.70 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Noise Dose:**

$$
D = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2}
$$

$$
D = \frac{6}{2.524} + \frac{2}{12.70}
$$

$$
D = 2.377 + 0.157 = 2.534
$$

**Step 3 — Express as Percentage:**

$$
D(\%) = 2.534 \times 100 = 253.4\%
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

Since $D = 253.4\% \gg 100\%$, the OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL of 85 dB(A) TWA) is **exceeded by approximately 2.5 times**.

**Step 5 — Calculate the Required NRR:**

The measured TWA must first be calculated. Using the standard OSHA formula:

$$
TWA = 16.61 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{8}\left[6 \cdot 10^{92/16.61} + 2 \cdot 10^{86/16.61}\right]\right) + 90
$$

$$
TWA \approx 91.2 \text{ dB(A)}
$$

Required NRR to bring exposure below 85 dB(A):

$$
NRR_{required} = \frac{TWA - 85 - 7}{0.5} = \frac{91.2 - 92}{0.5}
$$

The negative result indicates that even **maximum NRR ear muffs** would not adequately protect. Hence, **engineering controls (sound enclosures, isolation barriers, vibration dampers) are mandatory**. Until such controls are installed, double hearing protection (ear plugs + ear muffs) must be issued.

*(Marks: 2 for dose formula + 2 for calculation + 1 for interpretation + 2 for recommendation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) Discuss in detail the various types of hand protection used in industries. Provide a comparative matrix for material selection based on chemical and mechanical resistance.** `(7 Marks)`

**Model Answer:**

Hands are the most commonly injured body part in industrial workplaces, accounting for nearly **30%** of all reportable injuries. Hence, glove selection is critical.

**Types of Industrial Gloves:**

1. **Leather Gloves** — Heat, sparks, abrasion (welding, foundry).
2. **Cotton/Fabric Gloves** — Light-duty handling, anti-scratch.
3. **Rubber (Latex) Gloves** — Biological agents, mild acids.
4. **Nitrile Gloves** — Oils, hydrocarbons, solvents (chemical industry).
5. **PVC (Vinyl) Gloves** — Acids and bases, food processing.
6. **Neoprene Gloves** — Wide-spectrum chemicals, fuels.
7. **Butyl Rubber Gloves** — Ketones, esters, aldehydes.
8. **Kevlar / HPPE Gloves** — Cut resistance (sheet metal, glass handling).
9. **Metal Mesh Gloves** — Highest cut resistance (meat processing, blade work).
10. **Electrically Insulated Gloves** — Live electrical work (Class 00 to Class 4).

**Comparative Material Selection Matrix:**

| Glove Material | Chemical Resistance (Oils/Solvents) | Cut/Abrasion Resistance | Heat Resistance | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Latex | Poor | Moderate | Poor | Low |
| Nitrile | Excellent | Excellent | Moderate | Moderate |
| PVC | Moderate | Moderate | Poor | Low |
| Neoprene | Excellent | Good | Good | High |
| Butyl Rubber | Excellent (ketones) | Good | Excellent | Very High |
| Leather | Limited | Excellent | Excellent | Moderate |
| Kevlar | N/A | Excellent | Good | High |

**Selection Logic:**
The choice depends on:
- **Hazard Identification** (chemical class, mechanical risk, thermal risk).
- **Degradation and Permeation Data** (manufacturer's chemical compatibility chart).
- **EN 388 Mechanical Rating** (4-digit score for abrasion, cut, tear, puncture).
- **EN 374 Chemical Permeation Time** (must be > duration of exposure).
- **Comfort and Dexterity** (trade-off with protection).
- **Standards**: **IS 4770**, **EN 374**, **EN 388**, **ASTM F2675** (arc-rated gloves).

*(Marks: 2 for listing types + 3 for matrix + 2 for selection logic)*

---

**(b) What is the significance of fit testing for respirators? Describe the qualitative and quantitative fit test methods. A worker is exposed to silica dust at 4.5 mg/m³ for 5 hours per day. The OSHA PEL for silica is 0.05 mg/m³. Determine the minimum required APF.** `(7 Marks)`

**Model Answer:**

**Significance of Fit Testing:**

Fit testing ensures that the respirator's face-to-face seal is adequate for the individual wearer. Without proper fit, contaminated air bypasses the filter through leak paths, rendering the respirator ineffective. A respirator with APF = 10 that leaks 30% only offers an effective protection of 7 — exposing the worker.

**Qualitative Fit Test (QLFT):**

- Uses the worker's **sensory detection** (taste, smell, or irritation) of a challenge agent.
- Challenge agents: **Saccharin** (sweet taste), **Bitrex** (bitter taste), **Isoamyl acetate** (banana smell), or **Irritant smoke**.
- Pass/Fail basis — does not provide a numerical fit factor.
- Suitable for half-face APRs only.

**Quantitative Fit Test (QNFT):**

- Measures **actual fit factor** using instrumentation.
- Methods:
  - **Generated Aerosol** (CNC): Measures particle concentration inside vs. outside.
  - **Ambient Aerosol** (PortaCount): Uses ambient dust particles.
  - **Controlled Negative Pressure (CNC-REDON)**: Measures air leakage rate.
- Provides a numerical fit factor — required for full-face and tight-fitting respirators.

**Required APF Calculation:**

Hazard Ratio:

$$
HR = \frac{C_{measured}}{PEL} = \frac{4.5 \text{ mg/m}^3}{0.05 \text{ mg/m}^3} = 90
$$

Therefore:

$$
APF_{required} = 90
$$

**Decision:** The minimum respirator must have **APF ≥ 90**. Available options:
- Full-face APR (APF = 50) — **insufficient**.
- PAPR tight-fitting (APF = 50–1000) — **adequate**.
- SCBA (APF = 10,000) — **over-spec**.

**Recommendation:** A **tight-fitting PAPR with HEPA filter** is the minimum adequate respirator, with mandatory quantitative fit testing achieving a fit factor of **≥ 100** for half-face or **≥ 500** for full-face.

*(Marks: 2 for fit test significance + 2 for method description + 3 for APF calculation and selection)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on PPE Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the hierarchy of controls** — Always state that PPE is the last resort (loses up to 1 mark if omitted).
> 2. **Not quoting standards** — Indian Standards (IS codes) and OSHA/ANSI references carry dedicated mark weightage.
> 3. **Confusing APF with Filter Class** — APF describes overall respirator effectiveness; filter class (P100, N95) describes only the filter element.
> 4. **Misapplying the OSHA NRR derating formula** — The "-7 dB" and "÷0.5" corrections are frequently forgotten.
