```
│      └── Safety control: Limit switches, mechanical stops
├── 3. Hydraulic
│      ├── Risk: Moderate-High (stored energy)
│      └── Safety control: Pressure relief, lockout valves
├── 4. Pneumatic
│      ├── Risk: Moderate (compressibility hazards)
│      └── Safety control: FRL unit, exhaust silencers
├── 5. Automatic / CNC Servo
│      ├── Risk: Lower (isolated operation)
│      └── Safety control: Light curtains, interlocks, ESTOP
└── 6. Gravity / Vibratory
       ├── Risk: Variable
       └── Safety control: Guarding against ejection
```

## 2.2 Classification of Cutting Mechanisms

| Type | Energy Form | Example | Primary Hazard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Shearing** | Kinetic | Power press, shear, guillotine | Crush, amputation |
| **Abrasive** | Friction | Grinder, abrasive wheel | Burst, eye injury |
| **Thermal – Oxy-fuel** | Combustion | Gas cutter | Fire, UV burns |
| **Thermal – Plasma** | Ionised gas | Plasma cutter | Arc flash, toxic fumes |
| **Thermal – Laser** | Coherent light | Laser cutter | Eye damage, fire |
| **Abrasive Water Jet** | High-pressure water | AWJ cutter | Injection injury, noise |
| **EDM (Wire/Sinker)** | Electrical discharge | Die-sinker EDM | Electrical, fire (dielectric) |
| **Chip-removal (Machining)** | Multi-point cutting | Lathe, milling, drilling | Entanglement, chips |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the high-yield formula table for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. Memorise units carefully.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units (SI) | Safety Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Speed $V$ | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min | Determines tool temp & guarding radius |
| 2 | Feed Rate $f_n$ | $f_n = f \times N$ | mm/min | Determines operator exposure time |
| 3 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $\text{MRR} = V \times f \times d$ | mm³/min | Higher MRR → higher energy & hazard |
| 4 | Taylor's Tool Life | $V T^{n} = C$ | m/min, min | Dictates safe replacement schedule |
| 5 | Stopping Distance (Press) | $D_s = \tfrac{1}{2} a T_s^{2}$ | m | Must be < operator reach |
| 6 | Stored Hydraulic Energy | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} P V$ | J | LOTO critical before maintenance |
| 7 | Stored Pneumatic Energy | $E = \dfrac{P_1 V}{\gamma - 1}\left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]$ | J | Bleed-down before access |
| 8 | Burst Pressure (Thin wall) | $P_b = \dfrac{2 \, t \, \sigma_u}{D}$ | Pa | Limits max safe operating pressure |
| 9 | Safe Noise Limit | $L_p \le 85 \text{ dB(A)}$ (8 hr) | dB(A) | Hearing protection trigger |
| 10 | Hydraulic Press Force | $F = P \times A$ | N | Guard sizing & die selection |
| 11 | Two-Hand Trip Reaction Time | $t_{th} \ge 0.5$ | s | ISO 13851 minimum |
| 12 | Abrasive Wheel Peripheral Speed | $V_w = \pi D N$ | m/s | Must be < wheel rated max |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU formula sheets, **never use bare vertical pipes inside a table cell** — write `\vert` or use parentheses.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Topic Matters

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Industrial Linkage:** Kerala's growing MSME sector in **food processing, coir, cashew, marine, and rubber** industries uses feeding and cutting machinery extensively. The **Directorate of Factories & Boilers, Kerala**, mandates safeguarding under Rule 55–61 of the **Kerala Factories Rules, 1957**. Safety engineers in such plants are expected to identify the *energy source*, *failure mode*, and *protective device* for every cutting/feeding station — exactly what KTU ESE questions test.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stopping Distance of a Mechanical Power Press

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Context:** A hydraulic power press has a ram of mass **m = 250 kg**, descending at velocity **v₀ = 0.6 m/s** at the moment of operator hand-injury emergency. The press brake engages producing a constant deceleration **a = −4 m/s²**. Compute the *stopping distance* and verify safety.

### Step-by-Step Derivation (Exhaustive):

**Step 1:** Identify kinematic model.
Using Newton's equation of motion under constant deceleration:

$$
v^{2} = v_{0}^{2} + 2 \, a \, s
$$

**Step 2:** Apply final condition.
At safe stop, final velocity $v = 0$.

$$
0 = v_{0}^{2} + 2 \, a \, s
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for stopping distance **s**.

$$
s = -\dfrac{v_{0}^{2}}{2a}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute given numerical values (with unit consistency check).
- $v_{0} = 0.6$ m/s
- $a = -4$ m/s²  (negative sign indicates retardation)

$$
s = -\dfrac{(0.6)^{2}}{2 \times (-4)}
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate numerator.

$$
(0.6)^{2} = 0.36 \; \text{m}^{2}\text{/s}^{2}
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate denominator.

$$
2 \times (-4) = -8 \; \text{m/s}^{2}
$$

**Step 7:** Final numerical computation.

$$
s = -\dfrac{0.36}{-8} = 0.045 \; \text{m} = 45 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 8:** Engineering interpretation.
The ram travels **45 mm** after the emergency stop signal. For hand safety, this distance must be **less than the operator's reach into the danger zone minus the safety margin**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $s = 45$ mm. If the operator's hand can reach only up to 30 mm into the die during the down-stroke, the design is **unsafe** — guarding or a *light curtain retrigger system* must reduce $v_0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stored Hydraulic Energy in an Accumulators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Context:** A bladder-type hydraulic accumulator has gas pre-charge $P_2 = 50$ bar, system pressure $P_1 = 200$ bar, volume $V = 10$ L. Compute stored energy and discuss lockout/tagout (LOTO) implications.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Polytropic gas law for compression work.

$$
W = \dfrac{P_1 V}{\gamma - 1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Convert all pressures to SI base units.
- $P_1 = 200 \text{ bar} = 200 \times 10^{5}$ Pa $= 2.0 \times 10^{7}$ Pa
- $P_2 = 50 \text{ bar} = 5.0 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $V = 10$ L $= 0.01$ m³
- For nitrogen (isentropic), $\gamma = 1.4$

**Step 3:** Compute ratio $P_2 / P_1$.

$$
\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{6}}{2.0 \times 10^{7}} = 0.25
$$

**Step 4:** Compute exponent.

$$
\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate $(0.25)^{0.2857}$.

$$
0.25^{0.2857} \approx 0.6522
$$

**Step 6:** Compute bracket term.

$$
0.6522 - 1 = -0.3478
$$

**Step 7:** Compute denominator $(\gamma - 1) = 0.4$.

**Step 8:** Substitute.

$$
W = \dfrac{(2.0 \times 10^{7})(0.01)}{0.4} \times (-0.3478)
$$

**Step 9:** Multiply.

$$
W = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^{5}}{0.4} \times (-0.3478) = 5.0 \times 10^{5} \times (-0.3478)
$$

**Step 10:** Final energy.

$$
W \approx -1.739 \times 10^{5} \; \text{J} \approx -174 \; \text{kJ}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Magnitude:** $\vert W \vert \approx 174$ kJ of stored energy — equivalent to the kinetic energy of a 1-tonne car at 56 km/h. **LOTO and bleed-down valves are mandatory before maintenance.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cutting Speed & MRR for a Turning Operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Context:** A mild-steel shaft of diameter **D = 80 mm** is turned on a lathe at spindle speed **N = 600 rpm** with feed **f = 0.3 mm/rev** and depth of cut **d = 2 mm**. Compute cutting speed, feed rate, and MRR.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Cutting speed formula.

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $D = 80$ mm, $N = 600$ rpm.

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi \times 80 \times 600}{1000}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate numerator.

$$
\pi \times 80 \times 600 = 150796.4
$$

**Step 4:** Divide.

$$
V = \dfrac{150796.4}{1000} = 150.8 \; \text{m/min}
$$

**Step 5:** Feed rate formula.

$$
f_n = f \times N
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute.

$$
f_n = 0.3 \times 600 = 180 \; \text{mm/min}
$$

**Step 7:** Material removal rate formula.

$$
\text{MRR} = V \times f \times d
$$

**Step 8:** Convert $V$ to mm/min.

$$
V = 150.8 \times 1000 = 150800 \; \text{mm/min}
$$

**Step 9:** Substitute (using mm/min for V).

$$
\text{MRR} = 150800 \times 0.3 \times 2
$$

**Step 10:** Evaluate.

$$
\text{MRR} = 90480 \; \text{mm}^{3}\text{/min} \approx 9.05 \times 10^{4} \; \text{mm}^{3}\text{/min}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Safety takeaway:** Higher MRR correlates to higher heat generation, chip ejection velocity, and noise — all of which scale the guarding, PPE, and ventilation requirements.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hazard ↔ Control Mapping

| Hazard | Energy Source | Likely Failure Mode | Hierarchy-of-Control Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ram crash injury | Hydraulic / mechanical | Sticking valve | **Eliminate** → two-hand trip; **Engineer** → light curtain; **Admin** → SOP |
| Abrasive wheel burst | Centrifugal | Over-speed | Wheel rpm matching; ring-test; guard ≥ 1/2 wheel diameter |
| Laser retinal burn | Coherent light | Reflection | Class 1 enclosure; interlocks; wavelength-specific goggles |
| Chip entanglement | Rotating tool | Loose clothing | Chip guard; close-fitting clothing; hair net |
| Stored hydraulic release | Pressurised fluid | Fitting rupture | LOTO; bleed-down valve; whip-check on hoses |
| Oxy-fuel flashback | Combustion | Leak at regulator | flashback arrestor; reverse-flow check valves |
| Pneumatic injection | Compressed air | Hose burst | Pressure regulator; hose rating 4× working P |
| Guillotine shear | Mechanical | Repeat stroke | Foot-pedal guarded; two-hand; pull-back device |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between "feeding mechanism" and "cutting mechanism" in an engineering industry with two relevant examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation):**

| Aspect | Feeding Mechanism | Cutting Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Delivers material/tool to cutting zone | Removes material to shape workpiece |
| Examples | (1) Vibratory bowl feeder in cashew grading (2) Hydraulic carriage in ship plate cutting | (1) Plasma torch (2) Abrasive cutoff wheel |

*(Examiner awards: 1 mark for clear distinction, 1 mark for two feed examples, 1 mark for two cutting examples.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three safety hazards associated with hydraulic feeding systems and the corresponding control measures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Sudden pressure release** → Install pressure relief valve + LOTO. *(1 mark)*
2. **Hose whip** → Use whip-check cables and hose burst sleeves. *(1 mark)*
3. **Stored energy in accumulator** → Bleed-down valve + safe discharge procedure before maintenance. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module internal choice → answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a CNC plasma cutting workstation, identifying all major cutting and feeding subsystems. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A CNC plasma cutting workstation integrates three major subsystems:

1. **CNC Controller & Drive System (Feeding subsystem):** Generates G-code-driven motion commands. Drives X-axis gantry (rack & pinion) and Y-axis carriage (ballscrew). The **feed mechanism** controls torch height and traverse velocity. *(2 marks)*

2. **Plasma Power Unit & Torch (Cutting subsystem):** Converts electrical energy (typically 380 V, 3-phase) into a constricted arc plasma jet at 12,000–25,000 °C. Gas supply (O₂ / N₂ / air) is regulated through a gas console. *(2 marks)*

3. **Safety Interlock Layer:** Includes **arc-voltage height sensing**, **ohmic contact sensing**, **light curtain around perimeter**, **emergency stop**, **FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) for gas line**, and **ventilation hood for fumes**. *(2 marks)*

**Block flow:**
CNC Controller → Drives X/Y/Z → Torch Holder → Plasma Arc → Cut Plate → Slag & Fume Extraction → Interlock Feedback

**Conclusion:** Modern plasma workstations exemplify *integration of controlled feeding (CNC) with thermal cutting (plasma)* — every safety-critical function is sensor-monitored and PLC-interlocked. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b)** A hydraulic power press has a ram mass of 300 kg, descending velocity 0.8 m/s at the moment of emergency stop. The brake provides a uniform deceleration of 5 m/s². Compute the stopping distance and comment on whether the design is safe if the operator can reach 25 mm into the die during down-stroke. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
$m = 300$ kg (not directly used for kinematic distance)
$v_0 = 0.8$ m/s
$a = -5$ m/s² (retardation)
$s_{\text{safe reach}} = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m

**Step 1:** Apply $v^2 = v_0^2 + 2 a s$ with $v = 0$:

$$
0 = (0.8)^{2} + 2 \times (-5) \times s
$$

**Step 2:** Solve:

$$
s = \dfrac{v_0^{2}}{2a} = \dfrac{0.64}{10} = 0.064 \; \text{m} = 64 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with safe reach.

$$
s_{\text{computed}} = 64 \; \text{mm} \quad \text{vs.} \quad s_{\text{safe reach}} = 25 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion:

Since $64 \text{ mm} > 25 \text{ mm}$, the **design is UNSAFE**. The ram travels 64 mm after the emergency stop, which exceeds operator reach. Required actions:

- Reduce $v_0$ by using a **two-hand trip with anti-repeat** (typical $v_0 \le 0.1$ m/s).
- Install **light curtain + safety PLC** that triggers braking **before** ram enters danger zone.
- Conduct **risk assessment per ISO 12100** and re-engineer. *(1 mark for comment + corrective action)*

**Marks distribution:** Formula (1), substitution (1), calculation (2), comparison (1), conclusion + corrective action (2).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the classification of cutting mechanisms used in engineering industries. For each class, identify one major hazard and the corresponding engineering control. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Class | Example | Major Hazard | Engineering Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Shearing** | Power press, shear | Crush, amputation | Two-hand trip + light curtain |
| **Abrasive** | Bench grinder, cutoff wheel | Wheel burst, eye injury | Wheel guard ≥ ½ wheel dia + face shield |
| **Thermal – Oxy-fuel** | Gas cutter | Flashback, UV burns | Flashback arrestor + tinted goggles |
| **Thermal – Plasma** | Plasma cutter | Arc flash, fume inhalation | Enclosure + fume extractor + arc-rated PPE |
| **Thermal – Laser** | CO₂ / fibre laser cutter | Retinal damage | Class 1 enclosure + interlocked door |
| **Abrasive Water Jet** | AWJ | High-pressure injection, noise | Hose burst containment + ear protection |
| **Chip-removal (Machining)** | Lathe, milling | Entanglement, chip injury | Fixed chip guard + close clothing SOP |

**Marks:** 1 mark per row × 7 rows = 7 marks (table format acceptable).

---

**(b)** A pneumatic clamping and feeding system operates at 6 bar with a 20-litre receiver tank. Compute the stored pneumatic energy (assume isothermal compression, γ = 1.0 equivalent for isothermal, and discharge to atmospheric pressure). Comment on safety. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
$P_1 = 6$ bar $= 6 \times 10^{5}$ Pa
$P_2 = 1.013 \times 10^{5}$ Pa (atmospheric)
$V = 20$ L $= 0.020$ m³

**Step 1:** For isothermal expansion of compressed gas to atmosphere:

$$
W = P_1 V \ln\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
W = (6 \times 10^{5}) \times 0.020 \times \ln\left(\dfrac{6 \times 10^{5}}{1.013 \times 10^{5}}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute ratio.

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{6}{1.013} \approx 5.923
$$

**Step 4:** Take natural logarithm.

$$
\ln(5.923) \approx 1.779
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate.

$$
W = 12000 \times 1.779 \approx 21348 \; \text{J} \approx 21.35 \; \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 6:** Safety comment.

Stored energy of **~21 kJ** in a small receiver is substantial — equivalent to the muzzle energy of a rifle. **Mandatory safety measures:**

- Bleed-down valve and pressure gauge at every isolation point.
- Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) set at 110% of working pressure.
- LOTO procedure before any maintenance.
- Safety distance calculation per ASME Section VIII for pressure vessels.

**Marks:** Formula (1), substitution (1), calculation (3), safety comment (2).

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss zones identified by KTU board examiners (2018–2024 papers):**
>
> 1. **Confusing feed rate units** (mm/rev vs mm/min) — costs 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert bar → Pa** in energy/stored-energy problems — entire calculation marked wrong.
> 3. **Not stating units** in final answers — KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 4. **Skipping safety commentary** in numerical answers — KTU 2024 awards *application marks* (2 of 7) only when safety interpretation is explicitly stated.
> 5. **Writing "two-hand control" without specifying "anti-repeat / anti-tie-down"** — examiners expect ISO 13851 vocabulary.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin to Wall):**

- ✅ **Feeding mechanism** = delivery of material/tool; **Cutting mechanism** = material removal action.
- ✅ **Cutting speed $V = \pi D N / 1000$** (m/min) — *memorise units*.
- ✅ **MRR = $V \times f \times d$** — higher MRR → higher hazard.
- ✅ **Stopping distance $s = v_0^2 / (2a)$** — must be less than operator reach into danger zone.
- ✅ **Hydraulic stored energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2} P V$** for simple accumulator model.
- ✅ **Pneumatic stored energy** uses polytropic equation (γ = 1.4 adiabatic / 1.0 isothermal).
- ✅ **Abrasive wheel** peripheral speed must be **< wheel rated maximum** (typically 33–80 m/s).
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls** — Eliminate → Substitute → Engineer → Admin → PPE.
- ✅ **Mandatory KTU-cited standards:** ISO 12100, ISO 13849, ISO 13851, OSHA 29 CFR 1910, Factories Act 1948, Kerala Factories Rules 1957.
- ✅ **Two-hand trip** minimum reaction time = **0.5 s** (ISO 13851).
- ✅ **Safe noise exposure** ceiling = **85 dB(A)** for 8 hours.
- ✅ Always include **safety comment** in numerical answers — KTU rewards application-level thinking.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hand or Foot-Operated Press** is a manually actuated mechanical forming machine that uses the muscular effort of an operator — applied either through the **hands (via levers, handles, or cranks)** or through the **feet (via treadles, pedals, or foot-stompers)** — to drive a ram (or slide) downwards against a workpiece placed on a die, thereby performing operations such as blanking, piercing, bending, drawing, embossing, riveting, or stamping.

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), hand/foot-operated presses fall under the broader classification of **Power Presses and Manually Operated Presses**, governed for safety purposes by the **Indian Factories Act, 1948 (Section 21 & 28)** and the **IS 12474 / IS 12631** standards on mechanical power press safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Although these presses are *manually actuated*, they store mechanical energy during operation (through over-center toggle linkages, springs, or flywheels). Hence, the kinetic energy at the point of operation can cause **severe crushing, shearing, and amputation injuries** identical in severity to powered presses.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **heavy office paper punch** — but scaled up to several tonnes. You place a metal sheet under the punch, then pull down a long handle with both hands (or step on a foot pedal). The handle's mechanical advantage converts your small force into a massive crushing force on the metal sheet.

The danger arises because:
1. Your **hands may still be under the punch** when it descends (slow reflexes vs. fast moving ram).
2. A **repeat (continuous) stroke** mechanism may cause the ram to fire multiple times unexpectedly.
3. A **single-stroke clutch** failure could cause an unintended full-force descent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Manual does NOT mean safe. The ram of a foot press can descend with a force of several tonnes in a fraction of a second. Safety engineering for these presses focuses on **guarding the point of operation** and ensuring the operator's hands/feet are mechanically isolated from the danger zone at the moment of stroke.

### 1.3 Classification of Hand/Foot-Operated Presses

| Type | Actuation | Typical Capacity | Typical Application |
|------|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Hand Lever Press (Arch Press)** | Hand lever | 1–10 tonnes | Small blanking, stamping, embossing |
| **Toggle Press (Over-Center)** | Hand lever via toggle linkage | 5–50 tonnes | Piercing, forming |
| **Foot/Treadle Press** | Foot pedal | 1–20 tonnes | Riveting, light forming |
| **Bench Press (Hand Screw)** | Hand wheel + screw | 0.5–5 tonnes | Jewelry, precision blanking |
| **Combination Press** | Hand OR foot | 2–15 tonnes | General workshop |
| **Pneumatic-Mechanical Foot Press** | Foot pedal + air cylinder assist | 5–100 tonnes | High-speed blanking |

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Operator hand speed (reflex withdrawal):** **1500 mm/s** (used in safety distance calculations, ANSI B11.19 / IS 12474).
- **Typical closing velocity of mechanical press ram at point of operation:** **100–500 mm/s** — significantly faster than human reflex.
- **Stopping time of press after de-energization:** **0.05–0.20 seconds** depending on clutch/brake design.
- **Standard safety reach distance formula (K):** **K = 1.6 × Ts + 63.8 mm** (where Ts = stopping time in seconds), per **ANSI B11.19-2019** adopted by IS 12474.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Breakdown

#### Step 1: Energy Storage & Transmission Chain

Manual presses convert **human mechanical energy** through a sequence of stages:

$$
\text{Human Effort} \rightarrow \text{Lever/Toggle Linkage} \rightarrow \text{Clutch Engagement} \rightarrow \text{Flywheel/Kinetic Energy} \rightarrow \text{Ram Descent} \rightarrow \text{Workpiece Deformation}
$$

#### Step 2: The "Why" Behind Each Component

- **Lever / Foot Pedal:** Provides **mechanical advantage** so a small human force (≈200 N typical hand push, ≈500 N typical foot push) generates a much larger ram force.
- **Toggle / Over-Center Linkage:** Holds the ram at bottom dead centre (BDC) without continuous operator effort — but this is also the **most dangerous moment** if the operator's hands are still in the die area.
- **Clutch (Single-Stroke or Repeat):** Engages the flywheel to the crankshaft, allowing the ram to complete exactly one revolution.
- **Flywheel:** Stores rotational kinetic energy to deliver the **impact force** required for blanking/piercing.
- **Brake:** Stops the crankshaft rotation at the precise top dead centre (TDC) position, ready for the next stroke.
- **Ram & Slide:** The moving die member that carries the punch.

#### Step 3: The Stroke Cycle

$$
\text{TDC} \xrightarrow{\text{Clutch ON}} \text{Rotation} \xrightarrow{\text{180°}} \text{BDC (Working)} \xrightarrow{\text{180°}} \text{TDC} \xrightarrow{\text{Brake ON}} \text{Stop}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Safety Principle:** The total cycle time of a manual press is **shorter than human reaction time**. Therefore, the only reliable safety method is **physical guarding or restraint**, not operator vigilance.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Symbols / Units | Application |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| Mechanical Advantage (Lever) | $\text{MA} = \dfrac{L_e}{L_o}$ | $L_e$ = effort arm, $L_o$ = load arm (m) | Force amplification |
| Ram Force (Lever Press) | $F_r = F_h \times \dfrac{L_e}{L_o} \times \eta$ | $F_h$ = hand force (N), $\eta$ = efficiency (0.7–0.9) | Force calculation |
| Ram Force (Toggle Press) | $F_r = \dfrac{F_h \cdot L_e}{L_o \cdot \tan(\theta)}$ | $\theta$ = toggle angle at BDC (°) | Toggle press design |
| Kinetic Energy of Flywheel | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | $I$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²), $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s) | Stroke energy |
| Work Done on Workpiece | $W = F_{avg} \times t$ | $F_{avg}$ = avg blanking force (N), $t$ = sheet thickness (m) | Cutting energy |
| Safety Distance (Reach) | $K = 1.6 \, T_s + 63.8$ | $T_s$ = stopping time (s), $K$ in mm | Guard placement |
| Reaction Time Allowance | $D_s = 1.6 \, T_s + C$ | $C$ = constant (63.8 mm hand, 914 mm foot) | ANSI / IS 12474 |
| Blanking Force (Shear) | $F_b = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \tau_s$ | $d$ = punch diameter, $\tau_s$ = shear strength (Pa) | Press capacity sizing |
| Strokes Per Minute | $\text{SPM} = \dfrac{60}{T_{cycle}}$ | $T_{cycle}$ = cycle time (s) | Production rate |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam answers, always state the **units** alongside numerical values and explicitly mention the **clause of the Factories Act / IS standard** whenever safety provisions are discussed.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

Hand/foot-operated presses are extensively used in:
- **Sheet metal workshops** (small-scale blanking, piercing).
- **Automobile ancillaries** (gaskets, washers, brackets).
- **Jewellery & coinage** (precision stamping).
- **Leather & rubber goods manufacturing** (die cutting).
- **Electronics hardware** (connector pins, lead frames).

**Engineering safety relevance:** These machines represent the **highest injury rate per machine-hour** among forming equipment, primarily because operators develop **over-confidence ("I can do it fast")** and **bypass guards** to improve productivity.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mechanical Advantage of a Hand Lever Press

**Given:**
- Effort arm length, $L_e = 600 \text{ mm}$
- Load arm length, $L_o = 80 \text{ mm}$
- Operator hand force, $F_h = 250 \text{ N}$
- Mechanical efficiency, $\eta = 0.85$

**Find:** The ram force $F_r$ delivered at the workpiece.

**Step 1 — Define Mechanical Advantage (Ideal, no losses):**

$$
\text{MA}_{ideal} = \frac{L_e}{L_o} = \frac{600 \text{ mm}}{80 \text{ mm}} = 7.5
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Hand Force into Load Force (Ideal):**

$$
F_{r,\text{ideal}} = F_h \times \text{MA}_{ideal} = 250 \text{ N} \times 7.5 = 1875 \text{ N} \approx 1.88 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Mechanical Efficiency (Real Case):**

$$
F_r = F_{r,\text{ideal}} \times \eta = 1875 \text{ N} \times 0.85 = 1593.75 \text{ N} \approx 1.59 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Common Engineering Units:**

$$
F_r = 1.59 \text{ kN} \approx 162 \text{ kgf}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A modest hand force of **250 N** (≈25 kg push) generates a ram force sufficient to **blank soft aluminium sheets up to ~2 mm thick** — illustrating why the **operator's fingers must never enter the die area**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Safety Distance for a Foot-Operated Press Guard

**Given:**
- Press stopping time, $T_s = 0.18 \text{ s}$
- Foot-operated press; constant $C = 914 \text{ mm}$ (per IS 12474 / ANSI B11.19)

**Find:** Minimum safety distance $D_s$ from the point of operation to the nearest guard opening.

**Step 1 — State the Standard Formula:**

$$
D_s = 1.6 \, T_s + C
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$
D_s = 1.6 \times 0.18 + 914
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Time-Based Term:**

$$
1.6 \times 0.18 = 0.288 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the Constant:**

$$
D_s = 0.288 + 914 = 914.288 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Round Up to a Practical Engineering Value:**

$$
D_s \approx 915 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(practically rounded to } 1.0 \text{ m)}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The guard opening or the operator's reach limit must be **at least 915 mm away** from the point of operation. This ensures that even if the operator reflexively reaches in, the **clutch/brake stopping time** prevents the ram from descending before the hand can be withdrawn.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Blanking Force Capacity Verification

**Given:**
- Punch diameter, $d = 25 \text{ mm}$
- Sheet thickness, $t = 3 \text{ mm}$
- Material: Mild Steel, shear strength $\tau_s = 320 \text{ MPa}$

**Find:** Required press capacity in tonnes to perform this blanking operation.

**Step 1 — State the Blanking Force Formula:**

$$
F_b = \pi \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \tau_s
$$

**Step 2 — Convert All Quantities to SI Units:**

$$
d = 0.025 \text{ m}, \quad t = 0.003 \text{ m}, \quad \tau_s = 320 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$
F_b = \pi \times 0.025 \times 0.003 \times 320 \times 10^6
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerically:**

$$
F_b = 3.1416 \times 0.025 \times 0.003 \times 320 \times 10^6
$$

$$
F_b = 3.1416 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 320 \times 10^6
$$

$$
F_b = 3.1416 \times 24000 = 75{,}398 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Tonnes (1 tonne ≈ 9.81 kN):**

$$
F_b = \frac{75{,}398}{9810} \approx 7.68 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Safety Factor (×1.25 for practical selection):**

$$
F_{b,\text{design}} = 7.68 \times 1.25 \approx 9.6 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Conclusion:** A press of **at least 10-tonne rated capacity** must be selected for this job. A hand-lever press of this rating is physically very large — illustrating why **larger blanking operations move to power presses**, while foot presses handle smaller workpieces where the risk of **bypassing guards** becomes the dominant hazard.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hazard vs. Control Measure

| Hazard Scenario | Consequence | Engineering Control | Administrative Control | PPE |
|-----------------|-------------|---------------------|------------------------|-----|
| Hands under ram at BDC | Crushing, amputation | Fixed perimeter guard, pullbacks | Two-hand control, training | Kevlar gloves |
| Repeat stroke unexpected | Multiple impacts | Anti-repeat mechanism | Foot pedal guarded against accidental actuation | Safety shoes |
| Foot pedal inadvertent | Unexpected stroke | Pedal guard, shroud | Light curtain interlock | — |
| Flywheel coast-down | Delayed stop | Disc brake, dynamic brake | Brake test before shift | — |
| Die-set ejection | Flying fragment | Die enclosure, mesh guard | Tooling inspection log | Face shield |
| Trip on foot pedal | Fall, secondary injury | Flush-mount pedal, anti-slip floor | Housekeeping | Anti-slip footwear |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Foot-Operated Press

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interaction architecture** of a foot-operated press, illustrating the flow of mechanical energy, control signals, and safety interlocks from operator input to workpiece deformation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Foot Input] --> B[Foot Pedal with Guard Shroud]
    B --> C[Linkage Pivot Assembly]
    C --> D[Toggle / Lever Linkage]
    D --> E[Clutch Engagement Lever]
    E --> F[Single Stroke Clutch]
    F --> G[Flywheel with Stored Kinetic Energy]
    G --> H[Crankshaft and Pitman Arm]
    H --> I[Ramslide Vertical Descent]
    I --> J[Upper Punch / Tool]
    J --> K[Workpiece on Lower Die]
    K --> L[Lower Die Bolster Bed]
    L --> M[Waste Strip or Finished Part Exit]

    N[Anti Repeat Mechanism] -.controls.-> F
    O[Brake Assembly] -.stops.-> H
    P[E Stop Circuit] -.interlocks.-> F
    Q[Light Curtain Guard] -.interlocks.-> E
    R[Two Hand Control] -.interlocks.-> E
    S[Pressure Relief Valve] -.protects.-> D
```

### 4.2 Functional Sub-Architecture — Safety Interlock Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Operator Side
        OP1[Operator Hands]
        OP2[Operator Foot]
    end

    subgraph Sensing Layer
        S1[Light Curtain Sensor]
        S2[Pressure Mat Sensor]
        S3[Guard Interlock Switch]
        S4[Foot Pedal Return Spring]
    end

    subgraph Control Layer
        C1[Safety Relay Logic Module]
        C2[Anti Repeat Counter]
        C3[Brake Control Solenoid]
    end

    subgraph Actuator Layer
        A1[Clutch Solenoid Valve]
        A2[Brake Pad Caliper]
        A3[Ram Lock Pin]
    end

    OP1 -->|Reaches into Die| S1
    OP2 -->|Steps on Pedal| S2
    OP1 -->|Opens Guard| S3
    OP2 -->|Releases Pedal| S4

    S1 --> C1
    S2 --> C1
    S3 --> C1
    S4 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> A1
    C3 --> A2
    C1 --> A3
```

### 4.3 Operational State Machine of the Press Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> PedalPressed : Operator depresses foot pedal
    PedalPressed --> ClutchEngaged : Both hand buttons pressed and guard closed
    ClutchEngaged --> RamDescending : Flywheel drives crankshaft
    RamDescending --> BottomDeadCentre : Punch contacts workpiece
    BottomDeadCentre --> RamAscending : Crankshaft completes half rotation
    RamAscending --> TopDeadCentre : Brake engages
    TopDeadCentre --> StrokeComplete : Ram held at TDC
    StrokeComplete --> Idle : Operator releases pedal
    PedalPressed --> StrokeComplete : Repeat mode active
    ClutchEngaged --> EmergencyStop : Light curtain interrupted
    RamDescending --> EmergencyStop : Guard door opened
    EmergencyStop --> Idle : Reset required
```

### 4.4 Schematic of Guard Reach Geometry (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operator Standing Position] -->|Reach Distance D_s| B[Guard Opening Edge]
    B -->|Guard Depth G| C[Point of Operation POO]
    C --> D[Punch and Die Set]
    D --> E[Bolster Bed]

    F[Stopping Time Ts] -.determines.-> B
    G[Hand Speed 1500 mm per s] -.determines.-> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading Guide:** In the guard-reach schematic above, $D_s$ must satisfy $D_s \geq 1.6 \, T_s + 63.8$ mm for hand-fed operations and $D_s \geq 1.6 \, T_s + 914$ mm for foot-operated presses, per IS 12474 / ANSI B11.19.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**"Distinguish between a single-stroke and a continuous (repeat) clutch in a hand/foot press. Which is safer and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Single-Stroke Clutch | Repeat (Continuous) Clutch |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Operation | Ram makes one stroke per pedal actuation | Ram strokes continuously while pedal is held |
| Safety | **Safer** — operator must release and re-press pedal for each stroke | Risk of unintentional multiple strokes; fingers may be in die |
| Use | Recommended for hand-fed operations | Used for high-speed automatic feeding |

A **single-stroke clutch** is preferred for hand/foot presses because it prevents the ram from stroking again automatically, giving the operator time to reposition the workpiece or remove hands from the die area. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**"List any three safety guards used on foot-operated presses and state the purpose of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Fixed Perimeter Guard** — Physically prevents the operator's hands from entering the point of operation during the stroke. (1 mark)
2. **Foot Pedal Guard/Shroud** — Prevents accidental actuation of the pedal due to falling objects or unintended stepping. (1 mark)
3. **Two-Hand Control Device** — Requires both hands to be on separate buttons away from the die, ensuring hands are clear when the ram descends. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **foot-operated toggle press**. Discuss the role of the **anti-repeat mechanism** in ensuring operator safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **hand-lever press** has an effort arm of 500 mm and a load arm of 60 mm. If the operator applies a force of 300 N at the lever and the mechanical efficiency is 80%, calculate the ram force delivered. Comment on the safety implications of this force on hand injuries. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working of a Foot-Operated Toggle Press

**Construction (4 marks):**

A foot-operated toggle press consists of the following major components:

1. **Frame (C-Frame or H-Frame):** Rigid cast iron or fabricated steel structure that houses all components and provides rigidity against deflection.
2. **Foot Pedal (Treadle):** Located at floor level; connected via a vertical rod to the linkage below.
3. **Linkage Assembly (Toggle Mechanism):** Two links connected at a central knee joint; converts small pedal motion into large force at the ram.
4. **Clutch and Brake:** Single-stroke jaw clutch engages the flywheel; brake stops the crankshaft at top dead centre.
5. **Flywheel and Crankshaft:** Stores kinetic energy and converts rotation into reciprocating motion.
6. **Ram / Slide:** Vertical moving member that carries the upper die (punch).
7. **Bolster Plate:** Fixed lower bed on which the lower die is clamped.
8. **Bed, Stand, and Guards:** Mechanical support and operator protection devices.

**Working (2 marks):**

When the operator steps on the foot pedal, the vertical pull operates the linkage that releases the brake and engages the clutch. The flywheel drives the crankshaft, which pulls the pitman arm down, forcing the ram to descend. The punch contacts the workpiece, performs the forming operation, and the ram returns to top dead centre as the brake engages. The operator must release the pedal to repeat the cycle.

**Anti-Repeat Mechanism (1 mark):**

The anti-repeat device is a **mechanical latch** that prevents the clutch from re-engaging until the pedal is fully released and re-pressed. This ensures **one pedal depression = one stroke**, preventing inadvertent multiple strokes that could catch the operator's hands under the descending ram.

---

#### Part (b) — Ram Force Calculation

**Given:**
- Effort arm, $L_e = 500 \text{ mm}$
- Load arm, $L_o = 60 \text{ mm}$
- Hand force, $F_h = 300 \text{ N}$
- Mechanical efficiency, $\eta = 0.80$

**Step 1: Compute Ideal Mechanical Advantage:**

$$
\text{MA} = \frac{L_e}{L_o} = \frac{500}{60} = 8.33
$$

**Step 2: Compute Ideal Ram Force:**

$$
F_{r,\text{ideal}} = F_h \times \text{MA} = 300 \times 8.33 = 2500 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Efficiency:**

$$
F_r = F_{r,\text{ideal}} \times \eta = 2500 \times 0.80 = 2000 \text{ N} = 2.0 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to kgf for Intuitive Understanding:**

$$
F_r = \frac{2000}{9.81} \approx 204 \text{ kgf}
$$

**Safety Implications (3 marks):**

A ram force of **204 kgf** is more than sufficient to:
- **Crush finger bones** (phalange fracture threshold ≈ 100 kgf for adults).
- **Cause partial or complete amputation** if fingers are placed between die components.
- **Generate pinch-point shearing** at the die edges.

**Conclusion:** Even though the operator pushes with only **300 N (≈30 kg)**, the press multiplies this by 8.33×, delivering a **2-tonne class crushing force**. The hand/finger injury threshold is exceeded by a factor of ~2. This quantitatively proves why **fixed guards and pullback devices** are mandatory — operator reflexes cannot withdraw in time.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **hazards associated with hand and foot-operated presses** in engineering industry. Discuss the engineering and administrative control measures recommended by the **Indian Factories Act and IS 12474** to mitigate these hazards. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A foot-operated press has a stopping time of 0.15 seconds. Using the IS 12474 safety distance formula, calculate the **minimum safety distance** required between the operator's reach and the point of operation. If the operator uses hand-feed instead, recalculate the safety distance. Comment on the difference. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Hazards and Control Measures

**Major Hazards (3 marks):**

1. **Crushing / Amputation Injuries at Point of Operation:** Hands or fingers caught between the punch and die during ram descent.
2. **Inadvertent Stroke from Accidental Pedal Actuation:** Falling objects, double-strike, or unintentional stepping.
3. **Repeat Stroke Injuries:** Anti-repeat failure leading to multiple strokes before operator withdraws hands.
4. **Flywheel Run-On:** Flywheel coasting after power cut, causing ram to descend unexpectedly.
5. **Die Fragment Ejection:** Broken punch or die fragments flying outward at high velocity.
6. **Musculoskeletal Disorders:** Repetitive foot/hand actuation leading to strain injuries.

**Engineering Controls (2.5 marks):**

- Fixed perimeter guards with reach distance ≥ $D_s = 1.6 T_s + C$ mm.
- Two-hand control devices.
- Light curtains / pressure mats / guard interlock switches.
- Anti-repeat mechanisms and single-stroke clutches.
- Mechanical brake with monitored stopping time.
- Emergency stop circuits (Category 0 / Category 1 per IS 12631).
- Die enclosure / polycarbonate shields.

**Administrative Controls (1 mark):**

- Operator training and certification under Section 21 of the Factories Act.
- Periodic inspection logs (per IS 12474 Clause 6).
- Permit-to-work for die setup and maintenance.
- Lock-out / Tag-out (LOTO) procedures.
- PPE provision: safety shoes, gloves, eye protection.

**Legal Reference (0.5 mark):** Indian Factories Act 1948, **Sections 21, 22, 28, and Schedule** prescribe guarding, fencing, and safe operation of machinery including power and manual presses.

---

#### Part (b) — Safety Distance Calculation

**Given:** Stopping time $T_s = 0.15 \text{ s}$

**Step 1: Safety Distance for Foot-Operated Press ($C = 914$ mm per IS 12474):**

$$
D_{s,\text{foot}} = 1.6 \times T_s + 914 = 1.6 \times 0.15 + 914 = 0.24 + 914 = 914.24 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Safety Distance for Hand-Fed Operation ($C = 63.8$ mm per IS 12474):**

$$
D_{s,\text{hand}} = 1.6 \times T_s + 63.8 = 1.6 \times 0.15 + 63.8 = 0.24 + 63.8 = 64.04 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Comparison and Interpretation:**

$$
\Delta D_s = D_{s,\text{foot}} - D_{s,\text{hand}} = 914.24 - 64.04 = 850.2 \text{ mm}
$$

**Engineering Commentary (4 marks):**

- For a **foot press**, the operator's **foot is on the pedal** at the moment of stroking — so the standard assumes that only **hand reach** is the residual risk. However, the larger constant (914 mm) reflects the **proximity of the operator's body to the press** in a standing posture, requiring greater clearance.
- For **hand-fed operation**, the operator's hand is intentionally placed near the die, so the constant is much smaller (63.8 mm), but **two-hand control + light curtain are mandatory**.
- The **stopping-time component** ($1.6 \, T_s = 0.24$ mm) is negligible here because $T_s$ is small — the constant $C$ dominates.
- **Practical implication:** Foot-operated presses require **larger physical guarding** and **greater floor-space planning**, while hand-fed presses require **active electronic safeguards** (light curtains, safety mats).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the constants C = 63.8 mm (hand) and C = 914 mm (foot).** Students frequently interchange them and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Stopping time is in seconds, not milliseconds.** Writing $T_s = 150$ ms instead of 0.15 s gives a distance off by a factor of 1000.
> 3. **Always state the standard being referenced** (IS 12474 / ANSI B11.19) — examiners award a mark for citing the regulation.
> 4. **Avoid one-line answers** — even for 3-mark questions, structure as **definition + key point + example**.
> 5. **In numerical problems, show units at every step.** A numerical answer without units carries partial credit only.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Hand or Foot-Operated Presses**

- **Definition:** Manually actuated press using hand lever or foot treadle to drive a ram against a workpiece.
- **Key Components:** Frame, lever/pedal, toggle/clutch, flywheel, crankshaft, ram, bolster, brake, guards.
- **Energy Path:** Human input → Lever/Linkage → Clutch → Flywheel → Crankshaft → Ram.
- **Most Common Injury:** Crushing / amputation of hands/fingers at the point of operation.
- **Single-Stroke Clutch:** Preferred for hand-fed operations; one pedal press = one stroke.
- **Anti-Repeat Mechanism:** Prevents unintended multiple strokes; mandatory per IS 12474.
- **Safety Distance Formula (Foot Press):** $D_s = 1.6 \, T_s + 914 \text{ mm}$.
- **Safety Distance Formula (Hand Fed):** $D_s = 1.6 \, T_s + 63.8 \text{ mm}$.
- **Stopping Time:** Typically 0.05–0.20 s; must be measured and recorded periodically.
- **Mechanical Advantage Range:** 5×–15× for typical hand-lever presses.
- **Typical Ram Force:** 1–20 tonnes for manual presses; 5–100 tonnes for pneumatic-foot assist.
- **Legal Framework:** Indian Factories Act 1948 (Sections 21, 22, 28) + IS 12474 + IS 12631.
- **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering (guards, interlocks) → Administrative (training, LOTO) → PPE (gloves, shoes, face shield).
- **KTU Favourite Keywords:** "Point of operation," "Anti-repeat," "Single-stroke clutch," "Two-hand control," "Safety distance," "Stopping time," "Mechanical advantage."
- **Examiner Tip:** Always end safety answers with a **statutory reference** (Factories Act / IS standard) — this is a guaranteed 1-mark booster.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Press Electric Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Press Electric Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Power Press** is a mechanically powered machine used in sheet metal working operations such as blanking, piercing, bending, deep drawing, and forming, where a ram (slide) is driven through a controlled stroke to apply force on a workpiece held between a die and a punch. **Electric Controls** in a power press refer to the integrated network of electrical, electronic, and electromechanical components that govern the starting, stopping, cycling mode (single-stroke/continuous), clutch engagement, brake actuation, and operator-safety interlocks of the press.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> *Power Press Electric Controls encompass the design, selection, and functioning of electrical control circuits that ensure safe operation of mechanical and hydraulic presses. These include anti-repeat (anti-restart) circuits, two-hand control devices, guard interlock circuits, emergency stop circuits, clutch-brake control, foot-switch interlocks, and modern PLC/Electronic Safety Relay based systems that conform to Indian Standard IS 11188 and Indian Factory Act, 1948 (Amended) provisions.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving an old manual-transmission car on a steep, busy hill. Before starting, you must:
- Press the clutch (disengage drive)
- Press the brake (stop the wheels)
- Release the handbrake
- Then ONLY signal "GO" by releasing both feet in a coordinated way

If you release the brake too early, the car rolls backward — **DANGEROUS**. A power press electric control does *exactly* this, but in reverse for a flying-metal machine that can amputate a hand in 0.05 seconds. The control system demands that **two deliberate, simultaneous, and time-bound actions** occur before the ram moves, and it refuses to "auto-repeat" the stroke if the operator just keeps a button pressed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world metric for context:** A typical 100-ton mechanical power press ram can travel at speeds of **0.5 m/s to 1.5 m/s**. The stopping time of a flywheel-clutch press is **0.2 to 0.5 seconds** — meaning the operator's reaction time (~0.25 s) is *inside* the machine's danger window. Electric controls close this gap.

## 1.3 Standard Constants & Normative Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Stopping time of mechanical press:** **0.2 s – 0.5 s** (governed by clutch-brake design)
- **Operator safety distance formula constant (OSHA/IS):** **D = K × T** where **K = 1.6 m/s** (hand speed constant) and **T = total stopping time in seconds**
- **Anti-repeat circuit re-arm delay:** typically **0.5 s – 2.0 s**
- **Two-hand control synchronism window:** **≤ 0.5 seconds** (ISO 13851 Type III requirement)
- **E-Stop category (per ISO 13850):** **Category 0 (uncontrolled stop)** or **Category 1 (controlled stop)**
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** **SIL 2 minimum** for press controls (per IEC 62061)
- **Performance Level (PL):** **PL d minimum** (per ISO 13849-1)

## 1.4 Syllabus Mapping (KTU 2024 OBE Alignment)

| Course Outcome | Mapped Concept | Cognitive Level |
|----------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **CO 3** | Identify hazards of power presses and electric control subsystems | Remember / Understand |
| **CO 4** | Design / evaluate safety circuits for presses | Apply / Analyse |
| **CO 5** | Recommend control-based safety upgrades | Evaluate |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU's OBE pattern, students are expected to **draw**, **explain**, and **justify** the choice of each control element — not merely list them. Always include the *function* alongside the *name* in answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Power Presses (for Context)

Before studying controls, students must recognize the press type, because control philosophy differs:

1. **Mechanical Power Press (Flywheel type)** – Uses clutch-brake; ram driven by stored flywheel energy. *Most common in industry; highest hazard.*
2. **Hydraulic Press** – Ram driven by hydraulic cylinder; inherently slower; can hold pressure.
3. **Pneumatic Press** – Low-tonnage, fast cycling.
4. **Servo Press** – Modern; direct-drive; software-controlled stroke profile.

The remainder of this module focuses on **Mechanical Power Press Electric Controls**, as they dominate KTU exam questions.

## 2.2 Functional Block Decomposition of Electric Control

The electric control system of a power press is logically partitioned into the following sub-systems:

### Block A: **Power Supply & Isolation**
- Main isolator (lockable, padlock-type)
- MCCB (Moulded Case Circuit Breaker) for short-circuit protection
- Control transformer (440/110 V or 230/24 V AC/DC) for control circuit
- Surge Protection Device (SPD)

### Block B: **Motor Starting Circuit**
- Star-Delta / DOL starter for press motor (typically 5 HP to 50 HP)
- Overload relay (OLR) with single-phasing protection
- Motor ON indication (Green lamp) and TRIP indication (Red lamp)

### Block C: **Clutch-Brake Control (The Heart of Press Safety)**
- **Clutch:** Engages flywheel to crankshaft → ram descends.
- **Brake:** Stops crankshaft → ram halts at Top Dead Centre (TDC).
- These are *mutually exclusive* — they must NEVER engage simultaneously.
- Actuation: Solenoid-operated pneumatic/hydraulic valve OR direct electric solenoid.

### Block D: **Operator Command Devices**
- Foot switch (with protective guard) — single-hand or two-foot type
- Two-hand push button station (preferred for low-tonnage presses)
- Inch mode selector (for die setting — single short strokes)

### Block E: **Safeguarding Circuits (Critical KTU High-Yield Area)**

| Safeguard | Function | Standard Reference |
|-----------|----------|--------------------|
| Anti-repeat (Anti-restart) circuit | Prevents automatic re-stroke if operator holds/run-locks the button | IS 11188 : 1991 |
| Two-hand control | Forces operator to keep both hands out of die area | ISO 13851 |
| Guard interlock | Disables press if fixed/movable guard is open | ISO 14119 |
| Light curtain / Safety mat | Detects operator intrusion into danger zone | IEC 61496 |
| Emergency stop (E-Stop) | Hardwired, category-0/1 stop | ISO 13850 |
| Overload / Stroke-limit switch | Detects jammed stock or overload | Press OEM spec |

### Block F: **Indication & Annunciation**
- Run lamp, Trip lamp, Guard open lamp, Lubrication fault lamp
- Hooter/Buzzer for pre-stroke warning (typical 2 s audible pre-trip)

## 2.3 Why Each Control Exists — The "Why-How" Map

- **Why Anti-repeat?**
  *How:* A latching relay (holding contact) drops out at end of every stroke. The operator *must release and re-press* the run button to initiate the next stroke. This prevents the press from "auto-cycling" if the operator faints or is pulled into the die.

- **Why Two-hand Control?**
  *How:* Two push buttons must be pressed *within 0.5 s of each other* (synchronism). A timing relay (on-delay, t = 0.5 s) and a logic AND-gate (relay ladder) enforce this. If only one button is pressed → no stroke. Operator's hands *must* be on the buttons → *cannot* be in the die area.

- **Why Guard Interlock?**
  *How:* A position switch (limit switch or magnetic proximity switch) on the guard is wired in *series* with the clutch coil circuit. If guard is open → switch open → clutch cannot energize → ram cannot descend.

- **Why Light Curtain (Opto-electronic)?**
  *How:* A series of infrared beams cross the danger zone. Any beam broken → output relay drops → clutch de-energises → brake applies → ram stops within stopping time T. Requires the *safety distance* formula to position correctly.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-memorize** for KTU ESE. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to protect markdown tables.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Units | Where Used |
|---|----------------------|--------------------|---------------|------------|
| 1 | $D_s = K \times (T_s + T_r)$ | $D_s$ = minimum safety distance; $K$ = hand-speed constant (**1.6 m/s**); $T_s$ = stopping time of press; $T_r$ = response time of safeguard | metres, seconds | Positioning light curtains / guards |
| 2 | $t_{sync} \leq 0.5\ s$ | Synchronism time between two-hand buttons | seconds | ISO 13851 Type III |
| 3 | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | Kinetic energy stored in flywheel | Joules | Clutch sizing / brake energy |
| 4 | $T_{brake} = \dfrac{J \cdot \omega}{M_{brake}}$ | Brake stopping time (J = moment of inertia, $\omega$ = angular velocity, M = braking torque) | seconds | Mechanical design |
| 5 | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{F_{ram} \times v_{ram}}{\eta_{press}}$ | Press motor power sizing | Watts / kW | Selection of motor |
| 6 | $t_{pre\text{-}trip} \geq 2\ s$ | Minimum pre-stroke warning (hooter) | seconds | IS 11188 |
| 7 | $PL = f(\text{Cat}, \ DC_{avg},\ MTTF_d)$ | Performance Level from ISO 13849-1 | dimensionless | Risk-graph assessment |
| 8 | $PFH_d \leq 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-7}$ per hour | Probability of dangerous failure per hour | h$^{-1}$ | SIL 2 / SIL 3 verification |

> [!NOTE]
> The **safety distance formula** $D_s = K(T_s + T_r)$ is the single most-asked formula in KTU Module 3. Always state **K = 1.6 m/s** as the OSHA/standard value, and explicitly mention that for *reaching through* safeguards, an additional reach factor (C) is added: $D_s = K(T_s + T_r) + C$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Safety Distance for Light Curtain / Guard Positioning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **highest-weight numerical problem** in KTU Module 3 (worth 7–14 marks). Master this.

### Problem Statement
A mechanical power press has a measured **stopping time** $T_s$ = 0.30 s. The light curtain (opto-electronic safeguard) has a **response time** $T_r$ = 0.020 s. The operator must reach *into* the die area (over the light curtain) to remove a finished part. The reach factor beyond the sensing field is **C = 0.85 m**. Compute the **minimum safety distance** at which the light curtain must be mounted from the danger point.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the governing equation from the standard.**

The minimum safety distance is given by:
$$D_s = K \times (T_s + T_r) + C$$

**Step 2 — Identify and substitute the known parameters.**

- $K = 1.6\ \text{m/s}$ (hand-speed constant, per OSHA 1910.217 and IS 11188)
- $T_s = 0.30\ \text{s}$ (given, stopping time of the press measured at the mid-point of the stroke at the worst-case one-stroking mode)
- $T_r = 0.020\ \text{s}$ (response time of the light curtain, per manufacturer datasheet)
- $C = 0.85\ \text{m}$ (reach factor for the operator's hand reaching past the sensing field into the danger zone)

**Step 3 — Compute the operator's approach time component.**

$$K \times (T_s + T_r) = 1.6 \times (0.30 + 0.020)$$

$$= 1.6 \times 0.320$$

$$= 0.512\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Add the reach factor.**

$$D_s = 0.512 + 0.85$$

$$D_s = 1.362\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Round up to the nearest practical mounting increment.**

Per IS 11188, the safety distance must be rounded *up* to the nearest 0.05 m increment. Therefore:

$$D_s = 1.40\ \text{m (minimum, recommended 1.45 m)}$$

**Step 6 — Verification by physical reasoning.**

If the light curtain were placed at, say, 0.5 m, the operator's hand at 1.6 m/s would travel 0.512 m in 0.320 s and could reach the die *before* the press stops. The reach factor of 0.85 m represents the residual hand travel *after* the beam is broken and *before* the press halts. The total physical distance the hand can travel is 0.512 + 0.85 = 1.362 m. Hence the curtain *must* be mounted at *at least* this far so that the operator cannot reach the die before the ram stops.

$$\boxed{D_s^{\min} \approx 1.40\ \text{m}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Press Control Safeguards

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks 14-mark comparison questions. This matrix is your ready-reckoner.

| Parameter | Two-Hand Control | Light Curtain | Guard Interlock | Safety Mat | E-Stop |
|-----------|------------------|---------------|-----------------|------------|--------|
| **Operator hands free?** | No (both occupied) | Yes | Yes | Yes | N/A |
| **Type of safeguard** | Distance + behaviour | Electro-sensitive | Mechanical/position | Pressure-sensitive | Hardwired stop |
| **ISO Standard** | ISO 13851 | IEC 61496 | ISO 14119 | ISO 13856 | ISO 13850 |
| **PL achievable** | PL d | PL e | PL e | PL d | PL e |
| **Detection principle** | Logic + synchronism | Infrared beam break | Position switch | Pressure threshold | Direct break |
| **Risk reduction (per ISO 12100)** | Medium | High | High | Medium | High |
| **Stopping time tolerance** | Independent of T | Requires T for distance | Independent of T | Independent of T | Critical for Cat 0 |
| **Common failure mode** | Operator bypass | Beam defeat | Guard wedged open | Mat damage | Welding of contact |
| **Anti-repeat integration** | Built-in | Requires external relay | Built-in (via NC contact) | External logic | External logic |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2: Flywheel Stored Energy

A mechanical power press has a flywheel with moment of inertia $I = 25\ \text{kg·m}^2$ rotating at $N = 300\ \text{rpm}$. Find the stored kinetic energy available for one stroke.

**Step 1 — Convert RPM to angular velocity.**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 300}{60}$$

$$= \frac{600 \pi}{60} = 10 \pi \approx 31.416\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the kinetic energy formula.**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 25 \times (31.416)^2$$

$$= 12.5 \times 986.96$$

$$= 12{,}337\ \text{J} \approx 12.34\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to work-equivalent force at the ram.**

For a press with mechanical advantage (ram force per unit crank torque) — if the ram develops a force of **100 kN over a stroke of 50 mm** for one stroke:

$$W_{stroke} = F \times d = 100{,}000 \times 0.050 = 5{,}000\ \text{J} = 5\ \text{kJ}$$

The flywheel must therefore store more than 5 kJ to complete the stroke without significant speed drop. The 12.34 kJ stored comfortably exceeds 5 kJ — design is safe.

$$\boxed{E_k \approx 12.34\ \text{kJ},\ \text{adequate margin over } 5\ \text{kJ per stroke}}$$

## 3.4 Anti-Repeat Circuit Logic — Boolean Derivation

The anti-repeat function is implemented using a **seal-in (latching) relay** with timed drop-out.

Let:
- $S$ = Start (run) push button (NO, momentary)
- $G$ = Guard interlock contact (NC, closed when guard shut)
- $F$ = Foot switch contact (NO, momentary)
- $R$ = Anti-repeat seal-in relay (coil)
- $C$ = Clutch solenoid coil

**Step 1 — Initial energisation (start of stroke).**

Operator presses run button and foot switch simultaneously; guard is closed:

$$R_{energise} = S \land G \land F$$

**Step 2 — Seal-in (latching).**

Once R energises, a NO auxiliary contact of R parallel-bypasses S (the start button), so the operator can release the button:

$$R_{hold} = (R_{aux\ NO}) \land G \land F$$

**Step 3 — Stroke completion and drop-out.**

At TDC, a cam-operated limit switch (NC contact, called *anti-repeat cam*) opens *before* the operator releases foot switch. This breaks the holding path:

$$R_{drop} = \overline{CR_{cam}} \lor \overline{G} \lor \overline{F}$$

Thus, R drops at every TDC, *forcing* the operator to release and re-press the run button for the next stroke. This is the **anti-repeat** behaviour.

**Step 4 — Clutch interlock logic.**

The clutch coil C can energise *only* when R is energised *and* the brake limit switch confirms brake is released:

$$C_{energise} = R \land \overline{B_{limit}}$$

This is **fail-safe**: any loss of power → R drops → C de-energises → brake spring applies → press stops.

## 3.5 Truth Table for Two-Hand Control (Type III per ISO 13851)

| Button A | Button B | Time between A & B | Output (Clutch) | Valid? |
|----------|----------|---------------------|-----------------|--------|
| 0 | 0 | — | 0 | Yes (idle) |
| 1 | 0 | — | 0 | No — one-hand only |
| 0 | 1 | — | 0 | No — one-hand only |
| 1 | 1 | $\leq 0.5\ s$ | 1 | **Yes** |
| 1 | 1 | $> 0.5\ s$ | 0 | No — out of sync |
| 1 → 0 | 1 | — | 0 | No — must re-start both |
| 1 | 1 → 0 | — | 0 | No — must re-start both |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Writing "if both buttons pressed, press operates" without specifying the *synchronism window* or the *re-initiation requirement*. Always state **all three** conditions: (1) both pressed, (2) within 0.5 s, (3) re-initiation required after any release.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Power Press Electric Control — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwrA[AC Mains Supply 415V 3Phase] --> isoA[Isolator MCCB Lockable]
    isoA --> xfmrA[Control Transformer 415 to 110V AC]
    isoA --> mtrA[Press Motor 3Phase Induction]
    xfmrA --> ctrlBus[110V AC Control Bus]
    mtrA --> starterA[StarDelta Starter with OLR]
    starterA --> mtrLamp[Motor ON Green Lamp]
    starterA --> tripLamp[OLR Trip Red Lamp]

    ctrlBus --> es1{EStop PB NC Hardwired}
    es1 --> gIntSw{Guard Interlock Limit Switch NC}
    gIntSw --> arbSw{AntiRepeat Cam Switch NC at TDC}

    arbSw --> thc1{TwoHand Button A NO}
    arbSw --> thc2{TwoHand Button B NO}
    thc1 --> sync1[Synchronism Timer 0 to 5s]
    thc2 --> sync1
    sync1 --> andG1[AND Gate Logic]

    andG1 --> sealR[SealIn AntiRepeat Relay R]
    sealR --> aux1[Aux NO of R Bypass Start]
    aux1 --> arbSw
    sealR --> brkInt{Brake Released Limit Switch}
    brkInt --> clutchCoil[Clutch Solenoid Coil C]
    clutchCoil --> flyClutch[Flywheel Clutch Engaged]
    flyClutch --> ramDescend[Ram Descends Stroke]

    ctrlBus --> fSw{Foot Switch NO with Guard}
    fSw --> sealR

    ctrlBus --> ltCurtain[Light Curtain Safety Relay Output NC]
    ltCurtain --> arbSw

    ctrlBus --> preTrip[PreTrip Hooter 2 second Delay]
    preTrip --> clutchCoil
    sealR --> preTrip

    pwrA --> plc1[Optional PLC CPU SIL2 Rated]
    plc1 --> hmi1[Operator HMI Touchscreen]
    plc1 --> ctrlBus
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart is the **functional architecture**, mapping how every safety-critical signal (E-Stop, guard, anti-repeat, light curtain, foot switch, two-hand) is *series-wired* into the clutch-coil energisation path. Notice the AND-gate and synchronism timer for two-hand control, and the latching auxiliary that implements anti-repeat.

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram: Power Press Operational Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> GuardCheck : Power ON
    GuardCheck --> Ready : All interlocks closed
    GuardCheck --> Fault : Any interlock open
    Fault --> Ready : Reset after fault clearance
    Ready --> PreTrip : Operator initiates stroke
    PreTrip --> StrokeRun : Hooter 2s elapsed, conditions OK
    PreTrip --> Abort : Operator releases within 2s
    StrokeRun --> StrokeComplete : Cam switch opens at TDC
    StrokeRun --> EmergencyStop : EStop pressed
    StrokeComplete --> Idle : Seal-in relay drops, brake applies
    EmergencyStop --> Fault : Cat 0 hard stop
    Abort --> Ready : Operator releases all controls
```

## 4.3 Annotated Relay Ladder Logic (Textual Schematic)

Below is a **textual ladder representation** of a typical press control circuit. KTU often asks students to draw or trace this.

```
            L1                                                        L2
            |                                                         |
            |---[ E-STOP NC ]---[ GUARD NC ]---[ ANTI-REPEAT NC ]-----+
                                            |                          |
                                            +--[ START NO ]-----------+
                                            |                          |
                                            +--[ SEAL-AUX NO of R ]---+
                                            |                          |
            |---[ FOOT-SWITCH NO ]----------+                          |
            |                                                         |
            |---[ TIMER ON-DELAY 0.5s ]                              |
            |       (                                              )  |
            |       ( [A-NO] --[B-NO] in series  )                   |
            |       (                                              )  |
            |       (--------------------[ TDR NO, t=0.5s ]-------)--+
                                            |                          |
                                            +--[ R COIL ]--------------+
                                            |                          |
                                            +--[ R-AUX NO ] (seal) ----+
                                            |                          |
                                            +--[ BRAKE-LIMIT NC ]------+
                                            |                          |
                                            +--[ C COIL: CLUTCH ]-----+
            |---[ HOOTER via T-on 2s ]---- (parallel to C) -----------+
            |                                                         |
            L1                                                        L2
```

**Reading the ladder (Rung-by-rung):**
1. **Rung 1** — Series chain of NC contacts: E-Stop, Guard, Anti-repeat cam. *All must be closed for any action.*
2. **Rung 2** — Parallel combination: Start push button (NO) **OR** the seal-in auxiliary of R. This is the *latching path*.
3. **Rung 3** — Foot switch must be closed (operator's foot on pedal).
4. **Rung 4** — Synchronism timer: both two-hand buttons (A and B) must close within 0.5 s; the timer's NO contact then closes.
5. **Rung 5** — R (seal-in relay) coil energises when all rungs 1–4 conditions are met.
6. **Rung 6** — R's auxiliary contact *bypasses* the Start button → holding.
7. **Rung 7** — Brake-limit switch (NC when brake *is* released) → clutch coil C can energise.
8. **Rung 8** — Hooter (with 2 s on-delay) announces the imminent stroke.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU answers, **redraw this ladder neatly** with each rung numbered and labelled. Examiner-friendly ladder diagrams score higher than text descriptions.

## 4.4 Mermaid Component-Wise Wiring Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bus110[110V AC Control Bus L1]
    busN[Neutral L2]
    bus110 --> esPB[EStop Push Button NC]
    esPB --> gLim[Guard Limit Switch NC]
    gLim --> camTDC[AntiRepeat Cam NC]
    camTDC --> startPB[Start Push Button NO]
    camTDC --> sealAux[SealIn R Aux NO]
    camTDC --> ftSW[Foot Switch NO]
    camTDC --> thA[TwoHand Button A NO]
    thA --> thB[TwoHand Button B NO]
    thB --> tdrNO[Timer TDR NO 0 to 5s]
    tdrNO --> rCoil[Relay R Coil]
    rCoil --> rAux1[R Aux NO for Seal]
    rAux1 --> camTDC
    rCoil --> brkLim[Brake Released Limit NC]
    brkLim --> cCoil[Clutch Solenoid C Coil]
    cCoil --> busN
    rCoil --> hooterTmr[OnDelay Timer 2s]
    hooterTmr --> hooter[Hooter Buzzer]
    hooter --> busN
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Schematic Pitfall:** Students often draw the **NO start button in parallel with the seal-in contact** but *forget* the cam-switch in series at the top of the rung. Without the cam switch, anti-repeat is **not implemented**. The cam switch at TDC is what *forces* the relay to drop and the operator to re-initiate.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "Anti-repeat circuit" as applied to a mechanical power press. State the relevant Indian Standard.**

**Model Answer:**

An **anti-repeat circuit** (also called *anti-restart* or *single-stroke safeguard*) is an electrical interlock that ensures the press **completes only one stroke per deliberate operator initiation**. The cam-operated limit switch at the press top dead centre (TDC) opens at the end of every stroke, breaking the holding path of the seal-in relay. Consequently, the clutch de-energises and the operator **must release and re-press** the run button to obtain the next stroke. This prevents the press from "running away" if the operator faints, slips, or is pulled into the die area while holding the button.

**Relevant Indian Standard:** **IS 11188 : 1991** — *Safety requirements for the use, care and protection of power presses* — Clause on "single-stroke devices and anti-repeat devices."

*(3 marks: definition 2 + standard 1)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any three functions of a "Two-Hand Control" device on a power press.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Forces the operator to keep both hands on the control buttons** during the initiation of a stroke, thereby keeping the hands away from the die/danger zone.
2. **Enforces synchronism** — both push buttons must be actuated within **0.5 seconds** of each other (per **ISO 13851 Type III**), preventing one-handed or accidental actuation.
3. **Demands re-initiation** after every release — if either button is released during the stroke, the clutch de-energises and a fresh two-hand start is required for the next stroke.
4. *(Bonus)* Complies with the safety distance requirement by acting as a *trip device* whose position is by definition outside the danger zone.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per correct function)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Draw the relay ladder logic diagram of a power press electric control circuit incorporating: anti-repeat cam, two-hand control with 0.5 s synchronism, guard interlock, E-Stop, and clutch-brake interlock. Explain the operation step-by-step. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A light curtain is to be installed on a 200-ton mechanical power press. The measured stopping time at mid-stroke is $T_s = 0.28$ s, the light curtain response time is $T_r = 0.025$ s, and the operator's reach beyond the sensing field is $C = 0.90$ m. Compute the minimum safety distance and state where the light curtain must be physically mounted. **(7 Marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Diagram (draw the ladder, 4 marks; explanation 3 marks):**

```
    L1 ──[E-Stop NC]──[Guard NC]──[Anti-Repeat Cam NC]──┬──[Start NO]──┐
                                                        │              │
                                                        └──[R-Aux NO]──┤
                                                        │              │
    L1 ──[Foot SW NO]────────────────────────────────────┤              │
                                                        │              │
    L1 ──[2HB-A NO]──[2HB-B NO]──[TDR NO t=0.5s]────────┤              │
                                                        │              │
                                                        └──[R COIL]────┤
                                                                   │
    L1 ──[R-Aux NO]──[Brake-Limit NC]──[C COIL]──[Hooter T-2s]───── L2
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Pre-start condition:** E-Stop closed, Guard closed, Anti-repeat cam closed (cam not at TDC), brake applied.
2. **Operator action:** Both 2HB-A and 2HB-B are pressed *within 0.5 s* → TDR contact closes. Foot switch is pressed. Start button is pressed.
3. **Seal-in:** R coil energises → R-Aux contact latches across the start button.
4. **Pre-trip:** Hooter sounds for 2 s; operator can abort within 2 s by releasing.
5. **Clutch engagement:** After 2 s, if R remains energised, Brake-Limit (NC) closes (brake is mechanically released) → Clutch coil C energises → flywheel drives the ram down.
6. **End of stroke:** At TDC, the **anti-repeat cam switch opens**, breaking R's holding path. R drops, C de-energises, brake spring reapplies. Operator *must release and re-press* start to get the next stroke.
7. **Failure modes:** Opening the guard, pressing E-Stop, releasing either 2HB, or releasing foot switch at any point → R drops → clutch disengages → brake applies → safe stop.

*(Marking: Ladder 4 marks with components labelled; explanation 3 marks for step sequence.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Write the standard safety distance formula:
$$D_s = K \times (T_s + T_r) + C$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:
- $K = 1.6\ \text{m/s}$
- $T_s = 0.28\ \text{s}$
- $T_r = 0.025\ \text{s}$
- $C = 0.90\ \text{m}$

**Step 3:** Compute the timing term:
$$K \times (T_s + T_r) = 1.6 \times (0.28 + 0.025) = 1.6 \times 0.305 = 0.488\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4:** Add the reach factor:
$$D_s = 0.488 + 0.90 = 1.388\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5:** Round up to the nearest 0.05 m increment:
$$\boxed{D_s^{\min} = 1.40\ \text{m}}$$

**Step 6:** Mounting position — the light curtain's sensing field must be located at least **1.40 m** from the nearest danger point (the die edge) in the direction of operator approach. The sensing field's *outer boundary* (farthest from the danger) should be **at or beyond** this 1.40 m mark.

*(Marking: Formula 2 marks; substitution 2 marks; arithmetic 2 marks; physical interpretation 1 mark.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **flywheel-clutch-brake** system in a mechanical power press. How does the electric control interact with this system? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the role of **emergency stop (E-Stop)** circuits, **guard interlocks**, and **light curtains** in power press safety. Quote relevant ISO/IS standards. **(7 Marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A mechanical power press has a **flywheel** mounted on a shaft, driven continuously by an electric motor through a V-belt or gear train. The flywheel stores rotational kinetic energy ($\tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$). Between the flywheel and the **crankshaft** (which drives the ram via a connecting rod), there is a **clutch** — typically a friction clutch (single-disc or multi-disc) or a pneumatic/hydraulic clutch.

On the same crankshaft (or its end), a **brake** is mounted — usually a spring-applied, solenoid-released (or pneumatic-released) disc brake. **Crucially, the clutch and brake are mechanically interlocked** so that engaging the clutch automatically releases the brake, and vice versa. They **cannot be engaged simultaneously**.

**Working (2 marks):**

- **Idle (motor running):** Flywheel spins; clutch disengaged; brake applied; ram held at TDC.
- **Stroke initiation:** Clutch coil energises → clutch friction surfaces engage flywheel to crankshaft → brake releases → ram descends under stored energy.
- **End of stroke:** Clutch de-energises → clutch disengages → brake spring applies → crankshaft halts → ram stops at TDC.

**Electric control interaction (2 marks):**

The electric control system is the *only* authority allowed to energise the clutch coil. It does so *only* when:
- Guard interlock is closed,
- Two-hand or foot command is valid (with synchronism),
- Anti-repeat logic allows the stroke,
- E-Stop is released,
- All other safety inputs are healthy.

The brake is *fail-safe* — spring-applied, electrically released. Loss of control power automatically applies the brake.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**E-Stop (2 marks):**
Per **ISO 13850**, an emergency stop on a power press must be a **hardwired, normally-closed** push button wired *directly* in series with the clutch coil circuit (or its safety relay). Category 0 (uncontrolled) or Category 1 (controlled, with monitored stop) is permitted. E-Stops must be **self-latching** (twist-to-release or key-reset) to prevent automatic restart. **Colour: red mushroom on yellow background.**

**Guard Interlock (2 marks):**
Per **ISO 14119**, fixed and movable guards on the press must carry a **position interlock switch** (mechanical limit switch or magnetic-coded switch). The switch's NC contact is wired in series with the clutch-coil circuit. Opening the guard → switch opens → clutch cannot energise. Use of **tamper-resistant, uniquely-coded** switches is recommended for PL e.

**Light Curtain (3 marks):**
Per **IEC 61496-1/-2**, an opto-electronic protective device (OEPD) of Type 2 or Type 4 is used. The light curtain creates a vertical or horizontal field of infrared beams between an emitter and a receiver. When any beam is broken, the safety output (OSSD — Output Signal Switching Device) drops, and the safety relay de-energises the clutch coil. **Positioning** is governed by the safety distance formula:
$$D_s = K(T_s + T_r) + C$$
with $K = 1.6\ \text{m/s}$ (or 2.0 m/s for reaching over a horizontal curtain). The light curtain must also incorporate **muting** (for part entry/exit) and **blanking** (for fixtured obstructions) features carefully designed to maintain safety.

**Integrated operation:** All three safeguards (E-Stop, guard, light curtain) are *wired in series* in the safety chain, so that *any one* of them opening causes an immediate stop. Modern implementations use **safety relays** or **safety PLCs** to monitor these signals with periodic self-testing, achieving **SIL 2 / PL d** or higher.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in this topic (drawn from KTU valuation keys):**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant K = 1.6 m/s** in the safety distance formula → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Not stating the unit of stopping time** (seconds) and not specifying "mid-stroke measurement" → loses 1 mark.
