> 3. **Drawing the clutch and brake as engaged together** — they are *mutually exclusive*. Examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the anti-repeat cam switch** in the ladder diagram → anti-repeat is *not implemented*; full 7-mark question may be reduced to 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Confusing NO and NC contacts** in the ladder — E-Stop, guard, anti-repeat, and brake-limit are **all NC**; only start, foot, and two-hand are **NO**.
> 6. **Writing "IS 11188" without the year** — the examiner expects "IS 11188 : 1991 (Reaffirmed 2014)" for full credit on standards questions.
> 7. **Not mentioning the brake being "spring-applied, electrically released" (fail-safe)** in clutch-brake descriptions — this is a hallmark of a complete answer.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — print or screenshot this for last-day revision.**

- [ ] **Power press types:** Mechanical (flywheel), Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Servo. Electric controls are most critical for *mechanical* presses.
- [ ] **Core safety distance formula:** $\mathbf{D_s = K(T_s + T_r) + C}$, with $\mathbf{K = 1.6\ \text{m/s}}$. Always round *up*.
- [ ] **Anti-repeat (IS 11188):** Seal-in relay + cam switch at TDC; operator must release & re-press every stroke.
- [ ] **Two-hand control (ISO 13851 Type III):** Both buttons within **0.5 s**; re-initiation after any release.
- [ ] **Clutch–Brake interlock:** Mutually exclusive; brake is **spring-applied, electrically released** (fail-safe).
- [ ] **E-Stop (ISO 13850):** Hardwired NC, red mushroom on yellow, twist/key reset, Category 0 or 1.
- [ ] **Guard interlock (ISO 14119):** Position switch in series; use coded switches for PL e.
- [ ] **Light curtain (IEC 61496):** Type 2 or 4, OSSD outputs, positioned per $D_s$ formula, with muting/blanking where justified.
- [ ] **Standards trio to remember:** **IS 11188 : 1991** (press), **ISO 13850** (E-Stop), **ISO 13849-1** (PL d/e), **IEC 61496** (light curtain), **ISO 14119** (interlocks).
- [ ] **Synchronism window = 0.5 s** for two-hand control.
- [ ] **Pre-trip hooter = 2 s minimum** before clutch engagement.
- [ ] **Stopping time** must be measured at mid-stroke, single-stroke mode, worst case.
- [ ] **NC contacts for safety, NO contacts for commands** — golden rule of safety relay logic.
- [ ] **Every safety device is wired in series** in the safety chain → any one opening = safe stop.
- [ ] **PL d minimum** for press safeguards per ISO 13849-1; **SIL 2** per IEC 62061.
- [ ] **Flywheel energy formula:** $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$; convert $N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s) using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$.
- [ ] **Brake stopping time:** $T_{brake} = J\omega / M_{brake}$ — included in $T_s$ for safety distance.
- [ ] Modern press control: **PLC + safety relay** combination; HMI for diagnostics; **OSSD** outputs from light curtains and interlocks.
- [ ] **Anti-repeat cam is at TDC** (top dead centre), not at the bottom — the cam *opens* at the top of the stroke to break the holding path.
- [ ] **Reaching over a horizontal light curtain** uses a different formula: $D_s = 1.6(H - 1.0) + 1.2$ (where H is height of curtain in metres) — bonus point in answer if recalled.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Press Set Up and Die Removal
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Press Set Up and Die Removal

# Power Press Set Up and Die Removal

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Press:** A mechanical or hydraulic machine used in metal forming operations that employs stored energy (typically from a flywheel) to apply a controlled, high-tonnage compressive force to a workpiece through a slide (ram) mechanism. It shapes, cuts, or forms sheet metal using a pair of matched tooling called a **die set** (comprising the upper/punch die and the lower/matrix die).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Die Set Up (Die Setting):** The systematic, procedure-driven operation of installing, aligning, and securing the upper and lower die components onto the press bed and slide of a power press, including the setting of **shut height**, **stroke length**, and **pressure adjustment** to produce defect-free components safely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Die Removal (Die Stripping):** The reverse procedure of dismantling, lifting, and transporting the die assembly from the press after production, executed under strict **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)** protocols to eliminate stored energy hazards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a giant hole puncher you use at a school desk — but scaled up to **50–2000 tons** of force. The **lower die** is like the hollow tube, the **upper die** is like the punch, and the metal sheet is the paper you insert between them. Now imagine changing those two parts twice a day for different shapes. That is **die setting**. The danger is equivalent to inserting your hand between two closing walls weighing a small car — and that is exactly why this topic is at the heart of **industrial safety engineering**.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard industry metric: A typical **100-ton mechanical power press** delivers a ram force of approximately **890 kN (kiloNewtons)** at full tonnage, sufficient to amputate a human limb in **< 0.05 seconds**.

### 1.3 Classification of Power Presses

| Type | Energy Source | Typical Use | Stroke Rate |
|------|---------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Mechanical (Crank/Shaft)** | Flywheel | Blanking, Piercing, Drawing | 30–120 SPM |
| **Hydraulic** | Hydraulic fluid pressure | Deep Drawing, Forging | 5–30 SPM |
| **Pneumatic** | Compressed air | Light blanking, Stamping | 60–200 SPM |
| **Servo Press** | Servo motor + ballscrew | Precision forming | Variable |
| **Knuckle Joint** | Toggle linkage | Coining, Embossing | 20–60 SPM |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Power Press (Key Components)

The power press consists of the following functional units that the operator must understand before performing die setup:

- **Frame (Crown/Horn):** The structural body (C-frame or straight-side) housing all components; rated in **tonnage capacity**.
- **Slide (Ram):** The vertically moving part that holds the upper die.
- **Bed (Bolster):** The stationary lower table that holds the lower die and includes **T-slots** for clamping.
- **Flywheel + Clutch:** Stores rotational energy; the **clutch** engages/disengages power to the crankshaft.
- **Brake:** Stops the crankshaft; must be capable of stopping the ram within a **safe stopping angle**.
- **Counterbalance / Air Cushion:** Neutralises slide weight to prevent unintended descent.
- **Stroking Mechanism:** Initiates ram movement; subject to **single-stroke / continuous / inch mode** controls.
- **Die Set:** A precision assembly of upper shoe, lower shoe, guide pins, and guide bushings that ensures alignment.
- **Bolster Plate:** Adds height to the die to align with the press shut height window.

## 2.2 Pre-Setup Safety Inspection (The "5-Point Check")

Before any die change, the operator **must** execute the following:

1. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Safety goggles, cut-resistant gloves (only during non-pinch tasks), steel-toe boots, hearing protection.
2. **Press Condition Check:** Inspect brake, clutch, guards, two-hand control, light curtain/photo-electric guard, and emergency stop.
3. **Die Inspection:** Visually inspect die for cracks, worn guide pins, damaged stripper springs, broken fasteners, and cleanliness.
4. **Material & Tool Readiness:** Verify the correct die, material gauge, lubrication, and corresponding **SOP (Standard Operating Procedure)**.
5. **Energy Isolation (LOTO):** De-energise the press, lock the main disconnect, and tag it with a personal lock and tag.

> [!WARNING]
> Per KTU/Central Government Factory Rules and the **Factories Act 1948 (India)**, unauthorised die change is a **cognizable offence** and may result in prosecution of the supervisor.

## 2.3 The KTU Die Setup Procedure (12-Step Standard)

The die setup operation is divided into **three distinct phases** for safe execution:

### Phase A — Preparation (Steps 1–4)

1. **Obtain authorisation** and the production job-card.
2. **Isolate energy** using the LOTO procedure (lock + tag + try).
3. **Position the die cart / die truck** adjacent to the press bed.
4. **Clean the bolster and slide face** with non-flammable solvent; remove swarf and oil.

### Phase B — Mounting (Steps 5–9)

5. **Hoist the lower die** using the overhead crane or chain block; verify sling/chain load rating (must be **≥ 1.5× die weight**).
6. **Set the lower die on the bolster** and align within the **T-slot pattern**; insert clamping bolts and tighten to specified torque.
7. **Set the shut height** using **height-adjusting screws / die set pins**; verify with a **feeler gauge or dial indicator** to ±0.05 mm tolerance.
8. **Mount the upper die** onto the slide using the slide adapter; bolt and torque.
9. **Engage the die set guides** (4-guide-pin die set); confirm smooth sliding action with no binding.

### Phase C — Verification (Steps 10–12)

10. **Inch the press** (jog mode) with the operator's hand on the **emergency stop**; observe the die closure for parallelism and clearance.
11. **Adjust the brake angle** so the ram stops at **0°–30° past bottom dead centre (BDC)** to prevent unintended restart.
12. **Run a few blank/stroke cycles in single-stroke mode** with a scrap stripper; confirm part quality, then resume production.

## 2.4 The KTU Die Removal Procedure (8-Step Standard)

Die removal is essentially the **reverse of setup** but with elevated risk because the die is hot (warm working), oily, and heavy:

1. **Stop the press** at top dead centre (TDC) and apply LOTO.
2. **Disconnect all utilities** (air, oil, coolant lines, sensors).
3. **Detach strippers, sensors, and feeders** from the die.
4. **Remove upper die bolts** and lift upper die with crane onto die rack.
5. **Remove lower die bolts** and lift lower die onto die cart.
6. **Clean the bolster and slide** and apply rust-preventive oil.
7. **Move the die to the storage rack**; label with die number, weight, last production date.
8. **Return LOTO lock and tag** to the operator, complete the **Die Change Logbook** entry.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Use |
|-----------|---------|------|-----|
| Press Tonnage Force | $F = P \times A$ | N | Hydraulic pressure × ram area |
| Shut Height (Closed) | $H_{closed} = H_{die} + H_{bolster}$ | mm | Die height + bolster height |
| Stroke Length | $L_s = 2 \times R$ (crank radius) | mm | For crank press |
| Kinetic Energy of Ram | $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | Energy at impact (for safety analysis) |
| Stopping Time (Brake) | $t_s = \dfrac{J \cdot \omega}{T_b}$ | s | Brake torque analysis |
| Safe Stopping Angle | $\theta_s \le 30°$ past BDC | degrees | Brake setting criterion |
| Crane Sling Load (Safety Factor) | $W_{sling} \ge 1.5 \times W_{die}$ | kg | Hoisting safety factor |
| Bolt Clamping Torque | $T = K \times F \times d$ | N·m | Bolster clamping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key constant:** Standard KTU-recommended **bolt tightening safety factor (K)** for M-grade die bolts is **K = 0.20** (typical dry steel-on-steel).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculation of Press Tonnage Requirement

A common KTU 2024 Scheme problem asks students to determine the required press tonnage. The derivation is shown below.

**Given:**
- Sheet metal thickness, $t = 3 \text{ mm}$
- Shear strength of material, $\tau = 350 \text{ MPa} = 350 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Perimeter of blanked shape, $L = 600 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Shear Force on the Cut Edge**

The force required to shear the metal along the blank perimeter is the shear strength times the cut area.

$$
F_{shear} = \tau \times A_{cut}
$$

where $A_{cut} = L \times t$.

$$
A_{cut} = 600 \text{ mm} \times 3 \text{ mm} = 1800 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
F_{shear} = 350 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 1800 \text{ mm}^2 = 630{,}000 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Tonnage**

$$
\text{Tonnage} = \dfrac{F_{shear}}{g} \times \dfrac{1}{1000} \text{ in tonnes-force}
$$

where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ and $1 \text{ tonne-force} = 9810 \text{ N}$.

$$
\text{Tonnage} = \dfrac{630{,}000 \text{ N}}{9810 \text{ N/tonne}} = 64.2 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Safety Factor**

Industrial practice and KTU safety guidelines recommend a **2.0× safety factor** for press selection to account for material variability, die wear, and lubrication fluctuations.

$$
\text{Press Capacity Required} = 64.2 \times 2.0 = 128.4 \text{ tonnes}
$$

> Therefore, a **130-tonne press** is the minimum recommended selection.

## 3.2 Derivation: Verification of Shut Height

**Given:**
- Press shut height range: **200 mm – 280 mm**
- Die closed height: $H_{die} = 150 \text{ mm}$
- Bolster plate height: $H_{bolster} = 75 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Compute Total Closed Height**

$$
H_{total} = H_{die} + H_{bolster}
$$

$$
H_{total} = 150 \text{ mm} + 75 \text{ mm} = 225 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify Against Press Range**

Check that the computed value lies between the minimum and maximum shut height of the press.

$$
200 \text{ mm} \le 225 \text{ mm} \le 280 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Adjustment Margin**

$$
\Delta H_{min} = 225 - 200 = 25 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta H_{max} = 280 - 225 = 55 \text{ mm}
$$

Both margins are **positive**, confirming safe installation. If $\Delta H_{min} < 0$, the die is too tall and must be re-machined or used with a shorter bolster.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hazards vs. Control Measures

| Hazard | Phase | Consequence | Engineering Control | Administrative Control |
|--------|-------|-------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Unexpected ram descent | Setup | Crushing fatality | Counterbalance check, mechanical block | LOTO, two-person rule |
| Stored flywheel energy | Removal | Rotational trauma | Brake inspection | Energy isolation checklist |
| Falling die | Hoisting | Fatal head injury | Sling inspection, rated shackle | Trained rigger, exclusion zone |
| Pinch point at die face | Mounting | Amputation | Light curtain, two-hand control | Safe distance rule (≥ 500 mm) |
| Hydraulic injection injury | Removal | Tissue necrosis | Pressure relief | PPE (face shield) |
| Slips on oil | All phases | Fall injury | Drip trays, anti-skid floor | Housekeeping SOP |
| Repetitive strain | Setup | Musculoskeletal | Die cart, roller track | Job rotation |
| Hand in die | Stroke test | Amputation | Fixed perimeter guard | Single-stroke mode only |

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Brake Stopping Angle Verification

**Given:**
- Press crankshaft speed, $N = 60 \text{ rpm}$
- Brake torque, $T_b = 250 \text{ N·m}$
- Moment of inertia of rotating parts, $J = 5 \text{ kg·m}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute Angular Velocity**

$$
\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 60}{60} = 2\pi \text{ rad/s} = 6.283 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Stopping Time**

$$
t_s = \dfrac{J \cdot \omega}{T_b} = \dfrac{5 \times 6.283}{250} = 0.1257 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Stopping Angle**

$$
\theta_s = \dfrac{1}{2} \omega t_s = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 6.283 \times 0.1257 = 0.395 \text{ rad}
$$

Converting to degrees:

$$
\theta_s = 0.395 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 22.6°
$$

Since $22.6° \le 30°$, the brake is **compliant with KTU safety standards**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Power Press Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Main Power Supply] --> B[Main Disconnect Switch]
    B --> C[Motor]
    C --> D[Flywheel]
    D --> E[Clutch Assembly]
    E --> F[Brake Assembly]
    F --> G[Crankshaft]
    G --> H[Slide Ram]
    H --> I[Upper Die Holder]
    I --> J[Upper Die]
    K[Bed Bolster] --> L[Lower Die]
    H --> M[Shut Height Adjuster]
    K --> M
    N[LOTO Lockout Station] --> B
    N --> E
    N --> F
    O[Two Hand Control] --> E
    P[Light Curtain Guard] --> O
    Q[Emergency Stop Circuit] --> E
    Q --> F
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Die Setup Procedure Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Start Die Change] --> step1[Obtain Authorization and Job Card]
    step1 --> step2[Apply LOTO Lockout Tagout]
    step2 --> step3[Position Die Cart Adjacent]
    step3 --> step4[Clean Bolster and Slide Face]
    step4 --> step5[Hoist Lower Die Using Crane]
    step5 --> step6[Mount Lower Die on Bolster T Slots]
    step6 --> step7[Tighten Clamping Bolts to Torque Spec]
    step7 --> step8[Set Shut Height Using Feeler Gauge]
    step8 --> step9[Mount Upper Die on Slide Adapter]
    step9 --> step10[Engage Die Set Guide Pins]
    step10 --> step11[Inch Press with Hand on E Stop]
    step11 --> step12[Run Single Stroke Test Cycles]
    step12 --> step13[Remove LOTO Resume Production]
    step13 --> stopA[End Die Setup]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Die Removal Procedure Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    startB[Start Die Removal] --> r1[Stop Press at Top Dead Centre]
    r1 --> r2[Apply LOTO and Verify Zero Energy]
    r2 --> r3[Disconnect Air Oil Coolant Lines]
    r3 --> r4[Detach Strippers and Sensors]
    r4 --> r5[Remove Upper Die Bolts and Lift]
    r5 --> r6[Place Upper Die on Storage Rack]
    r6 --> r7[Remove Lower Die Bolts and Lift]
    r7 --> r8[Place Lower Die on Die Cart]
    r8 --> r9[Clean Bolster Apply Rust Preventive]
    r9 --> r10[Move Die to Storage and Label]
    r10 --> r11[Update Die Change Logbook]
    r11 --> stopB[End Die Removal]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Safety Control Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Level 1 Elimination] --> L2[Level 2 Substitution]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Engineering Controls]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Administrative Controls]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 PPE]
    L3 --> E1[Light Curtain]
    L3 --> E2[Two Hand Control]
    L3 --> E3[Fixed Perimeter Guard]
    L3 --> E4[Counterbalance Check]
    L4 --> A1[LOTO Procedure]
    L4 --> A2[Safe Operating Procedure SOP]
    L4 --> A3[Training and Certification]
    L4 --> A4[Die Change Logbook]
    L5 --> P1[Safety Goggles]
    L5 --> P2[Cut Resistant Gloves]
    L5 --> P3[Steel Toe Boots]
    L5 --> P4[Hearing Protection]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term "Die Set" in the context of a power press. List its main components. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A die set is a precision framework that holds the upper and lower dies in accurate alignment during press operations. Its main components are:

1. **Upper Shoe** — Mounted on the press slide, carries the punch.
2. **Lower Shoe** — Mounted on the press bolster, carries the matrix.
3. **Guide Pins (Pins / Posts)** — Ensure concentric alignment of upper and lower dies.
4. **Guide Bushings** — Provide low-friction sliding surfaces for guide pins.
5. **Stripper Plate** — Removes the workpiece from the punch after forming.
6. **Heel / Back Plates** — Resist bending forces during forming.

### Q2. State the importance of LOTO (Lockout-Tagout) during power press die change. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

LOTO is a **mandatory safety procedure** that physically isolates all energy sources of a power press to prevent accidental start-up during die setup or removal. Its importance is:

1. Protects the operator from **stored flywheel energy** and **hydraulic/pneumatic pressure**.
2. Provides a **visible, tamper-proof indication** of maintenance state.
3. Complies with **Factories Act 1948** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147** standards.
4. Prevents unauthorised re-energisation by other workers.
5. Reduces fatalities — die change accidents are among the **top 5 causes** of press-related deaths in India.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure for setting up a die in a mechanical power press, with safety precautions. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Phase A — Preparation**

1. **Authorization:** Receive the job card and die identification number from the supervisor. Verify the die number matches the work order.

2. **PPE:** Wear safety goggles, cut-resistant gloves (for non-pinch tasks), safety shoes, and hearing protection.

3. **Press Condition Check:** Inspect the brake, clutch, anti-restart devices, two-hand control, light curtain, and emergency stop. The press must be in a "ready to run" condition.

4. **LOTO Application:** Isolate electrical supply at the main disconnect, drain pneumatic/hydraulic pressure, lower the counterbalance, and apply the personal lock and tag. **Test-start** the press to confirm zero energy state.

5. **Die Inspection:** Examine the die for cracks, worn guide pins, broken stripper springs, and cleanliness. Verify the **shut height** of the die set.

6. **Die Cart Positioning:** Move the die cart/dolly adjacent to the press; align within the swing radius of the overhead crane.

**Phase B — Mounting**

7. **Bolster Cleaning:** Wipe the bolster and slide face with non-flammable solvent. Remove chips, swarf, and oil.

8. **Lower Die Hoisting:** Using the overhead crane and a sling rated for **1.5× the die weight**, hoist the lower die and lower it onto the bolster.

9. **Lower Die Alignment:** Align the die within the **T-slot pattern** of the bolster. Insert clamping bolts and torque to specification (typically **T = K × F × d** with K ≈ 0.20).

10. **Shut Height Setting:** Set the press shut height to match the die closed height plus bolster height using height-adjusting screws. Verify with a **feeler gauge** at ±0.05 mm.

11. **Upper Die Mounting:** Hoist the upper die using the slide adapter, attach to the slide, and torque the slide bolts.

12. **Die Set Engagement:** Engage the four guide pins into the bushings; confirm smooth, binding-free sliding.

**Phase C — Verification**

13. **Inching Test:** Switch to **inch (jog) mode**. Place hand on the emergency stop. Inch the press to bring the die faces within 6 mm of the workpiece. Check for parallelism using a dial indicator.

14. **Single-Stroke Test:** Run 3–5 single strokes on a scrap strip. Verify part quality, burr level, and dimensional accuracy.

15. **Production Start:** Remove the LOTO lock (only the authorised operator), switch to production mode, and commence.

**Key Safety Precautions:**

- Never place hands inside the die area during inching.
- Maintain a **500 mm safe distance** during stroke.
- Always use **single-stroke mode** during setup.
- Verify brake stopping angle ≤ 30° past BDC.
- Use a **die safety block** when working under the ram.

#### (b) Discuss the major hazards during die change and their engineering control measures. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

| S.No | Hazard | Consequence | Engineering Control |
|------|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Unexpected ram descent** | Crushing of limbs/fatality | Mechanical die block, counterbalance, dual anti-restart valve |
| 2 | **Stored flywheel energy** | Sudden rotation, entanglement | Brake inspection, clutch isolation |
| 3 | **Falling die during hoisting** | Fatal head injury | Rated slings, shock-absorbing hooks, exclusion zone |
| 4 | **Pinch point at die face** | Amputation of fingers | Light curtain, two-hand control, fixed perimeter guard |
| 5 | **Hydraulic fluid injection** | Tissue necrosis, amputation | Pressure relief, face shield, leak detection |
| 6 | **Slip on oil** | Fall, fracture | Anti-skid mats, drip trays, drip-free lubrication |
| 7 | **Repetitive strain injury** | Back pain, hernia | Die cart, roller conveyor, mechanical lift |
| 8 | **Hand inside die** | Crush, amputation | Single-stroke mode, two-hand control, presence-sensing |
| 9 | **Electrical shock** | Burn, cardiac arrest | Lockout, earthing, residual current device |
| 10 | **Noise > 90 dB** | Hearing loss | Acoustic enclosure, ear plugs/muffs |

The hierarchy of controls (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE) must be applied. **Engineering controls are the most reliable** because they do not depend on operator behaviour.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Describe the procedure for safe removal of a die from a power press. Explain the role of LOTO in this process. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The die removal process follows the **8-step KTU standard**:

1. **Stop Press at TDC:** Bring the ram to top dead centre using inch mode. This aligns the upper die for safe removal.

2. **Apply LOTO:** De-energise at the main disconnect, lock with personal padlock, attach danger tag, and **test** to confirm zero energy.

3. **Disconnect Utilities:** Disconnect air lines (after bleeding pressure), hydraulic lines (after depressurising), coolant lines, and electrical sensor cables from the die. Cap open ports to prevent contamination.

4. **Detach Ancillary Components:** Remove stripper plates, feed fingers, stock guides, sensors, and scrap cutters from the die.

5. **Remove Upper Die:** Loosen slide bolts using torque wrench in reverse order (cross-pattern to avoid binding). Attach the crane sling to the upper die's lifting eye, take the slack, and remove the last bolt. Lift slowly and place on a designated die rack.

6. **Remove Lower Die:** Loosen bolster clamps in cross-pattern. Attach sling, take slack, and remove remaining bolts. Lift and place on the die cart.

7. **Clean and Preserve:** Clean bolster and slide face; apply rust-preventive oil (e.g., **SAE 30 mineral oil**). Cover with a tarpaulin.

8. **Documentation and Storage:** Label the die with its identification number, weight, last production date, and operator name. Move to the storage rack in the die library. Complete the **Die Change Logbook** and **Maintenance Job Card**.

**Role of LOTO in Die Removal:**

- **Energy Isolation:** The flywheel may have residual kinetic energy; the clutch/brake system must be locked to prevent re-engagement.
- **Hydraulic/Pneumatic Safety:** Residual pressure in hydraulic accumulators or air receivers can cause sudden ram descent.
- **Electrical Safety:** Prevents inadvertent start from a control panel or remote switch.
- **Legal Compliance:** Required under **Section 21 of the Factories Act 1948** and Indian Standard **IS 11952**.
- **Multi-Worker Protection:** Each worker applies their **own** personal lock; the press cannot be re-energised until **all** locks are removed.

#### (b) Calculate the press tonnage required to blank a circular disc of 80 mm diameter from a 4 mm thick mild steel sheet. Also verify the shut height if the die closed height is 160 mm and bolster height is 60 mm (press range 180–260 mm). Take shear strength = 350 MPa and safety factor = 2.0. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Perimeter of the Blank**

For a circular disc of diameter $D = 80 \text{ mm}$:

$$
L = \pi \times D = \pi \times 80 = 251.33 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Shear Area**

$$
A_{cut} = L \times t = 251.33 \text{ mm} \times 4 \text{ mm} = 1005.3 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Shear Force**

$$
F_{shear} = \tau \times A_{cut} = 350 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 1005.3 \text{ mm}^2 = 351{,}855 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Tonnage**

$$
\text{Tonnage} = \dfrac{F_{shear}}{9810} = \dfrac{351{,}855}{9810} = 35.87 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Safety Factor**

$$
\text{Required Press Capacity} = 35.87 \times 2.0 = 71.74 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Therefore, a **75-tonne press** is the minimum recommended selection.

**Step 6 — Verify Shut Height**

$$
H_{total} = H_{die} + H_{bolster} = 160 + 60 = 220 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify Against Press Range**

$$
180 \text{ mm} \le 220 \text{ mm} \le 260 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 8 — Compute Margins**

- Margin to minimum: $220 - 180 = 40 \text{ mm}$
- Margin to maximum: $260 - 220 = 40 \text{ mm}$

The die fits comfortably within the press window with **40 mm adjustment margin on each side**. ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mark Loss #1 (1 mark):** Students often forget to convert Newtons to tonnes by dividing by **9810 N/tonne**. Always include the unit conversion.
> - **Mark Loss #2 (1 mark):** Skipping the **safety factor** in press selection. The examiner will deduct 1 mark if the design capacity is reported as 35.87 tonnes without the 2× factor.
> - **Mark Loss #3 (2 marks):** Failing to verify **shut height window compliance** — this is a **mandatory step** in any press setup question.
> - **Mark Loss #4 (1 mark):** Writing "shut height = 220 mm" without comparing it to the press range.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Power press** is a high-tonnage forming machine using a die set (upper + lower die) to cut, form, or shape sheet metal.
- **Die setup** has **3 phases** (Preparation → Mounting → Verification) and **12 standard steps** as per KTU.
- **Die removal** has **8 standard steps** executed strictly in reverse order with elevated safety controls.
- **LOTO (Lockout-Tagout)** is **non-negotiable** during any die change; it is the single most important safety control.
- **Shut height** is the sum of **die closed height + bolster height**; it must fall within the press's permissible window.
- **Brake stopping angle** must be **≤ 30° past BDC** to prevent unintended restarts.
- **Crane sling** must have a safety factor of **≥ 1.5× die weight** to prevent catastrophic drop.
- **Press tonnage** = $(\tau \times L \times t) / 9810$ **× Safety Factor** (typically 2.0).
- **Single-stroke mode** is **mandatory** during setup; **continuous mode** is forbidden.
- **Die set guide pins and bushings** must be inspected for wear before every setup.
- **Hierarchy of Controls** must be applied: Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE.
- **Die Change Logbook** entry is a legal requirement under the Factories Act 1948.
- **Press operators** must be **trained and certified**; unauthorised die change is a punishable offence.
- **Two-hand control + light curtain** are the most reliable engineering controls against hand-in-die accidents.
- **Hydraulic accumulator** must be bled to **zero pressure** before any die work begins.
- **Energy isolation** = **Electrical + Mechanical (flywheel) + Hydraulic + Pneumatic + Gravitational (counterbalance)**.
- Always use a **die safety block (mechanical stop)** when working under the ram.
- **Standard unit conversions:** $1 \text{ tonne-force} = 9810 \text{ N} = 9.81 \text{ kN}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspection and Maintenance - Metal Shears - Press Brakes
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inspection and Maintenance - Metal Shears - Press Brakes

---

# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Metal Shears — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metal Shears (Power Shearing Machines):** A metal shear is a machine used in sheet metal fabrication to make straight-line cuts by applying a shearing force between two blades — a **stationary lower blade** (bed) and a **movable upper blade** (rams). The action severs the metal along a thin shear zone where **shear stress exceeds the ultimate shear strength** of the workpiece material.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Industrial Paper Cutter on Steroids"

Imagine the office paper guillotine used to trim stacks of A4 sheets. Now scale that up: replace paper with **steel plates up to 25 mm thick**, replace the hand lever with a **hydraulic ram producing 100–1000 tonnes of force**, and add sensors, light curtains, and emergency stops. That is essentially a metal shear. The cutting action is identical — a descending upper blade slicing against a fixed lower blade — only the magnitude of force and the safety stakes are dramatically higher.

## 1.2 Press Brake — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Press Brake:** A press brake is a machine tool used in sheet metalworking to bend, fold, punch, or form sheet and plate material by clamping it between a **punch (top tool)** and a **die (bottom tool)** through a controlled pressing action. It is distinct from a shear because the goal is **plastic deformation**, not cutting.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Giant Metal Pancake Flipper"

Think of a waffle iron in a diner. You place dough (metal sheet), close the lid (ram descends), and the shaped plates (punch and die) press the dough into the desired shape (bent angle). The press brake works identically — except the "dough" is **cold-rolled steel up to several metres long**, the "lid" closes with **tonnes of force**, and an operator's hand cannot be near it when active.

## 1.3 Why Inspection and Maintenance Matter — The Safety Nexus

> [!NOTE]
> Under the **Indian Factories Act 1948 (Section 21 & 22)** and the **OSH Code 2020 (Section 18)**, every dangerous part of machinery including shears and press brakes must be securely fenced and periodically inspected. Failure of these machines is a leading cause of **amputation injuries** in fabrication shops worldwide.

### 1.3.1 Classification of Shears and Press Brakes

| Machine Type | Drive Mechanism | Typical Capacity | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (Flywheel) Shear** | Flywheel + clutch | Up to **6 mm mild steel** | High-volume production |
| **Hydraulic Shear** | Hydraulic cylinder | Up to **25 mm mild steel** | Heavy plate cutting |
| **Guillotine Shear** | Mechanical swing beam | Up to **10 mm mild steel** | General fabrication |
| **Mechanical Press Brake** | Flywheel + eccentric | Up to **200 tonnes** | Simple bends |
| **Hydraulic Press Brake** | Hydraulic cylinder | Up to **3000 tonnes** | Heavy bending |
| **CNC Press Brake** | Servo-hydraulic | Up to **1000 tonnes** | Precision work |

---

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operating Principles

### 2.1.1 Metal Shear — The Shearing Action

The shear works on the principle that metal failure occurs along a **shear plane** when **applied shear stress (τ) exceeds the ultimate shear strength (S_us)** of the material.

**Step-by-step shearing process:**

- **Step 1: Clamping** — The workpiece is held against a back gauge (alignment fence) and clamped by hold-downs (pressure feet) to prevent spring-back.
- **Step 2: Penetration** — The upper ram descends, pressing the upper blade into the material. Blade penetration depth is typically **0.5 to 1.5 mm** before fracture initiates.
- **Step 3: Fracture Propagation** — Once the shear zone is sufficiently stressed, micro-cracks initiate at the cut edges and propagate inward through the sheet thickness.
- **Step 4: Separation** — The two halves of the sheet are separated with a clean cut; the cut quality depends on **blade clearance, rake angle, and blade sharpness**.
- **Step 5: Retraction** — The ram retracts to the up position; the workpiece is removed and the cycle repeats.

### 2.1.2 Press Brake — The Bending Action

The press brake uses **three-point bending** mechanics where the punch forces the sheet into a V-die opening.

**Step-by-step bending process:**

- **Step 1: Loading** — The sheet is positioned on the die with the bend line aligned to the die centre.
- **Step 2: Clamping** — The ram descends and the punch contacts the sheet, pressing it against the die shoulders.
- **Step 3: Plastic Deformation** — As the ram continues, the sheet fibres on the outside of the bend elongate (tension) and inner fibres compress; the neutral axis shifts inward.
- **Step 4: Spring-back** — When the ram retracts, the sheet elastically recovers by an angle **θ_s** (typically 1°–5° depending on material yield strength).
- **Step 5: Unloading** — The formed part is removed; air-bending, coining, or bottoming methods may be used.

## 2.2 Inspection Categories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Inspection of shears and press brakes is classified into **four tiers** based on frequency and depth — this is a **KTU high-yield** classification worth memorising.

### Tier 1 — Pre-Shift / Daily Inspection (Operator Level)

Performed at the start of every shift by the machine operator:

- Visual check of blades for chips, cracks, or excessive wear.
- Verification of all guards, light curtains, and two-hand controls.
- Test of emergency stop circuits.
- Lubrication top-up at grease points.
- Hydraulic fluid level and pressure check.
- Back gauge alignment and squareness verification.
- Functional test of foot pedal / palm buttons (run in inch mode).

### Tier 2 — Weekly Inspection (Supervisor Level)

- Measure **blade clearance** (should be **5–10% of sheet thickness** for mild steel).
- Inspect **hold-down foot pressure** with a pressure gauge.
- Check ram parallelism (max deviation **0.05 mm/m** for CNC).
- Examine electrical wiring and earth continuity.
- Test brake/clutch stopping time (must stop within **fraction of a single stroke**).

### Tier 3 — Monthly Inspection (Maintenance Engineer Level)

- Detailed blade edge profile measurement using a profilometer.
- Hydraulic system analysis: filter condition, oil sampling for **particle count (ISO 4406)**, and water content (**Karl Fischer titration**).
- Back gauge ball-screw backlash measurement.
- Slide-way / ram guide clearance check.

### Tier 4 — Annual / Statutory Inspection (Competent Person)

- Full machine rebuild assessment.
- Load test verification using certified load cells.
- Verification of all safety devices against the original manufacturer's specification.
- Replacement of worn blades and seals.
- Re-certification of pressure relief valves and overload protection.
- Compliance audit against **IS 11952** (mechanical presses), **IS 11200** (hydraulic presses), and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.217**.

## 2.3 Maintenance Strategy

| Maintenance Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Reactive (Breakdown)** | Repair after failure | Unacceptable for safety-critical parts |
| **Preventive (Scheduled)** | Routine service at fixed intervals | Standard for shears and press brakes |
| **Predictive (Condition-Based)** | Service based on monitored condition (vibration, oil analysis) | Modern CNC machines |
| **Reliability-Centered (RCM)** | Logic-tree analysis to determine optimal task frequency | World-class fabricators |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\vert`-pipe-in-table. The table below uses `\` (backslash) escape-free formatting suitable for markdown.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shear Force on Blade | $F_s = \tau \cdot A = S_{us} \cdot L \cdot t$ | $F_s$ in N, $S_{us}$ in MPa, $L$ in mm (cut length), $t$ in mm (thickness) |
| 2 | Cutting Pressure | $P_c = \dfrac{F_s}{A_{ram}}$ | $P_c$ in MPa, $A_{ram}$ in mm² |
| 3 | Shear Stress Distribution | $\tau = \dfrac{F_s}{L \cdot t}$ | $\tau$ in MPa |
| 4 | Clearance-to-Thickness Ratio | $c = \dfrac{C_t}{t}$ | $c$ dimensionless, $C_t$ in mm, ideal **0.05 to 0.10** |
| 5 | Bend Allowance (BA) | $BA = \dfrac{\pi \cdot \theta}{180} \cdot (R + k \cdot t)$ | $\theta$ bend angle (°), $R$ inside bend radius (mm), $k$ = 0.33 for soft, 0.50 for hard |
| 6 | Spring-back Angle | $\theta_s = \dfrac{3 \cdot S_y}{E} \cdot \dfrac{R}{t}$ (approx.) | $S_y$ yield strength (MPa), $E$ Young's modulus (MPa) |
| 7 | Tonnes Capacity (Bending) | $T = \dfrac{L \cdot t^2 \cdot S_u}{C_f \cdot V}$ | $T$ in tonnes, $C_f$ die opening factor, $V$ die width (mm), $L$ bend length (mm) |
| 8 | Machine Tonnage Required | $T_{req} = \dfrac{1.33 \cdot L \cdot t^2 \cdot S_u}{V \cdot 1000}$ | Result in tonnes (safety factor 1.33 applied) |
| 9 | Hydraulic Cylinder Force | $F = P \cdot A_{piston}$ | $P$ in bar, $A_{piston}$ in cm², $F$ in daN |
| 10 | Punch Penetration (Coining) | $d_p \geq t$ for full coining | $d_p$ penetration depth (mm), $t$ material thickness (mm) |
| 11 | Stopping Time (Mechanical Press) | $t_{stop} = \dfrac{J \cdot \omega}{T_{brake}}$ | $J$ inertia, $\omega$ angular velocity, $T_{brake}$ brake torque |
| 12 | Safety Distance (Light Curtain) | $D_s = K \cdot T_s + C$ | $K$ hand-speed constant (1.6 m/s OSHA), $T_s$ stop time (s), $C$ penetration constant (mm) |

### 2.4.1 Formula Explanations

- **Formula 1** — Shear force equals the material's ultimate shear strength times the cut cross-sectional area. Determines the minimum ram force required.
- **Formula 4** — The clearance ratio determines cut quality; too low causes jamming, too high produces burrs and deformed edges.
- **Formula 6** — Spring-back is the elastic recovery of the bent material; must be compensated by **over-bending**.
- **Formula 8** — Press brake tonnage rule-of-thumb with safety factor; critical for tool selection.
- **Formula 12** — Used to position safety devices such that the operator cannot reach the hazard zone before the machine stops.

---

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Shear Force Required to Cut Mild Steel Plate

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the ram force required to shear **1500 mm long, 8 mm thick** mild steel plate, given the ultimate shear strength of mild steel is **345 MPa**.

### Given Data

- Cut length: $L = 1500$ mm
- Plate thickness: $t = 8$ mm
- Ultimate shear strength: $S_{us} = 345$ MPa $= 345$ N/mm²

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$\begin{aligned}
F_s &= S_{us} \cdot L \cdot t \\[6pt]
F_s &= 345 \text{ N/mm}^2 \cdot 1500 \text{ mm} \cdot 8 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
F_s &= 345 \cdot 12000 \text{ N} \\[6pt]
F_s &= 4{,}140{,}000 \text{ N} \\[6pt]
F_s &= 4140 \text{ kN} \approx \mathbf{422 \text{ tonnes-force}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Multiply strength (N per mm²) by the two perpendicular dimensions (mm × mm) to get force in Newtons. Convert kN to tonnes-force by dividing by 9.81. This is the *minimum* force — actual machine capacity should be **1.5–2× higher** to account for blade wear, material variability, and dynamic effects.

## 3.2 Derivation — Press Brake Tonnage for an Air Bend

**Problem Statement:** A 6 mm mild steel sheet of length **2000 mm** is to be bent to 90° in a die with V-opening **V = 24 mm** (eight times the material thickness, the standard rule). The ultimate tensile strength is **400 MPa**. Calculate the required tonnage.

### Given Data

- Sheet length: $L = 2000$ mm
- Material thickness: $t = 6$ mm
- Ultimate tensile strength: $S_u = 400$ MPa
- Die opening: $V = 8 \cdot t = 48$ mm (revised for accuracy)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{req} &= \dfrac{1.33 \cdot L \cdot t^2 \cdot S_u}{V \cdot 1000} \\[6pt]
T_{req} &= \dfrac{1.33 \cdot 2000 \cdot (6)^2 \cdot 400}{48 \cdot 1000} \\[6pt]
T_{req} &= \dfrac{1.33 \cdot 2000 \cdot 36 \cdot 400}{48{,}000} \\[6pt]
T_{req} &= \dfrac{38{,}304{,}000}{48{,}000} \\[6pt]
T_{req} &= \mathbf{798 \text{ tonnes}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** First compute $L \cdot t^2 \cdot S_u$ (gives N·mm²), multiply by safety factor 1.33, then divide by $V \cdot 1000$ to convert mm³ to tonnes-force (using the approximation 1 tonne ≈ 10⁷ N/mm² for steel tonnage rules). This tonnage determines machine selection.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes and Inspection Targets

| # | Component | Failure Mode | Root Cause | Inspection Method | Maintenance Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Upper Blade | Edge rounding, chipping | Material hardness mismatch, impacts | Visual + edge profile | Regrind or replace |
| 2 | Lower Blade | Grooving, mushrooming | Reverse use of blades | Visual + dimension check | Rotate or replace |
| 3 | Hydraulic Seal | Oil leakage | Heat aging, contamination | Oil analysis + visual | Replace seal kit |
| 4 | Hydraulic Cylinder | Drift, chatter | Air ingress, worn seals | Pressure decay test | Re-bore or replace |
| 5 | Brake Disc | Slipping, slow stop | Wear, oil contamination | Stop-time measurement | Re-bed or replace |
| 6 | Clutch | Failure to engage/disengage | Worn friction surface | Engagement test | Reface or replace |
| 7 | Back Gauge | Inaccuracy > ±0.1 mm | Ball-screw wear, backlash | Dial indicator check | Adjust preload or replace |
| 8 | Light Curtain | False trip, blind spot | Lens contamination, misalignment | Daily test with test rod | Clean, realign, or replace |
| 9 | Pressure Relief Valve | Set pressure drift | Spring fatigue, seat wear | Bench test with gauge | Re-set or replace |
| 10 | Electrical Wiring | Earth fault, short circuit | Insulation aging | Megger test at **500 V DC** | Replace damaged cables |
| 11 | Lubrication System | No oil delivery | Blocked line, pump failure | Flow test at each point | Flush, replace filter |
| 12 | Die Set (Press Brake) | Pillow effect, marking | Misalignment, scoring | Visual + go/no-go gauge | Re-grind, shim, replace |

## 3.4 Derivation — Safety Distance for Light Curtain Installation

**Problem Statement:** A mechanical power press has a measured **stopping time of 90 ms (0.090 s)**. Calculate the minimum safety distance at which the light curtain must be mounted, per OSHA standards.

### Given Data

- Stopping time: $T_s = 0.090$ s
- OSHA hand-speed constant: $K = 1.6$ m/s (= 63 in/s)
- Penetration constant (two-beam curtain): $C = 25$ mm

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$\begin{aligned}
D_s &= K \cdot T_s + C \\[6pt]
D_s &= 1.6 \text{ m/s} \cdot 0.090 \text{ s} + 0.025 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
D_s &= 0.144 \text{ m} + 0.025 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
D_s &= \mathbf{0.169 \text{ m} \approx 169 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Multiply the constant hand approach speed by the machine stop time to find the distance the hand travels before the ram halts. Add the penetration depth the fingers can reach through the curtain. The total is the minimum distance the curtain must be from the hazard — measured from the hazard to the *closest* beam interruption point.

---

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hydraulic Shear Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply 415V 3phase] --> B[Motor Starter DOL]
    B --> C[Hydraulic Pump Axial Piston]
    C --> D[Pressure Relief Valve Set 200 bar]
    D --> E[Directional Control Valve 4by3]
    E --> F[Hydraulic Cylinder Double Acting]
    F --> G[Ram and Upper Blade]
    G --> H[Workpiece Shear Zone]
    H --> I[Lower Blade Fixed Bed]
    I --> J[Scrap Chute and Bin]
    K[Light Curtain Type 4 PLe] --> L[Safety PLC]
    M[E Stop Push Button] --> L
    N[Foot Pedal Inch Mode] --> L
    L --> E
    O[Operator HMI Touchscreen] --> E
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Press Brake Safety System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Operator Left Hand Button] --> SC[Safety Relay]
    P2[Operator Right Hand Button] --> SC
    P3[Foot Pedal Guarded] --> SC
    LC1[Light Curtain Bottom Beam] --> SC
    LC2[Light Curtain Top Beam] --> SC
    ES1[E Stop Cat 0] --> SC
    ES2[E Stop Cat 1] --> SC
    SC --> V1[Hydraulic Valve Block]
    SC --> V2[Ram Direction Control]
    V1 --> RAM[Ram and Punch]
    V2 --> RAM
    RAM --> DIE[Die Bed]
    BEND[Workpiece Bend Zone] --> DIE
    SP[Speed Monitor Encoder] --> SC
    RP[Ram Position Sensor] --> SC
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Tiered Inspection Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Shift Start] --> T1{Pre-Shift Check}
    T1 -->|Pass| Op[Production Operation]
    T1 -->|Fail| Maint1[Call Maintenance]
    Maint1 --> Op
    Op --> T2{Weekly Visual}
    T2 -->|Pass| Cont[Continue Production]
    T2 -->|Fail| SchedM[Schedule Maintenance]
    SchedM --> T3{Monthly Detail}
    T3 -->|Pass| Cert[Continue with Log]
    T3 -->|Fail| Plan[Plan Major Service]
    Plan --> T4{Annual Statutory}
    T4 -->|Pass| Rec[Re-certify Machine]
    T4 -->|Fail| Repl[Replace Components]
    Repl --> T4
    Rec --> Op
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Maintenance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Vibration Increased?} -->|Yes| VIB[Check Bearings and Coupling]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Oil Temperature Above 65C?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| OIL[Check Cooler and Oil Level]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Stop Time Exceeded 100ms?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| BRK[Inspect Brake and Clutch]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Cut Quality Degraded?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| BLD[Inspect Blade Sharpness and Clearance]
    Q4 -->|No| OK[Continue Normal Operation]
    VIB --> REC[Record in Maintenance Log]
    OIL --> REC
    BRK --> REC
    BLD --> REC
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> **Q:** List any **three daily pre-shift inspection checks** to be carried out on a mechanical power shear before commencing production.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Visual inspection of blades:** Check upper and lower blades for nicks, chips, cracks, and rounded edges. Damaged blades produce burrs and may fracture during cutting, causing ejection of fragments.
2. **Guard and safety device test:** Verify that fixed perimeter guards, light curtains, and two-hand control buttons are functional. Press the emergency stop and confirm the ram halts within the rated stop time.
3. **Hydraulic and lubrication check:** Inspect hydraulic reservoir level, oil clarity, and lubricate all grease points as per the manufacturer's chart. Top-up to the recommended grade (typically ISO VG 46 for hydraulic systems).

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

> **Q:** Define **spring-back** in press brake bending. Why must it be compensated, and how?

**Model Answer:**

**Spring-back** is the elastic recovery of a bent sheet metal part when the bending force is removed. It is caused by the elastic strain component of the deformation; once the punch retracts, the outer fibres (which were in tension) and inner fibres (which were in compression) partially return to their original shape, causing the bend angle to open by **1°–5°** depending on material yield strength and bend radius-to-thickness ratio.

It must be compensated because the **finished part dimension** would otherwise be incorrect. Compensation methods include:
- **Over-bending** — bending past the target angle by the spring-back amount.
- **Bottoming** — forcing the punch to the bottom of the die to plastically deform the elastic component.
- **Coining** — high-pressure penetration that compresses the bend zone beyond yield.
- **Adjusting the die opening** — tighter V-openings reduce spring-back.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **types of metal shears** with neat sketches and state the **safety devices** that must be fitted on each.
>
> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Describe the **monthly inspection procedure** for a hydraulic press brake with reference to the **IS standards** applicable.

#### Model Solution for Q (a)

**Types of Metal Shears:**

**1. Mechanical (Flywheel) Power Shear**
A flywheel stores rotational kinetic energy and delivers it to the ram through a clutch. The ram is driven by a connecting rod from the crankshaft. Stroke rate is high (up to **60 strokes per minute**) but capacity is limited to **6 mm mild steel**.

**2. Hydraulic Guillotine Shear**
A hydraulic cylinder drives the upper blade in a vertical or swing-beam configuration. Capacity is high (**up to 25 mm plate**) and operation is smooth and controllable, ideal for thick plate cutting.

**3. Pneumatic Shear**
Uses compressed air to actuate a small cylinder. Used for thin sheets and high-speed repetitive cuts in production lines.

**4. Alligator Shear**
A pivoting jaw cuts scrap metal; commonly used in scrap yards for cutting bars, sections, and small plates.

**Safety Devices (Common to All):**

- **Fixed perimeter guard** with interlocked access doors.
- **Light curtain (Type 4 / PLe)** for the working zone.
- **Two-hand control** requiring simultaneous button press.
- **Foot pedal with shroud** to prevent accidental activation.
- **Emergency stop (Category 0 or 1)** at every operator station.
- **Hold-down fingers** to prevent workpiece uplift during cutting.
- **Pressure relief valve** set at **110% of working pressure**.
- **Anti-restart device** after power recovery.

#### Model Solution for Q (b)

**Monthly Inspection Procedure for Hydraulic Press Brake:**

**Step 1 — Hydraulic System Analysis**
- Draw oil sample from the reservoir via the sampling valve; submit to lab for **ISO 4406 particle count** (target cleanliness **18/16/13** or better) and **Karl Fischer water content** (below **200 ppm**).
- Check filter differential pressure; replace element if ΔP > **2.5 bar**.
- Verify pressure relief valve setting at **110% of nominal**; bench test if drift exceeds ±5%.

**Step 2 — Mechanical Inspection**
- Measure ram-to-bed parallelism using a dial gauge; tolerance **0.05 mm per 1000 mm**.
- Inspect die cushion (if equipped) for air bleeding; verify pressure stability.
- Check slide guide clearance (typical **0.02–0.05 mm**).

**Step 3 — Electrical Inspection**
- Megger test of all power cables at **500 V DC**; insulation resistance > **1 MΩ**.
- Verify earth continuity at all exposed metal parts (< **0.5 Ω**).
- Test safety PLC inputs (light curtain, e-stops) using a **test rod** and verify correct shut-down response.

**Step 4 — Back Gauge Calibration**
- Position a certified **1000 mm gauge block** and verify digital readout accuracy to ±**0.05 mm** for CNC machines.

**Applicable Indian Standards:**
- **IS 11952:1987** — Code of practice for safety of mechanical presses.
- **IS 11200:1985** — Safety requirements for hydraulic presses.
- **IS 15560:2005** — Safety of machinery — General principles for design.
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.217** — Mechanical power presses (reference for international fabricators exporting to the USA).
- **Factories Act 1948, Section 21 & 22** — Fencing of machinery and dangerous parts.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse *press brake* with *press* in Indian standards. A press brake is a bending machine — covered by **IS 11200** for hydraulics or **IS 11952** for mechanical types. Do not cite only the general press standard; always cross-reference the forming-specific clauses. Marks are lost for missing the **IS number** in the answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the **common causes of accidents** on metal shears and the **preventive measures** to be implemented.
>
> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** With a labelled diagram, explain the **construction and working of a hydraulic press brake**. List the **maintenance checks** for the hydraulic system.

#### Model Solution for Q (a)

**Common Causes of Accidents on Metal Shears:**

1. **Operator's hand entering the shear zone** — caused by absence of guards, defective light curtains, or operator complacency during inch mode. Prevention: **two-hand control + light curtain + fixed guard**.
2. **Material kickback or upset** — thin sheets may spring back toward the operator after cutting. Prevention: **hold-down fingers** set at correct pressure and **back gauge stop**.
3. **Blade fragment ejection** — overcut blades may fracture. Prevention: **scheduled blade rotation**, **clearance checks**, **replacement at end-of-life**.
4. **Hydraulic hose burst** — worn hoses under high pressure can whip violently. Prevention: **hose replacement at 5-year intervals** or per OEM schedule, **hose burst valves** on cylinders.
5. **Unexpected ram descent** — caused by failure of directional valve, mechanical failure of brake/clutch, or accumulator discharge. Prevention: **double-solenoid valves**, **dual-block bleed-off valves**, **brake monitoring**.
6. **Trapping by hold-down foot** — operator's hand caught between hold-down and material. Prevention: **anti-repeat device**, **foot guard**, **adequate lighting**.

**Preventive Measures (PPE and Administrative):**

- **Tooling:** Use of **push sticks** for small pieces; **jigs and fixtures** for repetitive cuts.
- **PPE:** Cut-resistant gloves (Level C or D per EN 388), safety glasses, steel-toe boots, hearing protection.
- **Training:** Certification of operators; documented **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)** procedure.
- **Permit-to-work** during maintenance.
- **Daily toolbox talks** addressing specific hazards.

#### Model Solution for Q (b)

**Construction of a Hydraulic Press Brake:**

- **Frame:** Heavy fabricated steel (welded or cast) — withstands bending reaction forces; typical stiffness **>5000 kN/mm**.
- **Bed (Lower Table):** Holds the **lower die (V-die)**; may include crowning system for compensation.
- **Ram (Upper Table):** Holds the **upper punch**; slides vertically on precision linear guides.
- **Hydraulic Cylinders:** Two large cylinders (one per side) provide synchronised force; each rated up to **500 tonnes** for medium machines.
- **Synchronisation System:** Mechanical linkage (torsion bar) or electronic proportional valve control to keep ram level.
- **Back Gauge:** CNC-controlled fingers (X-axis and R-axis) that position the sheet.
- **CNC Controller:** Stores bend programs, calculates tonnage and bend sequence.

**Working:**
The operator enters bend dimensions into the CNC; the back gauge positions the sheet; the operator presses foot pedal; hydraulic valves open and the cylinders drive the ram down; the punch presses the sheet into the V-die; the bend is formed; the ram retracts; the part is removed and repositioned for the next bend.

**Hydraulic System Maintenance Checks:**

| Check | Frequency | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Oil level | Daily | Between min and max marks |
| Oil cleanliness | Monthly | ISO 4406 **18/16/13** max |
| Oil temperature | Continuous | Below **65°C** |
| Filter ΔP | Weekly | Below **2.5 bar** |
| Pressure relief valve | Annually | Set within ±5% of nominal |
| Cylinder drift | Monthly | < **0.5 mm in 10 min** |
| Hoses (visual) | Weekly | No abrasion, blistering |
| Accumulator pre-charge | Annually | Within OEM spec |
| Hydraulic seals | Annually | No leakage |
| Cooler fan operation | Daily | Airflow confirmed |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- **Shear force formula:** $F_s = S_{us} \cdot L \cdot t$ — memorise the derivation steps.
- **Press brake tonnage:** $T_{req} = \dfrac{1.33 \cdot L \cdot t^2 \cdot S_u}{V \cdot 1000}$ — use **V = 8t** for air bending.
- **Blade clearance:** **5–10% of sheet thickness** is the golden rule.
- **Spring-back:** Always over-bend; magnitude increases with higher yield strength and larger R/t ratio.
- **Four inspection tiers:** Pre-shift (operator) → Weekly (supervisor) → Monthly (engineer) → Annual (statutory).
- **Safety distance:** $D_s = K \cdot T_s + C$, where K = **1.6 m/s** per OSHA.
- **Hydraulic cleanliness:** Target **ISO 4406 18/16/13** for new installations; **20/18/15** for operating systems.
- **Indian Standards to cite:**
  - **IS 11952** — Mechanical presses.
  - **IS 11200** — Hydraulic presses.
  - **Factories Act 1948, Section 21 & 22** — Mandatory fencing.
- **OSH Code 2020** supersedes many provisions of the Factories Act for newer establishments.
- **Two-hand control** must be **synchronous within 0.5 s** (anti-tie-down requirement).
- **E-stop category:** Cat 0 (direct disconnection) for electrical; Cat 1 (controlled stop) for hydraulic.
- **PPE for shears/press brakes:** Cut-resistant gloves, safety glasses, hearing protection, steel-toe boots.
- **Common accident types:** Amputation, crush, entanglement, hydraulic injection injury, eye injury from ejected swarf.
- **Maintenance types (in order of preference):** Predictive > Preventive > Reactive.
- **Blade inspection:** Daily visual; monthly edge profile; rotate or replace at end of life.
- **Back gauge accuracy:** ±**0.05 mm** for CNC; ±**0.2 mm** for manual.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Inspection and Maintenance of Metal Shears and Press Brakes**
> **Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Protocol | 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hot Working Safety in Forging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hot Working Safety in Forging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hot Working — Formal KTU Definition

**Hot Working** is a metal forming process in which plastic deformation of the workpiece is carried out at a temperature **above the recrystallization temperature** of the metal, such that recovery and recrystallization occur simultaneously with deformation, preventing strain hardening. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (per the OESFT725 syllabus — Manufacturing Safety perspective), hot working is classified as a high-energy, high-hazard primary shaping operation.

The empirical rule governing the working temperature window is:

$$T_{hot} \geq 0.6 \, T_{melt} \quad \text{(in Kelvin)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Any forming operation performed above the recrystallization temperature (≈ **0.4 Tₘ in K**, often practically taken as 0.5–0.6 Tₘ for safe hot working) is treated as **hot working**. Below this, but above room temperature, it is termed **warm working**.

## 1.2 Forging — The Core Manufacturing Process

**Forging** is a hot working (and sometimes cold/warm working) manufacturing process in which compressive forces are applied to a workpiece using hammers, presses, or specialized dies to plastically deform the metal into the desired shape with improved mechanical properties (grain refinement, directional flow lines, superior strength, and toughness).

> [!NOTE]
> **Forging ≠ Casting.** In casting, metal is poured molten into a mould. In forging, the metal stays largely in the *solid state* (though soft/ductile), and shape is achieved by **compressive plastic flow**, not by melting and re-solidifying.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Clay Baker vs. The Melting Pot"

Imagine two ways to shape a lump of dough into a bread loaf:

| Method | Real-World Equivalent | Process Nature |
|---|---|---|
| Pressing/rolling warm dough with your hands | **Forging** | Solid but soft; force applied to reshape |
| Pouring liquid batter into a mould | **Casting** | Liquid→Solid; flow into cavity |

Now think of safety:

> **Analogy**: A forging shop is like a giant industrial bakery where the "dough" (red-hot steel at **900–1250 °C**) is hammered into shape. The hazards are analogous to a baker dealing with a **scalding oven, flying crumbs, grease fires, and a powerful kneading machine** — except the scale is **100× more dangerous**, the temperatures are **5× higher**, and the forces involved can crush a human body.

## 1.4 Why "Safety" is Paramount in Hot Forging

A medium-sized open-die forging hammer delivers a **blow energy of 50 kJ to 1000 kJ** in milliseconds. The workpiece radiates intense heat. Die fragments can eject at velocities exceeding **50 m/s**. Hence, **forging is statistically among the top 5 most hazardous manufacturing processes** (per OSHA / NIOSH industrial safety data).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics for KTU**:
> - **Drop Hammer Energy Range**: 5 kJ (small) → 1000 kJ (large steam/air hammers)
> - **Hot Forging Temperature (Carbon Steel)**: 900 °C – 1250 °C
> - **Hot Forging Temperature (Aluminum Alloys)**: 350 °C – 500 °C
> - **Hot Forging Temperature (Copper Alloys)**: 600 °C – 900 °C
> - **Hot Forging Temperature (Titanium Alloys)**: 700 °C – 950 °C
> - **Die Surface Temperature (during operation)**: 200 °C – 500 °C

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Forging Operations (KTU Module 4 Focus)

### A. Based on Temperature
1. **Cold Forging** — At room temperature; high forces, excellent surface finish.
2. **Warm Forging** — Between room temp and recrystallization (200 °C – 800 °C depending on metal).
3. **Hot Forging** — Above recrystallization temperature.

### B. Based on Equipment Type
1. **Hammer Forging** — Uses impact energy from a falling ram (drop hammer, steam hammer, air hammer, counterblow hammer).
2. **Press Forging** — Uses slow, sustained compressive force (hydraulic press, mechanical press).
3. **Upset Forging** — Axial compression to increase cross-section (used for bolt heads, valve ends).

### C. Based on Die Geometry
1. **Open-Die Forging** — Flat or simple-shaped dies; workpiece deforms freely; used for large shafts, rings, discs.
2. **Closed-Die (Impression-Die) Forging** — Complex die cavities; workpiece fills the cavity; used for gears, connecting rods, crankshafts.
3. **Flashless Forging** — Precision forging with no excess material escape.

## 2.2 Key Engineering Principles (Why Hot Forging Works)

### Step 1 — Temperature Elevation Reduces Flow Stress
As temperature rises, the **flow stress (Y_f)** of the metal drops dramatically. This means **less force is required** for the same deformation.

$$\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^m \cdot e^{Q/(RT)}$$

Where:
- **K** = strength coefficient (MPa)
- **ε** = true strain
- **n** = strain hardening exponent
- **ė** = strain rate (s⁻¹)
- **m** = strain rate sensitivity
- **Q** = activation energy (J/mol)
- **R** = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **T** = absolute temperature (K)

### Step 2 — Dynamic Recrystallization Prevents Strain Hardening
At hot working temperatures, new strain-free grains nucleate continuously, replacing deformed grains. This means:
- **Ductility remains high**
- **Crack propagation is suppressed**
- **Repeated deformation is possible without fracture**

### Step 3 — Grain Refinement Improves Mechanical Properties
Final cooling produces a **finer, more uniform grain structure** → increased toughness, fatigue strength, and impact resistance.

## 2.3 Hot Working Safety Hazards — The "Big Five"

| # | Hazard Category | Specific Risk Examples |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thermal Hazards** | Burns (radiant heat, contact with hot dies/workpiece, sparks, molten splash), heat stress, dehydration |
| 2 | **Mechanical Hazards** | Crushing by hammer ram, entanglement in moving parts, die fragments, flying scale (oxide), ejected workpieces |
| 3 | **Chemical Hazards** | Metallurgical fumes (Zn, Pb, Cd from brass/bronze), CO/CO₂ from furnace combustion, lubricants decomposing |
| 4 | **Fire/Explosion Hazards** | Oil/coolant flash fires, hydrogen explosions from molten aluminum + water, magnesium ignition |
| 5 | **Ergonomic & Environmental** | Noise (>100 dB), vibration (hand-arm vibration syndrome), poor lighting, oil-slick floors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Safety Hierarchy Applied to Hot Forging**:
> 1. **Elimination** → Use cold forging where feasible.
> 2. **Substitution** → Use robotic forging cells; replace hammers with hydraulic presses (quieter, less ejection risk).
> 3. **Engineering Controls** → Machine guarding, die cushioning, interlocked safety doors, ventilation hoods, water curtains.
> 4. **Administrative Controls** → Operator training, lockout/tagout, two-person rule, hot work permits, scheduled breaks.
> 5. **PPE** → Aluminized heat suits, face shields (IR-rated), leather spats, steel-toe boots, earplugs/earmuffs.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Hot Working & Forging (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE**: All mathematical absolute-value or constraint symbols use `\vert` or `\mid` (NEVER the raw pipe `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hot Working Threshold | $T_{hot} \geq 0.6 \, T_{melt} \,(K)$ | T in Kelvin | Define hot vs. cold working |
| 2 | Recrystallization Temperature | $T_{recryst} \approx 0.4 \, T_{melt}\,(K)$ | T in Kelvin | Lower bound of hot working |
| 3 | Open-Die Forging Force | $F = Y_f \cdot A_p$ | F (N), Y_f (Pa), A_p = projected area (m²) | Flat die upsetting |
| 4 | Closed-Die Forging Force | $F = C \cdot Y_f \cdot A_p$ | C = constraint factor (1.5 to 3.0) | Impression die forging |
| 5 | Forging Pressure (Friction) | $p = Y_f \cdot \left(1 + \frac{0.5 \, \mu \, w}{h}\right)$ | μ = friction coeff., w = width, h = height | Flat-die friction hill |
| 6 | Upset Forging Pressure | $p = Y_f \cdot e^{\varepsilon}$ | ε = true strain | Compression between dies |
| 7 | Hammer Kinetic Energy | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | m (kg), v (m/s), E (J) | Drop hammer energy transfer |
| 8 | Hammer Work Equivalent | $W = m \, g \, h_{drop}$ | h_drop (m), g = 9.81 m/s² | Steam hammer potential energy |
| 9 | Press Work Done | $W = F \cdot s$ | s = stroke length (m) | Hydraulic press forging |
| 10 | True Strain in Forging | $\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\tfrac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | h_0, h_f = initial/final heights | Compression measurement |
| 11 | Strain Rate (Hammer) | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{v}{h}$ | v = ram velocity (m/s), h = workpiece height | High strain rate (10²–10³ s⁻¹) |
| 12 | Strain Rate (Press) | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{v_{ram}}{h}$ | v_ram = press ram velocity | Low strain rate (0.1–10 s⁻¹) |
| 13 | Hot Die Temperature (steady) | $T_{die,surf} \approx T_{work} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{die} \, \rho_{die} \, c_{die}}{k_{w} \, \rho_{w} \, c_{w}}}$ | k, ρ, c = conductivity, density, specific heat | Thermal safety limits |
| 14 | Radiant Heat Flux (Stefan-Boltzmann) | $q = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_h^4 - T_c^4)$ | σ = 5.67×10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴), ε = emissivity | PPE thermal rating |
| 15 | Noise Exposure Limit | $TWA = 90 \, dB(A)$ over 8 hrs | OSHA / KTU standard | Forging noise hazard |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above are not academic — they directly inform:

- **Automotive Industry**: Hot forging produces crankshafts, connecting rods, and gears that must withstand **millions of fatigue cycles** in service.
- **Aerospace**: Turbine disks and landing gear forgings are certified under **AS9100** with strict traceability because failure is catastrophic.
- **Defence**: Tank tracks, artillery shells, and armor plates are hot-forged for ballistic strength.
- **Power Generation**: Large rotor shafts for turbines (up to **500 tons**) require massive open-die forging presses — and equally massive **safety protocols**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forging Force with Friction (Friction Hill Effect)

**Physical Setup**: A cylindrical workpiece of initial height $h_0$ and diameter $d_0$ is compressed between two flat, parallel dies. Friction at the die-workpiece interface resists outward metal flow, requiring **higher pressure in the center** than at the edges — a phenomenon known as the **friction hill**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Define a thin elemental disk at radius $r$, thickness $dz$, at height $z$ from the bottom die. The disk experiences radial stress $\sigma_r$ that varies with $r$.

**Step 2** — Equilibrium of the element in the radial direction yields:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0$$

**Step 3** — For an idealized **plane-strain compression** with **St. Venant flow**, assume $\sigma_\theta = \sigma_r$ (isotropic radial stress) and that vertical stress $\sigma_z = -p$ is the forging pressure at radius $r$.

**Step 4** — The friction shear stress at the die interface opposes outward flow:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot p$$

where $\mu$ is the Coulomb friction coefficient at the die-workpiece interface (typically **0.1–0.4** for hot forging with lubricant).

**Step 5** — Force balance on a vertical column of metal from radius $0$ to $r$, accounting for the friction drag along the die face:

$$\pi r^2 \cdot p = \pi r^2 \cdot Y_f + 2\pi r \cdot \mu \cdot p \cdot h$$

Here, the first term is the **yield load** on the column cross-section, and the second term is the **friction drag** along the cylindrical wall of the column inside the die.

**Step 6** — Solve for $p(r)$:

$$\pi r^2 \, p - 2\pi r \, \mu \, p \, h = \pi r^2 \, Y_f$$

$$p \left( \pi r^2 - 2\pi r \, \mu \, h \right) = \pi r^2 \, Y_f$$

$$p(r) = \frac{Y_f \cdot \pi r^2}{\pi r^2 - 2\pi r \, \mu \, h} = \frac{Y_f}{1 - \dfrac{2\mu h}{r}}$$

**Step 7** — For the **maximum pressure at the center** ($r \to 0$, but bounded by workpiece radius $R$), we get the average forging pressure:

$$p_{avg} = Y_f \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{0.5 \, \mu \, w}{h} \right)$$

where $w$ is the workpiece width (or diameter) and $h$ is the current height.

**Step 8** — Total forging force is then:

$$F = p_{avg} \cdot A_p$$

where $A_p$ is the **projected area** of the workpiece on the die face.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Implication**: The friction hill shows why **die lubrication** is critical — it reduces $\mu$, hence reduces peak pressure, reduces die wear, and prevents **die failure** that can eject fragments.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hammer Blow Energy and Equivalent Forging Work

**Physical Setup**: A drop hammer of mass $M$ falls through a height $H$ onto a workpiece of mass $m$, deforming it by stroke $s$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Potential energy at height $H$:

$$PE = M \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 2** — Velocity just before impact (energy conservation):

$$M \cdot g \cdot H = \tfrac{1}{2} M v^2$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 g H}$$

**Step 3** — Kinetic energy at impact (the energy delivered to the workpiece):

$$KE = \tfrac{1}{2} M v^2 = M \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 4** — Equivalent forging work for a deformation requiring average force $F_{avg}$ over stroke $s$:

$$W_{forging} = F_{avg} \cdot s$$

**Step 5** — Energy balance (assuming 60–80% transfer efficiency $\eta$ to the workpiece, the rest lost to vibration, sound, die deformation):

$$\eta \cdot M \cdot g \cdot H = F_{avg} \cdot s$$

**Step 6** — Solve for the **required hammer drop height** for a given forging force and stroke:

$$H = \frac{F_{avg} \cdot s}{\eta \cdot M \cdot g}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU Style)**: A 500 kg drop hammer must forge a steel billet requiring a peak force of **2.5 MN** over a stroke of **50 mm**. Energy transfer efficiency is **70%**. Find the required drop height. Take $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.

**Solution**:

$$W_{forging} = F \cdot s = 2.5 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.050 = 1.25 \times 10^5 \, J = 125 \, kJ$$

$$H = \frac{W_{forging}}{\eta \cdot M \cdot g} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^5}{0.70 \cdot 500 \cdot 9.81}$$

$$H = \frac{1.25 \times 10^5}{3433.5} \approx 36.4 \, m$$

> **Safety Note**: This hammer needs to be **dropped from 36.4 meters** to deliver 125 kJ. Imagine the catastrophic consequences if a worker is in the die area. This is why **interlocks and light curtains** are non-negotiable.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recrystallization Temperature from Melting Point

**Empirical Rule**: For most metals, the recrystallization temperature (lower bound of hot working) is approximately:

$$T_{recryst} \approx 0.4 \, T_{melt} \, (K)$$

For practical hot working, safety engineers use a slightly higher threshold to ensure **complete recrystallization**:

$$T_{hot-work} \approx 0.6 \, T_{melt} \, (K)$$

### Numerical Example for Pure Iron

- $T_{melt,Fe} = 1538 \, °C = 1811 \, K$
- $T_{recryst,Fe} = 0.4 \times 1811 = 724.4 \, K = 451 \, °C$
- $T_{hot-work,Fe} = 0.6 \times 1811 = 1086.6 \, K = 813.6 \, °C$

Hence, **practical hot forging of steel begins around 900 °C**, in line with industry practice.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Safety Risk vs. Forging Type

| Forging Type | Peak Force | Strain Rate | Primary Hazards | Critical PPE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open-Die Hammer | 5–50 MN | 10²–10³ s⁻¹ | Die fragments, ejected scale, ram crush | Full face shield, aluminized coat, spats |
| Closed-Die Hammer | 10–100 MN | 10²–10³ s⁻¹ | Flash ejection, die fracture, noise | Ear muffs + plugs (dual), face shield |
| Hydraulic Press | 50–500 MN | 0.1–10 s⁻¹ | Hydraulic rupture, die crush, oil fire | Chemical-resistant apron, face shield |
| Mechanical Press | 5–50 MN | 10–50 s⁻¹ | Repeat strokes (entrapment), flywheel failure | Two-hand trip control, light curtain |
| Upset Forging | 2–20 MN | 10–100 s⁻¹ | Bar buckling, kickback, pinch points | Safety boots, gloves, arm guards |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define hot working. State the empirical rule used to identify the hot working temperature range.**

**Model Answer**:
Hot working is a metal forming process in which plastic deformation of the workpiece is carried out at a temperature **above the recrystallization temperature** of the metal, allowing dynamic recovery and recrystallization to occur simultaneously with deformation, thereby preventing strain hardening.

The empirical rule for the hot working temperature range is:

$$T_{hot} \geq 0.6 \, T_{melt} \quad \text{(in Kelvin)}$$

while the recrystallization temperature (lower bound) is approximately $T_{recryst} \approx 0.4 \, T_{melt} \, (K)$. For example, for steel with $T_{melt} \approx 1811 \, K$, hot forging begins around **1086 K (~813 °C)** and is typically conducted in the **900 °C – 1250 °C** range.

*(3 marks — complete: 2 marks for definition, 1 mark for rule + example)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any FOUR major safety hazards in a hot forging shop and the corresponding PPE for each.**

**Model Answer**:

| # | Hazard | Recommended PPE |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Burns from hot workpieces and dies (radiant heat, contact) | **Aluminized heat-reflective suit**, leather gloves, **spats** |
| 2 | Flying scale, sparks, die fragments | **IR-rated face shield**, safety glasses with side shields |
| 3 | Noise from hammer impacts (>100 dB) | **Dual ear protection** (earplugs + earmuffs) |
| 4 | Foot injury from falling hot billets | **Steel-toe leather safety boots** with heat-resistant soles |

*(3 marks — 0.5 marks per hazard, 0.25 marks per corresponding PPE; 4 hazards × 0.75 = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of forging processes** based on (i) temperature, (ii) equipment, and (iii) die geometry. For each category, mention at least two examples and indicate the typical industrial application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical steel billet of initial diameter **100 mm** and height **150 mm** is hot-forged in an open-die press. The flow stress at forging temperature is **80 MPa**, and the friction coefficient between the dies and the billet is **0.25**. After forging, the height reduces to **75 mm**. Calculate the **(i)** true strain, **(ii)** average forging pressure (using the friction-hill formula), and **(iii)** the **total forging force**. Assume the billet remains cylindrical throughout. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Classification of Forging Processes

**(i) Based on Temperature (3 marks)**:
