```mermaid
    style A2 fill:#52b788,color:#000000
    style A3 fill:#f4a261,color:#000000
    style A4 fill:#e76f51,color:#ffffff
    style A5 fill:#9d0208,color:#ffffff
    style A6 fill:#264653,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree: Five Families of Hazards

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[WORKPLACE HAZARDS] --> B1[PHYSICAL HAZARDS]
    B0 --> B2[CHEMICAL HAZARDS]
    B0 --> B3[BIOLOGICAL HAZARDS]
    B0 --> B4[ERGONOMIC HAZARDS]
    B0 --> B5[PSYCHOSOCIAL HAZARDS]
    B1 --> B1A[Mechanical\nRotating parts\nFalling objects]
    B1 --> B1B[Electrical\nShock, arc flash]
    B1 --> B1C[Thermal\nHot, cryogenic]
    B1 --> B1D[Noise Vibration]
    B1 --> B1E[Radiation\nIonizing non ionizing]
    B2 --> B2A[Toxic]
    B2 --> B2B[Flammable]
    B2 --> B2C[Corrosive]
    B2 --> B2D[Reactive]
    B3 --> B3A[Bacteria Virus]
    B3 --> B3B[Fungi Mold]
    B3 --> B3C[Blood borne pathogens]
    B4 --> B4A[Repetitive motion]
    B4 --> B4B[Poor posture]
    B4 --> B4C[Manual lifting]
    B5 --> B5A[Workplace stress]
    B5 --> B5B[Harassment]
    B5 --> B5C[Fatigue burnout]
    style B0 fill:#003049,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#d62828,color:#ffffff
    style B2 fill:#f77f00,color:#000000
    style B3 fill:#fcbf49,color:#000000
    style B4 fill:#06a77d,color:#ffffff
    style B5 fill:#9d4edd,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Risk Assessment & Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[STEP 1: HAZARD IDENTIFICATION\nWalk-through, JSA, HAZOP] --> C2[STEP 2: RISK ESTIMATION\nLikelihood x Severity]
    C2 --> C3[STEP 3: RISK EVALUATION\nCompare against criteria]
    C3 --> C4{Risk Acceptable?}
    C4 -->|Yes| C5[MONITOR and REVIEW]
    C4 -->|No| C6[STEP 4: APPLY CONTROLS\nHierarchy of Controls]
    C6 --> C7[STEP 5: REASSESS RESIDUAL RISK]
    C7 --> C3
    C5 --> C8[RECORD and DOCUMENT]
    C8 -.-> C1
    style C1 fill:#001233,color:#ffffff
    style C2 fill:#001233,color:#ffffff
    style C3 fill:#001233,color:#ffffff
    style C4 fill:#ca6702,color:#ffffff
    style C5 fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff
    style C6 fill:#9d0208,color:#ffffff
    style C7 fill:#001233,color:#ffffff
    style C8 fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Concept Map: How Controls Reduce Risk

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[INITIAL HAZARD\nH present] --> D2[EXPOSURE PATHWAY\nWorker contact]
    D2 --> D3[HARM EVENT\nInjury, illness]
    D3 --> D4[CONSEQUENCES\nLoss, damage]
    E1[ELIMINATION] -.->|Remove H| D1
    E2[SUBSTITUTION] -.->|Replace H| D1
    E3[ENGINEERING] -.->|Block pathway| D2
    E4[ADMINISTRATIVE] -.->|Change behavior| D2
    E5[PPE] -.->|Shield worker| D3
    style D1 fill:#6a040f,color:#ffffff
    style D2 fill:#9d0208,color:#ffffff
    style D3 fill:#dc2f02,color:#ffffff
    style D4 fill:#e85d04,color:#ffffff
    style E1 fill:#1b4332,color:#ffffff
    style E2 fill:#2d6a4f,color:#ffffff
    style E3 fill:#52b788,color:#000000
    style E4 fill:#f4a261,color:#000000
    style E5 fill:#9d4edd,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: The top horizontal arrows (red) trace the **pathway from hazard to harm**. The bottom arrows (green/purple) show **where each control tier intercepts** the pathway. Notice that higher-tier controls (Elimination, Substitution) break the chain at its **root**, while lower-tier controls (PPE) only protect the worker *after* the harm event has already begun — this is why the hierarchy exists.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define a hazard. Differentiate between hazard and risk with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **hazard** is any source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm in terms of human injury, ill-health, or property damage *(1 mark)*. A **risk** is the combination of the *likelihood* of an incident occurring and the *severity* of the resulting harm *(1 mark)*.

- **Example of Hazard**: An unguarded rotating shaft in a workshop.
- **Example of Risk**: A worker with loose clothing passing near the shaft has a *Medium-to-High* probability of entanglement, which could be *severe (fatal)* — hence risk is *High* *(1 mark)*.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the five tiers of the Hierarchy of Controls in correct order from most to least effective.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Elimination** (most effective — remove hazard completely) *(1 mark)*
2. **Substitution** (replace with less hazardous alternative) *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Engineering Controls** (isolate people from hazard via design — guards, ventilation) *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Administrative Controls** (change work procedures — SOPs, training, rotation) *(0.5 mark)*
5. **PPE — Personal Protective Equipment** (least effective — protect the individual worker) *(0.5 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: A common mistake is to list PPE first or in random order. KTU examiners specifically test whether students know the **descending effectiveness order**. Wrong order = 1 mark deduction.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Provided** — Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### **Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] (14 Marks)**

**(a) Classify the major types of workplace hazards with at least two examples for each category. Explain with a neat diagram how the Hierarchy of Controls is applied in engineering industries. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Hazards (5 marks):**

| # | Category | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Physical** | Rotating unguarded shaft | High-noise punch press (>85 dBA) |
| 2 | **Chemical** | Benzene (toxic, flammable) | Sulphuric acid (corrosive) |
| 3 | **Biological** | Legionella bacteria in cooling towers | Hepatitis B (blood-borne) |
| 4 | **Ergonomic** | Repetitive wrist motion → Carpal Tunnel | Manual lifting of >25 kg |
| 5 | **Psychosocial** | Long working hours → burnout | Workplace harassment |

**Hierarchy of Controls Diagram (2 marks):**

A 5-tier inverted pyramid is drawn (similar to Section 4.1):
- Top (most effective) → **Elimination** (e.g., remove the toxic process)
- **Substitution** (e.g., water-based paint)
- **Engineering** (e.g., local exhaust ventilation, machine guards)
- **Administrative** (e.g., training, job rotation, SOPs)
- Bottom (least effective) → **PPE** (e.g., respirators, helmets)

The diagram must be labelled with the **descending effectiveness** order.

---

**(b) A grinding machine operator faces the following hazard profile: Likelihood = 4, Severity = 3, Exposure = 5 (using Fine-Kinney scale). Calculate the risk score. If an engineering control reduces risk by 80%, what is the residual risk? Comment on the acceptability and suggest a course of action. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Initial Risk Calculation (3 marks):**
$$R_{\text{initial}} = L \times S \times E = 4 \times 3 \times 5 = 60$$

**Step 2 — Apply Control Effectiveness (2 marks):**
Control effectiveness $E_c = 0.80$ (80% reduction).
$$R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{initial}} \times (1 - E_c) = 60 \times 0.20 = 12$$

**Step 3 — Acceptability & Action (2 marks):**
- Initial score 60 = **Very High** (intolerable, immediate stoppage required before any control).
- Residual score 12 = **High Risk** — operation can be allowed with strict controls:
  - Add a **machine guard** (engineering), **safe operating procedure** (administrative), and **safety goggles + gloves** (PPE).
- Apply **ALARP** principle: continue to refine controls to bring residual risk below 5 (Low/Acceptable).

---

### **Question B [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the procedure of conducting a Risk Assessment using a Risk Assessment Matrix (RAM). Use a suitable example to demonstrate how $R = L \times S$ is calculated and classified. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure of Risk Assessment (3 marks):**
1. **Identify hazards** — conduct a walk-through survey, consult workers, review past incident records.
2. **Determine who may be harmed and how**.
3. **Evaluate the risks** — for each hazard, assign Likelihood (L) and Severity (S) scores.
4. **Decide on control measures** using the Hierarchy of Controls.
5. **Record the findings** in a Risk Register.
6. **Review and update** the assessment periodically or after any incident.

**Risk Assessment Matrix (2 marks):**
A 5×5 grid (L vs S) is constructed mapping scores to risk levels (Low / Medium / High / Very High). (Refer to Section 2.3 of these notes for the standard matrix.)

**Worked Example (2 marks):**
*Task*: Maintenance work on a live 11 kV panel.
- L = 2 (Unlikely with proper permit)
- S = 5 (Catastrophic — electrocution fatality)
$$R = L \times S = 2 \times 5 = 10$$
Score 10 = **High Risk** — operation must be deferred or done only with full Lock-Out–Tag-Out (LOTO), insulated tools, Class-2 gloves, and a standby supervisor.

---

**(b) Discuss in detail the different categories of control measures with at least three examples for each. Why is PPE considered the least preferred control? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Control Categories (5 marks):**

1. **Engineering Controls** *(most reliable — built into design)*
   - Machine guarding, interlock systems
   - Local exhaust ventilation (LEV) for fumes
   - Sound enclosures for noisy machines
   - ELCBs/earth-fault relays for electrical safety

2. **Administrative Controls** *(depend on human behaviour)*
   - Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)
   - Permit-to-Work (PTW) systems
   - Job rotation to limit exposure duration
   - Safety training and toolbox talks
   - Warning signs and barricading

3. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** *(protects the worker, not the hazard)*
   - Hard hats, safety helmets
   - Safety goggles, face shields
   - Respirators, dust masks
   - Ear plugs/muffs
   - Safety shoes, gloves

**Why PPE is Least Preferred (2 marks):**
- PPE **does not eliminate the hazard** — it only shields the individual.
- Effectiveness depends on **correct fit, proper use, and consistent wearing** by the worker (human-factor failure risk).
- It can be **forgotten, damaged, or worn out** without immediate replacement.
- It protects only the **wearer**, not other co-workers.
- Higher-tier controls (elimination, engineering) **remove the hazard at source**, giving blanket protection to everyone.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**:
> - For 14-mark questions, students often **write short paragraphs without sub-headings** — examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark per missing key term** like *Hierarchy*, *ALARP*, *LOTO*, *LEV*, *JSA*.
> - **Diagrams are mandatory** for full marks. A hand-drawn Risk Matrix and Hierarchy Pyramid typically adds **+1.5 marks**.
> - **Numerical problems must show all steps**: $L$, $S$, $R$, classification, control — partial marking depends on showing intermediate steps.
> - Avoid one-line definitions for terms like "Hazard" and "Risk" — they must include both **definition + example** to secure full marks.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Hazard** = *potential* for harm; **Risk** = *probability × impact*; **Incident** = *actual* event.
- ✅ Five hazard families: **P-C-B-E-P** (Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial).
- ✅ Hierarchy of Controls in **strict order**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- ✅ **PPE is the LAST resort** — KTU loves testing this.
- ✅ Risk Score: $R = L \times S$ (basic) or $R = L \times S \times E$ (Fine-Kinney).
- ✅ Standard 5×5 matrix classifies risk: **1–4 Low, 5–9 Medium, 10–15 High, 16–25 Very High**.
- ✅ **Residual Risk**: $R_{\text{res}} = R_{\text{initial}} \times (1 - E_c)$.
- ✅ LTIFR uses multiplier $10^6$ (ILO); TRIR uses $200{,}000$ (US OSHA).
- ✅ **ALARP** principle: residual risk must be as low as reasonably practicable.
- ✅ **Risk Assessment** procedure: Identify → Evaluate → Control → Record → Review.
- ✅ Controls at the **engineering tier** (guards, ventilation, interlocks) are **most reliable** because they don't depend on worker behaviour.
- ✅ **PPE limitations**: depends on correct use, can fail, protects only wearer, no source control.

> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme)**: For 14-mark questions, always structure answers under **sub-headings**, include **at least one diagram** (Risk Matrix or HOC pyramid), and provide **numerical examples with full calculation steps**. This is what differentiates a 10-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety in Gas Furnace Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety in Gas Furnace Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gas Furnace Operation Safety** refers to the comprehensive set of engineering controls, administrative procedures, protective devices, and operational protocols implemented to prevent fire, explosion, asphyxiation, burns, and toxic exposure during the operation, maintenance, and emergency handling of industrial gas-fired furnaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A gas furnace is an enclosed combustion chamber in which a **hydrocarbon fuel gas** (typically Natural Gas $\text{CH}_4$, LPG $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$/$\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$, or producer gas) is mixed with air and burned to release thermal energy for industrial processes such as melting, heat treatment, forging, ceramic firing, and steam generation. *Safety in Gas Furnace Operation* is the discipline that ensures this energy release occurs in a **controlled, predictable, and contained** manner, preventing uncontrolled combustion events and harm to personnel, equipment, and the environment.

The discipline is governed by statutory codes such as:
- **NFPA 54** (National Fuel Gas Code)
- **NFPA 85** (Boiler and Combustion Systems Hazards Code)
- **IS 4246** (Indian Standard for Gas-fired Furnaces)
- **OISD-150** (Oil Industry Safety Directorate - Gas Distribution)
- **Factory Act 1948, Section 14 & 36** (India)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The "Burning Kitchen Pressure Cooker" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are cooking with a pressure cooker on a gas stove. The flame needs three things: **fuel (gas)**, **air (oxygen)**, and **heat (ignition source)**. Now, if too much gas leaks before lighting, the moment you strike a match, the whole kitchen explodes. A safe gas furnace behaves exactly like a stove with **three intelligent guards**:
> 1. A *Gas Guard* that only opens the valve when it senses a working flame.
> 2. An *Air Guard* that confirms sufficient oxygen is present before allowing combustion.
> 3. A *Watchdog* (flame scanner) that immediately slams the gas valve shut the instant the flame goes out.
> 
> **Safety in Gas Furnace Operation = These three guards working in perfect synchronization.**

## 1.3 Critical Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Metric | Symbol |
|-----------|----------------|--------|
| Auto-Ignition Temperature of Methane | **537 °C (1000 °F)** | $T_{ig}$ |
| Flammability Limits of Natural Gas (LEL/UEL) | **5% – 15% (volume in air)** | LEL/UEL |
| Stoichiometric A/F Ratio (CH₄) | **17.2 : 1 (mass basis)** | $(A/F)_{stoich}$ |
| Maximum Flame Speed (Methane-Air) | **0.4 m/s** | $S_L$ |
| Permissible CO in Workplace (OSHA/IS 5182) | **< 50 ppm (8-hr TWA)** | $\text{CO}_{ppm}$ |
| LEL Safety Threshold for Alarms | **25% of LEL = 1.25% CH₄** | Alarm Set Point |
| Furnace Skin Temp Limit (PPE Trigger) | **< 60 °C (140 °F)** | $T_{surface}$ |
| Minimum Purge Volume (NFPA 85) | **4 × furnace volume** | $V_{purge}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> Working inside the **LEL–UEL range** (5–15% gas-in-air) for natural gas means the atmosphere is **EXPLOSIVE**. Even a tiny spark from a static discharge (which can be as low as **0.2 mJ**) is enough to trigger a deflagration or detonation.

## 1.4 Principal Hazards in Gas Furnace Operation

1. **Gas Leakage & Explosion** (Unconfined Vapor Cloud Explosion — UVCE)
2. **Flashback & Sustained Combustion inside Piping**
3. **Carbon Monoxide (CO) Poisoning**
4. **Burn Injuries** (radiant + contact)
5. **Asphyxiation** (oxygen displacement in confined spaces)
6. **Boiler/Furnace Implosion** (vacuum on cold start)
7. **Noise-Induced Hearing Loss** (burner roar > 100 dBA)
8. **Hot Corrosion & Refractory Failure**
9. **Pressurized Gas Jet Injuries**
10. **Failure of Burner Management System (BMS)**

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operational Concept — The "Combustion Triangle + Fourth Element"

Safe gas furnace operation rests on controlling the **Fire Tetrahedron**:

1. **Fuel** (Gas at correct pressure and flow)
2. **Oxidizer** (Air at correct A/F ratio, properly mixed)
3. **Heat** (Ignition source — pilot or spark)
4. **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** (Free radicals: $\text{H}^\bullet$, $\text{OH}^\bullet$, $\text{O}^\bullet$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cardinal Rule of Gas Furnace Safety:** *Remove ANY ONE side of the Fire Tetrahedron and combustion cannot occur.* Every safety interlock, flame scanner, and purge cycle is engineered to **break one side** of this tetrahedron the moment an abnormal condition is detected.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Safety Logic

The safe ignition sequence (governed by **NFPA 85 / BMS logic**) proceeds in this strict order — violating any step is a **near-miss event**:

1. **Pre-Start Checks**
   - Verify gas pressure at train header (**typically 2–4 bar** for industrial burners)
   - Check air damper operation (mechanical linkage free)
   - Confirm flame scanner (UV or IR) is clean and functional
   - Validate that all **interlock limit switches** are made (door closed, water flow, draft fan ON)

2. **Pre-Purge Phase** (NFPA-mandated)
   - Damper opens, forced-draft fan runs
   - **Minimum 4 volume changes** of the furnace combustion chamber with fresh air
   - Purpose: **Sweep out any residual gas** that may have leaked during shutdown
   - Timer holds gas valves closed for the entire purge duration

3. **Pilot Ignition**
   - Pilot gas solenoid opens → spark igniter fires
   - Flame scanner confirms pilot flame within **3–10 seconds (Trial-for-Ignition Period)**
   - If no flame is proven, **Master Safety Relay (MSR) trips → Post-Purge → Lockout**

4. **Main Burner Light-Off**
   - Main gas valve opens in **staged steps** (low-fire → high-fire)
   - Flame monitoring continuous; **Failure to Fire (FTF)** logic in place

5. **Steady-State Operation with Continuous Monitoring**
   - Flame signal > threshold (UV: 4–8 μA; IR: 4–20 mA)
   - Furnace pressure within ±0.5 mbar
   - Stack temperature within design band
   - CO and O₂ in flue gas within limits (O₂ = 2–4%, CO < 100 ppm)

6. **Normal Shutdown**
   - Close main gas valve → close pilot valve → **post-purge** (4 volume changes) → fan off

7. **Emergency Shutdown (ESD) / Flame Failure**
   - **Hard-wired** safety circuit drops out
   - All gas valves close within **< 1 second** (safety shutoff valve reaction time)
   - Lockout requires **manual reset** — no auto-restart allowed

## 2.3 Burner Management System (BMS) — The Brain of Furnace Safety

> [!NOTE]
> The **Burner Management System (BMS)** is a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) classified under **IEC 61508 SIL 2 or SIL 3**. It is *the* core of furnace safety. In a KTU exam, you may be asked to "draw & explain BMS architecture" — always mention:
> - **Permissive Logic** (what MUST be true before the next step can start)
> - **Interlock Logic** (what MUST stop the process if abnormal)
> - **Hard-wired vs. Software-based** (hard-wired is preferred for safety-critical trips)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are repeated every year in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Stoichiometric combustion of methane | mol |
| 2 | $(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{2 \times 32 \times 4.76}{16} = 17.16$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass, CH₄) | kg/kg |
| 3 | $\text{Excess Air } \% = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual} - (A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} \times 100$ | Combustion air quality indicator | % |
| 4 | $\eta_{combustion} = \dfrac{Q_{input} - Q_{loss,flue}}{Q_{input}} \times 100$ | Combustion efficiency | % |
| 5 | $Q_{release} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \times \text{CV}$ | Heat release rate (CV = Calorific Value) | kW |
| 6 | $\dot{V}_{gas} = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{gas}}}$ | Orifice gas flow rate (Bernoulli) | m³/s |
| 7 | $S_L = S_{L,ref} \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_{ref}}{T}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{P_{ref}}\right)$ | Laminar flame speed correction | m/s |
| 8 | $PE_{gas} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^2}{2}$ | Kinetic energy of gas jet (impalement hazard) | Pa |
| 9 | $t_{purge} = \dfrac{4 \cdot V_{chamber}}{\dot{V}_{air,fan}}$ | Minimum purge time (NFPA 85) | s |
| 10 | $P_{furnace} = P_{atm} - \Delta P_{stack}$ | Furnace draft (negative pressure) | Pa |
| 11 | $T_{adiabatic,flame} = T_{ref} + \dfrac{\text{CV}}{m_{products} \cdot C_{p,products}}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature | K |
| 12 | $\text{LEL Margin} = \dfrac{C_{gas,actual}}{\text{LEL}_{gas}} \times 100\%$ | Atmosphere safety check (must be < 25% LEL) | % |

> **Important Notation Reminder:** In all formula derivations below, vertical bars for absolute value are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table corruption.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gas furnace safety principles are applied in:
- **Steel Reheating Furnaces** (rolling mills)
- **Cement Kilns** (preheater & calciner)
- **Glass Furnaces** (regenerative + recuperative)
- **Aluminum Melting & Holding Furnaces**
- **Heat Treatment Furnaces** (carburizing, annealing)
- **Steam Boilers** (package & industrial)
- **Petrochemical Process Heaters** (ethylene cracking furnaces)
- **Domestic HVAC** (smaller scale)

> [!NOTE]
> The same BMS, purge logic, and flame-failure-trip philosophy used in a **$100\text{ Crore}$ ethylene cracking furnace** is the same one used in your home LPG water heater — just with a much higher Safety Integrity Level (SIL).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Methane

The stoichiometric air-fuel ratio is the **theoretical minimum air required** to burn the fuel completely, with zero oxygen left over. Any less air = incomplete combustion (CO, soot); any more air = heat wasted in flue gas.

### Step 1: Write the balanced chemical equation
For complete combustion of methane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 &\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\text{(1 mol fuel)} &\quad \text{(2 mol O}_2\text{)} \quad \text{(1 mol CO}_2\text{)} \quad \text{(2 mol H}_2\text{O)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Convert to mass basis
Molecular weights:
- $\text{CH}_4$ = $1(12) + 4(1) = 16 \text{ kg/kmol}$
- $\text{O}_2$ = $2(16) = 32 \text{ kg/kmol}$
- $\text{N}_2$ (in air) = $2(14) = 28 \text{ kg/kmol}$

### Step 3: Calculate mass of oxygen required per kg of fuel
For **1 kg of CH₄**, moles of CH₄ = $\dfrac{1}{16} \text{ kmol}$
Moles of O₂ required = $2 \times \dfrac{1}{16} = \dfrac{1}{8} \text{ kmol}$
Mass of O₂ = $\dfrac{1}{8} \times 32 = 4 \text{ kg O}_2$

### Step 4: Account for nitrogen in air
Air composition by volume (and approximately mass) = **21% O₂ + 79% N₂**
Mass of air per kg of O₂ = $\dfrac{100}{23}$ ≈ **4.35 kg air / kg O₂**
*(Why 23? Because O₂ is 23% by mass in air: 32×0.21 / (32×0.21 + 28×0.79) ≈ 0.233)*

### Step 5: Calculate stoichiometric A/F ratio
$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/F)_{stoich} &= \dfrac{\text{Mass of air}}{\text{Mass of fuel}} = \dfrac{4 \times 4.35}{1} \\
(A/F)_{stoich} &= 17.4 \text{ kg air / kg CH}_4
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result (rounded, KTU board-accepted value): $(A/F)_{stoich} = 17.2 : 1$ for methane.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Excess Air Percentage from Flue Gas Oxygen Reading

This is the most common KTU numerical problem.

### Given:
A gas furnace flue gas analysis (Orsat) shows: $\text{O}_2 = 4\%$, $\text{CO}_2 = 10\%$, $\text{CO} = 0\%$, $\text{N}_2 = 86\%$.

### Step 1: Recall relation between O₂ in flue gas and excess air
For a fuel burning with **dry products only** (H₂O condensed out), the relation is:
$$
\% \text{ Excess Air} = \dfrac{\text{O}_2 \text{ in flue gas}}{0.264 \times \text{N}_2 \text{ in flue gas} - \text{O}_2 \text{ in flue gas}} \times 100
$$
*(The 0.264 comes from the 21/79 ratio of O₂/N₂ in air.)*

### Step 2: Substitute the values
$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Excess Air} &= \dfrac{4}{0.264 \times 86 - 4} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{4}{22.704 - 4} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{4}{18.704} \times 100 \\
&= 21.38 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Determine actual A/F ratio
$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/F)_{actual} &= (A/F)_{stoich} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{\% \text{ Excess Air}}{100}\right) \\
&= 17.2 \times (1 + 0.2138) \\
&= 17.2 \times 1.2138 \\
&= 20.88 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Combustion Efficiency (Heat Loss Method)

### Given:
- Calorific value of gas (CV) = **38,000 kJ/m³**
- Flue gas temperature $T_f = 600$ °C
- Ambient air temperature $T_a = 30$ °C
- Excess air = 21.38% (from above)
- Specific heat of flue gas $C_{pg} = 1.05 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- Specific heat of dry air $C_{pa} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$

### Step 1: Mass of flue gas per kg of fuel
Stoichiometric flue gas mass = $1 + (17.2 - 4) = 14.2 \text{ kg}$ (approx, dry basis)
With 21.38% excess air, additional air = $17.2 \times 0.2138 = 3.68 \text{ kg}$
This additional air passes through unreacted → becomes part of flue gas.
$$
m_{flue} = 14.2 + 3.68 = 17.88 \text{ kg/kg fuel}
$$

### Step 2: Heat loss in flue gas
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{loss} &= m_{flue} \cdot C_{pg} \cdot (T_f - T_a) \\
&= 17.88 \times 1.05 \times (600 - 30) \\
&= 17.88 \times 1.05 \times 570 \\
&= 10{,}701.18 \text{ kJ/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Heat input per kg of fuel (CV in kJ/kg)
For CH₄: CV ≈ **55,500 kJ/kg**, so:
$$
Q_{input} = 55{,}500 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

### Step 4: Combustion efficiency
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{comb} &= \left(1 - \dfrac{Q_{loss}}{Q_{input}}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - \dfrac{10{,}701.18}{55{,}500}\right) \times 100 \\
&= (1 - 0.1928) \times 100 \\
&= 80.72 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** Industry benchmark for a well-tuned gas furnace = **82–88%**. Below 80% = maintenance trigger.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Minimum Purge Time (NFPA 85 Requirement)

### Given:
- Furnace combustion chamber volume $V_c = 50 \text{ m}^3$
- Forced-draft fan flow rate $\dot{V}_{fan} = 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Required air changes per NFPA 85 = **4**

### Step 1: Apply the purge formula
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{purge} &= \dfrac{n \times V_c}{\dot{V}_{fan}} \\
&= \dfrac{4 \times 50}{5} \\
&= 40 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **n = 4** factor. Writing $t = V/\dot{V}$ will lose you 1 mark.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: LEL Safety Margin for Confined Space Entry

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a KTU favorite** for the 7-mark question on confined space entry permits.

### Given:
- LEL of methane = **5% by volume**
- Measured CH₄ concentration in furnace pit = **1.0% by volume**
- LEL safety threshold for entry = **25% of LEL**

### Step 1: Calculate the LEL safety margin
$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ LEL} &= \dfrac{C_{actual}}{\text{LEL}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.0}{5.0} \times 100 = 20\% \text{ LEL}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Interpret
Since **20% LEL < 25% LEL threshold → Entry is permitted** with continuous monitoring.
If reading crosses **25% LEL → All hot work MUST stop, evacuate, ventilate.**
If reading crosses **50% LEL → Atmosphere is hazardous — no entry, ignition sources eliminated.**

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Hazard → Control → Standard

| Hazard | Engineering Control | Administrative Control | Standard |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|----------|
| **Gas Leakage** | Double block & bleed valves, gas detectors | Permit-to-Work, leak testing | NFPA 54, IS 4246 |
| **Flame Failure** | UV/IR flame scanner, MSR trip | Logbook trip analysis | NFPA 85 |
| **CO Poisoning** | Catalytic oxidation, flue gas monitor | Health surveillance, blood test | OSHA 1910.1020 |
| **Burns** | Insulation, refractory, interlocks | PPE (aluminized suit), lockout | IS 11871 |
| **Explosion** | Explosion doors, purge system | Area classification (Zone 1/2) | IS 5572, IEC 60079 |
| **Implosion** | Inlet air pressure interlock | Cold start procedure | NFPA 85 |
| **Asphyxiation** | O₂ monitor with alarm at 19.5% | Confined Space Entry Permit | OSHA 1910.146 |
| **Noise** | Acoustic enclosure, silencer | Hearing protection, audiometry | IS 7194 |
| **Manual Valve Operation** | Chain-wheel guards, position indicator | Tag-out/Lock-out (LOTO) | IS 15058 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Double Block & Bleed (DBB)** is a *mandatory* configuration in industrial gas furnace trains. The bleed valve between the two safety shutoff valves provides a **positive verification of valve closure** — if the bleed valve leaks gas when both safety valves are closed, it proves that at least one valve has failed, and the furnace must be shut down for maintenance.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Flammability Limits" and explain why it is critical for safe gas furnace operation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Flammability Limits** are the **range of fuel-gas concentrations in air** within which the mixture can be ignited and propagate flame. The lower boundary is the **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** and the upper boundary is the **Upper Explosive Limit (UEL)**.

For **Natural Gas (Methane):** LEL = 5%, UEL = 15% (by volume in air).
For **LPG (Propane):** LEL = 2.1%, UEL = 9.5%.

**Critical importance in gas furnace safety:**

1. **Pre-Purge Operation:** Before ignition, the furnace chamber must be purged to ensure residual gas is below **25% of LEL** (i.e., below 1.25% for methane). This guarantees the mixture is too lean to explode.
2. **Leak Detection Triggering:** Fixed gas detectors are calibrated to alarm at **25% LEL** and trip emergency shutdown at **50% LEL**.
3. **Confined Space Entry:** Personnel can only enter a furnace pit or chamber when atmosphere is verified to be **< 10% LEL**.

> If a furnace is started with a **rich mixture** (gas above LEL but below UEL) inside, lighting the pilot will cause a **deflagration inside the chamber**, leading to explosion door lifting, refractory damage, and possible structural failure.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is a "Burner Management System" (BMS)? List any four safety functions it performs.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Burner Management System (BMS)** is a **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** designed to ensure the safe start-up, operation, and shut-down of a gas-fired furnace or boiler. It is typically rated **SIL 2 or SIL 3** per **IEC 61508**.

**Four safety functions of a BMS:**

1. **Pre-Ignition Purge Control:** Verifies the furnace combustion chamber has undergone at least **4 volume changes** with fresh air before allowing gas to enter.

2. **Trial-for-Ignition Timing:** Allows a finite period (typically **3–10 seconds**) for the pilot to establish flame; if not proven, gas valves close and a lockout occurs.

3. **Flame-Failure Trip:** Continuously monitors the flame via **UV scanner, IR scanner, or flame rod**. Loss of flame signal during operation triggers **emergency shutdown** within **< 1 second**.

4. **Interlock Verification:** Confirms that all safety permissives are satisfied (water flow, fan running, gas pressure, door closed, air pressure) before allowing each next step in the light-off sequence.

> Modern BMS is implemented using **redundant PLCs** (e.g., Allen-Bradley ControlLogix SIL, Siemens AS-410S) or **electromechanical relay logic** for smaller furnaces.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Answer ANY ONE full question from the two choices below. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat labeled block diagram, explain the **complete safety interlock sequence** for the start-up of an industrial gas furnace. State the role of each component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A natural gas furnace has the following flue gas analysis by Orsat: $\text{CO}_2 = 9.8\%$, $\text{O}_2 = 4.2\%$, $\text{CO} = 0\%$, $\text{N}_2 = 86\%$. The calorific value of the gas is **38,000 kJ/m³**, flue gas temperature is **620 °C**, and ambient temperature is **30 °C**. Determine the **(i)** percentage of excess air, **(ii)** actual A/F ratio, and **(iii)** combustion efficiency. Assume $C_{pg} = 1.05 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ and stoichiometric A/F = 17.2.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Safety Interlock Sequence for Gas Furnace Start-up:**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes):**

The start-up sequence comprises the following **sequential stages**, each with its own safety interlock:

| Step | Action | Safety Interlock | Failure Consequence |
|------|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Operator presses START | All pre-permissives must be TRUE (fan running, water flow, gas pressure, door closed, flame scanner healthy) | Alarm — "Permissive Fail" |
| 2 | **Pre-Purge** begins | Forced-draft fan ON, air damper open, **timer set for 4 volume changes** | Purge timer incomplete → no gas |
| 3 | Pilot ignition | Pilot solenoid opens, **spark igniter** fires for max 10 s | **FTF (Failure to Fire)** → MSR trip + lockout |
| 4 | Pilot flame proven | UV/IR scanner signal > threshold | No flame → trip |
| 5 | Main gas valve opens | **Staged light-off** (low-fire hold for 5 s) | Sudden opening → flashback |
| 6 | Steady-state operation | Continuous flame monitoring, furnace pressure control, O₂/CO trim | Trip to ESD |
| 7 | Emergency shutdown | Flame fail / low air / high temp / loss of draft | **All valves close in < 1 s** |
| 8 | Post-purge | Fan runs for 4 volume changes after flame fail | Removes unburnt gas |

**Key Components:**
- **Master Safety Relay (MSR):** The hard-wired final-trip device; failure of MSR = plant must shut down.
- **Flame Scanner:** UV (1.5–6.0 eV photon range, detects OH* radicals) or IR (detects CO₂ and H₂O emission).
- **Pressure Switches:** Gas pressure (low/high), air pressure (proves fan is working), furnace pressure (vacuum/positive).
- **Safety Shutoff Valves:** Two in series (DBB) — typically **solenoid-actuated** with spring-closure on power loss (fail-closed).
- **Ignition Transformer:** Generates **6–10 kV spark** for 5–10 s.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $\text{O}_2 = 4.2\%$, $\text{N}_2 = 86\%$, $T_f = 620$ °C, $T_a = 30$ °C, CV = 38,000 kJ/m³, $(A/F)_{stoich} = 17.2$.

**(i) Percentage Excess Air:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%EA &= \dfrac{\text{O}_2}{0.264 \times \text{N}_2 - \text{O}_2} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{4.2}{0.264 \times 86 - 4.2} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{4.2}{22.704 - 4.2} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{4.2}{18.504} \times 100 \\
&= 22.70 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Actual A/F Ratio:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/F)_{actual} &= (A/F)_{stoich} \times (1 + EA/100) \\
&= 17.2 \times 1.2270 \\
&= 21.10 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Combustion Efficiency:**

Stoichiometric flue gas mass per kg CH₄ = $1 + (17.2 - 4) = 14.2 \text{ kg}$
Excess air mass per kg fuel = $17.2 \times 0.227 = 3.90 \text{ kg}$
Total flue gas mass per kg fuel = $14.2 + 3.90 = 18.10 \text{ kg}$

For **1 m³ of natural gas** (density ≈ 0.72 kg/m³ at NTP), mass = 0.72 kg.
Total flue gas per m³ of gas = $0.72 \times 18.10 = 13.03 \text{ kg}$

Heat loss in flue gas:
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{loss} &= 13.03 \times 1.05 \times (620 - 30) \\
&= 13.03 \times 1.05 \times 590 \\
&= 8072 \text{ kJ/m}^3 \text{ gas}
\end{aligned}
$$

Combustion efficiency:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \left(1 - \dfrac{8072}{38000}\right) \times 100 \\
&= (1 - 0.2124) \times 100 \\
&= 78.76 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Efficiency of **78.76% is low** — indicates either **excess air is too high (22.7%)** or **stack temperature is too high (620 °C)**. Recommendation: tune air-fuel ratio to 15% EA; expect efficiency to rise to ~85%.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **various types of personal protective equipment (PPE)** required for personnel working in and around a gas furnace. State the relevant IS/ANSI standard for each item.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Permit-to-Work (PTW) system** as applied to:
   - (i) Hot work near a gas furnace
   - (ii) Confined space entry inside a furnace chamber
   
   What are the common pitfalls that lead to PTW failures?

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) PPE for Gas Furnace Operations:**

| PPE Item | Purpose | Standard | Hazard Mitigated |
|----------|---------|----------|------------------|
| **Aluminized Heat-Protective Suit** | Reflects 95% of radiant heat | IS 11871 / EN 531 | Burn injury |
| **Face Shield (Heat-Resistant)** | Protects face from IR & sparks | ANSI Z87.1 | Burns, eye damage |
| **Welding Goggles (Shade 5–6)** | UV/IR eye protection | IS 5983 | Photokeratitis |
| **Insulated Leather Gloves** | Heat + cut resistance | EN 407 | Hand burns |
| **Safety Helmet with Chin Strap** | Falling object + heat | IS 2925 / ANSI Z89.1 | Impact |
| **Safety Boots (Steel Toe, Heat Resistant Sole)** | Hot metal splash | IS 15298 | Foot burns |
| **Ear Muffs / Plugs** | Burner noise > 100 dBA | IS 7194 / ANSI S3.19 | Hearing loss |
| **Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA)** | Confined space / CO atmosphere | IS 10245 | Asphyxiation |
| **Portable Gas Detector (4-Gas)** | LEL/O₂/H₂S/CO monitoring | IEC 60079 | Toxic gas exposure |
| **Flame-Resistant Coverall (Nomex IIIA)** | Flash fire | NFPA 2112 / EN 531 | Burns |

> [!NOTE]
> The **4-Gas Personal Monitor** is the **single most important PPE** for furnace entry — it must be worn and active **before opening the furnace door**, not after.

---

**(b) Permit-to-Work (PTW) System:**

A PTW is a **formal written document** that authorizes specific work to be done in a defined area, after all hazards have been identified and controls put in place. It is the **administrative backbone** of furnace safety.

**(i) Hot Work Permit (for work near a gas furnace):**

**Required Information:**
1. Job description and location
2. Validity period (date, start, end time)
3. Gas detector readings (LEL < 1% for hot work)
4. List of hot work performed: welding, cutting, grinding
5. **Fire watchers** designated (one per shift minimum, 30-min fire watch after job)
6. **Fire extinguishers** (DCP + CO₂) within 5 m radius
7. **Combustible material removal** within 11 m
8. **Gas isolation** confirmed: main valve closed, locked, tagged
9. **Atmospheric testing** before and during work
10. Authorizing signatories: Issuer, Receiver, Approver (typically Area In-charge + Safety Officer)

**(ii) Confined Space Entry Permit (furnace chamber):**

**Steps:**
1. **Isolation:** Lock all energy sources (gas, electric, hydraulic, mechanical).
2. **Purging & Ventilation:** Forced fresh air for the entire work duration.
3. **Atmospheric Testing:** Test for **O₂ (19.5–23.5%)**, **LEL (< 10%)**, **CO (< 25 ppm)**, **H₂S (< 10 ppm)**.
4. **Standby Person:** Trained attendant outside the space with continuous communication.
5. **Rescue Plan:** Specific, rehearsed; SCBA-equipped rescue team available.
6. **Time Limit:** Permit valid for a **maximum 8 hours**; re-issue if work continues.
7. **Cancellation:** Permit cancelled on completion; all isolation re-applied until next work.

**Common Pitfalls in PTW Failure:**
- ❌ **Permit issued without atmospheric testing** (cause of the 2010 Bangalore factory blast that killed 6 workers)
- ❌ **Permit extended orally** without re-issue
- ❌ **Fire watcher absent** or untrained
- ❌ **Isolation not verified** — workers assume valves are closed
- ❌ **Permit not closed** after job — next shift assumes work is authorized
- ❌ **Multiple contractors** working on same equipment with conflicting permits
- ❌ **Permit not re-tested** after a break (e.g., lunch)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> When answering PTW questions, students **frequently lose marks** for the following:
> 1. **Failing to mention atmospheric testing in % LEL** — just saying "test the gas" is incomplete. State specific LEL% thresholds.
> 2. **Ignoring the role of the Standby Person** — the attendant is *not* optional, they are a *legal* requirement under IS 17696.
> 3. **Confusing Hot Work Permit with Confined Space Entry Permit** — they are **two separate permits** for overlapping work; you may need **both simultaneously**.
> 4. **Omitting the fire watcher's 30-minute post-work duty** — many students end the permit at the end of the job, but the fire watcher must remain **30 minutes minimum** after the last spark.
> 5. **Writing generic answers** — KTU examiners reward answers that cite **specific IS standard numbers** (e.g., IS 17696 for PTW, IS 4246 for gas furnaces).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points for KTU 2024 ESE**

### ✅ Definitions You MUST Memorize
- [ ] **LEL & UEL** of methane (5% & 15%), propane (2.1% & 9.5%), hydrogen (4% & 75%)
- [ ] **Stoichiometric A/F ratio** for methane = **17.2 : 1** (mass)
- [ ] **Auto-ignition temperature** of methane = **537 °C**
- [ ] **NFPA 85 purge requirement** = **4 volume changes**
- [ ] **LEL alarm set points**: 25% LEL = warning, 50% LEL = trip
- [ ] **Maximum CO in workplace** = **50 ppm (8-hr TWA)**
- [ ] **Confined space O₂ range** = **19.5%–23.5%**
- [ ] **Trial-for-ignition period** = **3–10 seconds** (pilot)
- [ ] **Emergency shutdown response time** = **< 1 second**

### ✅ The Fire Tetrahedron (Fuel + O₂ + Heat + Chain Reaction)
- [ ] Break **any one side** → combustion stops
- [ ] Flame scanners detect **chain reaction** side (free radicals)

### ✅ The 8-Stage BMS Light-Off Sequence
1. Pre-Start Checks
2. Pre-Purge (4× volume)
3. Pilot Ignition (with Trial-for-Ignition timer)
4. Pilot Flame Proving
5. Main Gas Valve Open (staged)
6. Steady-State Operation
7. Continuous Monitoring
8. Shutdown (normal or emergency) → Post-Purge

### ✅ The 4-Gas Atmosphere
- [ ] **O₂** — must be 19.5–23.5%
- [ ] **LEL** — must be < 10% (entry), < 25% (alarm)
- [ ] **CO** — must be < 25 ppm (entry), < 50 ppm (workplace TWA)
- [ ] **H₂S** — must be < 10 ppm (entry)

### ✅ Critical Standards to Quote in Exam
- [ ] **NFPA 85** — Boiler & Combustion Systems Hazards Code
- [ ] **NFPA 54** — National Fuel Gas Code
- [ ] **IEC 61508** — Functional Safety of E/E/PE Systems
- [ ] **IEC 60079** — Explosive Atmospheres (Area Classification)
- [ ] **IS 4246** — Gas-fired Industrial Furnaces
- [ ] **IS 17696** — Permit-to-Work Systems
- [ ] **OISD-150** — Gas Distribution Safety

### ✅ Key Formulas (one-line memory)
- [ ] $(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{2 \times 32 \times 4.76}{16} = 17.2$
- [ ] $\%EA = \dfrac{O_2}{0.264 \cdot N_2 - O_2} \times 100$
- [ ] $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \dfrac{m_{flue} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_f - T_a)}{\text{CV}}$
- [ ] $t_{purge} = \dfrac{4 \cdot V_c}{\dot{V}_{fan}}$
- [ ] $\%LEL = \dfrac{C_{actual}}{\text{LEL}} \times 100$

### ✅ Mnemonics for the Furnace Safety Sequence
> **"Please Pre-Purge, Pilot, Prove, Proceed"** — the four Ps of safe light-off.
> 
> **"Lockout, Tag, Try, Test, Then Touch"** — the five Ts of confined space entry.

### ✅ Common Marks-Dropping Mistakes
- ❌ Confusing **purge volume** (4×) with **purge air velocity** (> 0.5 m/s)
- ❌ Forgetting **N₂ in flue gas** in excess air calculation
- ❌ Not stating **staged light-off** in main burner opening
- ❌ Omitting **hard-wired MSR** description in BMS
- ❌ Writing **"fire extinguisher"** without specifying type (DCP for Class B, CO₂ for electrical)
- ❌ Not mentioning **manual reset** requirement after ESD

### ✅ Two-Liners for Last-Minute Revision
> *"A gas furnace does not explode by itself — it explodes because a safety interlock was bypassed."*
>
> *"In furnace safety, slow start is fast safety; quick light-off is a quick fatality."*

---
**End of Module 4 — Safety in Gas Furnace Operation | OESFT725 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cupola
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cupola

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Cupola Furnace — Foundational Definition & Industrial Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Within Module 4 (Foundry / Furnace Safety), the *Cupola* is treated as the **anchor topic** because it represents one of the highest-risk installations in a typical engineering industry. Both constructional defects and operational lapses in cupolas historically account for the largest share of foundry accidents in India.

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

A **Cupola** is a **vertical, cylindrical, refractory-lined shaft furnace** used in foundries to melt **ferrous metals** (predominantly **cast iron**, but also **bronze** and **brass** in modified designs) for the purpose of producing molten metal for **sand casting** operations. It operates on a **continuous-charging / intermittent-tapping** principle, fueled primarily by **metallurgical coke**, with the energy released by **exothermic oxidation of carbon** used to melt descending metal charges.

$$Q_{\text{melt}} = \dot{m}_{\text{metal}} \times L_{\text{fusion}}$$

where $\dot{m}_{\text{metal}}$ is the mass feed rate of the metallic charge and $L_{\text{fusion}}$ is the **latent heat of fusion** of the metal (≈ $247 \;\text{kJ/kg}$ for grey cast iron).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-grade operational reading:** A standard small foundry cupola of **60 cm internal diameter** produces between **0.8 and 1.5 tonnes of molten iron per hour** with a **coke-to-metal ratio** of approximately **8 % – 12 %**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vertical Forge Tower"

Think of a cupola as a **continuously burning vertical forge tower**, much like a **deep, controlled campfire** enclosed inside a brick chimney.

| Real-World Object | Cupola Equivalent | Functional Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| Campfire in a brick pit | Refractory-lined cylindrical shell | Provides the hot, confined reaction zone |
| Bellows pumping air | **Tuyeres** + **blower** | Forces oxygen through the fuel bed |
| Wood logs burning downward | **Coke bed** + descending metal charge | Releases heat progressively |
| Ash falling to the bottom | **Slag hole / tap hole** | Removes waste and delivers molten metal |
| Smoke going up the chimney | **Stack** + **spark arrestor** | Vents flue gases safely |

The **operator** (cupola tender) acts as the **master of the fire**, regulating air supply, fuel feed, and metal charge rhythm to keep the "fire" in a safe, stable state. If any of these three controls is lost — fire, feed, or air — the cupola transitions from an asset to a **catastrophic hazard**.

## 1.3 Why Cupola Safety is a Standalone Module Topic

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course: OESFT725, Module 4), the cupola is singled out because it is the **only standard industrial furnace** that simultaneously combines **five high-severity hazard classes**:

1. **Thermal hazards** — molten metal at **1300 °C – 1500 °C**
2. **Chemical hazards** — generation of **CO, CO₂, NOₓ, SO₂**
3. **Mechanical hazards** — risk of **furnace explosion** ("blow-up")
4. **Physical hazards** — **dust, fumes, falling materials**
5. **Ergonomic hazards** — **heat stress** on operators

> [!TIP]
> **For 2-mark / 3-mark KTU questions:** Memorise the **five hazard classes** above and the **standard operating temperature window (1300 °C – 1500 °C)**. Examiners consistently test these in **Part A**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Architecture of a Cupola

A modern cupola is not merely a "chimney with fire" — it is a **precision-engineered pressure vessel** with specific zones, each designed for a unique thermodynamic function.

### 2.1.1 Major Constructional Components

| # | Component | Material of Construction | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Outer Steel Shell** | Mild steel plate (10–20 mm) | Mechanical strength; resists internal pressure |
| 2 | **Refractory Lining** | Fireclay bricks / silica bricks | Thermal insulation; contains the melt |
| 3 | **Charging Door** | Steel + refractory | Top-feed entry for coke, iron, flux |
| 4 | **Stack / Chimney** | Steel with refractory lining | Draft generation; gas venting |
| 5 | **Tuyeres** | Copper / cast iron | Inject combustion air into the coke bed |
| 6 | **Blower / Fan** | Centrifugal blower | Supplies pressurized combustion air |
| 7 | **Hearth** | Refractory + bottom doors | Collects molten metal and slag |
| 8 | **Tap Hole (Spout)** | Refractory-lined channel | Continuous discharge of molten iron |
| 9 | **Slag Hole** | Refractory-lined, slightly above tap hole | Periodically drains floating slag |
| 10 | **Drop (Bottom) Doors** | Hinged steel plates | Quick emergency emptying |
| 11 | **Spark Arrestor** | Wire mesh / baffle plates | Quenches sparks at stack exit |
| 12 | **Water Curtain / Dust Collector** | Spray headers + cyclone | Captures airborne particulates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the tuyere zone is the "heart" of the cupola:** The tuyere level is where **all three phases (solid coke, liquid metal, gaseous air) meet simultaneously**. Any imbalance here — e.g., water leakage, excess moisture in charge, or tuyere blockage — triggers the dreaded **"cupola explosion"** (also called *blow-up*).

### 2.1.2 The Four Thermodynamic Zones (from top to bottom)

| Zone | Location | Temperature Range | Dominant Process |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stack (Pre-heating) Zone** | Top 30 % of shell | 100 °C – 1100 °C | Charge pre-heating; flue gas ascent |
| **Reduction / Melting Zone** | Middle 40 % | 1100 °C – 1500 °C | Iron melting; CO + C → 2CO endothermic; C + O₂ → CO₂ |
| **Combustion (Tuyere) Zone** | Above tuyere level | 1500 °C – 1800 °C | Coke combustion; maximum heat release |
| **Hearth (Collection) Zone** | Below tuyere level | 1300 °C – 1500 °C | Molten metal + slag accumulation; tap discharge |

## 2.2 Combustion Chemistry — The Heart of Cupola Operation

The cupola is fundamentally a **counter-current gas-solid heat exchanger** with simultaneous **heterogeneous chemical reactions**.

### 2.2.1 Primary Combustion Reactions

**Reaction 1 — Complete Combustion at tuyere level:**
$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Reaction 2 — Boudouard (Solution Loss) Reaction — endothermic, consumes heat:**
$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{C} \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The **Boudouard reaction** is the **principal source of CO hazard** in cupola operations. Approximately **20 % – 35 %** of all carbon monoxide generated in a cupola originates from this reaction above the tuyere zone.

**Reaction 3 — Water-Gas Reaction (most dangerous — hydrogen + CO generation):**
$$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +131.3 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Why this is the No. 1 cause of cupola explosions:** Any moisture entering the cupola (wet charge, rain ingress, tuyere cooling-water leak) immediately reacts with incandescent coke to generate a **highly explosive H₂ + CO mixture**. The auto-ignition temperature of H₂ is **500 °C — well below cupola stack temperature**. A single drop of water at the tuyere can trigger a **detonation**.

### 2.2.2 Stoichiometric Air Calculation

The theoretical (stoichiometric) air required to burn 1 kg of carbon completely is:

$$m_{\text{air,st}} = \frac{32}{12} \times \frac{1}{0.232} = 11.6 \;\text{kg air/kg C}$$

**Volumetric equivalent at NTP (0 °C, 101.325 kPa):**
$$V_{\text{air,st}} = \frac{m_{\text{air,st}}}{\rho_{\text{air}}} = \frac{11.6}{1.293} = 8.97 \;\text{m}^3/\text{kg C}$$

In practice, cupolas operate with an **excess-air factor** $\lambda$:
$$V_{\text{air,actual}} = \lambda \times V_{\text{air,st}} \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Formulas — Cupola Operation & Safety.** Memorise the units; markers award half-marks for correct units in numericals.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{melt}} = \dot{m}_{\text{metal}} \times L_f$ | Heat absorbed by metal | $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, $L_f$ in kJ/kg |
| 2 | $V_{\text{air,st}} = 8.97 \;\text{m}^3/\text{kg C}$ | Stoichiometric air for coke | At NTP |
| 3 | $V_{\text{air,actual}} = \lambda \times V_{\text{air,st}}$ | Actual air supply | $\lambda = 1.5 - 2.0$ |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{melt}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \times \text{CV}_{\text{coke}}}$ | Thermal efficiency | 30 % – 50 % |
| 5 | $\text{CR} = \frac{m_{\text{coke}}}{m_{\text{metal}}} \times 100 \%$ | Coke ratio | 8 % – 15 % |
| 6 | $\Delta P_{\text{draft}} = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{\text{air}} - \rho_{\text{gas}})$ | Natural draft (stack effect) | Pascals |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{\text{metal}} = \rho_{\text{iron}} \cdot A_{\text{cs}} \cdot v_{\text{desc}}$ | Mass feed rate (descending charge) | kg/s |
| 8 | $\text{TLV}_{\text{CO}} \le 50 \;\text{ppm}$ | Threshold limit value (8-hr TWA) | OSHA / ACGIH |
| 9 | $\text{TLV}_{\text{SO}_2} \le 2 \;\text{ppm}$ | Threshold limit value | OSHA / ACGIH |
| 10 | $\text{TLV}_{\text{NO}_2} \le 3 \;\text{ppm}$ | Threshold limit value | OSHA / ACGIH |

> [!TIP]
> **The "Three-Formula Trinity"** that examiners love to ask: (i) Stoichiometric air, (ii) Coke ratio, (iii) Thermal efficiency. If you master these three with units, you can solve 80 % of cupola numericals.

## 2.4 Operational Workflow & Safety Interlocks

The **safe operational cycle** of a cupola is a **closed-loop process** with hard-wired interlocks:

1. **Pre-start safety check** — refractory integrity, tuyere condition, blower function, water-curtain nozzles, spark arrestor, drop-door release mechanism.
2. **Lighting-up** — bed of kindling wood ignited on the bottom doors; first coke charge added; blower started **only after** ignition is verified.
3. **Coke-bed establishment** — initial bed height of coke sufficient to bring tuyere zone to combustion temperature within **30 – 45 minutes**.
4. **Charging cycle** — alternate layers of **metal → coke → flux (limestone)** charged through the charging door at the top.
5. **Melting & tapping** — molten iron continuously tapped from the tap hole into **ladles**; slag periodically drained from the slag hole.
6. **Blow-down & emptying** — at end of campaign, blower stopped, drop doors released, contents fall into a **sand pit** for emergency quenching.
7. **Post-campaign maintenance** — refractory inspection, tuyere cleaning, refractory patching.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-grade "Stop-the-Line" Trigger Conditions** (any one → immediate blower cut-off):
> - Water seepage in tuyere cooling circuit
> - Sudden flame reversal at the charging door (back-flash)
> - Refractory temperature spike (above **1600 °C** — indicates tuyere burn-through)
> - CO detector reading **> 75 ppm** at operator's breathing zone
> - Drop-door interlock failure

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air Required to Melt 1 Tonne of Cast Iron

**Problem Statement (typical KTU numerical):** *A cupola furnace uses 12 % coke (by mass of iron charged) to melt cast iron. The coke contains 85 % fixed carbon and 15 % ash. Calculate the stoichiometric air required (in m³ at NTP) to combust the carbon in the coke, given that air contains 23 % oxygen by mass and 1.293 kg/m³ density at NTP.*

**Step 1 — Determine mass of coke per 1 tonne of iron charge.**
$$m_{\text{coke}} = \text{CR} \times m_{\text{iron}} = 0.12 \times 1000 = 120 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Determine mass of fixed carbon available for combustion.**
$$m_{\text{C}} = 0.85 \times m_{\text{coke}} = 0.85 \times 120 = 102 \;\text{kg}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the stoichiometric carbon-to-CO₂ reaction stoichiometry.**

From the balanced equation $\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$:

$$12 \;\text{kg C} \;\; \text{requires} \;\; 32 \;\text{kg O}_2$$

Therefore, the oxygen demand for 102 kg of carbon is:
$$m_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{32}{12} \times 102 = 272 \;\text{kg O}_2$$

**Step 4 — Convert oxygen demand to air demand using mass fraction of O₂ in air (23.2 %).**
$$m_{\text{air}} = \frac{m_{\text{O}_2}}{0.232} = \frac{272}{0.232} = 1172.4 \;\text{kg air}$$

**Step 5 — Convert mass of air to volume at NTP using air density $1.293 \;\text{kg/m}^3$.**
$$V_{\text{air,st}} = \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{\rho_{\text{air}}} = \frac{1172.4}{1.293} = 906.7 \;\text{m}^3 \text{ at NTP}$$

**Step 6 — Apply excess-air factor (typical $\lambda = 1.6$).**
$$V_{\text{air,actual}} = \lambda \times V_{\text{air,st}} = 1.6 \times 906.7 = 1450.7 \;\text{m}^3/\text{tonne iron}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result rounded:** $V_{\text{air,actual}} \approx 1451 \;\text{m}^3$ of air per tonne of cast iron melted. **Reality check:** this matches empirical foundry data of **1200 – 1500 m³/tonne** in standard cupola practice.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Thermal Efficiency of a Cupola

**Problem Statement:** *A cupola melts 2 tonnes of cast iron per hour. The initial iron temperature is 25 °C; final tapping temperature is 1400 °C. Coke consumption is 240 kg/hr with calorific value 28 MJ/kg. Specific heat of iron $c_p = 0.67 \;\text{kJ/kg·K}$. Latent heat of fusion $L_f = 247 \;\text{kJ/kg}$. Calculate the thermal efficiency.*

**Step 1 — Compute total useful heat absorbed by 1 kg of iron (sensible + latent).**

The total enthalpy rise per kg of iron is:
$$\Delta h = c_p (T_{\text{tap}} - T_{\text{initial}}) + L_f$$

Substituting the values:
$$\Delta h = 0.67 \times (1400 - 25) + 247$$

$$\Delta h = 0.67 \times 1375 + 247 = 921.25 + 247 = 1168.25 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute useful heat for 2000 kg/hr charge.**
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}} = m \cdot \Delta h = 2000 \times 1168.25 = 2{,}336{,}500 \;\text{kJ/hr} = 2336.5 \;\text{MJ/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Compute heat supplied by coke per hour.**
$$\dot{Q}_{\text{supplied}} = m_{\text{coke}} \times \text{CV} = 240 \times 28 = 6720 \;\text{MJ/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Compute thermal efficiency.**
$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{supplied}}} = \frac{2336.5}{6720} = 0.3477 = 34.77 \;\text{%}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** $\eta_{\text{thermal}} \approx 34.8 \;\%$. This is **typical for cold-blast cupolas** (range 30 % – 50 %). Hot-blast cupolas (pre-heated air) can reach **55 % – 60 %**. The remaining energy is lost in flue gases, slag, and radiation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Air Volume Through Tuyere (Continuity Equation)

**Problem Statement:** *A cupola has 6 tuyeres, each of 100 mm diameter. The air velocity at tuyere exit is measured at 50 m/s. Calculate the volumetric air supply in m³/min and the mass flow in kg/min (at 25 °C, $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.184 \;\text{kg/m}^3$).*

**Step 1 — Compute area of a single tuyere.**
$$A_{\text{tuyere}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply continuity: $Q = A \cdot v$ for one tuyere.**
$$Q_1 = A_{\text{tuyere}} \times v = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 = 0.3927 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Total volumetric flow for 6 tuyeres.**
$$Q_{\text{total}} = 6 \times 0.3927 = 2.356 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to m³/min.**
$$Q_{\text{total}} = 2.356 \times 60 = 141.4 \;\text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 5 — Compute mass flow rate.**
$$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \rho_{\text{air}} \times Q_{\text{total}} = 1.184 \times 2.356 = 2.79 \;\text{kg/s} = 167.4 \;\text{kg/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For a small cupola producing **0.8 t/hr** of iron, expected air supply is approximately **140 – 160 m³/min**. Our calculated **141.4 m³/min** falls exactly in this range, validating the solution.

---

## 3.4 Safety Risk Assessment Matrix (Analytical)

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** expects students to *quantify* risks, not just list them. Use the standard **Severity × Likelihood** matrix:

| Hazard | Severity (S) | Likelihood (L) | Risk Score (R = S × L) | Risk Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Furnace explosion (blow-up) | 5 (Catastrophic) | 2 (Rare) | **10** | **HIGH** |
| CO poisoning | 5 | 3 (Possible) | **15** | **CRITICAL** |
| Molten metal splash burn | 4 (Major) | 4 (Likely) | **16** | **CRITICAL** |
| Heat stress | 3 (Moderate) | 5 (Almost Certain) | **15** | **CRITICAL** |
| Falling charge material | 3 | 3 | **9** | **MEDIUM** |
| Dust / silicosis | 4 | 5 | **20** | **CRITICAL** |
| Slag explosion (water contact) | 5 | 2 | **10** | **HIGH** |
| Tuyere water leak → H₂ detonation | 5 | 2 | **10** | **HIGH** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legend (Risk Scoring):** S: 1 = Negligible, 2 = Minor, 3 = Moderate, 4 = Major, 5 = Catastrophic. L: 1 = Rare, 2 = Unlikely, 3 = Possible, 4 = Likely, 5 = Almost Certain. **R > 15 → CRITICAL → Mandatory engineering control + PPE + training.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cupola Functional Architecture & Safety Interlock Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a block diagram and not a physical cross-section?** In a markdown-rendered KTU note, a physical cross-section with refractory layers, tuyeres, charging door, etc. loses visual fidelity. A **functional block diagram** showing the *flow of materials* and *safety interlocks* is far more examination-relevant because it directly maps to **process flow diagrams** that foundry safety officers use.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coke Storage Silo] --> B[Weigh Hopper & Conveyor]
    F[Iron Scrap Yard] --> B
    L[Limestone Flux Bin] --> B
    B --> C[Charging Skip Hoist]
    C --> D[Charging Door at Stack Top]
    D --> E[Pre-heating Zone 100 to 1100 C]
    E --> G[Melting Zone 1100 to 1500 C]
    G --> H[Combustion Zone at Tuyere Level 1500 to 1800 C]
    H --> I[Hearth Collection Zone 1300 to 1500 C]
    I --> J[Tap Hole Spout]
    I --> K[Slag Hole Drain]
    J --> M[Transfer Ladle]
    K --> N[Slag Granulation Pit]
    H --> P[Centrifugal Blower]
    P --> Q[Combustion Air Header]
    Q --> H
    H --> R[Flue Gas Exit at Stack]
    R --> S[Spark Arrestor]
    S --> T[Water Curtain Scrubber]
    T --> U[ID Fan to Stack Emission]
    X[CO and NO2 Gas Detector] -.-> Y[Safety PLC Controller]
    Z[Refractory Temperature Sensor] -.-> Y
    AA[Tuyere Cooling Water Flow Switch] -.-> Y
    Y --> AB[Emergency Blower Cut-off Relay]
    Y --> AC[Audible Visual Alarm]
    AB --> P
    Y --> AD[Drop Door Release Mechanism]
    AD --> V[Sand Pit Emergency Emptying]
```

**Reading guide for the Mermaid diagram:**

- **Solid arrows** = material / air flow path.
- **Dotted arrows** (with `-.->`) = **sensor signal lines** into the Safety PLC.
- The **Safety PLC `Y`** is the central interlock node — if any one of the three sensors (CO/NO₂, refractory temp, tuyere water flow) trips, the PLC simultaneously:
  1. Stops the blower `AB → P`
  2. Activates the alarm `AC`
  3. Releases the drop doors `AD → V`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When KTU asks "Draw a block diagram of a cupola with safety interlocks," this is the **exact representation** the examiner expects. Bonus marks for explicitly showing the **three sensor → PLC → actuator loop** because it tests **outcome-based competency** (CO4: Apply safety interlocks in industrial furnace systems).

## 4.2 Schematic — Counter-Current Heat Exchange Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    direction LR
    subgraph DESC["Descending Solid Charge"]
        D1[Iron Scrap 25 C] --> D2[Iron Scrap 800 C] --> D3[Iron Scrap 1400 C - Molten]
    end
    subgraph ASC["Ascending Flue Gas"]
        A1[Flue Gas 1500 C] --> A2[Flue Gas 600 C] --> A3[Flue Gas 150 C to Stack]
    end
    DESC -.->|Heat Transfer| ASC
    D3 --> TAP[Tap Hole]
    A3 --> STK[Stack]
```

**Reading guide:**
- The **descending arrow chain** (top-to-bottom) is the **solid charge** falling under gravity.
- The **ascending arrow chain** (bottom-to-top) is the **hot flue gas** rising due to buoyancy.
- The two streams exchange heat **counter-currently** — the **hottest gas meets the hottest, almost-molten iron at the bottom**, maximizing heat transfer efficiency.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for safety:** The counter-current design means the **stack-top temperature is the lowest (150 °C – 200 °C)**, which dramatically reduces **fire risk from spark emission**. If a student can explain this in a 2-mark answer, it demonstrates **deep conceptual mastery** beyond rote learning.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a cupola furnace. List any four major constructional components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **cupola** is a vertical, cylindrical, refractory-lined shaft furnace used in foundries to melt cast iron and other non-ferrous metals by the **continuous combustion of metallurgical coke**, with **counter-current heat exchange** between the descending solid charge and the ascending hot flue gases.

**Four major constructional components:**

1. **Refractory-lined steel shell** — provides mechanical strength and contains the melt at **1300 °C – 1500 °C**.
2. **Tuyeres** — copper / cast-iron nozzles that inject **pressurized combustion air** from the blower into the coke bed.
3. **Charging door** — top entry point for alternate layers of **metal, coke, and limestone flux**.
4. **Tap hole and slag hole** — bottom outlets for **molten iron discharge** and **periodic slag drainage** respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking scheme (KTU pattern):** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for components (½ mark each).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State the Threshold Limit Values (TLV) for carbon monoxide and sulfur dioxide in a foundry environment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Pollutant | TLV (8-hr TWA) | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Carbon monoxide (CO)** | **50 ppm** | OSHA / ACGIH |
| **Sulfur dioxide (SO₂)** | **2 ppm** | OSHA / ACGIH |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Confusing the *8-hr TWA* with the *STEL (15-min Short-Term Exposure Limit)*. CO STEL is **200 ppm**, but the KTU question almost always refers to TWA. Read the question carefully.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q7(a)]`

**Q7(a) (7 marks):** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture and operation** of a cupola furnace. Highlight the four thermodynamic zones.

**Q7(b) (7 marks):** Discuss **five major safety hazards** associated with cupola operation and the corresponding **engineering control measures** to mitigate them.

---

#### Model Solution for Q7(a) — Functional Architecture (7 marks)

**(i) Definition and classification (1 mark):**

A cupola is a vertical shaft furnace operating on the **counter-current heat exchange principle** between descending solid charge (iron + coke + flux) and ascending hot flue gases from coke combustion. It is classified as a **continuous-charging, semi-continuous-tapping** furnace.

**(ii) Four thermodynamic zones (3 marks):**

| Zone | Location | Temperature | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-heating Zone** | Top 30 % | 100 – 1100 °C | Charge pre-heated by rising flue gas |
| **Melting / Reduction Zone** | Middle 40 % | 1100 – 1500 °C | Iron melts; CO₂ + C → 2CO (Boudouard) |
| **Combustion Zone** | Tuyere level | 1500 – 1800 °C | Peak coke combustion; C + O₂ → CO₂ |
| **Hearth Zone** | Below tuyere | 1300 – 1500 °C | Molten metal + slag collection |

**(iii) Material flow path (1 mark):** Iron scrap → charging door → descends through zones → melts in middle zone → collects in hearth → exits through tap hole.

**(iv) Gas flow path (1 mark):** Combustion air → blower → tuyeres → burns coke → flue gas ascends → pre-heats charge → exits through stack → spark arrestor → water scrubber → atmosphere.

**(v) Block diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the **Mermaid functional block diagram** in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

#### Model Solution for Q7(b) — Five Safety Hazards & Controls (7 marks)

**Hazard 1: Furnace Explosion (Blow-up) — 1.5 marks**

*Cause:* Moisture in charge contacts incandescent coke → **water-gas reaction** generates explosive **H₂ + CO** mixture → detonates.
*Control:* (i) **Dry and pre-heated charge** before loading; (ii) **blow-dry the cupola** for 15 – 20 minutes before charging metal; (iii) install **tuyere cooling-water flow interlocks** with automatic blower cut-off.

**Hazard 2: Carbon Monoxide Poisoning — 1.5 marks**

*Cause:* Boudouard reaction above tuyere zone generates **20 – 35 %** of total CO emissions.
*Control:* (i) Install **CO detectors** at operator's breathing zone (set point **75 ppm** → alarm); (ii) provide **fresh-air ventilation hoods**; (iii) enforce **TLV ≤ 50 ppm**; (iv) mandatory **SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus)** during tapping.

**Hazard 3: Molten Metal Splash & Burn Injury — 1.5 marks**

*Cause:* Tapping at 1400 °C; ladle failure; slag carry-over.
*Control:* (i) **Full PPE**: aluminized suit, face shield, leggings, leather gloves; (ii) **dry ladles pre-heated to 100 °C** before receiving metal; (iii) **safety spouts and tap-hole shrouds**; (iv) **exclusion zone of 3 m** around tap hole.

**Hazard 4: Heat Stress — 1.5 marks**

*Cause:* Ambient temperature **40 – 50 °C** in cupola cabin; radiant heat load **> 1 kW/m²**.
*Control:* (i) **Acclimatization schedule**; (ii) **work-rest cycle** (20 min work / 15 min cool-down); (iii) **water & electrolyte intake**; (iv) **air-conditioned control cabin** with remote charging.

**Hazard 5: Dust & Silicosis — 1.5 marks**

*Cause:* Foundry dust contains **free silica (SiO₂)** from sand, refractory, and flux.
*Control:* (i) **Water curtain** + **cyclone dust collector** + **bag-house filter**; (ii) **wet sweeping** instead of dry sweeping; (iii) **FFP2 / FFP3 respirators** mandatory; (iv) **annual pulmonary function tests**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (lose 2-3 marks):**
> - ❌ Writing generic "wear PPE" without **specifying the type** (aluminized suit vs. leather gloves).
> - ❌ Not linking the **Boudouard reaction** to CO generation — examiners award a full mark only for the chemical equation.
> - ❌ Confusing **TLV-TWA** and **TLV-STEL** — both are accepted in foundry work but state the correct one for the specific pollutant.
> - ❌ Drawing a **physical diagram** instead of a **block diagram** when the question explicitly asks for one.
> - ❌ Forgetting the **drop-door emergency emptying** procedure — it is the single most important **engineering control** for controlling a blow-up.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Q8(b)]`

**Q8(a) (7 marks):** A cupola furnace is charged with **1500 kg/hr** of cast iron. The coke-to-metal ratio is **10 %**, and the coke contains **88 % fixed carbon**. The coke calorific value is **29 MJ/kg**. The tapping temperature is **1380 °C** and the charge enters at **30 °C**. Specific heat of iron $c_p = 0.67 \;\text{kJ/kg·K}$; latent heat of fusion $L_f = 247 \;\text{kJ/kg}$. **Calculate the thermal efficiency of the cupola.**

**Q8(b) (7 marks):** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the **counter-current heat exchange principle** in a cupola. Why is this principle important for both **energy efficiency** and **safety**?

---

#### Model Solution for Q8(a) — Numerical on Thermal Efficiency (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{\text{iron}} = 1500 \;\text{kg/hr}$
- $\text{CR} = 10 \;\% \Rightarrow \dot{m}_{\text{coke}} = 150 \;\text{kg/hr}$
- $T_{\text{initial}} = 30 \;\text{°C}$, $T_{\text{tap}} = 1380 \;\text{°C}$
- $c_p = 0.67 \;\text{kJ/kg·K}$, $L_f = 247 \;\text{kJ/kg}$
- $\text{CV}_{\text{coke}} = 29 \;\text{MJ/kg}$

**Step 1 — Total enthalpy rise per kg of iron.**

$$\Delta h = c_p (T_{\text{tap}} - T_{\text{initial}}) + L_f$$

$$\Delta h = 0.67 \times (1380 - 30) + 247 = 0.67 \times 1350 + 247 = 904.5 + 247 = 1151.5 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Useful heat absorbed per hour.**

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}} = \dot{m}_{\text{iron}} \times \Delta h = 1500 \times 1151.5 = 1{,}727{,}250 \;\text{kJ/hr} = 1727.25 \;\text{MJ/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Heat supplied by coke per hour.**

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{supplied}} = \dot{m}_{\text{coke}} \times \text{CV} = 150 \times 29 = 4350 \;\text{MJ/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Thermal efficiency.**

$$\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{supplied}}} = \frac{1727.25}{4350} = 0.3970 = 39.70 \;\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Answer:** $\boxed{\eta_{\text{thermal}} \approx 39.7 \;\%}$. This is **within the cold-blast cupola standard range (30 – 50 %)**, validating the calculation.

---

#### Model Solution for Q8(b) — Counter-Current Heat Exchange (7 marks)

**(i) Definition (2 marks):**

In a cupola, the **descending solid charge (cold iron, coke, flux)** and the **ascending hot flue gas** move in **opposite directions**, exchanging heat through the refractory wall and the gas-solid interface. This is the **counter-current heat exchange principle**.

**(ii) Directional flow analysis (2 marks):**

- **Solid charge flow:** Top → bottom (driven by **gravity**), entering at ambient temperature and exiting as molten metal at 1400 °C.
- **Gas flow:** Bottom → top (driven by **buoyancy / stack effect**), entering as cold air at tuyeres and exiting as flue gas at 150 °C – 200 °C at the stack.

**(iii) Why this is important for energy efficiency (1.5 marks):**

The **temperature profile** of gas and solid cross at every point along the cupola height. The hottest gas meets the nearly-molten metal at the tuyere zone, while the coolest gas meets the cold incoming charge at the stack. This **maximizes the log-mean temperature difference (LMTD)** and recovers otherwise wasted sensible heat in the flue gas, raising thermal efficiency from ~ 20 % (co-current) to **30 – 50 %** (counter-current).

**(iv) Why this is important for safety (1.5 marks):**

- The **cool stack-top temperature (150 – 200 °C)** prevents **spark-induced fires** in the foundry roof and surrounding yard.
- The **cool charge** at the top never exceeds the **auto-ignition temperature of common contaminants** (grease, oil, paint on scrap), reducing risk of **deflagration** during charging.
- **No high-temperature gas reversal** is encountered, which means **no back-flash** into the operator's face at the charging door.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse **counter-current** with **co-current** and lose 2 marks. Always remember: **solid goes DOWN, gas goes UP — opposite directions = counter-current.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this section and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Cupola** = vertical, cylindrical, refractory-lined shaft furnace for melting cast iron.
- [x] **Fuel** = metallurgical coke; **energy source** = C + O₂ → CO₂ exothermic reaction.
- [x] **Standard tapping temperature** = **1300 °C – 1500 °C**.
- [x] **Coke ratio (CR)** = $m_{\text{coke}} / m_{\text{iron}} \times 100 \;\%$ = typically **8 % – 15 %**.
- [x] **Stoichiometric air for carbon** = **8.97 m³/kg C at NTP** (or **11.6 kg air/kg C**).
- [x] **Actual air** = $\lambda \times$ stoichiometric, with $\lambda = 1.5 - 2.0$.
- [x] **Thermal efficiency** = **30 % – 50 %** (cold-blast); up to **60 %** (hot-blast).
- [x] **Four zones** = pre-heating → melting → combustion (tuyere) → hearth.
- [x] **Boudouard reaction** (CO₂ + C → 2CO) is the **main CO hazard** generator.
- [x] **Water-gas reaction** (C + H₂O → CO + H₂) is the **main explosion** cause.
- [x] **TLV (8-hr TWA):** CO = **50 ppm**; SO₂ = **2 ppm**; NO₂ = **3 ppm**.
- [x] **Five hazard classes:** thermal, chemical, mechanical (blow-up), physical (dust), ergonomic (heat stress).
- [x] **Mandatory PPE for cupola operators:** aluminized suit, face shield, leggings, leather gloves, FFP2/3 respirator.
- [x] **Critical safety interlock:** Tuyere cooling-water flow switch → automatic blower cut-off.
- [x] **Emergency control:** Drop-door release mechanism for instant sand-pit emptying during blow-up.
- [x] **Counter-current principle** = solid down, gas up → maximizes LMTD, lowers stack temperature to 150 – 200 °C.
- [x] **Typical air supply** for a small cupola = **140 – 160 m³/min** (0.8 t/hr melting rate).
- [x] **Most dangerous single failure mode** = water in tuyere / wet charge → H₂ detonation.
- [x] **Always state the chemical equation** when explaining any hazard — examiners award the **highest mark band** for equation-based answers.

> [!TIP]
> **Final exam tip:** In a 14-mark question, structure the answer in **4–5 paragraphs** with a **diagram**, **two formulas**, and **one engineering control** per hazard. This maps perfectly to the KTU 2024 Scheme **Course Outcome (CO4): "Apply engineering controls to mitigate safety risks in industrial furnaces."** A well-structured answer always fetches the **top mark band (12–14)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 4
