<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚒️ Crucibles – KTU OESFT725 Module 4 Study Notes

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **crucible** is a high-temperature-resistant container, typically made of refractory ceramic, graphite, clay-graphite, silicon carbide, or metal alloys, used for holding and melting metals, alloys, or other materials at elevated temperatures (often between **600 °C and 2000 °C**) in foundries, metallurgical laboratories, and engineering industries.

In the context of **Safety in Engineering Industry**, crucibles are classified as **Class A thermal hazards** because they routinely handle molten metals and operate near or above the auto-ignition temperatures of most combustible materials used in industrial workplaces.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker for Metals":**
> Think of a crucible as the industrial cousin of a kitchen pressure cooker — except instead of steaming vegetables, it is busy melting aluminium at 660 °C or copper at 1085 °C. The walls of the crucible are like the thick, heat-resistant walls of a pressure cooker: they contain the chaos inside (the molten metal) and prevent it from spilling out and burning the cook (the operator). Just as a pressure cooker would explode if its walls were thin, a crucible with compromised refractories can crack, splash molten metal, and cause catastrophic burns. The **tongs used to lift a crucible** are like the long handles of a frying pan — they keep your hand far from the heat source.

### 1.3 Why Crucibles Matter in Industrial Safety

> [!WARNING]
> **Industry Statistic (Foundry Safety Council Indicative Data):**
> Molten metal splash incidents account for nearly **18–22%** of all severe burn injuries in metal-casting facilities worldwide. Crucible failure is one of the top three root causes cited in foundry accident investigation reports.

The crucible is essentially the **last line of containment** between a pool of molten metal at temperatures exceeding the boiling point of water many times over, and the human operator. A failure here translates directly to life-threatening injuries, fire outbreaks, and equipment loss.

### 1.4 Industrial Standards & Reference Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key Reference Temperatures (Bolded for Quick Recall):**
> - **Melting point of Aluminium:** 660 °C
> - **Melting point of Copper:** 1085 °C
> - **Melting point of Cast Iron:** 1150 – 1300 °C
> - **Melting point of Steel:** 1370 – 1510 °C
> - **Maximum service temperature of clay-graphite crucibles:** ≈ 1600 °C
> - **Maximum service temperature of silicon carbide crucibles:** ≈ 1800 °C
> - **Maximum service temperature of pure alumina (Al₂O₃) crucibles:** ≈ 1900 °C

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Crucibles (KTU 2024 Module 4 Expectation)

### 2.1.1 Based on Manufacturing Material

> **1. Clay-Graphite Crucibles**
> - **Composition:** ~ 50% fireclay + 50% flake graphite + bonding agents (silicon carbide additions optional).
> - **Why this works:** Graphite has very high thermal conductivity (≈ 100 W/m·K) and resists wetting by molten metals; clay provides structural rigidity and shape retention.
> - **Maximum service temperature:** ≈ **1600 °C**
> - **Typical use:** Non-ferrous melting — brass, bronze, aluminium.

> **2. Silicon Carbide (SiC) Crucibles**
> - **Composition:** Bonded silicon carbide with carbon and ceramic binders.
> - **Why this works:** SiC has exceptional thermal-shock resistance and high thermal conductivity (≈ 120 W/m·K). It oxidises slowly to form a protective SiO₂ skin.
> - **Maximum service temperature:** ≈ **1800 °C**
> - **Typical use:** Heavy-duty ferrous and non-ferrous foundries.

> **3. Alumina (Al₂O₃) Crucibles**
> - **Composition:** Sintered high-purity aluminium oxide (≥ 95% Al₂O₃).
> - **Why this works:** Excellent chemical inertness; resists attack by acidic and basic slags.
> - **Maximum service temperature:** ≈ **1900 °C**
> - **Typical use:** Laboratory-grade analytical chemistry, rare-earth metallurgy.

> **4. Zirconia (ZrO₂) Crucibles**
> - **Composition:** Stabilised zirconium dioxide (often with Y₂O₃ stabiliser).
> - **Why this works:** Highest refractory temperature among common oxides; low thermal conductivity protects the operator.
> - **Maximum service temperature:** ≈ **2400 °C**
> - **Typical use:** Specialty super-alloy and platinum-group metal melting.

> **5. Cast Iron / Steel Crucibles**
> - **Composition:** Cast iron or fabricated steel.
> - **Why this works:** Mechanically tough; can be repaired by welding.
> - **Limitation:** They *themselves* melt at typical operating temperatures, so they are only used for low-melting metals (zinc, lead, tin) or as **outer protective shells** holding a refractory liner.
> - **Maximum service temperature:** ≈ 1100 °C (limited by base-metal softening).

> **6. Graphite (Pure) Crucibles**
> - **Composition:** High-density pressed graphite, sometimes with clay binder.
> - **Why this works:** Outstanding thermal-shock resistance and non-wetting behaviour.
> - **Limitation:** Oxidises rapidly above ≈ 400 °C in air unless protected.
> - **Typical use:** Vacuum or inert-atmosphere furnaces; induction melting.

### 2.1.2 Based on Heating Method

> **1. Tilting / Hand-Pour Crucibles** — lifted and poured manually using tongs.
> **2. Stationary Crucibles** — used inside pit furnaces; metal is ladled out separately.
> **3. Induction Crucibles** — act as the susceptor for eddy-current heating (typically silicon-carbide/graphite grades).
> **4. Resistance-heated Crucibles** — embedded in electric resistance furnaces.
> **5. Gas-fired Crucibles** — used in coke-fired or natural-gas tilting furnaces.

### 2.2 Engineering Properties a Crucible MUST Have

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Five Pillars" of Crucible Material Properties:**
> 1. **High Refractoriness** — must withstand operating temperature with a safety margin of ≈ 200–300 °C above the molten metal.
> 2. **Thermal-Shock Resistance** — must survive rapid heating/cooling cycles without cracking.
> 3. **Chemical Inertness** — must not react with the molten metal or the slag layer.
> 4. **Mechanical Strength at Temperature** — must support the hydrostatic pressure of molten metal at 1000 °C+.
> 5. **Oxidation & Erosion Resistance** — especially for graphite grades exposed to flue gases.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the standard KTU 2024 formula set for thermal/crucible sizing problems. Memorise the symbol, the unit, and the *meaning* of each.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Variables | Typical Units |
|---|----------|----------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | Volumetric capacity of a cylindrical crucible | $V = \pi r^{2} h$ | $r$ = internal radius; $h$ = internal height | m³ |
| 2 | Mass of molten metal held | $m = \rho \cdot V$ | $\rho$ = density of molten metal | kg |
| 3 | Mass of metal held (cylindrical) | $m = \rho \cdot \pi r^{2} h$ | — | kg |
| 4 | Hydrostatic pressure at crucible base | $P = \rho g h$ | $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | Pa |
| 5 | Wall-thrust force on side wall | $F = P \cdot A = \rho g h \cdot 2 r h$ | $A$ = side-wall area | N |
| 6 | Energy required to raise metal to melting point | $Q_{\text{sensible}} = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T$ | $c_{p}$ = specific heat; $\Delta T$ = temperature rise | J |
| 7 | Latent heat to melt the metal | $Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot L_{f}$ | $L_{f}$ = latent heat of fusion | J |
| 8 | Total heat to melt metal from room temperature | $Q_{\text{total}} = m \left( c_{p} \Delta T + L_{f} \right)$ | — | J |
| 9 | Heat required (in kWh) | $E_{\text{kWh}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}}$ | — | kWh |
| 10 | Melting rate (mass/time) for a given power input | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_{\text{net}} \cdot \eta}{L_{f}}$ | $P_{\text{net}}$ = power delivered to metal; $\eta$ = thermal efficiency | kg/s |
| 11 | Steady-state heat conduction through crucible wall | $q = \dfrac{k A \Delta T}{x}$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity; $x$ = wall thickness | W |
| 12 | Thermal resistance of wall | $R_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{x}{k A}$ | — | K/W |
| 13 | Rate of oxidation of graphite (mass loss) | $\dot{m}_{\text{ox}} = k_{\text{ox}} \cdot A \cdot e^{-E_{a}/RT}$ | $E_{a}$ = activation energy; $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K | kg/s |
| 14 | Time-to-melt (charging from cold) | $t_{m} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{in}} \cdot \eta}$ | $P_{\text{in}}$ = rated furnace input power | seconds |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** In Part A questions, examiners often give you a molten metal density (e.g., $\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2400$ kg/m³) and ask for *m* from *V*. In Part B numericals, the most common setup is a *time-to-melt* problem combining equations (8) and (14). Practice both.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The crucible system is the **thermal engine room** of every foundry, jewellery shop, and metallurgical laboratory. Beyond just "holding metal," crucible engineering directly affects:

- **Energy efficiency of a foundry** — better conductivity → faster melt cycles → lower kWh/kg of metal.
- **Metal purity** — chemical inertness prevents contamination of high-grade alloys.
- **Operator safety** — proper wall thickness prevents burn-through accidents.
- **Process repeatability** — uniform heating produces consistent castings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Molten Metal in a Cylindrical Crucible

We want a closed-form expression for the **mass of molten metal a crucible can hold** given its internal dimensions and the density of the metal.

### Step 1 — Start with the geometry of the internal cavity

A standard crucible has an internal cavity that is approximately a right circular cylinder (the bottom is flat or slightly rounded; we approximate as flat for engineering analysis).

$$
V = \text{(cross-sectional area)} \times \text{(height)}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the area of a circle

$$
V = \pi r^{2} \cdot h
$$

### Step 3 — Relate volume to mass through density

By definition, **density** is mass per unit volume:

$$
\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad m = \rho \cdot V
$$

### Step 4 — Combine the two results

Substitute equation (Step 2) into equation (Step 3):

$$
m = \rho \cdot \pi r^{2} h
$$

### Step 5 — Engineering interpretation

> This is the **master mass-capacity equation** for cylindrical crucibles. If a foundry plans to melt 100 kg of aluminium (ρ ≈ 2400 kg/m³), the required internal volume is $V = m/\rho \approx 0.0417$ m³ = 41.7 L. A crucible of radius **0.15 m** must then be at least **0.59 m** deep internally.

### Worked Numerical (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** A silicon-carbide crucible has an internal diameter of **30 cm** and an internal depth of **45 cm**. It is filled to **85%** of its capacity with molten brass (ρ = 8800 kg/m³). Find the mass of brass held.

**Solution Steps:**

Step 1 — Convert all dimensions to SI (metres).

$$
d = 30 \text{ cm} = 0.30 \text{ m}, \quad r = 0.15 \text{ m}, \quad h = 0.45 \text{ m}
$$

Step 2 — Full internal volume.

$$
V_{\text{full}} = \pi r^{2} h = \pi (0.15)^{2} (0.45)
$$

Step 3 — Evaluate.

$$
V_{\text{full}} = \pi \cdot 0.0225 \cdot 0.45 = 0.03181 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

Step 4 — Actual volume filled (85%).

$$
V = 0.85 \times 0.03181 = 0.02704 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

Step 5 — Mass of brass.

$$
m = \rho \cdot V = 8800 \times 0.02704 = 237.96 \text{ kg}
$$

> **Answer:** The crucible holds approximately **238 kg of molten brass**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Heat Required to Melt a Charge

We derive the energy that the furnace must deliver to take a cold metal charge from ambient temperature to the fully molten state.

### Step 1 — Identify the two thermal stages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stage 1 — Sensible heating:** Raise the solid metal from ambient $T_{0}$ to its melting point $T_{m}$.
> **Stage 2 — Latent heating:** Convert the solid at $T_{m}$ to liquid at $T_{m}$ (no temperature change, but energy is absorbed as *latent heat of fusion*).

### Step 2 — Write the energy for Stage 1

$$
Q_{1} = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{m} - T_{0})
$$

### Step 3 — Write the energy for Stage 2

$$
Q_{2} = m \cdot L_{f}
$$

### Step 4 — Sum the two stages

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = m \, c_{p} \, (T_{m} - T_{0}) + m \, L_{f}
$$

### Step 5 — Factor out m (clean engineering form)

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = m \left[ c_{p} (T_{m} - T_{0}) + L_{f} \right]
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to kWh (for utility billing / KTU numericals)

$$
E_{\text{kWh}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}}
$$

### Worked Numerical (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** Melt **50 kg of aluminium** from **25 °C** to its melting point. Given: $c_{p} = 900$ J/(kg·K), $L_{f} = 3.97 \times 10^{5}$ J/kg, $T_{m} = 660$ °C, $T_{0} = 25$ °C.

Step 1 — Temperature rise.

$$
\Delta T = T_{m} - T_{0} = 660 - 25 = 635 \text{ K}
$$

Step 2 — Sensible heat.

$$
Q_{1} = 50 \times 900 \times 635 = 2.8575 \times 10^{7} \text{ J}
$$

Step 3 — Latent heat.

$$
Q_{2} = 50 \times 3.97 \times 10^{5} = 1.985 \times 10^{7} \text{ J}
$$

Step 4 — Total heat.

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 2.8575 \times 10^{7} + 1.985 \times 10^{7} = 4.8425 \times 10^{7} \text{ J}
$$

Step 5 — Convert to kWh.

$$
E_{\text{kWh}} = \dfrac{4.8425 \times 10^{7}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} \approx 13.45 \text{ kWh}
$$

> **Answer:** Approximately **13.45 kWh** of thermal energy is required.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time-to-Melt under Finite Furnace Power

### Step 1 — Recognise the energy balance

If a furnace delivers a constant electrical/chemical power $P_{\text{in}}$ with overall thermal efficiency $\eta$, the **useful power** reaching the metal is $P_{\text{net}} = \eta \cdot P_{\text{in}}$.

### Step 2 — Set useful power equal to the rate of energy storage in the metal

$$
P_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{dQ_{\text{total}}}{dt} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P_{\text{in}} \cdot \eta = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{t_{m}}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for melting time

$$
t_{m} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{in}} \cdot \eta}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Q expression

$$
t_{m} = \dfrac{m \left[ c_{p} (T_{m} - T_{0}) + L_{f} \right]}{P_{\text{in}} \cdot \eta}
$$

### Worked Numerical

> **Problem:** A 50 kg aluminium charge is to be melted using a 20 kW electric resistance furnace with 60% thermal efficiency. From the previous example, $Q_{\text{total}} = 4.8425 \times 10^{7}$ J. Find the **time to melt**.

Step 1 — Compute the useful power.

$$
P_{\text{net}} = 0.60 \times 20 \times 10^{3} = 1.2 \times 10^{4} \text{ W}
$$

Step 2 — Time to melt.

$$
t_{m} = \dfrac{4.8425 \times 10^{7}}{1.2 \times 10^{4}} \approx 4035 \text{ s}
$$

Step 3 — Convert to hours.

$$
t_{m} = \dfrac{4035}{3600} \approx 1.12 \text{ h} \approx 67 \text{ minutes}
$$

> **Answer:** The aluminium charge will take about **67 minutes** to melt.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hydrostatic Pressure on Crucible Base

The molten metal exerts pressure on the crucible bottom. This pressure determines the **minimum compressive strength** the crucible material must possess.

### Step 1 — Start with the hydrostatic pressure formula

$$
P = \rho \, g \, h
$$

### Step 2 — Interpret h

Here $h$ is the **depth of the molten metal** above the point in question. At the base, $h$ is the full internal height of the metal column.

### Step 3 — Compute the total thrust force on the base

$$
F = P \cdot A_{\text{base}} = \rho \, g \, h \cdot \pi r^{2}
$$

### Worked Numerical

> **Problem:** A crucible holds 45 cm of molten aluminium (ρ = 2400 kg/m³). Find the pressure at the base and the total force on a 30 cm-diameter base.

Step 1 — Pressure.

$$
P = 2400 \times 9.81 \times 0.45 = 10\,595 \text{ Pa} \approx 10.6 \text{ kPa}
$$

Step 2 — Base area.

$$
A = \pi (0.15)^{2} = 0.0707 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Step 3 — Total force.

$$
F = 10\,595 \times 0.0707 \approx 749 \text{ N}
$$

> **Answer:** Pressure ≈ **10.6 kPa**, total thrust on base ≈ **749 N**. This is small in absolute terms, but it is concentrated on a ceramic wall that may already be weakened by thermal gradients.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Crucible

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 "Compare-and-Justify" Style Table (Use `vert` for absolute values to avoid breaking markdown):**

| Selection Criterion | Clay-Graphite | Silicon Carbide | Alumina | Zirconia | Cast Iron |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max service T (°C) | 1600 | 1800 | 1900 | 2400 | 1100 |
| Thermal-shock resistance | Good | Excellent | Fair | Poor | Excellent |
| Chemical inertness | Good | Very Good | Excellent | Excellent | Poor |
| Cost (relative) | Low | Medium | Medium-High | Very High | Low |
| Typical melt | Brass, Bronze, Al | Steel, Cast Iron | Lab analytical | Pt-group metals | Zn, Pb, Sn |
| Risk of burn-through | Medium | Low | Low | Very Low | High (if overheated) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Crucible Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps how a crucible interacts with its surrounding subsystems (furnace, lifting gear, operator) — a typical KTU Module 4 architecture-flow question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    powerInput[Power Input Source]:::source
    furnaceShell[Refractory Furnace Lining]:::furnace
    crucibleBody[Crucible Body refractory walls]:::crucible
    moltenMetal[Pool of Molten Metal 600 to 1800 C]:::hot
    slagLayer[Slag Layer floating flux]:::slag
    tongGrip[Tong and Shank Lifting System]:::tool
    operator[Trained Operator wearing PPE]:::human
    pouringLip[Pouring Lip discharge spout]:::output
    ingotMould[Receiving Ingot or Ladle]:::output

    powerInput --> furnaceShell
    furnaceShell --> crucibleBody
    crucibleBody --> moltenMetal
    moltenMetal --> slagLayer
    tongGrip --> crucibleBody
    operator --> tongGrip
    moltenMetal --> pouringLip
    pouringLip --> ingotMould

    classDef source fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#1f2937
    classDef furnace fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#1f2937
    classDef crucible fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#1f2937
    classDef hot fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#1f2937
    classDef slag fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#1f2937
    classDef tool fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#1f2937
    classDef human fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#1f2937
    classDef output fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#1f2937
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows trace the **flow of thermal energy** (Power → Furnace → Crucible → Molten Metal) and the **flow of mechanical action** (Operator → Tongs → Crucible → Pouring Lip → Ingot Mould). The **Slag Layer** floats on top because it is less dense than the molten metal and acts as a protective blanket, reducing oxidation and absorbing impurities.

## 4.2 Crucible Failure-Mode Safety Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    coldCharge[Cold Metal Charge]:::input
    rapidHeat[Rapid Heating]:::cause
    thermalShock[Thermal Shock]:::cause
    crackInitiation[Crack Initiation]:::cause
    moltenLeak[Molten Metal Leak]:::hazard
    burnInjury[Operator Burn Injury]:::outcome
    fireOutbreak[Fire Outbreak]:::outcome
    equipDamage[Equipment Damage]:::outcome

    coldCharge --> rapidHeat
    rapidHeat --> thermalShock
    thermalShock --> crackInitiation
    crackInitiation --> moltenLeak
    moltenLeak --> burnInjury
    moltenLeak --> fireOutbreak
    moltenLeak --> equipDamage

    classDef input fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#1f2937
    classDef cause fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#1f2937
    classDef hazard fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#1f2937
    classDef outcome fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#1f2937
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **safety-critical path**. Each box is a "step" that converts a routine foundry action into a catastrophe. The standard mitigation is to **insert a "Preheating Stage"** between Cold Charge and Rapid Heating — i.e., bring the crucible up to ≈ 200 °C over 30–60 minutes before applying full furnace power.

## 4.3 Crucible Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Start Selection]:::start
    q1{Melting point of metal?}:::decision
    lowT[Below 500 C]:::branch
    midT[500 to 1100 C]:::branch
    highT[Above 1100 C]:::branch
    veryHighT[Above 1900 C]:::branch
    chemResist{Is chemical inertness critical?}:::decision
    aluminaChoice[Use Alumina Crucible]:::choice
    sicChoice[Use Silicon Carbide Crucible]:::choice
    clayGraphiteChoice[Use Clay Graphite Crucible]:::choice
    ironChoice[Use Cast Iron or Steel Crucible]:::choice
    zirconiaChoice[Use Zirconia Crucible]:::choice

    start --> q1
    q1 --> lowT --> ironChoice
    q1 --> midT --> clayGraphiteChoice
    q1 --> highT --> chemResist
    chemResist --> aluminaChoice
    chemResist --> sicChoice
    q1 --> veryHighT --> zirconiaChoice

    classDef start fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#1f2937
    classDef decision fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#1f2937
    classDef branch fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#1f2937
    classDef choice fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#1f2937
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **logic flowchart** a KTU examiner expects when asking a *compare-and-justify* question. The four branches cover the four most common industrial scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, Module 4]**
> **Q1.** Define a crucible. List any **four** essential properties a crucible material must possess for safe industrial use.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A crucible is a refractory container used to hold and melt metals or other materials at very high temperatures in foundries and metallurgical processes.
>
> Four essential properties:
> 1. **High refractoriness** — must withstand temperatures 200–300 °C above the melting point of the charge.
> 2. **Thermal-shock resistance** — must survive rapid heating/cooling without cracking.
> 3. **Chemical inertness** — must not react with molten metal or slag.
> 4. **Mechanical strength at high temperature** — must support the hydrostatic load of molten metal.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, Module 4]**
> **Q2.** Why must a new clay-graphite crucible be **pre-heated slowly** before being charged with molten metal? Mention any two consequences of skipping this step.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A new clay-graphite crucible contains absorbed moisture and thermal stresses from manufacturing. **Pre-heating slowly** (typically up to ≈ 200 °C over 30–60 min) drives out moisture and allows the walls to expand uniformly, preventing thermal shock.
>
> **Consequences of skipping pre-heating:**
> 1. **Steam explosion / crucible cracking** when moisture turns to steam inside the porous refractory walls.
> 2. **Thermal-shock fracture** when cold walls meet molten metal, leading to metal spillage and severe burn injury to the operator.

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, Module 4]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

> **(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **functional architecture** of a crucible-based melting system. Label the furnace shell, crucible body, molten metal, slag layer, tong system, and pouring lip clearly. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A crucible of internal diameter **25 cm** and internal height **40 cm** is filled to **80%** with molten copper (ρ = 8960 kg/m³). Find (i) the mass of copper held, (ii) the hydrostatic pressure at the base, and (iii) the thrust force on the base. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Provide a **labelled block diagram** showing the following six subsystems, each with arrows indicating the flow of energy, materials, or control:

1. **Power Input Source** (electrical / gas) — feeds energy into the furnace.
2. **Refractory Furnace Lining** — contains and reflects heat back to the crucible.
3. **Crucible Body** (refractory walls) — holds the molten metal.
4. **Molten Metal Pool** — the actual charge being melted.
5. **Slag Layer** — floats on top, protects metal from oxidation, absorbs impurities.
6. **Tong and Shank Lifting System** — operated by a trained human, lifts the crucible for pouring.
7. **Pouring Lip** — directs molten metal into the receiving ingot mould or ladle.

(Use the **Block-Level Functional Architecture diagram** from Section 4.1 above as the reference. Award 1 mark per correctly labelled subsystem, plus 1 mark for showing direction of energy/material flow.)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** d = 0.25 m → r = 0.125 m; h = 0.40 m; fill fraction = 0.80; ρ = 8960 kg/m³; g = 9.81 m/s².

**(i) Mass of copper:**

Step 1 — Full internal volume.

$$
V_{\text{full}} = \pi r^{2} h = \pi (0.125)^{2} (0.40) = 0.01963 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

Step 2 — Actual filled volume.

$$
V = 0.80 \times 0.01963 = 0.01571 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

Step 3 — Mass.

$$
m = \rho \cdot V = 8960 \times 0.01571 = 140.7 \text{ kg}
$$

**(ii) Hydrostatic pressure at base:**

Step 4 — Effective metal height.

$$
h_{\text{eff}} = 0.80 \times 0.40 = 0.32 \text{ m}
$$

Step 5 — Pressure.

$$
P = \rho g h_{\text{eff}} = 8960 \times 9.81 \times 0.32 = 28\,128 \text{ Pa} \approx 28.13 \text{ kPa}
$$

**(iii) Thrust force on base:**

Step 6 — Base area.

$$
A = \pi r^{2} = \pi (0.125)^{2} = 0.04909 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

Step 7 — Force.

$$
F = P \cdot A = 28\,128 \times 0.04909 \approx 1381 \text{ N}
$$

> **Final Answers:**
> (i) **m ≈ 140.7 kg**
> (ii) **P ≈ 28.13 kPa**
> (iii) **F ≈ 1381 N**

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

> **(a)** Compare the **four main types of crucibles** (clay-graphite, silicon carbide, alumina, and zirconia) with respect to (i) maximum service temperature, (ii) thermal-shock resistance, (iii) chemical inertness, and (iv) typical industrial application. Use a **comparison table** in your answer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A foundry melts **75 kg of brass** (c_p = 380 J/(kg·K), L_f = 2.0 × 10⁵ J/kg) from **30 °C** to its melting point of **930 °C** in a furnace rated at **30 kW** with an overall efficiency of **55%**. Find (i) the total heat required in kJ, (ii) the time to melt in minutes, and (iii) the **pre-heat** time that should be added as a safety margin if it is 25% of the melting time. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Property | Clay-Graphite | Silicon Carbide | Alumina (Al₂O₃) | Zirconia (ZrO₂) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max service T (°C) | 1600 | 1800 | 1900 | 2400 |
| Thermal-shock resistance | Good | Excellent | Fair | Poor |
| Chemical inertness | Good | Very Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| Typical application | Brass, Bronze, Aluminium | Steel, Cast Iron, heavy-duty ferrous | Laboratory analysis, rare-earth alloys | Platinum-group metals, super-alloys |

**Marking scheme:** 4 marks for the table (1 mark per property, 0.25 per cell), 3 marks for a written justification of at least two distinguishing choices (e.g., why SiC over clay-graphite for steel melting, why zirconia for platinum-group metals).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** m = 75 kg, c_p = 380 J/(kg·K), L_f = 2.0 × 10⁵ J/kg, T₀ = 30 °C, T_m = 930 °C, P_in = 30 kW, η = 0.55.

**(i) Total heat required in kJ:**

Step 1 — Temperature rise.

$$
\Delta T = 930 - 30 = 900 \text{ K}
$$

Step 2 — Sensible heat.

$$
Q_{1} = m \, c_{p} \, \Delta T = 75 \times 380 \times 900 = 2.565 \times 10^{7} \text{ J} = 25\,650 \text{ kJ}
$$

Step 3 — Latent heat.

$$
Q_{2} = m \cdot L_{f} = 75 \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} = 1.5 \times 10^{7} \text{ J} = 15\,000 \text{ kJ}
$$

Step 4 — Total.

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 25\,650 + 15\,000 = 40\,650 \text{ kJ}
$$

**(ii) Time to melt in minutes:**

Step 5 — Useful power.

$$
P_{\text{net}} = \eta \cdot P_{\text{in}} = 0.55 \times 30 \times 10^{3} = 16\,500 \text{ W} = 16.5 \text{ kW} = 16\,500 \text{ kJ/s}
$$

Step 6 — Time.

$$
t_{m} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{40\,650 \text{ kJ}}{16.5 \text{ kJ/s}} = 2463.6 \text{ s}
$$

Step 7 — Convert to minutes.

$$
t_{m} = \dfrac{2463.6}{60} \approx 41.06 \text{ minutes}
$$

**(iii) Pre-heat safety margin (25% of melt time):**

Step 8 — Pre-heat time.

$$
t_{\text{preheat}} = 0.25 \times 41.06 \approx 10.27 \text{ minutes}
$$

> **Final Answers:**
> (i) **Q_total ≈ 40 650 kJ** (or 4.065 × 10⁷ J)
> (ii) **t_m ≈ 41.06 minutes**
> (iii) **t_preheat ≈ 10.27 minutes** (recommend a 10–11 minute controlled pre-heat ramp before full power)

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Crucible Numericals (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert cm to m** — losing 1 full mark. Always write down unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Using solid metal density instead of molten metal density** — the difference is small (≈ 5–10%) but the KTU examiner will deduct a mark for not stating which one you used.
> 3. **Mixing up mass and weight** — the question usually asks for *mass in kg*; giving weight in N loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the latent-heat term in total-heat questions** — this is the single most common error. Always include both $c_p \Delta T$ and $L_f$ unless the question explicitly says "sensible heat only."
> 5. **Ignoring thermal efficiency in time-to-melt questions** — using $P_{\text{in}}$ directly instead of $\eta \cdot P_{\text{in}}$ over-estimates the melt rate.
> 6. **Not labelling diagrams** — in the 7-mark architecture questions, an un-labelled block diagram earns 0 marks for that sub-part. Always label every block.
> 7. **Stating the answer without units** — KTU strictly requires SI units; missing units can cost 0.5–1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and Stick on Your Wall):**
>
> **Definitions**
> - ☐ Crucible = refractory container for melting metals at 600–2000 °C.
> - ☐ Refractoriness = ability to withstand high temperature without deforming.
> - ☐ Thermal shock = failure due to rapid temperature change.
>
> **Crucible Types to Memorise**
> - ☐ Clay-graphite (≤ 1600 °C, non-ferrous)
> - ☐ Silicon carbide (≤ 1800 °C, ferrous + non-ferrous)
> - ☐ Alumina (≤ 1900 °C, lab use)
> - ☐ Zirconia (≤ 2400 °C, super-alloys)
> - ☐ Cast iron / steel (≤ 1100 °C, low-melt metals only)
> - ☐ Pure graphite (inert atmosphere only)
>
> **Master Formulas (Commit to Memory)**
> - ☐ $V = \pi r^{2} h$
> - ☐ $m = \rho \cdot V$
> - ☐ $P = \rho g h$
> - ☐ $Q_{\text{total}} = m [c_p (T_m - T_0) + L_f]$
> - ☐ $t_m = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{in}} \cdot \eta}$
>
> **Safety-Critical Items**
> - ☐ Always pre-heat a new/repaired crucible (≈ 200 °C, 30–60 min).
> - ☐ Use proper tongs — never lift by hand.
> - ☐ PPE: face shield, aluminised heat-suit, leather spats, safety boots.
> - ☐ Inspect for cracks before every heat.
> - ☐ Keep slag layer intact — it protects the metal and the crucible wall.
> - ☐ Never add cold/wet charge to a hot crucible (steam explosion risk).
> - ☐ Ensure good ventilation to remove toxic fumes (especially zinc, lead).
>
> **Numbers to Remember**
> - ☐ ρ(Al) ≈ 2400 kg/m³; ρ(Cu) ≈ 8960 kg/m³; ρ(Brass) ≈ 8500–8800 kg/m³; ρ(Fe) ≈ 7200 kg/m³
> - ☐ T_m(Al) = 660 °C; T_m(Cu) = 1085 °C; T_m(Steel) ≈ 1500 °C
> - ☐ 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ovens

# 🔥 OVENS — Industrial Safety Perspective

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Industrial Oven** is a thermally insulated, enclosed engineering equipment designed to provide controlled heating to materials, components, or substances for the purpose of achieving specific physical, chemical, or metallurgical transformations such as drying, curing, baking, annealing, hardening, tempering, ageing, or heat treatment — without the direct contact of combustion gases on the charge.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (As per OESFT725 Module 4):**  
> An oven is a heated enclosure used in industry for thermal processing, drying, or heat treatment operations where the products of combustion (in case of fuel-fired ovens) are kept separate from the work being heated.

### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an **industrial oven** as a "giant controlled kitchen oven on steroids." Just like your domestic microwave or OTG oven maintains a precise temperature to bake a cake uniformly, an industrial oven regulates very high temperatures (often between **100 °C and 1200 °C**) to transform raw materials — but at massive scales and under strict safety conditions.

**Real-world analogy:** Imagine a hospital incubator for newborn babies — it maintains a precise, gentle, controlled environment. Similarly, an industrial oven maintains a precise thermal environment, but for metal parts, paints, chemicals, or food products, where the slightest deviation can cause fire, explosion, or quality failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** A **Furnace** differs from an **Oven** primarily because in a furnace, the work (charge) is often exposed to combustion gases, whereas in an oven, the heat source (burner or electric elements) is isolated from the work by a heat exchanger or radiant tubes. Operating temperatures in ovens are generally below **750 °C**, while furnaces operate at higher temperatures.

### 🔑 Standard Operating Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Temperature Range | **100 °C – 1200 °C** |
| Maximum Safe Surface Temperature (External) | **65 °C** |
| Standard Reference (Indian) | **IS 3168 : 1981** |
| Standard Reference (Indian) | **IS 5455 : 1969** |
| Standard Reference (International) | **NFPA 86** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🏗️ Classification of Industrial Ovens (Why & How)

Industrial ovens are classified on the basis of:

**A. Mode of Heating**
1. **Gas-Fired Ovens** — Use Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG), Natural Gas (NG), or Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG).
2. **Oil-Fired Ovens** — Use Light Diesel Oil (LDO), High Speed Diesel (HSD), or furnace oil.
3. **Electrically-Heated Ovens** — Use resistance heating elements, infrared emitters, or induction coils.
4. **Steam-Heated Ovens** — Use steam coils as the heat transfer medium.

**B. Mode of Operation**

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch Oven** | Charge loaded, processed, then unloaded (cyclic). | Paint curing, annealing small parts. |
| **Continuous Oven** | Material moves continuously through the heated zone. | Powder coating, food drying, baking. |
| **Conveyor Oven** | Uses belt conveyors for throughput. | Annealing, brazing, drying. |
| **Tunnel Oven** | Long horizontal chamber; wagons pass through zones. | Ceramic firing, heavy components. |
| **Rotary Kiln Oven** | Cylindrical rotating drum. | Cement, mineral processing. |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind classification:** Different industrial processes demand different thermal uniformity, throughput, and atmosphere control. Selecting the wrong oven type leads to product rejection, fire risk, and energy wastage.

### ⚙️ Core Constructional Features

Every industrial oven consists of:

1. **Outer Shell** — Made of structural steel with mounting provisions.
2. **Insulation Layer** — Ceramic fibre, rock wool, calcium silicate, or vermiculite to minimize heat loss.
3. **Inner Lining (Refractory)** — High-alumina bricks, castable refractories to withstand high temperatures.
4. **Heating Source** — Burners (gas/oil) or electric heating coils.
5. **Heat Distribution System** — Fans, baffles, ducts for forced convection uniformity.
6. **Work Chamber** — Where the charge is loaded.
7. **Doors / Sealing Arrangement** — To prevent heat leakage.
8. **Ventilation / Exhaust System** — Removes moisture, vapours, fumes.
9. **Control & Safety Instrumentation** — Thermocouples, thermostats, pressure switches, flame scanners.

### 🧠 Engineering Principles Behind Heat Transfer in Ovens

Three primary modes of heat transfer occur inside an oven:

1. **Conduction** — Heat flows through solid walls, racks, and trays.
2. **Convection** — Heat carried by circulating hot air (forced or natural).
3. **Radiation** — Heat transfer by electromagnetic waves; dominates above **600 °C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** At temperatures below **200 °C**, convection dominates; between **200 °C–500 °C**, both convection and radiation are significant; above **600 °C**, radiation becomes the dominant mode of heat transfer.

---

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat required to raise temperature of mass $m$ | Joules (J) |
| 2 | $Q = m \cdot L$ | Latent heat for phase change | Joules (J) |
| 3 | $Q_{total} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1) + m \cdot L_v$ | Total heat for heating + drying (moisture removal) | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{supplied}} \times 100$ | Thermal efficiency of oven | Percentage (%) |
| 5 | $Q_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{mean}$ | Heat loss through walls (Fourier's law) | W (Watts) |
| 6 | $Q_{radiation} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Stefan-Boltzmann radiative heat transfer | W |
| 7 | $\dot{Q}_{convection} = h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_{\infty})$ | Newton's Law of Cooling | W |
| 8 | $P_{fan} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta p}{\eta_{fan}}$ | Power required for recirculation fan | W |
| 9 | $t = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{\dot{Q}_{input}}$ | Time to heat charge | Seconds (s) |
| 10 | $CFM = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | Cubic Feet per Minute for ventilation | ft³/min |

**Where:**
- $m$ = mass of material (kg)
- $c_p$ = specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K or °C)
- $L$ or $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/kg)
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = surface area (m²)
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity (dimensionless)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant $= 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $\Delta p$ = pressure drop (Pa)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Reminder (Markdown Safety):** All absolute value notations inside formulas have been written as `\vert x \vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table structure.

---

### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Oven Application |
|---|---|
| Automotive | Paint curing, powder coating, drying |
| Aerospace | Heat treatment of turbine blades |
| Food Processing | Baking, roasting, dehydration |
| Pharmaceuticals | Sterilization, drying of granules |
| Electronics | Curing of PCBs, drying of laminates |
| Ceramics / Glass | Pre-heating, sintering |
| Metallurgy | Annealing, tempering, ageing |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔢 Derivation 1: Total Heat Required in a Batch Drying Oven

**Problem Setup:** Calculate the total heat energy required to dry and heat **500 kg** of a metal component in an industrial oven, where:
- Initial Temperature $T_1 = 30 \, °C$
- Final Temperature $T_2 = 250 \, °C$
- Specific heat $c_p = 500 \, J/(kg \cdot K)$
- Moisture to be evaporated = **5 %** of total mass = $25 \, kg$
- Latent heat of vaporization $L_v = 2.26 \times 10^6 \, J/kg$

**Step 1 — Sensible Heat Required to Raise Temperature of Charge**

$$Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Substituting the values:

$$Q_{sensible} = 500 \times 500 \times (250 - 30)$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 500 \times 500 \times 220$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 55{,}000{,}000 \, J = 55 \, MJ$$

**Step 2 — Latent Heat Required to Evaporate Moisture**

$$Q_{latent} = m_{moisture} \cdot L_v$$

$$Q_{latent} = 25 \times 2.26 \times 10^6$$

$$Q_{latent} = 56{,}500{,}000 \, J = 56.5 \, MJ$$

**Step 3 — Total Heat Energy Required**

$$Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent}$$

$$Q_{total} = 55 + 56.5 = 111.5 \, MJ$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 111.5 \, MJ}$$

---

### 🔢 Derivation 2: Heat Loss Through Oven Walls (Steady-State Conduction)

**Problem Setup:** A continuous oven has wall dimensions **3 m (height) × 5 m (width)**. The wall is constructed with:
- Inner refractory thickness $L_1 = 0.15 \, m$, $k_1 = 1.0 \, W/(m \cdot K)$
- Insulation thickness $L_2 = 0.10 \, m$, $k_2 = 0.04 \, W/(m \cdot K)$
- Inner surface temperature $T_{in} = 700 \, °C$
- Outer surface temperature $T_{out} = 60 \, °C$

**Step 1 — Calculate Surface Area**

$$A = \text{height} \times \text{width} = 3 \times 5 = 15 \, m^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Thermal Resistance Concept**

For composite walls in series, the total thermal resistance is:

$$R_{total} = \dfrac{L_1}{k_1 \cdot A} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 \cdot A}$$

$$R_{total} = \dfrac{0.15}{1.0 \times 15} + \dfrac{0.10}{0.04 \times 15}$$

$$R_{total} = \dfrac{0.15}{15} + \dfrac{0.10}{0.60}$$

$$R_{total} = 0.01 + 0.1667$$

$$R_{total} = 0.1767 \, K/W$$

**Step 3 — Compute Heat Loss**

$$\dot{Q}_{loss} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{total}}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{loss} = \dfrac{700 - 60}{0.1767}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{loss} = \dfrac{640}{0.1767}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{loss} = 3622 \, W \approx 3.62 \, kW$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{loss} \approx 3.62 \, kW}$$

---

### 🔢 Derivation 3: Oven Efficiency Calculation

**Given:**
- Heat input to oven $\dot{Q}_{input} = 50 \, kW$ (from burner)
- Useful heat absorbed by charge $\dot{Q}_{useful} = 38 \, kW$
- Heat loss through walls $= 3.62 \, kW$ (from previous derivation)
- Heat carried by exhaust gases $\dot{Q}_{exhaust} = 6.0 \, kW$

**Step 1 — Apply Efficiency Formula**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{useful}}{\dot{Q}_{input}} \times 100$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{38}{50} \times 100$$

$$\eta = 76 \, \%$$

**Step 2 — Verify Energy Balance**

$$\dot{Q}_{input} = \dot{Q}_{useful} + \dot{Q}_{loss} + \dot{Q}_{exhaust}$$

$$50 = 38 + 3.62 + 6.0$$

$$50 \approx 47.62 \, kW$$

**Discrepancy** $= 50 - 47.62 = 2.38 \, kW$ — this represents unaccounted losses (radiation openings, door leakage, conveyor losses).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practice:** Energy balance should account for at least **95%** of input energy; differences larger than **5%** indicate measurement errors or unaccounted heat leakage paths.

---

### 📊 Analytical Matrix — Heat Transfer Mode vs. Temperature Range

| Temperature Range | Dominant Heat Transfer Mode | Key Design Consideration |
|---|---|---|
| $T < 200 \, °C$ | Forced Convection | Fan selection, duct design |
| $200 \, °C - 500 \, °C$ | Mixed Convection + Radiation | Surface emissivity of heaters |
| $T > 600 \, °C$ | Radiation dominates | Use high-emissivity refractory lining |
| $T > 1000 \, °C$ | Pure Radiative exchange | Reflective baffles needed |

---

### 🔢 Derivation 4: Heating Time of a Charge

**Problem:** Calculate the time required to heat a steel charge inside an oven.

**Given:**
- $\dot{Q}_{input} = 50 \, kW = 50{,}000 \, W$
- $Q_{total} = 111.5 \, MJ = 111.5 \times 10^6 \, J$ (from Derivation 1)

**Step 1 — Apply Heating Time Formula**

$$t = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{\dot{Q}_{input}}$$

$$t = \dfrac{111.5 \times 10^6}{50{,}000}$$

$$t = 2230 \, s$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Hours**

$$t = \dfrac{2230}{3600} \approx 0.62 \, hours \approx 37.2 \, minutes$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{t \approx 37.2 \, minutes}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
    Q4 --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q6[Useful Heat Energy to Workpiece]

    Q3 --> Q7
    Q4 --> Q7
    Q1 --> Q8[Exhaust Losses via Flue Gases]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define an industrial oven. Differentiate between an oven and a furnace.**

**Model Answer:**

An **industrial oven** is a thermally insulated enclosed equipment used for controlled heating operations such as drying, curing, baking, and heat treatment of materials, where the products of combustion are separated from the work being heated.

**Key Differences:**

| Parameter | Oven | Furnace |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature Range | Below **750 °C** (typically) | Above **750 °C** |
| Contact with Combustion Gases | No (indirect heating) | Yes (direct heating common) |
| Typical Use | Drying, curing, low-temp heat treatment | Smelting, melting, high-temp metallurgy |
| Atmosphere Control | Easy to maintain inert atmosphere | Difficult in fuel-fired types |

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any six safety devices that must be installed on a gas-fired industrial oven.**

**Model Answer:**

The six essential safety devices for a gas-fired oven are:

1. **Flame Failure Device (FFD)** — Cuts off gas supply if flame is extinguished.
2. **High Temperature Cutoff (HTCO)** — Shuts burner if temperature exceeds setpoint.
3. **Pressure Switch** — Detects abnormal gas pressure variations.
4. **Gas Leak Detector** — Continuous monitoring of LPG/NG leakage.
5. **Purge Timer** — Ensures 4-purge cycle before ignition to remove residual gases.
6. **Emergency Shutdown Push Button** — Manual isolation in emergencies.

Additionally: **Thermocouple**, **Explosion Vent**, **Ventilation Exhaust Fan**, **Door Interlock Switch**.

---

### 📚 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the constructional features and working of a **continuous conveyor-type industrial oven**. Discuss its applications. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **various hazards** associated with industrial ovens and the **safety measures** to be adopted. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Construction and Working of a Conveyor Oven:**

A conveyor oven consists of the following components:

1. **Loading Section** — Where components are placed on the moving belt.
2. **Pre-Heating Zone** — Initial gradual heating to avoid thermal shock.
3. **Main Heating Zone** — Where the process temperature is maintained; fitted with **gas burners** or **electric heaters**.
4. **Holding / Soaking Zone** — Allows uniform temperature penetration into the charge.
5. **Cooling Zone** — Controlled gradual cooling to prevent distortion.
6. **Unloading Section** — Finished components exit for further processing.
7. **Recirculation Fans** — Provide uniform hot air distribution.
8. **Exhaust System** — Removes fumes, moisture, vapours.
9. **Control Panel** — Temperature controllers, PID controllers, safety interlocks.

**Working:** The workpieces are continuously fed onto the conveyor belt, which moves at a controlled speed through the heating zones. Temperature is precisely controlled using **PID controllers**, and the entire process is automated.

**Applications:**
- Powder coating of automotive parts.
- Curing of paints and enamels.
- Annealing of metals in batches.
- Drying of printed circuits.
- Food processing (biscuits, bread, cereals).

---

**Part (b) — Hazards and Safety Measures:**

**Major Hazards:**

| Hazard | Cause | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Fire** | Flammable vapours, overheated oil | Property damage, fatality |
| **Explosion** | Accumulated LPG + ignition source | Blast, injuries |
| **Burn Injuries** | Hot surfaces (> 65 °C) | Skin burns |
| **Toxic Fume Inhalation** | Solvent vapours, combustion gases | Respiratory illness |
| **Electric Shock** | Faulty heating elements | Electrocution |
| **Carbon Monoxide Poisoning** | Incomplete combustion | Asphyxiation |

**Safety Measures:**

1. **Ventilation:** Adequate exhaust fans and fresh air supply (minimum **6 air changes/hour**).
2. **Temperature Control:** Use of **PID controllers**, redundant thermocouples.
3. **Fire Protection:** CO₂ / dry chemical extinguishers, fire dampers, fire-resistant insulation.
4. **Gas Safety:** FFD, leak detectors, emergency shutoff valves, purging cycles.
5. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Heat-resistant gloves, aprons, face shields.
6. **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO):** During maintenance.
7. **Training:** Operators trained in emergency procedures.
8. **Compliance:** Adherence to **IS 3168**, **IS 5455**, **NFPA 86**.
9. **Explosion Relief Panels:** Designed to release pressure at **0.05 bar** overpressure.
10. **Regular Maintenance:** Inspection of burners, refractory lining, door seals.

---

**Question B (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Classify industrial ovens based on **mode of operation** and explain the working principle of a **batch-type oven**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An industrial oven is used to heat **300 kg** of a metal component from **25 °C to 400 °C**. The specific heat of the material is **600 J/(kg·K)**. The oven operates on a **gas burner** with an input rating of **40 kW**. Calculate: (i) Total heat energy required, (ii) Time required for heating, (iii) Efficiency if useful heat is **85%** of total heat. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Classification and Working of Batch Oven:**

**Classification Based on Mode of Operation:**

1. **Batch Oven** — Charge is loaded, processed in one cycle, then unloaded.
2. **Continuous Oven** — Material moves continuously through heating zones.
3. **Conveyor Oven** — Uses belt conveyors; semi-continuous.
4. **Tunnel Oven** — Long chamber; wagons travel through zones.
5. **Rotary Kiln Oven** — Rotating drum for bulk solids.

**Working Principle of Batch Oven:**

1. **Loading:** The charge is placed on trays or fixtures and loaded into the work chamber.
2. **Sealing:** The oven door is closed and sealed tightly using gaskets.
3. **Heating:** The burners (or electric elements) are ignited. PID controllers regulate the temperature.
4. **Soaking:** Once target temperature is reached, the charge is held for the prescribed duration to ensure uniform temperature.
5. **Cooling:** The charge is allowed to cool inside (or moved to a separate cooling zone).
6. **Unloading:** The finished components are removed.

**Applications:** Annealing, tempering, paint curing, ageing of aluminium parts, stress relieving.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given Data:**
- Mass $m = 300 \, kg$
- Initial temperature $T_1 = 25 \, °C$
- Final temperature $T_2 = 400 \, °C$
- Specific heat $c_p = 600 \, J/(kg \cdot K)$
- Heat input $\dot{Q}_{input} = 40 \, kW = 40{,}000 \, W$

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Heat Energy Required**

$$Q_{total} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{total} = 300 \times 600 \times (400 - 25)$$

$$Q_{total} = 300 \times 600 \times 375$$

$$Q_{total} = 67{,}500{,}000 \, J = 67.5 \, MJ$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Time Required for Heating**

$$t = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{\dot{Q}_{input}}$$

$$t = \dfrac{67.5 \times 10^6}{40{,}000}$$

$$t = 1687.5 \, s$$

Converting to minutes:

$$t = \dfrac{1687.5}{60} \approx 28.13 \, minutes$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Efficiency**

$$\eta = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{total}} \times 100$$

$$\eta = 85 \, \% \quad (\text{given})$$

If efficiency is 85%, then the actual heat input from burner would be:

$$Q_{input\_actual} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{0.85} = \dfrac{67.5}{0.85} \approx 79.4 \, MJ$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 67.5 \, MJ; \quad t \approx 28.13 \, minutes; \quad \eta = 85\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Students often forget to convert $\Delta T$ in Kelvin when using °C — but since it's a difference, both units give the same numerical value. However, **always state units** clearly.
> - **Mistake 2:** In efficiency problems, students confuse "useful heat" with "heat absorbed by charge." The examiner expects **Q_useful** to be specifically defined.
> - **Mistake 3:** Many students write "40 kW/hr" instead of "40 kW" — power is in **W or kW**, not "W/hr."
> - **Mistake 4:** Failing to mention **safety standards (IS 3168, IS 5455, NFPA 86)** in descriptive answers leads to a **1–2 mark deduction**.
> - **Mistake 5:** Not drawing a **labeled block diagram** in part (a) questions results in loss of **2 marks** (visual aids are mandatory).

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ An **industrial oven** is an enclosed, insulated heating chamber where combustion gases do NOT contact the charge.
- ✅ Ovens operate typically below **750 °C**; furnaces operate above this temperature.
- ✅ Ovens are classified as **batch, continuous, conveyor, tunnel, and rotary kiln** based on operation.
- ✅ Heat transfer modes: **Conduction, Convection, Radiation** — radiation dominates above **600 °C**.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:** $Q = m c_p \Delta T$, $\eta = Q_{useful}/Q_{input} \times 100$, $\dot{Q}_{loss} = U A \Delta T$.
- ✅ **Major Hazards:** Fire, explosion, burns, toxic fumes, CO poisoning, electric shock.
- ✅ **Safety Devices:** Flame Failure Device (FFD), High Temperature Cutoff (HTCO), Purge Timer, Gas Leak Detector, Pressure Switch, Explosion Relief Vent.
- ✅ **External Surface Temperature** should not exceed **65 °C** to prevent accidental burns.
- ✅ **Standards to remember:** **IS 3168 : 1981**, **IS 5455 : 1969**, **NFPA 86**.
- ✅ **Ventilation requirement:** Minimum **6 air changes/hour** for solvent-based ovens.
- ✅ Always mention **PID controllers** for temperature regulation in descriptive answers.
- ✅ For conveyor ovens, remember zones: **Loading → Pre-heat → Heating → Soaking → Cooling → Unloading**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foundry Health Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Foundry Health Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Foundries: A Working Definition

A **foundry** is an industrial establishment dedicated to the production of metal castings through the process of pouring molten metal into pre-shaped mould cavities, allowing the metal to solidify, and then breaking out the finished casting. The term is derived from the Latin word *fundere*, meaning "to pour." Foundries form the backbone of virtually every manufacturing sector — automotive, aerospace, defence, energy, construction, and heavy machinery all depend on cast components ranging from a few grams to several tonnes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Recall Format):**
> A **foundry** is a manufacturing unit where metal is melted, poured into a mould of the desired shape, allowed to solidify, and then the casting is removed for further processing — a process known collectively as **foundry practice** or **metal casting**.

## 1.2 Why "Health Hazards" Demand Special Attention

A foundry is, in occupational safety terminology, a **"multihazard" workplace**. Within a single production hall, a worker may be simultaneously exposed to:

- Airborne **silica dust** capable of causing irreversible lung fibrosis,
- Ambient temperatures exceeding **45 °C** near furnaces,
- Sound pressure levels of **95–115 dB(A)** during knocking-out and shake-out,
- Toxic **fumes from metal pouring** (Zn, Pb, Cd, Be, Ni, Cr VI),
- **Ionizing radiation** from radiography-based non-destructive testing (NDT),
- **Fire and explosion risks** from molten metal, combustible dust clouds, and LPG/Diesel storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — Foundry Health Hazards):**
> KTU expects students to identify, classify, and prescribe control measures for the *full spectrum* of physical, chemical, mechanical, and radiological hazards encountered in a foundry.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Foundry as a "Kitchen with Explosive Ingredients"

Imagine a giant commercial kitchen. The cooks handle **boiling oil** (molten metal), **sharp knives** (fettling tools), **flour dust** that can ignite (organic binder dust), and **whirring fans** (noise sources). They also breathe **smoke** from caramelising food (pyrolysis fumes). A safe kitchen requires ventilation, PPE, training, and emergency plans. A foundry is the same — except the "food" is **molten iron at 1400 °C**, the "knives" weigh several kilograms, and the "smoke" contains **crystalline silica particles** that scar the lungs permanently. The "safety recipe" — **engineering control, administrative control, PPE, and training** — is identical in principle but far more rigorous in practice.

## 1.4 Classification of Foundry Hazards — KTU Board-Ready Taxonomy

For examination purposes, KTU 2024 Scheme groups foundry hazards into six families:

| Sl. No. | Hazard Class | Representative Agents / Sources |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Dust Hazards** | Silica, graphite, coal, metallic oxides |
| 2 | **Heat Hazards** | Furnaces, ladles, pouring streams, exothermic reactions |
| 3 | **Noise & Vibration** | Knock-out, shake-out, sand plant, fettling |
| 4 | **Chemical Hazards** | Metal fumes (Zn, Pb, Cd, Be), CO, CO₂, SO₂, resins, parting compounds |
| 5 | **Radiation Hazards** | X-ray/γ-ray radiography, UV/IR from molten metal |
| 6 | **Fire & Explosion** | Molten metal spillage, combustible dust, fuel storage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Statistic for Examinations:**
> The **Indian Factories Act, 1948 (Section 41 & Schedule II)** specifically lists **foundries as hazardous processes** and mandates periodic medical examination of workers exposed to **free silica dust**. The **Threshold Limit Value (TLV-TWA)** for respirable crystalline silica is **0.025 mg/m³** (ACGIH 2023). Use this constant wherever a quantitative answer is expected.

## 1.5 Foundries as a Statutorily "Dangerous Operation"

Under the **Indian Factories Act, 1948**, the manufacturing of **ferrous and non-ferrous castings** is included in **Schedule I** as a manufacturing process and **Section 87** read with **Schedule II / III** treats the foundry environment as a *notifiable hazardous process*. The **ILO Code of Practice on Safety and Health in the Iron and Steel Industry (2018)** and the **BIS Standard IS 9566:2007** further codify the safety requirements.

```
Recap Snapshot — Section 1
─────────────────────────
• Foundry = melting + moulding + pouring + solidification + fettling
• Six hazard families: dust, heat, noise, chemical, radiation, fire/explosion
• Free silica TLV-TWA = 0.025 mg/m³ (ACGIH)
• Foundry is a "notifiable hazardous process" under the Factories Act
```

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Foundry Process Chain — Where Hazards Originate

A modern foundry executes a **ten-step process chain**. Each step carries a distinct hazard profile. Mastering this chain is the key to KTU valuation, because the examiner expects candidates to *trace a hazard to its origin step*.

| Step | Operation | Primary Hazard Generated |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Sand preparation & reclamation** | Silica dust, noise |
| 2 | **Pattern making & core making** | Wood dust, resin fumes, noise |
| 3 | **Moulding & core setting** | Silica dust, physical strain |
| 4 | **Melting** (cupola / induction / arc furnace) | Heat, CO/CO₂ fumes, metal fumes |
| 5 | **Pouring** | Heat radiation, metal splash, fettling dust |
| 6 | **Cooling & shake-out** | Dust, noise, vibration |
| 7 | **Knock-out & decoing** | Dust, noise, flying debris |
| 8 | **Fettling, grinding, shot blasting** | Dust, sparks, noise, vibration |
| 9 | **Heat treatment** | Heat, fuel gases, oil mist |
| 10 | **NDT (Radiography, MPI, DPI)** | Ionising radiation, chemicals, UV |

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Dust Hazards

### 2.2.1 Silica Dust — The Silent Killer

The moulding sand used in a foundry is primarily **silica (SiO₂)** in the form of quartz. During shake-out, knock-out, fettling, and sand reclamation, the sand is pulverised into respirable particles of **aerodynamic diameter ≤ 10 µm (PM₁₀)**, of which the most dangerous are the **respirable fraction ≤ 4 µm** that penetrate the **alveolar region** of the lungs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diseases caused by respirable crystalline silica (RCS):**
> - **Silicosis** — irreversible pulmonary fibrosis
> - **Lung cancer** — IARC Group 1 carcinogen
> - **Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)**
> - **Autoimmune disorders** (scleroderma, rheumatoid arthritis)
> - **Renal disease** and **tuberculosis** (silicotuberculosis)

### 2.2.2 Heat Stress — The Physiological Cascade

Heat stress is measured using the **Wet Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT) Index** which integrates natural wet-bulb temperature, globe (radiant) temperature, and dry-bulb temperature.

The ACGIH TLV for heat stress uses a work–rest regime based on the WBGT:

| Workload | TLV (°C-WBGT) — Acclimatised | TLV (°C-WBGT) — Unacclimatised |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Light | **30.0** | **27.0** |
| Moderate | **26.7** | **25.0** |
| Heavy | **25.0** | **22.0** |
| Very Heavy | **23.0** | **18.0** |

### 2.2.3 Noise — The Invisible Aggressor

Noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL) is the **second most common occupational disease in India** (after respiratory illness). Permissible limits under the **Indian Factories Act (Schedule XI)** are:

- **90 dB(A)** for 8 hours
- Doubling of sound pressure reduces permissible exposure by half — the **"3 dB exchange rate"** (ISO/ACGIH) or **"5 dB exchange rate"** (OSHA legacy).

### 2.2.4 Chemical Hazards — Metal Fume Fever & Toxic Gases

**Zinc** (from galvanised scrap) and **Copper** produce **Metal Fume Fever (MFF)** — an acute, flu-like, self-limiting illness. **Beryllium** causes **Chronic Beryllium Disease (CBD)** — a granulomatous lung disorder. **Lead** and **Cadmium** cause systemic toxicity. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** from incomplete combustion in cupola furnaces can be lethal at concentrations > **1200 ppm in 30 min**.

### 2.2.5 Radiation Hazards

Two categories:

- **Ionising radiation** — X-rays (200–500 kVp) and γ-rays (Ir-192, Co-60) used in radiography. Controlled under the **Atomic Energy (Radiation Processing of Food and Allied Products) Rules, 2012** and the **Atomic Energy Act, 1962**.
- **Non-ionising radiation** — intense **infrared and visible radiation** from molten metal (radiant flux up to **10–15 kW/m²** at 1 m) and **UV radiation** during arc melting.

## 2.3 Control Measures — The Hierarchy of Controls

KTU expects candidates to recite the **hierarchy in the correct order**:

1. **Elimination** — substitute silica with non-silica sands (olivine, chromite, zircon).
2. **Substitution** — replace hot box / furane resins with water-based binders.
3. **Engineering Controls** — local exhaust ventilation (LEV), enclosures, baffles, dampers.
4. **Administrative Controls** — job rotation, rest cycles, training, signage.
5. **PPE** — respirators, ear defenders, aluminised suits, face shields, safety boots.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Hierarchy of Controls: "E-S-E-A-P"** → *Eliminate, Substitute, Engineer, Administer, Protect.*

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Constant | Unit | Remarks |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | TLV-TWA for respirable crystalline silica | $\mathbf{0.025}$ | mg/m³ | ACGIH 2023 |
| 2 | TLV-TWA for inhalable dust | $\mathbf{10}$ | mg/m³ | ACGIH |
| 3 | Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for noise | $\mathbf{90}$ | dB(A) | Indian Factories Act |
| 4 | OSHA exchange rate | $T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$ | hours | $L$ = measured level (dBA) |
| 5 | ISO/ACGIH exchange rate (3 dB) | $T = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/3}}$ | hours | Modern standard |
| 6 | WBGT index (outdoor, sun) | $\text{WBGT} = 0.7\,T_{nwb} + 0.2\,T_{g} + 0.1\,T_{db}$ | °C | $T_{nwb}$ = natural wet-bulb |
| 7 | WBGT index (indoor, no sun) | $\text{WBGT} = 0.7\,T_{nwb} + 0.3\,T_{g}$ | °C | Foundry = indoor |
| 8 | Ventilation rate (Q) for dust | $Q = \dfrac{G}{K_c \, (C_1 - C_2)}$ | m³/s | $G$ = generation rate, $K_c$ = K-factor |
| 9 | Sound pressure doubling | $L_p \uparrow 6\text{ dB} \Rightarrow p \times 2$ | dB | $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$ |
| 10 | IDLH for CO | $\mathbf{1200}$ | ppm | NIOSH |
| 11 | IDLH for silica | $\mathbf{25 000}$ | mg/m³ | NIOSH legacy |
| 12 | Capture velocity (canopy hood) | $\mathbf{0.5 \text{ to } 1.0}$ | m/s | Foundry dust hood |
| 13 | Transport velocity for mineral dust | $\mathbf{15 \text{ to } 25}$ | m/s | ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual |

**Kerala-Specific Note (Statute):** The **Kerala Factories and Boilers (KFB) Rules, 1957** plus the **Factories Act, 1948 (Central)** govern foundry inspections; the **Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB)** enforces emission norms under the **Water (P&CP) Act, 1974** and **Air (P&CP) Act, 1981**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundries supplying the Indian Railways, BHEL, and the Indian Navy** must satisfy the **Foundry Industry (Development) Council (FIDCO)** and **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 9566:2007)** safety benchmarks.
- **OEMs (Tata Motors, Ashok Leyland)** conduct **Tier-1 supplier audits** using the **SA 8000** and **IATF 16949** frameworks, both of which demand quantifiable hazard control data.
- Globally, the **WISDOM database (NIOSH)** catalogues silica-exposed industries; **WHO/ILO Joint Estimates of Work-related Burden of Disease (2017)** attribute **≥ 10 000 deaths/year** to occupational silica exposure in India.

```
Section 2 Take-Aways
─────────────────────
• 10-step process chain — every step has a "signature hazard"
• Silica is the IARC Group 1 carcinogen of the foundry
• WBGT governs heat exposure; 90 dB(A)/8 h governs noise
• Hierarchy: E-S-E-A-P (Eliminate → Substitute → Engineer → Administer → Protect)
• Capture velocity 0.5–1.0 m/s for canopy hoods, transport 15–25 m/s for mineral dust
```

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — OSHA Permissible Exposure Time for Noise

A worker is exposed to noise at $L = 102\,\text{dB(A)}$. Find the OSHA-permissible daily exposure.

**Step 1: Recall the OSHA exchange-rate formula.**

The OSHA standard uses a **5 dB exchange rate** and a **90 dB(A) criterion level** (TWA over 8 h).

$$
T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the measured level $L = 102$.**

$$
T = \frac{8}{2^{(102-90)/5}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the exponent.**

$$
(102-90)/5 = 12/5 = 2.4
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the power of 2.**

$$
2^{2.4} = e^{2.4 \ln 2} = e^{2.4 \times 0.6931} = e^{1.6635} = 5.2780
$$

**Step 5: Divide 8 by the result.**

$$
T = \frac{8}{5.2780} = 1.5157\,\text{h}
$$

**Step 6: State the result in the KTU-suggested format.**

$$
\boxed{T \approx 1.52\ \text{hours per 8-h shift}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning logic:** Each 5 dB increase halves the allowable time. From 90 dB → 95 dB → 100 dB → 102 dB (an extra 2 dB above 100). The 2 dB adds a factor of $2^{0.4} = 1.32$, halving the 2-h allowance to 1.52 h.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ISO/ACGIH Permissible Time (3 dB Exchange)

Same worker at $L = 102\,\text{dB(A)}$. The ISO 1999 / ACGIH method uses a **3 dB exchange rate**.

**Step 1: Recall the 3-dB formula.**

$$
T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/3}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $L = 102$.**

$$
T = \frac{8}{2^{(102-90)/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{12/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{4}}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator.**

$$
2^{4} = 16
$$

**Step 4: Compute the exposure time.**

$$
T = \frac{8}{16} = 0.5\ \text{h}
$$

**Step 5: Comparative inference.**

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{ISO}} = 0.5\ \text{h} < T_{\text{OSHA}} = 1.52\ \text{h}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU Answers:** Under modern ISO/ACGIH practice, the **3 dB rule is more conservative** and is the preferred answer for health-risk-based questions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — WBGT Index for a Foundry Pouring Bay

Given:
- Natural wet-bulb temperature $T_{nwb} = 28\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Globe temperature $T_g = 42\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Dry-bulb temperature $T_{db} = 36\ ^\circ\text{C}$

The worker performs *moderate* work *indoors* (no solar load). Determine compliance with the ACGIH TLV.

**Step 1: Select the indoor formula (foundry is indoor).**

$$
\text{WBGT}_{\text{indoor}} = 0.7\,T_{nwb} + 0.3\,T_g
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
\text{WBGT} = 0.7 \times 28 + 0.3 \times 42
$$

**Step 3: Compute the two products.**

$$
0.7 \times 28 = 19.6
$$

$$
0.3 \times 42 = 12.6
$$

**Step 4: Add the partial contributions.**

$$
\text{WBGT} = 19.6 + 12.6 = 32.2\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 5: Compare with the ACGIH TLV for moderate workload (acclimatised).**

$$
\text{WBGT}_{\text{measured}} = 32.2\ ^\circ\text{C} \quad\ ?\quad \text{TLV}_{\text{mod, accl}} = 26.7\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 6: Conclusion.**

$$
\boxed{\text{WBGT}_{\text{measured}} = 32.2\ ^\circ\text{C} > 26.7\ ^\circ\text{C} \Rightarrow \text{NON-COMPLIANT}}
$$

**Recommended corrective action:** Reduce work cycle to 25 % work / 75 % rest, install reflective shields between furnaces and workers, supply cooled water, and use aluminised PPE.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) Sizing

A sand-reclamation hopper generates respirable silica dust at $G = 0.5\,\text{g/min}$. The hood is to maintain a capture velocity of $v_c = 0.75\,\text{m/s}$ and the duct diameter is $D = 0.20\,\text{m}$. The transport velocity must be at least $v_t = 18\,\text{m/s}$. Calculate the required exhaust volumetric flow.

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area of the duct.**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.20)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.04}{4} = 0.03142\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Volumetric flow rate based on transport velocity.**

$$
Q = v_t \times A = 18 \times 0.03142 = 0.5655\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to m³/h for the KTU preferred unit.**

$$
Q = 0.5655 \times 3600 = 2035.8\ \text{m}^3/\text{h} \approx 2036\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 4: Verify capture-velocity compliance at the hood opening.**

If the hood is a $0.30\ \text{m} \times 0.30\ \text{m}$ flange, the flange area is $0.09\ \text{m}^2$ and the face velocity is

$$
v_f = \frac{Q}{A_f} = \frac{0.5655}{0.09} = 6.28\ \text{m/s}
$$

Because $v_f > v_c = 0.75\ \text{m/s}$, the hood capture is **adequate**.

**Step 5: Check the dust concentration downstream of the LEV (assuming capture efficiency $\eta = 0.85$).**

$$
C_{\text{ambient}} \le \frac{G \times (1-\eta)}{Q}
$$

$$
C = \frac{0.5 \times (1-0.85)}{0.5655} = \frac{0.075}{0.5655} = 0.1327\ \text{g/m}^3 = 132.7\ \text{mg/m}^3
$$

Wait — this is still **above** the TLV of 25 mg/m³. This is acceptable only if the LEV's *capture efficiency* is much higher. Re-design with $\eta \geq 0.999$ for compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Take-away:** LEV **must** be coupled with high-efficiency filtration (cyclone + bag-house) to bring emissions to the **TLV-TWA of 0.025 mg/m³** at the breathing zone.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Hazard × Control × PPE × Statute

| Hazard | Source Step | Engineering Control | Administrative Control | PPE | Indian Statute |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Silica dust** | Sand plant, fettling | LEV + bag-house | Job rotation, training | FFP2/FFP3 respirator | Factories Act §41, §87 |
| **Heat** | Furnace, pouring | Reflective shields, spot cooling | Hydration breaks, acclimatisation | Aluminised suit, IR face shield | IS 9566:2007 |
| **Noise** | Shake-out, fettling | Acoustic enclosures, dampers | Hearing-conservation programme | Ear muffs (≥ 30 dB SNR) | Factories Act, Sch. XI |
| **Metal fumes** | Pouring, alloying | Fume extraction at furnace | Pre-employment medical | Respirator with P100 filter | Factories Act, Sch. II |
| **CO** | Cupola | Stack monitoring | Confined-space permit | SCBA (rescue only) | OHS Code 2020 |
| **Ionising radiation** | Radiography NDT | Shielded enclosure, interlocks | Radiography permit | TLD badge, lead apron | AERB Safety Code (SSRP) |
| **Fire / Explosion** | Dust collector, fuel | Explosion vents, earthing | Hot-work permit, ignition control | Flame-resistant coverall | Factories Act §38, OISD |

## 3.6 Worked Example — Combined Dose Calculation (Silica)

A worker is exposed to $0.04\,\text{mg/m}^3$ for $4\,\text{h}$ and $0.10\,\text{mg/m}^3$ for $4\,\text{h}$. Determine the **8-h TWA** and compare with TLV.

**Step 1: Apply the TWA formula.**

$$
\text{TWA} = \frac{C_1 T_1 + C_2 T_2}{8}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
\text{TWA} = \frac{0.04 \times 4 + 0.10 \times 4}{8}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerator.**

$$
0.04 \times 4 = 0.16
$$

$$
0.10 \times 4 = 0.40
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.16 + 0.40 = 0.56
$$

**Step 4: Divide by 8.**

$$
\text{TWA} = \frac{0.56}{8} = 0.07\ \text{mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5: Compare with TLV.**

$$
\boxed{\text{TWA} = 0.07\ \text{mg/m}^3 > \text{TLV-TWA} = 0.025\ \text{mg/m}^3 \Rightarrow \text{NON-COMPLIANT}}
$$

**Action:** Reduce concentration by 64 % via LEV upgrade or halve the exposure time at the higher concentration.

```
Analytical Snapshot — Section 3
───────────────────────────────
• 3 dB exchange (ISO) is more conservative than 5 dB (OSHA)
• WBGT indoor = 0.7 Tnwb + 0.3 Tg
• LEV flow Q = v_t × (π D²/4)
• TWA = Σ(Cᵢ Tᵢ) / 8 h
• Compliance test: TWA ≤ TLV
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define the term *Silicosis*. State the permissible exposure limit (PEL) of respirable crystalline silica in India and globally.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Silicosis (1 mark):** Silicosis is a chronic, irreversible, fibrotic disease of the lungs caused by the inhalation, deposition, and pulmonary reaction to respirable crystalline silica (SiO₂) particles, typically of aerodynamic diameter ≤ 10 µm.
- **PEL globally (1 mark):** ACGIH TLV-TWA = **0.025 mg/m³** (respirable fraction).
- **PEL in India (1 mark):** Indian Factories Act / Schedule II indicates that free silica exposure must be "controlled to the lowest practicable level"; the **Directorate General Factory Advice Service (DGFASLI)** recommends following the **ACGIH/CIS standard** of **0.025 mg/m³** for new installations and **0.05 mg/m³** for legacy plants.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** List any **six** potential health hazards specific to a ferrous foundry.

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Respirable silica dust** from sand plant and fettling
2. **Heat stress** from furnace, ladle, and pouring
3. **Noise-induced hearing loss** from shake-out and fettling
4. **Metal-fume fever** from zinc/copper alloying
5. **Carbon monoxide poisoning** from cupola furnace
6. **Burn injuries** from molten metal splash

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice

> **Module-4 question pattern: 14 marks. Students must attempt one of the two choices.**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Length Q

**Q.A.** *With the help of a flowchart, classify the various health hazards encountered in a ferrous foundry. For each hazard, recommend suitable **engineering and administrative control measures** and the **PPE** to be provided to workers. State the relevant provisions of the **Indian Factories Act, 1948** and the **ILO Code of Practice**.*

**Model Answer Structure:**

**(a) Classification of Foundry Health Hazards (7 marks)**

The hazards are classified into **six** categories:

| Sl. No. | Hazard Class | Sources in Foundry | Health Effect |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Dust Hazards** | Silica sand, coal dust, metallic oxides | Silicosis, bronchitis, lung cancer |
| 2 | **Heat Hazards** | Cupola/induction furnace, ladle, pouring stream | Heat stroke, dehydration, burns |
| 3 | **Noise Hazards** | Shake-out, fettling, sand plant, blowers | Noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL) |
| 4 | **Chemical Hazards** | Fumes of Zn, Pb, Cd, Be; CO, CO₂, SO₂; resins | Metal-fume fever, CO poisoning, dermatitis |
| 5 | **Radiation Hazards** | X-ray/γ-ray radiography; UV/IR from molten metal | Radiation sickness, cataract, skin burn |
| 6 | **Fire & Explosion** | Molten metal, combustible dust, LPG | Burn injuries, fatality |

*(Each row — 1 mark, plus 1 mark for the lead classification statement.)*

**(b) Engineering & Administrative Controls and PPE (7 marks)**

For each hazard, the controls are:

1. **Dust (1.5 marks):**
   - *Engineering:* LEV at shake-out, dust collectors (cyclone + bag-house), use of silica-free sand (olivine, chromite, zircon), water spray for dust suppression.
   - *Administrative:* Job rotation, training, periodic medical examination, restricted-access zones.
   - *PPE:* FFP2/FFP3 respirators, protective goggles.

2. **Heat (1.5 marks):**
   - *Engineering:* Furnace insulation, reflective shields, spot cooling, ventilation.
   - *Administrative:* Hydration protocol, work-rest cycle based on WBGT, acclimatisation programme.
   - *PPE:* Aluminised suit (700–1000 °C rated), IR-reflective face shield, leather gloves, gum boots.

3. **Noise (1 mark):**
   - *Engineering:* Acoustic enclosures on shake-out, vibration dampers, silencers on exhaust.
   - *Administrative:* Hearing-conservation programme, audiometric testing every 6 months.
   - *PPE:* Ear muffs (SNR ≥ 30 dB), ear plugs.

4. **Chemical (1 mark):**
   - *Engineering:* Fume extraction at furnace, closed charging, gas monitors.
   - *Administrative:* MSDS training, air sampling, confined-space permits.
   - *PPE:* SCBA for confined spaces, gas-specific respirator cartridges.

5. **Radiation (1 mark):**
   - *Engineering:* Shielded radiography enclosure, interlocks, warning lights.
   - *Administrative:* AERB permit, badge dosimetry, controlled access.
   - *PPE:* TLD badge, lead apron, personal alarm dosimeter.

6. **Fire & Explosion (1 mark):**
   - *Engineering:* Explosion vents on dust collectors, earthing, sprinklers.
   - *Administrative:* Hot-work permits, ignition-source control, emergency drills.
   - *PPE:* Flame-resistant coverall, fire-extinguisher training.

**Statutory Provisions (Mandatory to state — 0 marks but adds value):**
- **Factories Act, 1948:** Sections 41 (ventilation), 42 (dust/fume), 87 read with Schedule II/III (notifiable hazardous process), Schedule XI (noise).
- **ILO Code of Practice on Safety and Health in Foundries (2018):** mandates risk assessment, hierarchy of controls, and health surveillance.
- **IS 9566:2007** — Indian Standard for foundry safety.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B.** *A worker in a foundry is exposed to noise levels of **102 dB(A) for 2.5 hours** and **95 dB(A) for 5.5 hours** during an 8-hour shift. Calculate the **8-hour Time-Weighted Average (TWA)** using both the **OSHA (5 dB exchange)** and the **ISO/ACGIH (3 dB exchange)** methods. State which is more conservative and recommend suitable **engineering and administrative noise control measures** for a foundry.*

**Model Answer Structure:**

**(a) Calculation of 8-h TWA — OSHA (5 dB) (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Convert each exposure to its "noise dose" fraction.**

The OSHA formula is

$$
T_{\text{allow}} = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}
$$

**For 102 dB(A):**

$$
T_1 = \frac{8}{2^{(102-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{2.4}} = \frac{8}{5.278} = 1.516\ \text{h}
$$

**For 95 dB(A):**

$$
T_2 = \frac{8}{2^{(95-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{1}} = \frac{8}{2} = 4.0\ \text{h}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the dose fraction for each exposure.**

$$
D_1 = \frac{2.5}{1.516} = 1.649
$$

$$
D_2 = \frac{5.5}{4.0} = 1.375
$$

**Step 3: Sum the dose fractions.**

$$
D_{\text{total}} = 1.649 + 1.375 = 3.024
$$

**Step 4: TWA from dose.**

$$
\text{TWA} = 16.61 \log_2\left(\frac{D_{\text{total}}}{100} + 1\right) + 90
$$

*(Note: this is a derived OSHA formula; an equivalent direct integration is also acceptable.)*

Using the direct integration method:

$$
\text{TWA}_{\text{OSHA}} = 90 + 5 \log_2\left(\frac{C_1 T_1 + C_2 T_2}{8}\right)
$$
