where $C_i$ is a normalised concentration (fraction of PEL). We can simplify by using:

$$
\text{TWA}_{\text{OSHA}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{T_1\, 10^{L_1/10} + T_2\, 10^{L_2/10}}{T_1+T_2}\right)
$$

Wait — for KTU, the simplest accepted method is the **dose fraction × 100 = % dose**, and the TWA is read from a table. We will show the **dose-percentage method**:

**Step 5: Recompute with dose percentages.**

$$
D_1 = \frac{T_{\text{exposure},1}}{T_{\text{allow},1}} \times 100 = \frac{2.5}{1.516} \times 100 = 164.9\ \%
$$

$$
D_2 = \frac{5.5}{4.0} \times 100 = 137.5\ \%
$$

$$
D_{\text{total}} = 164.9 + 137.5 = 302.4\ \%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TWA}_{\text{OSHA}} \approx 101.4\ \text{dB(A)}}
$$

**Acceptable working answer for KTU:** the 8-h TWA is **> 100 dB(A)** and is therefore **non-compliant** with the OSHA 90 dB(A) criterion.

**(b) Calculation of 8-h TWA — ISO/ACGIH (3 dB) (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Allowable time at each level.**

$$
T_{\text{allow}} = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/3}}
$$

**For 102 dB(A):**

$$
T_1 = \frac{8}{2^{(102-90)/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{4}} = \frac{8}{16} = 0.5\ \text{h}
$$

**For 95 dB(A):**

$$
T_2 = \frac{8}{2^{(95-90)/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{1.667}} = \frac{8}{3.175} = 2.52\ \text{h}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the dose-equivalent level in dB.**

The ISO 1999 / ACGIH energy-equivalent TWA is:

$$
L_{\text{eq}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{T_1 \cdot 10^{L_1/10} + T_2 \cdot 10^{L_2/10}}{8}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**

$$
L_{\text{eq}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2.5 \times 10^{10.2} + 5.5 \times 10^{9.5}}{8}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Compute the two terms.**

$$
2.5 \times 10^{10.2} = 2.5 \times 1.585 \times 10^{10} = 3.963 \times 10^{10}
$$

$$
5.5 \times 10^{9.5} = 5.5 \times 3.162 \times 10^{9} = 1.739 \times 10^{10}
$$

**Step 5: Add and divide by 8.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = (3.963 + 1.739) \times 10^{10} = 5.702 \times 10^{10}
$$

$$
\text{Mean power} = \frac{5.702 \times 10^{10}}{8} = 7.128 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 6: Take 10 log₁₀.**

$$
L_{\text{eq}} = 10 \log_{10}(7.128 \times 10^{9}) = 10 \times 9.853 = 98.53\ \text{dB(A)}
$$

**Step 7: Comparative statement.**

$$
\boxed{L_{\text{eq, ISO}} \approx 98.5\ \text{dB(A)}}
$$

The **ISO 3 dB method is more conservative** because it equates sound energy rather than time, reflecting the equal-energy hypothesis for hearing damage.

**Step 8: Control measures (mandatory listing).**

*Engineering Controls:*
- Acoustic enclosures on shake-out grids
- Vibration dampers on fettling benches
- Silencers on blower intakes
- Use of rubber-lined shake-out decks

*Administrative Controls:*
- Hearing Conservation Programme
- Job rotation to limit individual exposure
- Audiometric testing every 6 months
- Signage in 90 dB(A) zones

*PPE:*
- Ear muffs (SNR ≥ 30 dB) for 95–105 dB(A)
- Double protection (plugs + muffs) for > 100 dB(A)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not mix the 3 dB and 5 dB exchange rates in the same calculation** — pick one method per question and stick to it.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert 10.2 dB to 10^10.2** is a frequent cause of a 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Failing to state that ISO 3 dB is more conservative** loses 1 mark on the comparative sub-part.
> 4. **PPE alone is not a complete control** — the examiner expects engineering control FIRST, then administrative, then PPE. Listing PPE only will cap marks.
> 5. **Units in WBGT: always quote "°C-WBGT"** — not just °C.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Foundry Health Hazards**
>
> - **Definition of Foundry:** metal melting → moulding → pouring → solidification → fettling.
> - **Six hazard families:** dust, heat, noise, chemical, radiation, fire/explosion.
> - **Silica TLV-TWA = 0.025 mg/m³** (ACGIH); schedule II of Factories Act, 1948.
> - **Silicosis:** irreversible, IARC Group 1 carcinogen; NO cure.
> - **PEL noise = 90 dB(A) for 8 h** (Factories Act Sch. XI).
> - **Exchange rate:** 5 dB (OSHA) or **3 dB (ISO/ACGIH)**; ISO is more conservative.
> - **WBGT (indoor) = 0.7 T_nwb + 0.3 T_g.**
> - **ACGIH TLV for moderate work (acclimatised) = 26.7 °C-WBGT.**
> - **Capture velocity for canopy hood = 0.5–1.0 m/s; transport velocity for mineral dust = 15–25 m/s.**
> - **Hierarchy: E-S-E-A-P** (Eliminate → Substitute → Engineer → Administer → Protect).
> - **IDLH for CO = 1200 ppm**; respirable silica IDLH (legacy) = 25 000 mg/m³.
> - **Statutes to quote in every answer:** Factories Act 1948 §§ 38, 41, 42, 87; **IS 9566:2007**; **ILO Code 2018**; **AERB Safety Code (SSRP)**.
> - **PPE for pouring:** aluminised suit (700–1000 °C rated) + IR face shield + leather gloves + gum boots.
> - **PPE for fettling:** FFP3 respirator + ear muffs + safety goggles + leather apron.
> - **Always prefer engineering control over PPE** — examiners deduct marks for "PPE only" answers.
> - **Recall the 10-step process chain** when asked to "trace the hazard to its origin."
> - **Always state units** (mg/m³, dB(A), °C-WBGT, ppm) — unit errors are a 1-mark penalty.
> - **Mention "no safe exposure threshold for silica"** (IARC) — every exposure carries risk.
> - **Mandatory: medical surveillance** — pre-employment + annual + exit; chest X-ray (ILO classification) + spirometry.

```
End of Module 4 Notes — Foundry Health Hazards
───────────────────────────────────────────────
• Six hazard families, four control levels, one statutory umbrella
• Master the formulas, cite the statutes, and score full marks
```

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Work Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Environment (KTU 2024 Official Definition)**
> The *Work Environment* refers to the complete set of surrounding conditions, internal and external, in which an employee performs their assigned occupational tasks. It encompasses all physical, chemical, biological, ergonomic, and psychosocial factors that may affect the health, safety, productivity, and well-being of the worker during the course of employment.

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) syllabus for **OESFT725 – Safety in Engineering Industry**, the work environment is categorized into **five primary domains** recognized globally by the *International Labour Organization (ILO)* and *World Health Organization (WHO)*:

| Domain | Description |
|---|---|
| **Physical Environment** | Noise, vibration, lighting, temperature, humidity, radiation |
| **Chemical Environment** | Dusts, fumes, vapours, gases, mists |
| **Biological Environment** | Bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, bio-aerosols |
| **Ergonomic Environment** | Posture, workstation design, manual handling, repetitive motion |
| **Psychosocial Environment** | Stress, work pressure, fatigue, harassment, organizational culture |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Work Environment as the "Weather and Home" of a Worker.**
> Just as a plant needs the right sunlight, water, and soil to grow, an employee needs the right temperature, air, light, tools, and mental peace to work safely and productively. If the "weather" inside a factory is too hot, too noisy, or full of toxic fumes — the plant (worker) will wilt (get sick or injured). A safe work environment is like a **greenhouse** — it controls every harmful element so the worker can *flourish*.

### 1.3 Why It Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the **Factories Act, 1948 (India)** and the **Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions (OSH) Code, 2020**, the employer is *legally bound* to provide a safe and healthy work environment. KTU examiners frequently frame questions on this topic from a **statutory compliance** angle.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of a Healthy Work Environment

1. **Safety** – Absence of physical hazards and risk of accidents.
2. **Health** – Protection from occupational diseases and long-term illnesses.
3. **Comfort & Well-being** – Ergonomic support and psychological wellness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember the 5 domains:** ***"P-C-B-E-Psy"*** → *Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Work Environment

#### 2.1.1 Noise
Noise is **unwanted sound** that can cause hearing loss, stress, and reduced concentration. Measured in **decibels (dB)**.

- **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL):** **90 dB(A) for 8 hours** (Factories Act, 1948).
- Every **+5 dB** doubles the sound pressure energy.
- Every **+10 dB** is perceived as "twice as loud" to the human ear.

#### 2.1.2 Lighting
Adequate illumination prevents eye strain and accidents. Measured in **Lux (lx)**.

- Minimum illumination for general office work: **300 lux**.
- Detailed bench/machining work: **500–1000 lux**.
- Poor lighting is a silent cause of accidents.

#### 2.1.3 Temperature & Humidity
- Comfortable range: **20°C to 27°C** (dry bulb).
- Relative humidity (RH): **40% to 60%** is ideal.
- Heat stress indices: **WBGT (Wet Bulb Globe Temperature)** is the global standard.

#### 2.1.4 Vibration
- **Whole-Body Vibration (WBV):** From driving trucks, forklifts → spinal issues.
- **Hand-Arm Vibration (HAV):** From power tools → "Vibration White Finger" disease.

#### 2.1.5 Radiation
- **Ionizing:** X-rays, gamma rays → DNA damage, cancer.
- **Non-ionizing:** UV, infrared, microwave, laser → burns, cataract, skin damage.

### 2.2 Chemical Work Environment

Toxic substances enter the body through three routes:
1. **Inhalation** (most common — lungs).
2. **Skin Absorption** (contact).
3. **Ingestion** (eating with contaminated hands).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** = The maximum airborne concentration of a chemical a worker can be exposed to day after day without adverse health effects. Measured in **ppm (parts per million)** or **mg/m³**.

- **PEL** → set by OSHA (USA) / Indian Factories Rules.
- **TLV-TWA** → Time-Weighted Average over 8 hours.
- **STEL** → Short-Term Exposure Limit (15-min average).
- **IDLH** → Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health.

### 2.3 Biological Work Environment
Common in healthcare, food processing, sewage, agriculture:
- **Bacteria** → Tuberculosis, Legionnaires' disease.
- **Viruses** → Influenza, Hepatitis B/C, COVID-19.
- **Fungi** → Aspergillosis (in damp buildings).
- **Biological hazards** are controlled by **universal precautions**, PPE, and **ventilation**.

### 2.4 Ergonomic Work Environment

> [!NOTE]
> **Ergonomics** = "Fitting the job to the worker, not the worker to the job."

Key ergonomic issues:
- **Musculoskeletal Disorders (MSDs)**: Back pain, carpal tunnel syndrome.
- **Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI)**.
- Poor workstation design → awkward posture → chronic injury.
- **Manual handling** (lifting > **25 kg** for adult males, > **15 kg** for females) is a leading cause of workplace injury.

### 2.5 Psychosocial Work Environment

Modern workplaces face growing risks:
- **Occupational stress** → heart disease, mental illness.
- **Workplace bullying & harassment**.
- **Burnout syndrome** → emotional exhaustion, depersonalization.
- **Shift work disorder** → circadian rhythm disruption.
- **Karoshi** (Japan) → death from overwork.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to **WHO (2022)**, depression and anxiety cost the global economy **12 billion working days** and **US $1 trillion** in lost productivity annually. This makes the psychosocial environment a *top-priority* KTU topic.

### 2.6 Ventilation & Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)

- **Natural Ventilation:** Windows, doors, roof vents.
- **Mechanical Ventilation:** Exhaust fans, HVAC systems.
- **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV):** Captures contaminants at the source (e.g., welding fume extraction).

**IAQ Parameters (per ASHRAE Standard 62.1):**
- CO₂ < **1000 ppm** (indicator of fresh air adequacy).
- Minimum fresh air: **8.5 L/s per person** (offices).

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit | Reference / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, W/m^2$ |
| Sound Pressure Level | $L_P = 20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$ | dB | $P_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa$ |
| Noise Dose (OSHA) | $D = \sum (C_i / T_i) \times 100$ | % | $C_i$ = time at level $i$, $T_i$ = allowed time |
| WBGT Index (Outdoor) | $WBGT = 0.7\,T_{nwb} + 0.2\,T_{g} + 0.1\,T_{db}$ | °C | $T_{nwb}$ = natural wet bulb |
| WBGT Index (Indoor) | $WBGT = 0.7\,T_{nwb} + 0.3\,T_{g}$ | °C | No solar load |
| Lifting Index (NIOSH) | $LI = \text{Actual Load} / \text{Recommended Load Limit (RLL)}$ | — | $LI > 1$ = increased risk |
| Recommended Weight Limit | $RLL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$ | kg | $LC = 23 \, kg$ load constant |
| Ventilation Rate per Person | $Q = \text{Air Changes per Hour} \times \text{Room Volume} / \text{No. of occupants}$ | L/s | ASHRAE 62.1 |
| Air Changes per Hour | $ACH = (Q / V) \times 3600$ | hr⁻¹ | $Q$ in m³/s, $V$ in m³ |
| Lux to Foot-candle | $1 \, fc \approx 10.76 \, lux$ | — | Quick conversion |
| Dose-Response (Toxicity) | $LD_{50}$ | mg/kg | Lethal dose for 50% population |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The NIOSH Lifting Equation is one of the **most-tested formulas** in KTU papers. Always state the **6 multipliers** (HM, VM, DM, AM, FM, CM) and the **load constant LC = 23 kg**.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Critical Work Environment Factor |
|---|---|
| Mining | Dust, methane gas, confined spaces, noise |
| Construction | Falls, silica dust, noise, heat stress |
| Manufacturing | Noise, chemicals, repetitive motion |
| IT/Offices | Ergonomics, IAQ, lighting, psychosocial |
| Healthcare | Biological hazards, sharps, stress |
| Oil & Gas | Flammable gases, H₂S, confined spaces |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Sound Pressure Level (SPL) Conversion

**Problem:** A lathe machine produces a sound pressure of **0.2 Pa** at the operator's ear. Find the Sound Pressure Level in dB. (Reference pressure $P_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa$).

**Step 1 — Write the formula:**

$$L_P = 20 \, \log_{10}\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) \quad \text{(in dB)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$L_P = 20 \, \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.2}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) \quad \text{(in dB)}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the ratio:**

$$L_P = 20 \, \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.2}{0.00002}\right) = 20 \, \log_{10}(10000) \quad \text{(in dB)}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$L_P = 20 \times 4 = 80 \, \text{dB}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**
At **80 dB**, no hearing protection is required for 8-hour exposure (PEL is 90 dB). However, this is close to conversational level and may contribute to long-term hearing strain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical insight:** If the same machine's sound pressure *doubles* (from 0.2 Pa to 0.4 Pa), the SPL increases by only **6 dB** (not 100%). This logarithmic nature of dB confuses many students in KTU exams.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: OSHA Noise Dose Calculation

**Problem:** A worker is exposed to **95 dB for 2 hours** and **100 dB for 1 hour**. Calculate the daily noise dose and determine compliance with OSHA's PEL (TWA = 90 dB, Criterion Level = 90 dB, Exchange Rate = 5 dB).

**Step 1 — Use OSHA's Noise Dose Formula:**

$$D(\%) = 100 \times \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

where $C_i$ = actual exposure time at level $i$, $T_i$ = OSHA reference duration at level $i$.

**Step 2 — Find $T_i$ using OSHA's exchange rate formula:**

$$T_i = \frac{8}{2^{(L_i - 90)/5}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_i$ for $L_i = 95 \, dB$:**

$$T_{95} = \frac{8}{2^{(95-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^1} = \frac{8}{2} = 4 \, \text{hours}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $T_i$ for $L_i = 100 \, dB$:**

$$T_{100} = \frac{8}{2^{(100-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^2} = \frac{8}{4} = 2 \, \text{hours}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into dose formula:**

$$D = 100 \times \left(\frac{2}{4} + \frac{1}{2}\right) = 100 \times (0.5 + 0.5) = 100\%$$

**Step 6 — Calculate Time-Weighted Average (TWA):**

$$TWA = 16.61 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{D}{100}\right) + 90$$

$$TWA = 16.61 \times \log_{10}(1) + 90 = 16.61 \times 0 + 90 = 90 \, \text{dB}$$

**Step 7 — Interpret:**
- Dose = **100%** → **Exactly at the OSHA permissible limit**.
- $TWA = 90 \, dB$ → Marginal compliance.
- Hearing protection is *strongly recommended* to reduce risk.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: NIOSH Lifting Equation — Evaluating a Manual Handling Task

**Problem:** A male worker lifts a **20 kg crate** with:
- Horizontal distance (H) = 30 cm
- Vertical location (V) = 80 cm (from floor)
- Vertical travel distance (D) = 50 cm
- Asymmetric angle (A) = 30°
- Frequency Multiplier (FM) = 0.85 (lifts 2 per minute)
- Coupling (C) = Fair

Find the **Lifting Index (LI)** and comment on risk.

**Step 1 — Recall the six multipliers and their formulas:**

| Multiplier | Symbol | Formula / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Horizontal Multiplier | HM | $HM = 25 / H$ (H in cm) |
| Vertical Multiplier | VM | $1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert$ |
| Distance Multiplier | DM | $0.82 + (4.5 / D)$ |
| Asymmetric Multiplier | AM | $1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$ (A in degrees) |
| Frequency Multiplier | FM | From NIOSH table (0.85 for 2 lifts/min, 1 hr) |
| Coupling Multiplier | CM | 0.90 (Poor), 0.95 (Fair), 1.00 (Good) |

**Step 2 — Compute each multiplier:**

$$HM = \frac{25}{30} = 0.833$$

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 80 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \times 5 = 1 - 0.015 = 0.985$$

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{50} = 0.82 + 0.09 = 0.91$$

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \times 30 = 1 - 0.096 = 0.904$$

$$FM = 0.85 \quad (\text{given, from NIOSH table})$$

$$CM = 0.95 \quad (\text{Fair coupling})$$

**Step 3 — Compute Recommended Weight Limit (RWL):**

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.833 \times 0.985 \times 0.91 \times 0.904 \times 0.85 \times 0.95$$

**Step 4 — Multiply step-by-step:**

$$RWL = 23 \times 0.833 = 19.159$$

$$RWL = 19.159 \times 0.985 = 18.872$$

$$RWL = 18.872 \times 0.91 = 17.174$$

$$RWL = 17.174 \times 0.904 = 15.525$$

$$RWL = 15.525 \times 0.85 = 13.196$$

$$RWL = 13.196 \times 0.95 = 12.536 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Lifting Index:**

$$LI = \frac{\text{Actual Load}}{RWL} = \frac{20}{12.536} = 1.595$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result:**
- $LI \leq 1.0$ → Safe for most workers.
- $1.0 < LI \leq 3.0$ → Increased risk; **engineering/administrative controls needed**.
- $LI > 3.0$ → **Unacceptable risk** for most workers.

Since **LI ≈ 1.6**, this lifting task poses **moderate risk**. Recommended actions:
- Reduce crate weight below 12.5 kg, OR
- Improve posture (reduce H and A), OR
- Use mechanical aids.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Workplace Hazards and Their Controls

| Hazard Domain | Specific Hazard | Health Effect | Control Measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical | Noise > 85 dB | Hearing loss (NIHL) | Enclosures, ear plugs/muffs |
| Physical | Poor lighting | Eye strain, accidents | 300–500 lux, glare control |
| Physical | Heat stress | Heat stroke, dehydration | WBGT monitoring, hydration |
| Physical | Vibration | Raynaud's syndrome | Anti-vibration gloves |
| Chemical | Silica dust | Silicosis | Wet cutting, respirators |
| Chemical | Benzene | Leukemia | Substitution, LEV |
| Biological | Tuberculosis | Respiratory infection | Masks, ventilation |
| Ergonomic | Awkward posture | Back pain, MSDs | Adjustable workstations |
| Psychosocial | Long hours | Burnout, CVD | Work-life balance policies |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Work Environment Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Work Environment] --> B[Physical Environment]
    A --> C[Chemical Environment]
    A --> D[Biological Environment]
    A --> E[Ergonomic Environment]
    A --> F[Psychosocial Environment]

    B --> B1[Noise]
    B --> B2[Lighting]
    B --> B3[Temperature]
    B --> B4[Vibration]
    B --> B5[Radiation]

    C --> C1[Dusts]
    C --> C2[Fumes]
    C --> C3[Vapours]
    C --> C4[Gases]
    C --> C5[Mists]

    D --> D1[Bacteria]
    D --> D2[Viruses]
    D --> D3[Fungi]
    D --> D4[Parasites]

    E --> E1[Posture]
    E --> E2[Workstation Design]
    E --> E3[Manual Handling]
    E --> E4[Repetitive Motion]

    F --> F1[Stress]
    F --> F2[Fatique]
    F --> F3[Bullying]
    F --> F4[Shift Work Disorder]

    B1 --> X1[Engineering Controls]
    B2 --> X1
    B3 --> X1
    B4 --> X1
    B5 --> X1
    C1 --> X2[Administrative Controls]
    C2 --> X2
    C3 --> X2
    C4 --> X2
    C5 --> X2
    D1 --> X3[PPE and Hygiene]
    D2 --> X3
    D3 --> X3
    D4 --> X3
    E1 --> X4[Ergonomic Redesign]
    E2 --> X4
    E3 --> X4
    E4 --> X4
    F1 --> X5[Counselling and Policy]
    F2 --> X5
    F3 --> X5
    F4 --> X5
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Controls Applied to Work Environment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1 Elimination] --> L2[Level 2 Substitution]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 Engineering Controls]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Administrative Controls]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 PPE]

    L1 --> M1[Remove hazard entirely]
    L2 --> M2[Replace with less hazardous process]
    L3 --> M3[LEV, enclosures, machine guarding]
    L4 --> M4[Training, rotation, SOPs]
    L5 --> M5[Respirators, ear plugs, gloves]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Hierarchy of Controls (NIOSH)** is a *favorite KTU question*. Always draw it as a **pyramid** in exam answers — Elimination at the top (most effective), PPE at the bottom (least effective).

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow: Worker Exposure → Health Effect → Control Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Hazard Source] --> R[Route of Exposure]
    R --> W[Worker]
    W --> E[Health Effect]
    E --> C[Control Implementation]
    C --> M[Monitoring and Review]
    M --> H
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Indoor Air Quality (IAQ) Management System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Outdoor Air Intake] --> F1[Filtration Stage]
    F1 --> H[Heating or Cooling Coil]
    H --> M[Mixing Chamber]
    M --> D[Distribution Ducts]
    D --> Z[Occupied Zone]

    Z --> S1[CO2 Sensor]
    Z --> S2[Temperature Sensor]
    Z --> S3[Humidity Sensor]
    Z --> S4[Particulate Sensor]

    S1 --> CT[Central Controller]
    S2 --> CT
    S3 --> CT
    S4 --> CT

    CT --> ACT[Actuator - Damper Control]
    ACT --> F1

    Z --> EX[Exhaust Ventilation]
    EX --> OS[Outside Discharge]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Concept Map: Stress → Outcome Pathway (Psychosocial Environment)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Workplace Stressors] --> S1[Long Working Hours]
    S --> S2[Job Insecurity]
    S --> S3[Poor Management]
    S --> S4[Work Family Conflict]

    S1 --> P[Physiological Response]
    S2 --> P
    S3 --> P
    S4 --> P

    P --> P1[Cortisol Elevation]
    P1 --> P2[Hypertension]
    P2 --> P3[Cardiovascular Disease]

    S1 --> B[Behavioural Response]
    S2 --> B
    S3 --> B
    S4 --> B

    B --> B1[Smoking]
    B --> B2[Alcohol Use]
    B --> B3[Absenteeism]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** In KTU exam answers, always link the **psychosocial environment to physiological outcomes** using a clear cause-effect chain. Examiners reward **systems thinking**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Work Environment". List its five major classifications as per the KTU 2024 syllabus.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The *Work Environment* refers to the sum of all internal and external conditions — physical, chemical, biological, ergonomic, and psychosocial — in which an employee performs their tasks, affecting their safety, health, comfort, and productivity.
>
> **Five Classifications:**
> 1. **Physical Environment** – noise, lighting, temperature, vibration, radiation.
> 2. **Chemical Environment** – dusts, fumes, vapours, gases, mists.
> 3. **Biological Environment** – bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites.
> 4. **Ergonomic Environment** – posture, workstation design, manual handling.
> 5. **Psychosocial Environment** – stress, fatigue, harassment, work culture.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a Threshold Limit Value (TLV)? Differentiate between TLV-TWA and TLV-STEL.*

**Model Answer:**

> **TLV (Threshold Limit Value):** The airborne concentration of a chemical substance below which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed day after day without adverse health effects.
>
> | Parameter | TLV-TWA | TLV-STEL |
> |---|---|---|
> | Full Form | Time-Weighted Average | Short-Term Exposure Limit |
> | Duration | 8 hours/day, 40 hrs/week | 15 minutes (max 4×/day) |
> | Purpose | Long-term chronic exposure | Acute short-term peaks |
> | Example (Toluene) | 100 ppm | 150 ppm |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **physical work environment** factors. Discuss the **Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL)** for noise as per the Indian Factories Act, 1948.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **hierarchy of controls** used to manage workplace hazards. Why is elimination considered the most effective control?

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **physical work environment** encompasses all measurable physical agents present in the workplace that can cause harm to workers. The major factors are:

**1. Noise:**
- Definition: Unwanted sound that may cause hearing impairment, stress, and reduced productivity.
- Measurement: Sound Pressure Level (SPL) in **decibels (dB)**.
- Formula: $L_P = 20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$ where $P_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa$.
- **PEL (Factories Act, 1948):** **90 dB(A) for 8 hours** continuous exposure.
- For every **+5 dB** increase, permissible exposure time is **halved**.
- Continuous exposure > **115 dB(A)** is prohibited.
- Control: Engineering controls (enclosures, mufflers), administrative (job rotation), PPE (ear plugs/muffs).

**2. Lighting:**
- Adequate illumination is critical for visual tasks and accident prevention.
- Measurement: **Lux (lx)**.
- Standards (Indian Standard IS 3646):
  - General office: **300 lx**
  - Detailed work: **500–1000 lx**
  - Very fine work: **1500–3000 lx**
- Issues: Glare, flicker, shadows, poor contrast.

**3. Temperature & Humidity:**
- Comfort zone: **20°C to 27°C** (DBT).
- Relative humidity: **40–60%**.
- Heat stress assessed using **WBGT Index**.
- Cold stress can cause hypothermia, frostbite.

**4. Vibration:**
- **Whole-Body Vibration (WBV):** From vehicles → spinal disorders.
- **Hand-Arm Vibration (HAV):** From power tools → Vibration White Finger.
- ISO 2631 sets exposure limits.

**5. Radiation:**
- **Ionizing:** X-ray, gamma → cancer, genetic damage.
- **Non-ionizing:** UV → skin burn; IR → cataract; Laser → retinal damage.
- ALARA principle (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).

**Conclusion:** Managing the physical work environment requires engineering controls, periodic monitoring, and strict compliance with statutory limits.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The **Hierarchy of Controls** is a framework used by safety professionals to determine the most effective way to eliminate or reduce workplace hazards. From most effective to least effective:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Elimination] --> A2[Substitution]
    A2 --> A3[Engineering Controls]
    A3 --> A4[Administrative Controls]
    A4 --> A5[PPE]
```

| Level | Control | Example | Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Elimination** | Physically remove the hazard | Most effective |
| 2 | **Substitution** | Replace toxic chemical with safer one | Very effective |
| 3 | **Engineering Controls** | Machine guards, LEV, enclosures | Effective |
| 4 | **Administrative Controls** | Training, SOPs, job rotation | Moderately effective |
| 5 | **PPE** | Respirators, helmets, gloves | Least effective |

**Why Elimination is the most effective:**
- It *removes the hazard at the source*, so no exposure can occur.
- It does not depend on worker behavior (unlike PPE).
- It has **no ongoing cost** of replacement or maintenance.
- It permanently prevents the hazard from recurring.

**Why PPE is least effective:**
- Depends on correct usage by worker.
- Can fail (e.g., torn gloves).
- Requires continuous supply and training.
- Only protects the *individual*, not the *workplace*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students list the 5 levels in the wrong order or describe PPE first. Marks are awarded for the **correct pyramid sequence**. Always mention that **Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **ergonomic work environment** in detail. Explain the **NIOSH Lifting Equation** and its six multipliers with a suitable example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **psychosocial work environment** and its impact on worker health. List at least four occupational stress management techniques.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Ergonomic Work Environment:**

Ergonomics (or *Human Factors Engineering*) is the science of designing the workplace to fit the worker, thereby reducing fatigue, discomfort, and injury. Key ergonomic hazards include:

- **Poor Posture** → back pain, neck strain, repetitive strain injury (RSI).
- **Improper Workstation Design** → eye strain, carpal tunnel syndrome.
- **Manual Handling** → leading cause of workplace musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs).
- **Repetitive Motion** → tendonitis, tenosynovitis.
- **Forceful Exertion** → muscle tears, herniated discs.
- **Static Postures** → reduced blood circulation, fatigue.

**NIOSH Lifting Equation (1991):**

Used to evaluate manual lifting tasks and determine the **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)**:

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

where **LC = 23 kg** (load constant for most adult population).

| Multiplier | Symbol | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Horizontal Multiplier | HM | $HM = 25 / H$ (H in cm) |
| Vertical Multiplier | VM | $VM = 1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert$ |
| Distance Multiplier | DM | $DM = 0.82 + (4.5 / D)$ |
| Asymmetric Multiplier | AM | $AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$ |
| Frequency Multiplier | FM | From NIOSH table |
| Coupling Multiplier | CM | 0.90 / 0.95 / 1.00 |

**Lifting Index (LI):**

$$LI = \frac{\text{Actual Load}}{\text{RWL}}$$

- $LI \leq 1.0$ → Safe.
- $1.0 < LI \leq 3.0$ → Increased risk.
- $LI > 3.0$ → Unacceptable for most workers.

**Worked Example (also solved in Section 3.3):**

A 20 kg crate lifted with H=30 cm, V=80 cm, D=50 cm, A=30°, FM=0.85, CM=0.95 (Fair) gives **RWL ≈ 12.54 kg** and **LI ≈ 1.6** → moderate risk.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Psychosocial Work Environment:**

The psychosocial work environment refers to the *social, mental, and emotional* aspects of work. It includes organizational culture, management style, workload, interpersonal relationships, and job security.

**Common Psychosocial Hazards:**

1. **Excessive Workload & Long Working Hours** → chronic stress, cardiovascular disease.
2. **Job Insecurity** → anxiety, depression.
3. **Workplace Bullying & Harassment** → mental trauma, PTSD.
4. **Lack of Autonomy** → demotivation, learned helplessness.
5. **Shift Work** → circadian disruption, sleep disorders.
6. **Poor Work-Life Balance** → family conflicts, burnout.

**Health Impacts (WHO 2022 Report):**

- Depression and anxiety cost the global economy **US $1 trillion/year**.
- 12 billion working days lost annually.
- Linked to cardiovascular disease, type-2 diabetes, substance abuse.

**Occupational Stress Management Techniques:**

1. **Organizational Interventions:**
   - Job redesign and rotation.
   - Flexible working hours.
   - Clear role definition and communication.
   - Anti-harassment policies.

2. **Individual Interventions:**
   - Stress management training.
   - Employee Assistance Programs (EAPs).
   - Mindfulness and meditation.
   - Counselling services.

3. **Ergonomic & Environmental Support:**
   - Adequate lighting, ventilation, rest areas.
   - Reasonable workload targets.

4. **Health Promotion:**
   - Regular health check-ups.
   - Yoga, exercise programs.
   - Healthy canteen food.
   - Peer support groups.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *psychosocial hazards* with *physical hazards*. Always emphasize that psychosocial hazards are *invisible* but have *measurable physiological outcomes* (cortisol, BP, heart rate). Examiners expect at least one reference to **WHO or ILO statistics**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Five domains** of work environment: *Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial* (Mnemonic: **P-C-B-E-Psy**).
- **Noise PEL** (Factories Act 1948): **90 dB(A) for 8 hours**; **+5 dB halves exposure time**.
- **dB formula:** $L_P = 20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$, $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$, with $P_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa$.
- **Lighting standards:** Office = **300 lux**, Detailed = **500–1000 lux**.
- **Comfort zone:** **20–27°C**, **40–60% RH**.
- **WBGT** is the global heat-stress index (outdoor includes solar load).
- **TLV-TWA** = 8-hr average; **STEL** = 15-min short-term; **IDLH** = immediately dangerous.
- **Three exposure routes** for chemicals: *Inhalation, Skin, Ingestion*.
- **NIOSH Lifting Equation:** **6 multipliers** (HM, VM, DM, AM, FM, CM), LC = **23 kg**, **LI > 1** = increased risk.
- **Hierarchy of Controls:** *Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE* (top is best).
- **Psychosocial hazards** cost $1 trillion/year (WHO 2022).
- **OSH Code 2020** (India) governs modern workplace safety laws.
- **ALARA** principle for radiation exposure.
- **ASHRAE 62.1** IAQ standard: CO₂ < **1000 ppm**, fresh air **8.5 L/s/person**.
- **Manual handling limits:** Adult male **≤ 25 kg**, female **≤ 15 kg** (general guideline).
- **Vibration White Finger (VWF)** = classic HAV syndrome.
- **Silicosis, Asbestosis, Byssinosis** = classic occupational dust diseases.

> **Final Tip:** In KTU exams, **always draw diagrams** (Hierarchy pyramid, ergonomic workstation, IAQ system). Even a simple **sketch earns 1–2 extra marks** due to visual presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Handling in Foundries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Material Handling in Foundries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Material Handling in Foundries** refers to the systematic, controlled movement, storage, protection, and control of raw materials, in-process materials (sand, molten metal, cores), and finished castings throughout the foundry workflow — from receipt of raw stock to dispatch of machined components.

In KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) terminology, it is classified under the broader discipline of **Industrial Ergonomics and Occupational Safety**, governed by the **Factories Act 1948 (India)**, the **Indian Standard IS 2204 (Foundry – Safety Code)**, and the **OSHA Standards for the Metal Casting Industry (29 CFR 1910 Subpart Q)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Material handling is one of the highest-hazard activities in a foundry. Statistics show that **~25% of all foundry injuries** are directly or indirectly linked to improper material handling (IS 2204 & NIOSH data). Foundries are classified as **"Hazardous Processes"** under Schedule I of the Factories Act.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **large restaurant kitchen** during a dinner rush:

- The **chef** (melting furnace) needs raw ingredients (metal scrap, pig iron).
- The **waiters** (forklifts, conveyors, cranes) shuttle ingredients, hot pots, and finished plates across the kitchen.
- The **customers' plates** are heavy, hot, and must arrive intact.

A foundry works exactly like this kitchen — but instead of food, it handles **molten metal at 1400°C**, **sharp metal scrap**, and **silica dust**, all of which can be lethal if dropped, spilled, or mishandled. The "waiters" here are cranes, ladles, hoists, and conveyors, and any failure means **burns, crush injuries, explosions, or fatalities**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Foundry Material Movement Metrics (Bold Constants):**
> - **Molten iron density ≈ 7.2 g/cm³** at 1400°C
> - **Molten aluminum density ≈ 2.4 g/cm³** at 700°C
> - **Standard ladle capacity:** 1 to 30 tonnes
> - **Acceptable noise level in foundry:** **< 85 dB(A)** for an 8-hour exposure (IS 7194)

### 1.3 Scope of Material Handling in Foundries

Material handling covers six major stages:

1. **Receipt & Storage** of raw materials (pig iron, scrap, ferroalloys, sand, binders).
2. **In-process transport** (sand to molding station, molten metal from furnace to pouring station).
3. **Pouring & Pouring-bay operations** (ladle transfer, manual/semi-automatic pouring).
4. **Cooling, shake-out, and fettling** (removal of castings from molds).
5. **Shot blasting & heat treatment** movement.
6. **Despatch** of finished castings (palletization, crating, truck loading).

Each stage carries unique hazards and demands specific engineering controls.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Foundry Material Handling Systems

| Category | Examples | Application Stage | Hazard Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual Handling** | Hand trolleys, wheelbarrows, manual lifting | Sand, small castings | **High** (musculoskeletal) |
| **Mechanical – Non-Powered** | Hand pallet trucks, roller conveyors | Molding floor | **Low–Moderate** |
| **Mechanical – Powered** | Belt conveyors, bucket elevators, screw conveyors | Sand plant, charge handling | **Moderate** |
| **Overhead Lifting** | EOT cranes, jib cranes, hoists, chain blocks | Ladle transfer, heavy castings | **Very High** |
| **Hydraulic / Pneumatic** | Ladle skimmers, hydraulic presses | Pouring, knockout | **High** |
| **Automated / Robotic** | Pouring robots, AGVs | High-volume foundries | **Low (worker) / High (engineer)** |
| **Special Foundry Equipment** | Foundry ladles, pouring cups, mono-rail systems | Molten metal transfer | **Critical** |

### 2.2 Why Material Handling in Foundries is Inherently Hazardous

The "Why" behind foundry material handling being high-risk:

- **Thermal load:** Molten metal carries **~330 kJ/kg** of sensible heat for iron at pouring temperature — capable of causing **instant 3rd-degree burns**.
- **Weight density:** A standard 5-ton ladle filled with molten iron weighs **~5.7 tonnes** when full.
- **Reactivity:** Molten metal reacts violently with water (steam explosion) and certain contaminants.
- **Ergonomic load:** Sand molds, cores, and castings are often lifted in awkward postures leading to **lower-back injuries** (the most reported injury in foundries — **~30% of total**).
- **Dust exposure:** Silica sand handling generates **respirable crystalline silica** → causes **silicosis**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically exact and aligned with standard rigging/sling engineering principles referenced in **IS 2204** and **ASME B30.9**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vertical Sling Tension | $T_v = W$ | N or kgf | Straight vertical lift |
| 2 | Two-Leg Sling Tension (equal legs) | $T = \dfrac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$ | N or kgf | Two-leg chain/wire-rope sling |
| 3 | Multi-leg Sling Tension (n legs) | $T = \dfrac{W}{n \sin\theta}$ | N or kgf | n-leg sling |
| 4 | Effective Working Load Limit | $WLL = \dfrac{SWL_{sling}}{S.F.}$ | tonnes | Sling selection |
| 5 | Standard Safety Factor (sling) | $S.F. = 5$ (normal) | dimensionless | IS 2204 / ASME B30.9 |
| 6 | Crane Hook Load Moment | $M = W \times R$ | N·m | Crane stability check |
| 7 | Crane Tip-Over Margin | $R_{max} \leq \dfrac{W_{counter} \times d}{W_{load}}$ | m | Crane operating radius |
| 8 | Slag/Molten Metal Heat Loss (approx.) | $\Delta T = \dfrac{h \cdot A \cdot t}{m \cdot c_p}$ | °C | Ladle heat retention |
| 9 | Required Hoist Power | $P = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{\eta}$ | W or kW | Hoist motor sizing |
| 10 | Conveyor Belt Power | $P = T_1 - T_2 \times v$ | W | Horizontal/Inclined belts |

**Where:**
- $W$ = load weight (N or kg)
- $\theta$ = sling angle from horizontal (in degrees)
- $v$ = lifting/linear velocity (m/s)
- $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency (typically **0.75 to 0.90**)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of metal (J/kg·K)
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

These formulas are **not academic exercises** — they are **daily-used calculations** for:

- **Foundry rigging supervisors** selecting slings for ladles.
- **Maintenance engineers** calculating crane load charts.
- **Safety officers** verifying crane radius for a given ladle weight.
- **Design engineers** sizing hoists and ladle transfer cars.
- **Ergonomists** deciding manual vs. mechanical handling thresholds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manual Lifting Threshold (NIOSH Lifting Equation):**
> Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) = **23 kg** under ideal conditions. Any foundry item exceeding **25 kg per person** must be mechanically handled.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Two-Leg Sling Tension

**Problem Statement (KTU Standard):**
A foundry ladle weighing **$W = 1000$ kg** is to be lifted using a two-leg wire-rope sling. The sling legs make an angle $\theta = 60°$ with the horizontal. Find the tension in each leg.

**Step 1 – Define the Geometry**
Each sling leg makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. The vertical component of each leg's tension must together support the entire weight $W$.

**Step 2 – Free Body Diagram (conceptually)**
Let $T$ be the tension in each leg. The vertical component of each leg is:

$$
T_{vertical} = T \sin\theta
$$

**Step 3 – Force Equilibrium (vertical)**

$$
2T \sin\theta = W
$$

**Step 4 – Solve for Tension**

$$
T = \frac{W}{2 \sin\theta}
$$

**Step 5 – Substitute Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 1000 \text{ kgf (assuming } g \approx 10 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ for simplicity)} \\
\theta &= 60° \\
\sin 60° &= \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{1000}{2 \times 0.866} \\
T &= \frac{1000}{1.732} \\
T &\approx 577.35 \text{ kgf per leg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 – Interpretation**
Even though the load is only **1000 kg**, each sling leg carries **~577 kgf**, and as $\theta$ decreases (sharper sling angle), $T$ **increases dramatically**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** At $\theta = 30°$ (very sharp angle), $\sin 30° = 0.5$, so $T = 1000$ kgf per leg — **double** the value at $60°$. This is why KTU examiners and safety codes **forbid sling angles below $30°$**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Crane Maximum Operating Radius

**Problem Statement:**
A foundry EOT crane has a counterweight of **$W_c = 8$ tonnes** placed at a fixed distance **$d = 4$ m** from the crane centerline. The ladle weight is **$W_L = 5$ tonnes**. Determine the **maximum safe operating radius** $R_{max}$.

**Step 1 – Concept of Stability**
The crane is stable as long as the **overturning moment** by the load does not exceed the **restoring moment** by the counterweight.

**Step 2 – Equilibrium Equation**

$$
W_L \times R_{max} = W_c \times d
$$

**Step 3 – Solve for $R_{max}$**

$$
R_{max} = \frac{W_c \times d}{W_L}
$$

**Step 4 – Substitute Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \frac{8 \times 4}{5} \\
R_{max} &= \frac{32}{5} \\
R_{max} &= 6.4 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Conclusion**
The crane may operate safely up to a radius of **6.4 m**. Beyond this, the crane **tips over**. Always refer to the **manufacturer's load-radius chart**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hazard vs. Control Measure

| Material Handled | Hazard | Engineering Control | Administrative Control | PPE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Molten metal in ladle** | Burns, splash, explosion | Ladle preheating, dry ladles, refractory lining | SOP for ladle transfer, certified riggers | Aluminized suit, face shield, leggings |
| **Sand (silica)** | Silicosis, dust explosion | Wet methods, local exhaust ventilation, enclosed conveyors | Periodic medical examination, training | FFP2/FFP3 respirator |
| **Finished castings** | Crush injury, sharp edges | Use of C-hooks, mechanical tongs | Two-person lift ban above 25 kg, color-coded zones | Safety shoes (steel toe), gloves |
| **Scrap metal** | Cuts, tetanus, snagging | Magnetic separators, shredders, bins with covers | Pre-sorting, scrap inspection SOP | Cut-resistant gloves, arm guards |
| **Cores & binders** | Chemical burns (phenolic/ureic resins) | Closed binder systems, fume extraction | MSDS-based training, spill kits | Chemical-resistant gloves, goggles |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Foundry Material Handling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Receipt Yard] --> B[Scrap Inspection and Sorting]
    B --> C[Storage Bins]
    C --> D[Sand Plant]
    D --> D1[Sand Preparation]
    D --> D2[Core Making]
    D1 --> E[Molding Station]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Mold Assembly and Closing]
    F --> G[Charging Furnace]
    G --> H[Melting Furnace]
    H --> I[Molten Metal Transfer via Ladle]
    I --> J[Pouring Bay]
    J --> K[Cooling and Shakeout]
    K --> L[Fettling and Shot Blasting]
    L --> M[Heat Treatment]
    M --> N[Inspection and Dispatch]
    A1[In-Plant Logistics] -.controls.-> A
    A1 -.controls.-> B
    A1 -.controls.-> C
    A1 -.controls.-> E
    A1 -.controls.-> I
    A1 -.controls.-> J
    A1 -.controls.-> L
    A1 -.controls.-> N
    H1[Safety Systems: PPE, Fire Suppression, Fume Extraction, Ladle Preheat Station] -.protects.-> H
    H1 -.protects.-> I
    H1 -.protects.-> J
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Hierarchy of Material Handling Controls (Adapted from NIOSH)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Elimination: Avoid manual handling where feasible] --> L1[Substitution: Use mechanical aids]
    L1 --> L2[Engineering Controls: Cranes, hoists, conveyors]
    L2 --> L3[Administrative Controls: SOPs, training, rotation]
    L3 --> L4[PPE: Gloves, respirator, apron, face shield]
    L4 --> L5[Worker Last Line of Defence]
    L0 -.Top Priority.-> L4
    L4 -.Lowest Reliability.-> L5
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Ladle Transfer Safety Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Ladle Preheating to 600C] --> S2[Refractory Inspection]
    S2 --> S3[Slag Skimming]
    S3 --> S4[Weight Verification and Load Chart Match]
    S4 --> S5[Sling/Chain Selection Angle above 60 degrees]
    S5 --> S6[Test Lift 100mm and Hold 30 seconds]
    S6 --> S7[Full Lift and Transfer Along Designated Path]
    S7 --> S8[Controlled Pouring at Receiving Station]
    S8 --> S9[Post-Transfer Ladle Cleaning and Storage]
    S9 --> S10[Documented Shift Handover]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Decision Logic — Manual vs Mechanical Handling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the load under 25 kg?} -->|Yes| Q2{Is posture neutral and grip good?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A1[Manual Lift Permitted]
    Q2 -->|No| A2[Use Mechanical Aid]
    Q1 -->|No| A3[Mechanical Handling Mandatory]
    A1 --> P[Proceed with NIOSH Lift Equation]
    A2 --> P
    A3 --> P
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define material handling. List any four hazards associated with material handling in foundries. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

Material handling is the safe and efficient movement, storage, and control of materials throughout the production process.

In a foundry, four major hazards are:

1. **Burns and explosions** from molten metal splashes (especially with wet ladles).
2. **Crush injuries** from falling ladles, castings, or crane failures.
3. **Silicosis** from prolonged exposure to respirable crystalline silica during sand handling.
4. **Musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs)** from manual lifting of heavy molds, cores, and castings.

---

#### **Question 2: What is the recommended safe sling angle for lifting loads in foundries, and why? [KTU University Exam — Sample, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

The **recommended safe sling angle** is **≥ 60°** with the horizontal (and never below 30°).

**Reason:** As the sling angle $\theta$ decreases, the tension $T = \dfrac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$ in each leg **increases sharply**. A small angle multiplies the force on each sling leg, risking sling failure, hook damage, and load slippage.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose marks on the following:
> - **Forgetting to convert units** (kgf vs N, tonnes vs kg).
> - **Writing the formula but not substituting values** (valuator awards only 2–3 marks out of 7).
> - **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., "assume $\sin 60° = 0.866$").
> - **Skipping the standard safety factor of 5** when computing WLL.
> - **Ignoring the practical interpretation** ("if $\theta < 30°$, sling may snap").
> Always close with a **one-line engineering conclusion** for full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** *Choose either (a) or (b)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A foundry ladle weighing **$W = 2$ tonnes** (empty) is filled with **1.5 tonnes** of molten iron at **1400°C**. It is to be lifted using a **two-leg wire-rope sling** at an angle of **45°** with the horizontal. Calculate:
- (i) The total load to be lifted.
- (ii) The tension in each sling leg.
- (iii) The required Safe Working Load (SWL) of each sling leg assuming a safety factor of 5.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Load**

$$
W_{total} = 2000 + 1500 = 3500 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Sling Leg Tension**

$$
T = \frac{W_{total}}{2 \sin\theta} = \frac{3500}{2 \times \sin 45°}
$$

$$
T = \frac{3500}{2 \times 0.7071} = \frac{3500}{1.4142}
$$

$$
T \approx 2475 \text{ kgf per leg}
$$

**Step 3 — Required SWL of Each Sling**

$$
WLL = T \times S.F. = 2475 \times 5 = 12{,}375 \text{ kgf} \approx 12.4 \text{ tonnes per leg}
$$

**Conclusion:** Use a sling rated at least **13 tonnes per leg**, and prefer a **60° sling angle** to reduce tension.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hierarchy of controls** applied to foundry material handling hazards. Give one example for each level.

**Model Answer Outline (Valuation Key Style):**

1. **Elimination (most effective):** Replace manual sand handling with a fully automated sand plant.
2. **Substitution:** Substitute silica sand with **olivine or chromite sand** to eliminate silicosis risk.
3. **Engineering Controls:** Use **enclosed conveyors, local exhaust ventilation, ladle preheating stations**.
4. **Administrative Controls:** SOPs for ladle transfer, **mandatory PPE checklist**, job rotation, training.
5. **PPE (last line):** Aluminized suit + visor for pouring, respirators for sand handling.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A foundry EOT crane has a counterweight of **10 tonnes** at **3 m** from the centerline. The molten-metal ladle weight is **6 tonnes** (ladle + metal). Calculate the **maximum safe operating radius**. State what happens if the radius is exceeded.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Stability Equation**

$$
W_L \times R_{max} = W_c \times d
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
6 \times R_{max} = 10 \times 3 \Rightarrow R_{max} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:** The crane is stable only up to **5 m radius**. Beyond this, the **overturning moment exceeds the restoring moment**, and the crane **will tip over**, causing catastrophic failure. Operators must strictly follow the crane's **load-radius chart**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** List **eight safety precautions** to be observed while handling molten metal in a foundry. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (Valuation Key Format):**

1. **Dry ladles only** — preheat to remove all moisture (steam explosion prevention).
2. **Inspect refractory lining** before every shift.
3. **Wear full PPE** — aluminized suit, visor, leggings, gloves.
4. **Clear the pouring path** of water, debris, and personnel.
5. **Use only certified slings** with proper SWL.
6. **Never overfill the ladle** — maintain **50 mm freeboard** minimum.
7. **Two-person rule** for any ladle transfer above 2 tonnes.
8. **Keep fire-fighting equipment (Class D extinguisher)** within 5 m of pouring station.
9. **No smoking / no open flames** near ladle transfer zones.
10. **Document each transfer** in the shift logbook.

(Any **eight well-explained points** earn full marks.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (For Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ Foundries are classified as **Hazardous Processes** under the **Factories Act 1948, Schedule I**.
- ✅ **Material handling contributes ~25% of all foundry injuries** (IS 2204).
- ✅ **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit for manual lifting = 23 kg**; above **25 kg → mechanical handling is mandatory**.
- ✅ Standard sling safety factor in foundries = **5**.
- ✅ **Minimum sling angle = 30°**; **preferred angle = 60° or higher**.
- ✅ Two-leg sling tension formula: $T = \dfrac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$.
- ✅ Crane stability equation: $W_L \times R \leq W_c \times d$.
- ✅ **PPE hierarchy in pouring bay:** Aluminized suit → Face shield → Leggings → Gloves → Safety boots.
- ✅ **Molten iron density ≈ 7.2 g/cm³**, **aluminum ≈ 2.4 g/cm³** — useful for quick ladle weight estimation.
- ✅ **Class D fire extinguishers** are mandatory near molten-metal handling zones (NOT water-based).
- ✅ Always perform a **test lift of 100 mm and hold for 30 seconds** before full lift.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- ✅ Reference standards for exam answers: **IS 2204, IS 7194, ASME B30.9, OSHA 29 CFR 1910 Subpart Q, Factories Act 1948**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Foundry Production Cleaning and Finishing Foundry Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Foundry Production Cleaning and Finishing Foundry Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundry Cleaning** is defined as the sequence of post-casting operations performed to **separate the solidified casting from the molding material**, remove adhering sand, burnt-on core sand, gates, risers, runners, and surface scale to obtain a geometrically sound raw casting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foundry Finishing** is defined as the subsequent set of operations performed on a cleaned casting to achieve the **required dimensional accuracy (tolerance), surface finish (Ra value), metallurgical integrity, and visual appearance** as specified by the engineering drawing and quality standards.

The combined chain **Cleaning → Finishing → Inspection** constitutes the **value-addition corridor** of any foundry, where raw castings are transformed into market-ready engineering components.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Think of a foundry casting like a **cake freshly removed from a baking pan**:

| Baking Stage | Foundry Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Cake comes out with crumbs stuck to it | Shake-out — casting separated from sand |
| Trimming burnt edges with a knife | Degating — cutting off gates, risers, runners |
| Brushing off loose crumbs with a stiff brush | Shot/grit blasting — abrasive cleaning |
| Rolling cake gently in sugar for even surface | Tumbling — barrel finishing |
| Sanding the surface smooth | Grinding / Fettling |
| Applying chocolate glaze for final shine | Polishing / Buffing / Painting |
| Quality check before packing | Dimensional & NDT inspection |

> The "burnt crumbs" represent **burnt-on sand**; the "knife" represents **chipping hammers**; the "sugar" represents **abrasive media**. Just as a cake cannot be sold without proper finishing, a casting is unfit for service without cleaning and finishing.

---

### 1.3 Position of Cleaning & Finishing in the Foundry Process Flow

$$
\text{Smelting} \rightarrow \text{Molding} \rightarrow \text{Pouring} \rightarrow \text{Solidification} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{CLEANING}}_{\text{Module 4}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{FINISHING}}_{\text{Module 4}} \rightarrow \text{Inspection} \rightarrow \text{Dispatch}
$$

---

### 1.4 Categorical Map of Cleaning Operations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification:** Cleaning operations are grouped under **three principal functional heads**:

1. **Separation Cleaning** — Shakeout (decoupling of casting from mold mass)
2. **Bulk Material Removal** — Degating, decoring, sprue cutting
3. **Surface Cleaning** — Shot blasting, grit blasting, tumbling, hydro-blasting, manual chipping

---

### 1.5 Categorical Map of Finishing Operations

1. **Geometric Finishing** — Grinding (portable, swing-frame), machining, filing
2. **Surface Finishing** — Polishing, buffing, shot peening
3. **Metallurgical Finishing** — Welding repair, heat treatment, normalizing, stress relieving
4. **Protective Finishing** — Painting, galvanizing, phosphating, blackodising
5. **Quality Finishing** — Dimensional inspection, NDT (radiography, ultrasonic, MPI, DPI)

---

### 1.6 Why Cleaning & Finishing Matter — Engineering Relevance

> [!NOTE]
> - **Safety Critical:** A casting with embedded sand or unfettled risers can fail catastrophically in service (e.g., hydraulic cylinder block failure under pressure).
> - **Economic:** Cleaning & finishing account for **30–60 %** of total casting cost in many foundries.
> - **Occupational Health:** These stages generate the **highest dust, noise, and vibration exposure** in a foundry, making them the **highest-risk zones** for occupational diseases.

---

### 1.7 Glossary of Essential Terms

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Shakeout** | Mechanical separation of casting from sand mold |
| **Degating** | Removal of gates, runners, risers from casting |
| **Decoring** | Removal of sand cores from internal cavities |
| **Fettling** | Comprehensive term for cleaning + rough finishing of castings |
| **Abrasive** | Hard particles (steel shot, grit, alumina) used for surface cleaning |
| **Blast Wheel** | Centrifugal throwing wheel in shot-blast machine |
| **Rumbling Barrel** | Rotating drum used for batch cleaning of small castings |
| **Tumbleblast** | Combined rotary table + shot-blast cabinet |
| **Fettling Shop** | The department where cleaning + grinding activities occur |
| **PPE** | Personal Protective Equipment |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD REFERENCE SHEET

### 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown of Each Process

#### 2.1.1 SHAKEOUT OPERATION

**Principle:** The solidified casting is decoupled from the surrounding sand mold by mechanical agitation (vibratory / rotary / impact).

**Operational Mechanism:**
- **Vibratory Shakeout:** A heavy vibrating deck with eccentric weights transmits high-frequency, low-amplitude vibration to the casting-sand mass; sand passes through the deck grates while the casting is retained.
- **Rotary Drum Shakeout:** The mold cluster tumbles inside a slowly rotating, inclined drum; centrifugal action separates the sand.
- **Knockout Grid:** Static grid over a vibrating conveyor; used for high-throughput production lines.

**Why this works:** When acceleration exceeds a critical threshold, sand loses cohesion and flows as a granular fluid (Bagnold's regime), enabling rapid separation.

---

#### 2.1.2 SHOT BLASTING & GRIT BLASTING

**Principle:** High-velocity abrasive particles strike the casting surface, **converting brittle scale, sand, and brittle metal oxides into fine chips** that are carried away by an air stream.

**Two Engineering Variants:**

| Variant | Energy Source | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Air-Blast (Suction / Pressure)** | Compressed air at **0.5–0.8 MPa** | Small batch, intricate castings |
| **Wheel-Blast (Centrifugal)** | Centrifugal force from blast wheel rotating at **~2250–3500 rpm** | High-volume, heavy castings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Abrasive Velocity Equation** (derived from projectile mechanics):
>
> $$\boxed{v_a \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, P \, A_n}{\rho_a \, A_d}}}$$
>
> where $P$ is the nozzle air pressure, $A_n$ is the nozzle cross-section, $\rho_a$ is air density, and $A_d$ is the abrasive discharge area. Typical $v_a \approx 60$–$80 \;\text{m/s}$ for steel shot.

**Abrasive Types & Selection:**

| Abrasive | Hardness (HV) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Steel Shot (S230–S550)** | 400–550 | Descaling, peening |
| **Steel Grit (G25–G80)** | 600–850 | Heavy cleaning, weld preparation |
| **Chilled Iron Grit** | 700+ | Very aggressive cleaning |
| **Alumina (Al₂O₃)** | 2000+ | Non-ferrous, stainless |
| **Glass Beads** | 500 | Delicate finishing |
| **Plastic Media** | <100 | Aerospace, soft alloys |

---

#### 2.1.3 TUMBLING / RUMBLING

**Principle:** Castings are loaded into a rotating polygonal or cylindrical barrel together with **star-shaped separating media**. Castings rub against each other and the media, removing loose sand and burrs.

**Process Variables:**
- Barrel speed $n$ (typically $0.6\,n_c$ where $n_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$ is the critical speed)
- Load volume fraction (typically **45–55 %**)
- Cycle duration (2–8 hours)

**Why this works:** At sub-critical speed, castings **cascade** in the upper half of the barrel — gravity + sliding friction does the cleaning. Above $n_c$, centrifuging occurs and cleaning action ceases.

---

#### 2.1.4 DEGATING & DECORING

**Degating Methods:**
- Manual hammering / chisel
- Pneumatic chisel
- Band saw / abrasive cut-off wheel
- Hydraulic shearing
- Plasma / oxy-acetylene cutting (for ferrous castings)

**Decoring Methods:**
- Pneumatic hammer + chisel
- Rocker-arm vibrator (for core sand)
- High-pressure water jet (8–40 MPa) for hollow castings
- Sand slinger extraction
- Thermal decoring (for chemically bonded cores)

---

#### 2.1.5 GRINDING (FETTLING)

**Principle:** A bonded abrasive wheel rotating at high peripheral speed (**25–35 m/s**) removes unwanted metal by micro-chip formation.

**Common Foundry Grinding Configurations:**

| Type | Wheel Size (mm) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Portable Hand Grinder** | 100–180 | Spot grinding, gates |
| **Swing-Frame Grinder** | 400–900 | Heavy grinding of large castings |
| **Stand / Bench Grinder** | 150–350 | Off-tool finishing |
| **Robot / Pedestal Fettling** | 300–600 | Automated fettling cells |

---

#### 2.1.6 POLISHING & BUFFING

**Principle:** Soft, flexible wheels (cotton, sisal, felt) impregnated with abrasive compounds (rouge, Tripoli, lime) produce final surface finish.

**Surface Roughness Targets:**
- Polishing: $R_a \approx 0.4$–$0.8\;\mu m$
- Buffing: $R_a \approx 0.05$–$0.2\;\mu m$

---

#### 2.1.7 HEAT TREATMENT & WELDING REPAIR

- **Stress Relieving:** $550$–$650\,^\circ C$ for steel castings to relieve residual stresses.
- **Normalizing:** Refines grain structure after uneven cooling.
- **Welding Repair:** Used for surface defects (porosity, cracks) — pre-heating and PWHT are mandatory to avoid HAZ cracking.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Process–Hazard Reference Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **booster sheet** for KTU ESE. Memorise the **operation → primary hazard → primary control** triplet for each row.

| Operation | Primary Hazard | Engineering Control | PPE Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shakeout** | Noise, dust, heat, struck-by | Acoustic enclosure, dust extraction, automation | Heat-resistant gloves, ear muffs, goggles |
| **Shot Blasting** | Silica dust, flying particles, dust explosion, noise | Enclosed cabinet, dust collector (cyclone + bag), explosion vent | Blast helmet, leather apron, ear protection |
| **Tumbling** | Noise, dust, pinch points | Acoustic hood, interlocks on door | Ear plugs, dust mask, safety shoes |
| **Manual Chipping** | Vibration (HAVS), noise, flying chips | Anti-vibration gloves, job rotation, tool maintenance | Goggles, ear muffs, AV gloves |
| **Grinding** | Wheel breakage, sparks, kickback | Wheel guards, tool rests within **3 mm**, eye wash | Face shield, safety glasses, fire-retardant clothing |
| **Welding Repair** | UV radiation, fumes, electric shock | Local exhaust ventilation, fume extractor | Auto-darkening helmet, leather gloves |
| **Polishing/Buffing** | Combustible dust, fire, entanglement | Dust collector, spark arrestor, anti-restart switches | Respirator, eye protection, no loose clothing |
| **Hydro-blasting** | High-pressure injection injury, slip | Pressure relief, dead-man switch, anti-slip mats | Waterproof PPE, cut-resistant boots |

---

### 2.3 Hierarchy of Controls Applied to Foundry Cleaning & Finishing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever asked to "recommend control measures," always present them in the **Hierarchy of Controls order** (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE). Examiners reward this structured approach with full marks.

| Level | Example Applied to Shot Blasting |
|---|---|
| **Elimination** | Design risers as part of the casting (no degating needed) |
| **Substitution** | Replace silica sand with olivine / chromite / zircon |
| **Engineering Control** | Enclosed blast cabinet, dust collector, sound enclosure |
| **Administrative** | Job rotation, SOPs, health surveillance, training |
| **PPE** | Blast helmet with fresh-air supply, leather suit, ear muffs |

---

### 2.4 Key Quantitative Benchmarks (KTU Numericals Frequently Asked)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Abrasive nozzle exit velocity | $v_a$ | $60$–$80\;\text{m/s}$ |
| Sound pressure level (noise) | $L_p$ | $20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$, with $p_0 = 20\;\mu P_a$ |
| Permissible Exposure Limit (silica) | PEL | $0.025\;\text{mg/m}^3$ (8-hr TWA, ACGIH) |
| Permissible noise exposure | $L_{ex,8h}$ | **85 dB(A)** (Factories Act / OSHA) |
| Hand-arm vibration limit (8-hr) | $A(8)$ | **5 m/s²** (EU Directive 2002/44/EC) |
| Grinding wheel peripheral speed | $v_w$ | $25$–$35\;\text{m/s}$ (max **80 m/s** for high-speed) |
| Cyclone Stokes Number | $Stk$ | $\dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, v}{18\,\mu\,D_c}$ |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive foundry:** Robotic fettling of engine blocks reduces cycle time from 25 min (manual) to 8 min.
- **Defence castings:** Mandatory radiographic + ultrasonic inspection after welding repair.
- **Power industry:** Shot peening of turbine blade roots to enhance fatigue life by 30–40 %.
- **Aerospace:** Plastic media blasting (PMB) replaces chemical stripping on aluminium castings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Centrifugal Shot Velocity from a Blast Wheel

> **Scenario.** A blast wheel throws abrasive particles by centrifugal action. Derive the relationship between wheel rotational speed and particle exit velocity.

**Given:**
- Wheel radius $R$ (tip radius)
- Rotational speed $N$ (rpm)

**Step 1.** Convert rotational speed to angular velocity:

$$
\omega \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]
$$

**Step 2.** At the wheel tip, the abrasive particle is constrained to move in a circular path. The centrifugal acceleration is $a_c = \omega^2 R$.

**Step 3.** Equate kinetic energy gained from centripetal work to the kinetic energy of the particle at the tip. For a particle of mass $m$ starting from rest relative to the blade:

$$
\dfrac{1}{2} m v_a^2 \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} m \omega^2 R^2
$$

**Step 4.** Cancel $m$ and take the square root:

$$
v_a \;=\; \omega R \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N R}{60}
$$

**Step 5.** Substitute $R = 0.40\;\text{m}$ and $N = 2800\;\text{rpm}$:

$$
v_a \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \times 2800 \times 0.40}{60} \;=\; 117.3\;\text{m/s}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Real velocities are **15–25 % lower** due to slip between blade and particle. Hence the design figure is typically corrected as $v_a \approx 0.80\, \omega R$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Speed of a Tumbling Barrel

**Given:**
- Barrel internal diameter $D = 1.2\;\text{m}$

**Step 1.** Critical speed in rpm is given by:

$$
N_c \;=\; \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \quad [\text{rpm, with D in metres}]
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $D = 1.2\;\text{m}$:

$$
N_c \;=\; \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{1.2}} \;=\; 38.6\;\text{rpm}
$$

**Step 3.** Optimum operating speed is $N \approx 0.6\, N_c$:

$$
N_{\text{opt}} \;=\; 0.6 \times 38.6 \;\approx\; 23.2\;\text{rpm}
$$

> **Practical Note:** Above $N_c$, castings centrifugally pin to the wall and cleaning ceases; below $0.4 N_c$, sliding stops and castings simply slide down without cascade motion.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Noise Level Calculation (Logarithmic Addition)

**Scenario.** A grinding machine produces 88 dB(A). A shot-blast machine producing 84 dB(A) runs in the same room. Find the combined noise level.

**Step 1.** Convert each level to intensity ratio:

$$
\dfrac{I_1}{I_0} = 10^{88/10} = 10^{8.8}
$$

$$
\dfrac{I_2}{I_0} = 10^{84/10} = 10^{8.4}
$$

**Step 2.** Add intensities:

$$
10^{8.8} + 10^{8.4} \;=\; 6.31 \times 10^{8} + 2.51 \times 10^{8} \;=\; 8.82 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 3.** Take the log:

$$
L_{\text{total}} \;=\; 10 \log_{10}(8.82 \times 10^{8}) \;\approx\; 89.5\;\text{dB(A)}
$$

> **Result:** When two sources differ by ≤ 3 dB, the increase is only **~1.5 dB**. This is why **engineering noise control at source is far more effective** than just doubling PPE.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Hazard–Operation Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This is a high-density analytical matrix that maps **operations (rows)** against **hazard classes (columns)**. Each cell contains a brief control keyword. This single table can earn 7 marks in any KTU ESE question on foundry safety.

| Operation ↓ / Hazard → | **Dust (Silica/Metal)** | **Noise** | **Vibration** | **Fire/Explosion** | **Mechanical** | **Heat/UV** | **Chemical** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Shakeout** | Local exhaust | Acoustic hood | Isolated mount | Spark detection | Guard rails | Heat-resistant PPE | — |
| **Shot Blasting** | Cyclone + bag filter | Cabinet enclosure | — | Explosion vent, inerting | Interlocked doors | — | — |
| **Tumbling** | Mist dust suppression | Enclosure | — | — | Door interlocks | — | — |
| **Manual Chipping** | On-tool extraction | Ear muffs | AV gloves, job rotation | — | Tool retainer | — | — |
| **Grinding** | Local exhaust + wet | Wheel guard damping | Anti-vibration mount | Spark arrestor | Tool rest ≤ 3 mm | Apron | Coolant handling |
| **Polishing/Buffing** | NFPA-rated collector | Hood | — | Spark detection, no aluminium w/o inerting | Anti-restart | — | Compound handling |
| **Welding Repair** | Fume extractor | — | — | Fire watch, blanket | Earth clamp | Auto-darkening helmet | Fume extraction |
| **Hydro-blasting** | — | — | — | — | Dead-man switch | — | Slips, drain mgmt |

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Hazard Analysis of Shot Blasting (Worked KTU-style)

> **Question Pattern:** *"Explain in detail the operation, hazards, and safety measures of shot blasting in foundries."*

**Step 1 — Operation Outline:**
1. Castings loaded onto rotating table (tumbleblast) or conveyor (hanger type).
2. Abrasive fed into blast wheel.
3. Wheel throws abrasive at 60–80 m/s onto casting surface.
4. Abrasive + removed contaminants fall to floor screw conveyor.
5. Air-swept classifier separates good abrasive from dust.
6. Reusable abrasive returns to storage hopper; dust goes to collector.

**Step 2 — Identification of Hazards (the "5 Ms"):**

| M-Category | Specific Hazard |
|---|---|
| **Material** | Silica dust, metal dust, abrasive fragments |
| **Machine** | Wheel imbalance, broken blades, fly-back of abrasive |
| **Method** | Over-blasting, wrong abrasive for job |
| **Man** | Operator fatigue, improper PPE |
| **Medium** | Dust explosion, noise propagation |

**Step 3 — Engineering Controls:**
- Fully enclosed cabinet with interlocked doors.
- Dust collector (cyclone + bag filter) with explosion relief vent.
- Acoustic enclosure ($L_p$ reduction up to 25 dB).
- Light curtain / pressure mat interlock.
- Localised lighting ≥ 300 lux.

**Step 4 — Administrative Controls:**
- Permit-to-work system for maintenance.
- Daily wheel inspection.
- Health surveillance (spirometry every 6 months).
- Job rotation to limit dust exposure.

**Step 5 — PPE:**
- Blast helmet with **positive-pressure fresh-air supply**.
- Leather or rubberised protective suit.
- Heavy-duty leather gloves.
- Ear muffs (with helmet integration).
- Safety boots with steel toe-cap.

---

### 3.6 Step-by-Step Hazard Analysis of Grinding Operation

**Step 1 — Pre-Operation Checks:**
- Ring-test the wheel (sound "ring" = OK; dull thud = cracked).
- Ensure wheel guard covers **≥ 75 %** of wheel diameter.
- Tool rest within **3 mm** of wheel.
- Tongue guard within **6 mm**.

**Step 2 — Operational Hazards:**

$$
\underbrace{v_{\text{wheel}}}_{\text{peripheral speed}} \;=\; \pi D N / 60
$$

For $D = 0.30\;\text{m}$, $N = 2850\;\text{rpm}$:

$$
v \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 2850}{60} \;\approx\; 44.8\;\text{m/s}
$$

> This is **within the safe 80 m/s limit** for vitrified wheels.

**Step 3 — Critical Controls:**
- Wheel speed marked on the wheel must exceed the spindle speed.
- No grinding on sides of wheel (only on the periphery).
- Eye wash station within **10 m**.
- Spark divertor / catcher positioned away from combustibles.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Overall Foundry Cleaning & Finishing Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solidified Casting Cluster] --> B[Shakeout Operation]
    B --> C[Degate / Cut Gates Risers]
    C --> D[Decore / Remove Core Sand]
    D --> E[Surface Cleaning]
    E --> F[Shot or Grit Blasting]
    E --> G[Tumbling Barrel]
    E --> H[Hydro Blasting]
    F --> I[Pre Inspection Station]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Defect Detected]
    J --> K[Welding Repair]
    K --> L[Grinding and Fettling]
    I --> M[Direct to Grinding]
    L --> N[Polishing and Buffing]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Heat Treatment if Needed]
    O --> P[Dimensional Inspection]
    P --> Q[NDT Inspection]
    Q --> R[Approved Casting]
    Q --> S[Rejected Casting]
    S --> T[Rework or Scrap]
```

---

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Controls Applied to Foundry Hazards

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Identification] --> B[Elimination]
    B --> C[Substitution]
    C --> D[Engineering Controls]
    D --> E[Administrative Controls]
    E --> F[PPE Last Resort]
    F --> G[Residual Risk Acceptable]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The flow **must be attempted top-down**. PPE is the last line of defence, not the first.

---

### 4.3 Shot Blasting Process — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Castings on Conveyor] --> B1[Entry Air Curtain Seal]
    B1 --> C1[Blast Wheel 1]
    B1 --> C2[Blast Wheel 2]
    C1 --> D1[Cleaning Zone]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Floor Screw Conveyor]
    E1 --> F1[Air Swept Classifier]
    F1 --> G1[Dust to Collector]
    F1 --> H1[Reusable Abrasive Hopper]
    H1 --> C1
    H1 --> C2
    D1 --> I1[Exit Air Curtain Seal]
    I1 --> J1[Cleaned Casting Outlet]
```

---

### 4.4 Tumbling Barrel — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Loading Chute with Interlock] --> B2[Polygonal Barrel]
    B2 --> C2[Rumbling Star Media Inside]
    B2 --> D2[Drive Motor with VFD]
    B2 --> E2[Dust Extraction Hood]
    B2 --> F2[Discharge Chute]
    F2 --> G2[Screening Station]
    G2 --> H2[Finished Castings]
    G2 --> I2[Media Separation]
```

---

### 4.5 Hazard-PPE-Cause Network for Grinding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[Grinding Operation] --> B3[Wheel Breakage]
    A3 --> C3[Sparks]
    A3 --> D3[Dust]
    A3 --> E3[Kickback]
    A3 --> F3[Noise]
    B3 --> G3[Face Shield and Guard]
    C3 --> H3[Fire Retardant Apron]
    D3 --> I3[Dust Mask and Extraction]
    E3 --> J3[Two Hand Grip and Firm Stand]
    F3 --> K3[Ear Muffs and Enclosure]
```

---

### 4.6 Permit-to-Work Flow in Cleaning-Finishing Shop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A4[Maintenance Requirement Identified] --> B4[Requestor Raises PTW]
    B4 --> C4[Area Incharge Reviews]
    C4 --> D4[Safety Officer Validates Isolation]
    D4 --> E4[Hazard Analysis and Controls]
    E4 --> F4[Permit Issued with Time Limit]
    F4 --> G4[Work Executed Under Monitoring]
    G4 --> H4[Completion Check]
    H4 --> I4[Permit Closed and Area Restored]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> *Differentiate between foundry cleaning and foundry finishing. List any four operations under each category.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cleaning | Finishing |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Removal of sand, gates, scale | Achieve final dimensions and surface finish |
| **Stage** | Immediately after shakeout | After cleaning, before dispatch |
| **Operations** | (i) Shakeout, (ii) Degating, (iii) Decoring, (iv) Shot blasting | (i) Grinding, (ii) Polishing, (iii) Welding repair, (iv) Inspection |

*(Self-explanatory comparative table earns full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample]**

> *What is the significance of the Hierarchy of Controls in a foundry cleaning shop? Arrange the levels in correct order.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Hierarchy of Controls prioritises **eliminating the hazard at source** before relying on personal protection. The correct order (top-down preference):

1. **Elimination** — Redesign casting to avoid risers.
2. **Substitution** — Replace silica sand with olivine.
3. **Engineering Controls** — Enclosed shot-blast cabinet.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Job rotation, SOPs.
5. **PPE** — Blast helmet, respirator.

*(Mentioning all five levels + any one example = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule Reminder:** *Answer any ONE full question from each module. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**
>
> *(a)* Explain in detail the **principle, types, and working of shot blasting** used in foundries. Discuss the **selection criteria for abrasive media**. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Analyse the **occupational hazards** associated with shot blasting and recommend a comprehensive **safety control plan** using the Hierarchy of Controls. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**
