1. **Principle (1 mark):** High-velocity abrasive particles strike the casting surface, fracturing brittle scale and sand; the resulting dust is removed by an exhaust stream.
2. **Types (3 marks):**
   - **Air-blast (suction type):** Abrasive drawn into air stream by Venturi; low velocity, intricate parts.
   - **Air-blast (pressure type):** Abrasive pressurised in vessel; higher velocity (~80 m/s); medium duty.
   - **Wheel-blast (centrifugal):** Blast wheel throws abrasive at 60–80 m/s; highest productivity; for heavy castings.
3. **Working cycle:** Loading → blast cycle → automatic abrasive回收 (recovery) → air-swept classifier → dust collector → exit.
4. **Abrasive Selection (3 marks):** Based on (i) Hardness vs. casting hardness (must exceed by 30 HV), (ii) Shape (round = peening, angular = cleaning), (iii) Size (S230–S550), (iv) Density (must be heavy enough for kinetic energy transfer), (v) Recyclability, (vi) Cost.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

| Hazard | Engineering Control | Administrative | PPE |
|---|---|---|---|
| Silica dust | Enclosed cabinet + cyclone + bag filter, ≥ 0.5 m/s capture velocity | Wet mop, no dry sweeping, 6-monthly spirometry | FFP3 respirator |
| Noise (95–110 dB) | Acoustic enclosure, isolation mounts | Hearing conservation program | Ear muffs SNR ≥ 30 |
| Flying particles | Interlocked doors, light curtains | Permit to work for maintenance | Blast helmet, face shield |
| Dust explosion | Explosion relief vent, isolation valves, earthing | Housekeeping, no ignition sources | Flame-retardant suit |
| Vibration (minor) | Anti-vibration mounts | — | Anti-vibration gloves |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students commonly lose marks by **jumping straight to PPE**. Always begin with **Elimination / Substitution / Engineering controls** in a hierarchical answer. Also, do **not** mention generic "PPE" — specify *blast helmet with positive-pressure airline, FFP3 respirator, leather suit, ear muffs*.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model]**
>
> *(a)* Describe the **various grinding operations** used in foundry finishing with their **machine configurations and applications**. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Discuss the **fire and explosion hazards in polishing / buffing operations** and the **engineering and administrative controls** required. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

| Grinding Type | Machine Configuration | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Portable hand grinder** | 100–180 mm wheel, side handle, dead-man switch | Spot grinding, gates, risers |
| **Swing-frame grinder** | 400–900 mm wheel, counter-balanced arm | Heavy grinding of large castings |
| **Stand / bench grinder** | Fixed pedestal with two wheels | Off-tool grinding |
| **Robot fettling** | 6-axis robot with force-controlled grinder | High-mix, high-volume |
| **Abrasive belt grinder** | Endless belt on contact wheel | Flat surface finishing |

**Operational Safety (additional marks):**
- Ring test every wheel before mounting.
- Wheel speed rating must exceed spindle speed.
- Wheel guard covers ≥ 75 % diameter; tongue guard ≤ 6 mm.
- Tool rest within 3 mm of wheel.
- Eye-wash within 10 m.
- Spark arrestor / catcher away from combustibles.

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

1. **Hazard Source (2 marks):** Fine aluminium or magnesium dust from buffing can form **combustible dust clouds**. Static electricity from buffing wheel rotation and hot sparks provide ignition energy. **NFPA 652 / IS 16404** classifies such dusts.

2. **Engineering Controls (3 marks):**
   - NFPA-rated dust collector with **explosion relief vent**.
   - Spark detection + water spray suppression within **0.5 m** of dust collector inlet.
   - Earth-bonding of buffing machine and ducting.
   - Anti-restart interlock.
   - Use of **non-ferrous, non-sparking media** for aluminium.

3. **Administrative Controls (2 marks):**
   - No dry sweeping (use vacuum with HEPA filter).
   - Hot-work permit system.
   - Monthly inspection of dust collector.
   - Housekeeping — no dust accumulation > **1 mm thickness**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Many students **forget the dust explosion pentagon** (fuel, oxidiser, dispersion, confinement, ignition). Mentioning all five elements earns a **bonus conceptual point**. Also, never suggest using water directly on burning aluminium dust — this causes hydrogen evolution and explosion. Use **Class D extinguishers (sodium chloride / graphite powder)**.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Box this section for the night before the exam.**

- [x] Cleaning = separation of casting + removal of foreign matter; Finishing = dimensional & surface conformance.
- [x] The **three heads of cleaning:** Separation, Bulk removal, Surface cleaning.
- [x] **Shot blasting abrasive velocities** ~60–80 m/s; wheel tip $v_a = \omega R \times 0.8$.
- [x] **Tumbling** optimum speed ≈ $0.6\, N_c$; below $0.4 N_c$ = sliding, above $N_c$ = centrifuging.
- [x] **Grinding wheel guards** ≥ 75 % coverage; tool rest ≤ 3 mm; tongue guard ≤ 6 mm.
- [x] **Permissible Exposure Limits:** Silica 0.025 mg/m³; Noise 85 dB(A); HAV $A(8) = 5$ m/s².
- [x] **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- [x] **Dust explosion pentagon:** Fuel + Oxidiser + Dispersion + Confinement + Ignition.
- [x] **Class D fires** (metal dust): use dry powder, **never water**.
- [x] **NFPA 652** governs combustible dust; **IS 16404** is the Indian equivalent.
- [x] **Health surveillance** for silica-exposed workers: annual chest X-ray + spirometry.
- [x] **Abrasive selection** based on hardness (>30 HV > workpiece), shape, size, density.
- [x] **Ring test** every grinding wheel before mounting.
- [x] **Decoring** methods include pneumatic, vibratory, hydro-jet, and thermal.
- [x] **Welding repair** requires pre-heat + post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) for steel castings.
- [x] **Shot peening** enhances fatigue life by introducing compressive residual stresses.
- [x] **Robotic fettling** reduces cycle time by **~60 %** and improves safety by removing the operator from the hazard zone.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Foundry Production Cleaning and Finishing Foundry Processes.** *Best wishes for the KTU ESE — study safe, work safe.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Treatment Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heat Treatment Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Treatment Operation** is defined as a controlled sequence of heating and cooling processes applied to metals and alloys in the solid state to alter their internal microstructure and thereby achieve desired mechanical, physical, and chemical properties such as **hardness, tensile strength, toughness, ductility, and corrosion resistance** without changing the overall product shape.

In the context of KTU's *Safety in Engineering Industry* framework (OESFT725, Module 4), heat treatment operations are studied not merely as metallurgical processes, but as **high-hazard industrial activities** that integrate thermal energy, chemical agents, mechanical handling, and human-machine interaction within a single workflow.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine baking a cake. You mix the ingredients (raw material), pour the batter into a mould (shaping), and then bake it in an oven at a specific temperature for a specific time (heating). After baking, you let it cool naturally or place it in a refrigerator (cooling). The texture, taste, and firmness of the cake change drastically based on *how* you baked and cooled it — even though the *ingredients* and *shape* remained the same.

Heat treatment is the **"baking recipe"** of metallurgy. The metal's chemical composition stays the same; what changes is the *internal grain arrangement* (microstructure), which dictates how the metal behaves under load, wear, or heat.

### 1.3 Core Categories at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Pillars of Heat Treatment (KTU Module 4 Focus):**
> 1. **Annealing** – Softening for machinability.
> 2. **Normalizing** – Refining grain structure.
> 3. **Hardening (Quenching)** – Increasing surface hardness.
> 4. **Tempering** – Reducing brittleness after hardening.

A fifth, **Case Hardening**, is often included as a surface-engineering extension.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters for Safety Engineering

Heat treatment plants are classified as **high-risk zones** in any industrial safety audit. The hazards are multi-vector:

- **Thermal:** Furnaces operating at **800°C – 1200°C** (molten salt baths up to **1300°C**).
- **Chemical:** Cyanide salts, carburizing gases (CO, endothermic gas), nitriding ammonia (NH₃), and quenching oils.
- **Mechanical:** Heavy hot-work handling, lifting tongs, quenching tanks.
- **Radiological/EM:** Infrared and UV radiation from open furnaces.
- **Atmospheric:** Oil mist, fumes, flammable vapours.

The standard reference temperature cited in KTU safety modules is the **upper critical temperature (Ac₃ line)** on the Iron-Carbon (Fe-C) phase diagram, typically around **727°C – 910°C** depending on carbon content.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of a Heat Treatment Cycle

Every heat treatment operation, regardless of type, follows the same universal logic chain:

1. **Heating Stage** – The workpiece is gradually heated to a target temperature (above or below the critical temperature depending on the process).
2. **Soaking (Holding) Stage** – The metal is held at the target temperature for a calculated duration to allow uniform temperature penetration and complete phase transformation.
3. **Cooling Stage** – The metal is cooled at a controlled rate (furnace, air, oil, water, brine, or polymer) to lock in the desired microstructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** The **cooling rate** is the single most critical parameter that determines the final microstructure. Slow cooling → soft phases (ferrite, pearlite). Fast cooling → hard phases (martensite).

### 2.2 Soaking Time Calculation

A commonly used rule-of-thumb in industrial heat treatment:

$$t_s = k \cdot D$$

Where:

- $t_s$ = soaking time in **hours**
- $D$ = maximum cross-sectional thickness in **inches** (or mm)
- $k$ = empirical constant (**0.5** for simple carbon steels, **1.0** for high-alloy steels)

### 2.3 Hardness Conversion and Approximation

The **Rockwell C Hardness (HRC)** of a fully hardened plain carbon steel can be approximated from its **Brinell Hardness Number (BHN)** using the empirical relationship commonly cited in industrial references:

$$\text{HRC} \approx \frac{\text{BHN} - 10}{10}$$

For tougher alloy steels, the conversion factors are tabulated but typically fall within a ±2 HRC margin.

### 2.4 Heat Transfer in Furnaces (Fouling Factor Model)

Industrial safety calculations often require estimating heat flux through furnace walls. The **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** with a fouling factor is:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o} + R_f$$

Where:

- $h_i$ = inside convective heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²K**)
- $h_o$ = outside convective heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²K**)
- $x$ = wall thickness (**m**)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of wall (**W/mK**)
- $R_f$ = fouling resistance (**m²K/W**)

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soaking Time | $t_s = k \cdot D$ | hours | Holding time at temperature |
| Hardness Conversion | $\text{HRC} \approx (\text{BHN} - 10)/10$ | dimensionless | Quick field conversion |
| Heat Loss Through Furnace Wall | $q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W or kW | Energy audit & safety |
| Overall Heat Transfer | $1/U = 1/h_i + x/k + 1/h_o + R_f$ | m²K/W | Furnace design safety |
| Cooling Rate (Newton's Law of Cooling) | $dT/dt = -h(T - T_\infty)/\rho c_p$ | K/s | Quench severity |
| Hardenable Depth (Jominy Equivalent) | $D \approx 25.4 \cdot (J_{distance})^{0.5}$ | mm | Hardenability assessment |
| Radiative Heat Flux (Stefan-Boltzmann) | $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | W/m² | Furnace radiation safety |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Constants to Memorise:**
> - Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ **W/m²K⁴**
> - Ac₁ (eutectoid temperature for steel) ≈ **727°C**
> - Ac₃ (upper critical for hypoeutectoid steels) ≈ **727°C to 910°C**
> - Martensite Start (Ms) for plain carbon steel ≈ **200°C – 400°C**
> - Auto-ignition temperature of quenching oil ≈ **300°C – 400°C**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In the **automotive sector**, heat treatment ensures gears, crankshafts, and camshafts have wear-resistant surfaces with tough cores.
- In **aerospace**, critical turbine blades undergo solution treatment and ageing for high-temperature strength.
- In **tool manufacturing**, heat treatment determines tool life — a 1 HRC drop can reduce cutting tool life by **15–20%**.
- From a **safety engineering** perspective, improper heat treatment is the root cause of catastrophic failures such as brittle fracture of pressure vessels, fatigue cracking of railway axles, and creep failure of boiler tubes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cooling Time During Quenching (Newton's Law of Cooling)

The standard industrial model for cooling of a small workpiece in a quench medium assumes lumped capacitance (uniform internal temperature, valid when **Biot number Bi < 0.1**).

**Starting Equation (Energy Balance on the Workpiece):**

The rate of heat loss from the workpiece equals the rate of change of its internal energy:

$$-h A (T - T_\infty) = \rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt}$$

**Step 1:** Divide both sides by $\rho V c_p$:

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = -\frac{hA}{\rho V c_p}(T - T_\infty)$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables and integrate. Let $\theta = T - T_\infty$:

$$\int_{T_i - T_\infty}^{T_f - T_\infty} \frac{d\theta}{\theta} = -\frac{hA}{\rho V c_p}\int_0^t dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate both integrals:

$$\ln\left(\frac{T_f - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty}\right) = -\frac{hA}{\rho V c_p}\, t$$

**Step 4:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{T_f - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\left(-\frac{hA}{\rho V c_p}\, t\right)$$

**Step 5:** Solve explicitly for the cooling time $t$:

$$t = \frac{\rho V c_p}{hA}\,\ln\left(\frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T_f - T_\infty}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $V/A = L$ (characteristic length for a slab of thickness $L$):

$$t = \frac{\rho c_p L}{h}\,\ln\left(\frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T_f - T_\infty}\right)$$

This is the **master equation** used by KTU examiners for quench safety analysis.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A steel plate of thickness $L = 25$ mm is quenched from $T_i = 850°C$ in oil at $T_\infty = 60°C$. Given $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, $c_p = 486$ J/kg·K, $h = 1300$ W/m²K. Calculate the time to cool the plate to **200°C** (just above Ms to avoid quench cracking).

**Step 1:** Plug values into the master equation:

$$t = \frac{7850 \times 486 \times 0.025}{1300}\,\ln\left(\frac{850 - 60}{200 - 60}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the numerator:

$$7850 \times 486 \times 0.025 = 95377.5$$

**Step 3:** Compute the heat-transfer coefficient denominator:

$$\frac{95377.5}{1300} \approx 73.37$$

**Step 4:** Compute the logarithmic term:

$$\ln\left(\frac{790}{140}\right) = \ln(5.643) \approx 1.730$$

**Step 5:** Final multiplication:

$$t \approx 73.37 \times 1.730 \approx 126.9 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The plate takes roughly **127 seconds** to cool from 850°C to 200°C. If a safety interlock requires holding above 200°C for metallurgical reasons, operators must time the quench accordingly. A cooling rate of **5.13 °C/s** is implied, well above the critical cooling rate for plain carbon steel (~100 °C/s for thin sections), confirming martensite formation.

### 3.3 Derivation: Hardenability via Jominy Equivalent

The **Jominy end-quench test** produces a correlation between distance from the quenched end and hardness. An approximate analytical form derived from empirical data is:

$$J_{HRC} = J_{\max} - (J_{\max} - J_{\min}) \cdot \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{D}{D_0}\right)\right]$$

Where:

- $J_{\max}$ = maximum hardness at the quenched end (**HRC**)
- $J_{\min}$ = minimum hardness (fully annealed) (**HRC**)
- $D$ = distance from quenched end (**mm**)
- $D_0$ = characteristic distance constant (**mm**), typically **8–15 mm** for medium-carbon steels.

**Step-by-Step Expansion:**

For $D = 0$: $J_{HRC} = J_{\max}$ (correct, quenched end is hardest).
For $D \to \infty$: $J_{HRC} \to J_{\min}$ (correct, far end is fully annealed).
The **half-hardness distance** $D_{0.5}$ satisfies $J_{HRC} = (J_{\max} + J_{\min})/2$, giving:

$$D_{0.5} = D_0 \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.693 \, D_0$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process vs. Safety Profile

| Process | Temperature Range (°C) | Cooling Medium | Primary Safety Hazard | Typical PPE Required |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Annealing | 600 – 900 | Furnace (slow) | Long-duration heat exposure | Heat gloves, face shield |
| Normalizing | 800 – 950 | Air (still) | Hot part ejection | Aluminised suit |
| Hardening | 800 – 1000 | Water / Oil / Brine | Splatter, fire (oil) | Full face shield, apron |
| Tempering | 150 – 650 | Air | Lower-temp burn | Heat gloves |
| Carburizing | 850 – 950 | Oil quench | CO / cyanide fumes | SCBA, gas monitor |
| Nitriding | 500 – 580 | Air | NH₃ release | Chemical respirator |
| Induction Hardening | 900 – 1000 (surface) | Polymer / water | RF / EMF exposure | EMF-rated PPE |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term **heat treatment**. List any four objectives of heat treatment operations in the engineering industry.

**Model Answer:**

Heat treatment is a controlled process of heating and cooling metals in the solid state to achieve desired mechanical and physical properties without altering the shape of the component.

**Four objectives:**
1. To improve **hardness** and **wear resistance** (e.g., tool steels).
2. To improve **toughness** and **ductility** (e.g., gears, axles).
3. To relieve **internal stresses** induced during prior manufacturing operations like welding, forging, or machining.
4. To refine **grain structure** for improved mechanical uniformity and to enhance **machinability** during subsequent operations.

*(Valid alternative objectives: improve corrosion resistance, improve electrical/magnetic properties, prepare microstructure for further processing.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Differentiate between **annealing** and **normalizing** with respect to cooling medium, microstructure obtained, and typical application.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Annealing | Normalizing |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling Medium | Furnace cooling (very slow) | Air cooling (still or forced) |
| Microstructure | Coarse pearlite + ferrite | Fine pearlite + ferrite |
| Hardness | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Purpose | Maximum softening, stress relief | Grain refinement, uniformity |
| Typical Application | Machining pre-treatment, cold working pre-stage | Structural steel components, forgings |

Normalizing produces a **finer and more uniform grain** than annealing because the faster air cooling suppresses grain coarsening.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the complete **hardening and tempering** process for medium-carbon steel. Include the temperature ranges, microstructural changes at each stage, and the metallurgical reason for tempering after hardening. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **six major safety hazards** encountered in a heat treatment shop and recommend engineering controls for each. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q(a)

**1. Hardening Stage:**

- The medium-carbon steel (typically 0.4% – 0.6% C) is heated to **820°C – 860°C**, which is above the Ac₃ line, into the **austenite (γ-Fe) region**.
- Soaking time is maintained for approximately **k × D hours** to allow complete austenitization and carbide dissolution.
- The component is then rapidly quenched in **water, brine, or oil**. For sections above 25 mm, oil is preferred to avoid quench cracking.
- Rapid cooling suppresses the diffusion-controlled transformation to ferrite + pearlite, producing **martensite**, a super-saturated, body-centred tetragonal (BCT) phase.
- Martensite is extremely hard (**HRC 60+**) but **brittle** due to high internal stresses and carbon supersaturation.

**2. Tempering Stage:**

- The as-quenched component is reheated to a temperature between **150°C – 650°C**, held for 1–2 hours, then cooled.
- At **150°C – 250°C (Low-temperature tempering):** Reduces brittle white martensite to tempered martensite. Used for cutting tools, bearings. Hardness remains high (**HRC 58–62**).
- At **350°C – 500°C (Medium-temperature tempering):** Produces **troostite**. Used for springs, chisels. Hardness drops to **HRC 40–50**.
- At **500°C – 650°C (High-temperature tempering):** Produces **sorbite**. Used for gears, crankshafts. Hardness drops to **HRC 25–35**, with excellent toughness.

**Metallurgical Reason for Tempering:**
Martensite is in a metastable, stressed state. Tempering allows controlled diffusion of carbon from the BCT lattice to form fine, evenly distributed carbides (ε-carbide → cementite), reducing brittleness while retaining adequate hardness.

---

### Model Solution for Q(b)

**Six Major Safety Hazards and Engineering Controls:**

1. **Burns from hot workpieces and furnaces (Thermal Hazard)**
   - *Control:* Install interlocked furnace doors, use automated loading/unloading tongs, mark hot zones with yellow-black stripes, maintain cooling stations with water hoses.

2. **Fire and explosion from oil quench tanks**
   - *Control:* Install flame arrestors, nitrogen blanketing on oil tanks above **80°C**, fire suppression (CO₂ or dry chemical), automatic high-temperature cutoffs.

3. **Toxic gas release (CO, NH₃, HCN) from carburizing/nitriding**
   - *Control:* Local exhaust ventilation (LEV) with airflow ≥ **0.5 m/s** at the source, continuous gas monitors with alarms at **TLV levels**, emergency scrubbers.

4. **Electrical shock from furnace heating elements**
   - *Control:* ELCB/RCD protection (≤ **30 mA** trip current), proper earthing, double insulation, IP54-rated control panels, lockout-tagout during maintenance.

5. **Cyanide salt exposure from molten cyanide baths**
   - *Control:* Substitute with non-cyanide salts where possible, sealed bath covers, dedicated cyanide-handling PPE, antidote stations (hydroxocobalamin) on site.

6. **Ergonomic injuries from manual handling of hot/heavy components**
   - *Control:* Mechanise handling with overhead cranes, jibs, and robotic charge cars; operator training in safe lifting; job rotation to limit exposure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students commonly lose 2–3 marks on Part B questions because they **list hazards without pairing each with a specific engineering control**. KTU's marking scheme explicitly awards marks for the *control measure*, not just identification. Always use the **Hierarchy of Controls (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE)** structure in your answers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled **Iron-Carbon phase diagram**, explain the critical temperatures Ac₁, Ac₃, and Ac₁ₘ. Describe how these temperatures guide the selection of heat treatment process parameters for eutectoid steel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel plate of thickness **30 mm** is quenched from **870°C** in oil at **50°C**. Given density $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³, specific heat $c_p = 486$ J/kg·K, and convective heat transfer coefficient $h = 1200$ W/m²K, calculate the time required to cool the plate to **250°C** using the lumped capacitance model. State one assumption and one practical limitation of this model. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q(a)

**Critical Temperatures:**

- **Ac₁** = **727°C**: The temperature at which austenite begins to form on heating. Below Ac₁, the steel remains in ferrite + cementite (pearlite) region.
- **Ac₃** = The temperature at which transformation of ferrite to austenite is **complete** on heating. For hypoeutectoid steel, Ac₃ is between **727°C and 910°C** depending on carbon content.
- **Ac₁ₘ** = The temperature at which dissolution of cementite in austenite is complete on heating. For hypereutectoid steels, Ac₁ₘ lies between **727°C and 1147°C**.

**Selection for Eutectoid Steel (0.77% C):**

- For a eutectoid steel, Ac₁ = Ac₃ = **727°C**. There is no proeutectoid ferrite or cementite region.
- **Annealing:** Heat just above **727°C** (~750°C), hold, then furnace cool to obtain coarse pearlite.
- **Normalizing:** Heat to **750°C – 800°C**, hold, then air cool to obtain fine pearlite.
- **Hardening:** Heat to **760°C – 800°C**, hold for complete austenitization, then quench to obtain martensite.
- **Spheroidising:** Heat just below Ac₁ (~700°C) for prolonged periods to obtain globular carbides for maximum softness.

The choice of temperature above or below these critical lines directly determines the final microstructure and properties.

---

### Model Solution for Q(b)

**Given:**
- $L = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m
- $T_i = 870$ °C
- $T_\infty = 50$ °C
- $T_f = 250$ °C
- $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³
- $c_p = 486$ J/kg·K
- $h = 1200$ W/m²K

**Using the master equation:**

$$t = \frac{\rho c_p L}{h}\,\ln\left(\frac{T_i - T_\infty}{T_f - T_\infty}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Numerator $\rho c_p L$:

$$\rho c_p L = 7850 \times 486 \times 0.030 = 114453$$

**Step 2:** Divide by $h$:

$$\frac{114453}{1200} \approx 95.38$$

**Step 3:** Logarithmic term:

$$\ln\left(\frac{870 - 50}{250 - 50}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{820}{200}\right) = \ln(4.1) \approx 1.411$$

**Step 4:** Final answer:

$$t \approx 95.38 \times 1.411 \approx 134.6 \text{ seconds}$$

**Answer:** Approximately **135 seconds (≈ 2 min 15 s)**.

**Assumption:** The lumped capacitance model assumes **uniform temperature throughout the workpiece** (valid when Biot number Bi = $hL/k$ < 0.1).

**Practical Limitation:** In reality, **internal temperature gradients exist** in thick sections, leading to non-uniform microstructure. Also, the convective coefficient $h$ is **not constant** during quenching — it varies with temperature due to the Leidenfrost effect and the formation of a vapour blanket around the hot part, which the simple model does not capture.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> For this calculation question, KTU examiners specifically look for:
> 1. **Unit consistency** — converting mm to m (a common mistake is forgetting the 0.001 factor).
> 2. **Correct logarithmic evaluation** — students often mistakenly use $T_i/T_f$ instead of $(T_i - T_\infty)/(T_f - T_\infty)$.
> 3. **A clearly stated assumption and limitation** — failing to mention these typically costs **1.5 marks** out of the 7.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Heat treatment = Solid-state process** → No melting occurs; only microstructural changes.
- **Cooling rate** determines final microstructure: slow → soft; fast → hard.
- **Martensite** is the hardest but most brittle phase; **always temper after hardening.**
- **Annealing** = softest, slowest cooling (furnace); **Normalizing** = medium hardness, air cooling.
- **Ac₁ ≈ 727°C** is the magic temperature for plain carbon steels.
- **Soaking time rule:** $t_s = k \times D$ (hours).
- **Master cooling equation:** $t = (\rho c_p L / h) \ln[(T_i - T_\infty)/(T_f - T_\infty)]$.
- **Critical Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ (memorise for furnace radiation problems).
- **Oil auto-ignition:** 300°C – 400°C → always install flame arrestors.
- **Hazard hierarchy:** Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE.
- **Cyanide salts** demand the highest tier of safety controls: closed baths, dedicated ventilation, on-site antidotes.
- **Hardness conversion shortcut:** HRC ≈ (BHN − 10) / 10.
- **Quench cracking** occurs if cooling rate exceeds critical cooling rate OR if quench is too severe (water instead of oil for high-carbon steel).
- **Tempering ranges:** Low (150–250°C) → tools; Medium (350–500°C) → springs; High (500–650°C) → gears/shafts.
- **Always pair each hazard with a control** in exam answers — KTU marking scheme rewards completeness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroplating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electroplating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electroplating** is an *electrodeposition* process used in surface engineering whereby a thin, adherent, and uniform metallic coating is deposited onto a conductive substrate (the **cathode**) by passing a direct current (DC) through an electrolytic solution containing dissolved ions of the metal to be deposited (the **anode** or a sacrificial inert anode).

The process is governed fundamentally by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis (1833)** and is widely classified under the umbrella of **electrolytic metal finishing** operations in occupational health and industrial safety regulations.

### Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Cathode** | The workpiece to be plated; connected to the **negative terminal** of the DC source. |
| **Anode** | The metal source (soluble) or inert electrode (insoluble); connected to the **positive terminal**. |
| **Electrolyte** | Aqueous solution containing salts of the plating metal (e.g., CuSO₄ for copper plating). |
| **Current Density (CD)** | Current per unit area of cathode surface, expressed in **A/dm²**. Standard metric: **1 A/dm² = 100 A/m²**. |
| **Throwing Power** | Ability of the electrolyte to deposit uniform thickness over irregular geometries. |
| **Plating Bath** | The complete electrolytic cell containing electrolyte, anode, cathode, and additives. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Painting with Electricity"**
> Imagine you want to give a cheap iron spoon a thin, shiny gold finish. You cannot melt gold onto it (too expensive, uneven). Instead, imagine the spoon dipped in a bucket of "gold-flavored water" (gold ions, Au³⁺). You connect the spoon to a battery's **negative terminal**. The battery acts like a tiny magnetic pull, gently tugging the floating gold ions down onto the spoon, where they solidify into a smooth metallic skin. Meanwhile, a gold bar at the other end slowly dissolves into the water, replenishing the ions — like a self-refilling paint pot. This controlled "ionic painting" is **electroplating**.

### Why Electroplating is a Safety-Critical Operation
In the context of **OESFT725**, electroplating is a **Priority Hazard Process** because it routinely involves:
- **Strong acids** (sulfuric, hydrochloric, chromic, hydrofluoric)
- **Cyanide salts** (NaCN, KCN) — used in copper, zinc, silver, gold, and cadmium plating
- **Toxic metal fumes** (chromium VI, nickel, cadmium)
- **Electrical hazards** (low-voltage high-current DC rectifiers)
- **Fire and explosion risks** (hydrogen evolution at cathode)
- **Wastewater contamination** (heavy metals, classified as hazardous per EPA/PCB norms)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Examiners in *Safety in Engineering Industry* repeatedly stress the **hygienic**, **electrical**, **chemical**, and **environmental** safety dimensions of electroplating — not just its metallurgical principles.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Faraday's Constant (F):** **96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol) — charge per mole of electrons.
- **Standard Temperature for bath calculations:** **25 °C (298 K)**.
- **Current Efficiency (η):** Ratio of *actual mass deposited* to *theoretical mass predicted by Faraday's law*, expressed as a percentage. Typical values: **90–98 %** for copper sulfate baths; **15–25 %** for chromium plating (low due to H₂ evolution).
- **Industrial current densities:** **1 to 50 A/dm²** depending on metal and bath composition.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemical Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

Electroplating proceeds through five sequential phenomena at the cathode–electrolyte interface:

1. **Migration:** Under the applied DC field, positively charged metal cations (M^n+) migrate through the bulk electrolyte toward the negatively charged cathode.
2. **Diffusion:** Cations in the immediate vicinity of the **cathode diffusion layer** (Nernst layer) reach the surface via concentration-gradient-driven diffusion.
3. **Electron Transfer (Reduction):** At the cathode surface, the cation gains *n* electrons and is reduced to its atomic (metallic) state:
   > **M^n+ + n e⁻ → M (solid)**
4. **Nucleation & Crystal Growth:** Reduced metal atoms form nucleation sites, then grow into a crystalline lattice, producing a continuous, adherent film.
5. **Hydrogen Co-evolution (Side Reaction):** Competing reduction: **2 H⁺ + 2 e⁻ → H₂ (g)**. This is a major inefficiency and explosion hazard.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU Board Examination — Memorize with units!**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$ | Faraday's First Law — Mass deposited | $m$ (g), $I$ (A), $t$ (s) |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{A}{n}$ | Equivalent weight | $A$ = atomic mass, $n$ = valency |
| 3 | $m = \eta \cdot \dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$ | Actual mass (with current efficiency $\eta$) | $\eta$ dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot A_s}$ | Plating thickness | $T$ (cm), $\rho$ (g/cm³), $A_s$ (cm²) |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot E \cdot I \cdot t}{F \cdot \rho \cdot A_s}$ | Master thickness equation | — |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{T \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A_s}{\eta \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Time required to achieve thickness $T$ | seconds |
| 7 | $CD = \dfrac{I}{A_s}$ | Current density | A/dm² or A/m² |
| 8 | $V_{\text{bath}} = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$ | Joule heating in bath | Joules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Conversion Tip (Frequently tested!):** $1 \text{ A/dm}^2 = 100 \text{ A/m}^2 = 0.01 \text{ A/cm}^2$

## 2.3 Engineering Applications & Real-World Utility

- **Decorative Plating:** Chromium on automotive parts (corrosion resistance + aesthetics).
- **Functional/Industrial Plating:** Hard chromium on hydraulic shafts, zinc on fasteners (sacrificial corrosion protection), tin on food cans.
- **Electronics:** Gold plating on PCB contacts, copper via filling in semiconductor manufacturing.
- **Reclamation:** Electroforming — building up worn parts by thick deposition.
- **Safety Connection:** Plated coatings prevent **galvanic corrosion**, **fretting wear**, and **hydrogen embrittlement** — all critical failure modes in pressure vessels and structural components.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis

**Statement:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

**Starting Point — Definition of Electric Charge:**

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

where $Q$ is the charge in coulombs, $I$ is current in amperes, and $t$ is time in seconds.

**Step 1:** By experimental observation (Faraday, 1833), the mass $m$ deposited is proportional to $Q$:

$$m \propto Q$$

**Step 2:** Introducing the proportionality constant (electrochemical equivalent, $Z$):

$$m = Z \cdot Q$$

**Step 3:** Since one mole of electrons carries $F = 96{,}485$ coulombs, the charge required to deposit one mole of a metal with valency $n$ is:

$$Q_{\text{molar}} = n \cdot F$$

**Step 4:** The mass of one mole of metal equals its atomic mass $A$ (in grams). Therefore, the mass deposited per unit charge is:

$$Z = \dfrac{A}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting back:

$$\boxed{m = \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}}$$

where the **Equivalent Weight** $E = \dfrac{A}{n}$ is introduced.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Derivations — Analytical Matrix

### Worked Example 1: Mass of Copper Deposited

**Problem:** A current of **5 A** is passed through a CuSO₄ solution for **2 hours**. Calculate the mass of copper deposited, given: $A_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol. Assume **100 % current efficiency**.

**Solution:**

Given data:
- $I = 5$ A
- $t = 2 \text{ hours} = 2 \times 60 \times 60 = 7200$ s
- $A = 63.5$ g/mol
- $n = 2$
- $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol

Applying Faraday's law:

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{63.5 \times 5 \times 7200}{2 \times 96{,}500} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2{,}286{,}000}{193{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= 11.844 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{Cu}} \approx 11.84 \text{ g}}$$

### Worked Example 2: Time Required for Target Thickness

**Problem:** A steel shaft of surface area **0.5 m²** is to be nickel-plated to a thickness of **50 microns**. Current density is **3 A/dm²**, current efficiency is **92 %**, density of Ni is **8.9 g/cm³**, $A_{\text{Ni}} = 58.7$, $n = 2$. Find the plating time.

**Step 1:** Compute area in dm²:

$$A_s = 0.5 \text{ m}^2 \times 100 = 50 \text{ dm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute total current:

$$I = CD \times A_s = 3 \times 50 = 150 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** Apply thickness-time relation:

$$t = \dfrac{T \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A_s}{\eta \cdot E \cdot I}$$

**Step 4:** Convert thickness to cm:

$$T = 50 \mu m = 50 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute values (note: for unit consistency use $A_s$ in cm²):

$$A_s = 0.5 \text{ m}^2 = 5000 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 96{,}500 \times 8.9 \times 5000}{0.92 \times (58.7/2) \times 150} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 96{,}500 \times 44{,}500}{0.92 \times 29.35 \times 150} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{21{,}471{,}250}{4050.3} \\[6pt]
&\approx 5301 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 5301 \text{ s} \approx 88.35 \text{ minutes} \approx 1 \text{ hour } 28 \text{ minutes}}$$

### Worked Example 3: Current Efficiency Back-Calculation

**Problem:** In a silver-plating run, **2.16 g** of Ag was deposited using **2 A** for **1000 s**. Given $A_{\text{Ag}} = 108$, $n = 1$, find the current efficiency.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{actual}} &= 2.16 \text{ g} \\[6pt]
m_{\text{theoretical}} &= \dfrac{108 \times 2 \times 1000}{1 \times 96{,}500} = \dfrac{216{,}000}{96{,}500} = 2.238 \text{ g} \\[6pt]
\eta &= \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} = \dfrac{2.16}{2.238} \\[6pt]
&= 0.965 \text{ or } 96.5 \%
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 96.5\%}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Electroplating Cell Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[AC Mains Supply 230V] --> node2[Step Down Transformer]
    node2 --> node3[Rectifier Unit AC to DC]
    node3 --> node4[Ammeter and Voltmeter Monitoring]
    node4 --> node5[Positive Terminal]
    node4 --> node6[Negative Terminal]
    node5 --> node7[Anode Sacrificial Metal Bar]
    node6 --> node8[Cathode Workpiece Substrate]
    node7 --> node9[Electrolyte Bath Acidic Salt Solution]
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10[Metal Ions M to the power n plus in Solution]
    node10 --> node11[Cathode Surface Reduction Reaction]
    node11 --> node12[Adherent Metallic Deposit Layer]
    node9 --> node13[Hydrogen Gas Evolution Cathode]
    node9 --> node14[Oxygen Gas Evolution Anode]
    node13 --> node15[Ventilation and Scrubbing System]
    node14 --> node15
    node9 --> node16[Filtration and Temperature Control Unit]
    node16 --> node9
    node15 --> node17[Safety Stack Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    node14 --> node17
```

## 4.2 Process Logic Block Diagram — Safety Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT [INPUT SUBSYSTEM]
        nodeA1[Raw Workpiece Pre Treatment]
        nodeA2[Chemical Cleaning Acid Pickling]
        nodeA3[Rinsing Stage]
        nodeA1 --> nodeA2 --> nodeA3
    end

    subgraph PLATING [ELECTROPLATING SUBSYSTEM]
        nodeB1[Plating Bath Composition Control]
        nodeB2[DC Power Supply Rectifier]
        nodeB3[Anode Basket System]
        nodeB4[Cathode Workpiece on Rack]
        nodeB5[Temperature Controller]
        nodeB6[Agitation and Filtration]
        nodeB1 --> nodeB4
        nodeB2 --> nodeB3
        nodeB2 --> nodeB4
        nodeB5 --> nodeB1
        nodeB6 --> nodeB1
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT [POST TREATMENT SUBSYSTEM]
        nodeC1[Drag Out Recovery Rinse]
        nodeC2[Drying Oven]
        nodeC3[Quality Inspection Thickness Adhesion]
        nodeC4[Packaging]
        nodeC1 --> nodeC2 --> nodeC3 --> nodeC4
    end

    subgraph SAFETY [SAFETY SUBSYSTEM OVERLAY]
        nodeD1[Cyanide Detoxification Unit]
        nodeD2[Acid Fume Scrubber]
        nodeD3[Emergency Eye Wash Station]
        nodeD4[Explosion Vent for Hydrogen]
        nodeD5[Effluent Treatment Plant Heavy Metals]
        nodeD6[PPE Booth and Local Exhaust]
    end

    INPUT --> PLATING --> OUTPUT
    SAFETY -.-> PLATING
    SAFETY -.-> OUTPUT
```

## 4.3 Cathode Reaction Pathways — Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP1[Metal Ions Arrive at Cathode Diffusion Layer] --> nodeP2{Competing Reactions}
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Desired Reaction M to the power n plus plus n electrons yields M solid]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP4[Side Reaction 2 H plus plus 2 electrons yields H2 gas]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP5[Adherent Plating Layer Forms]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP6[Current Efficiency Loss plus Explosion Risk]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP7[Final Coating Inspection]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP8[Safety Ventilation Activated]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Define electroplating. List any four industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

Electroplating is an electrochemical surface-finishing process in which a metallic coating is deposited onto a conductive substrate (cathode) by passing direct current through an electrolyte containing ions of the coating metal.

**Four Industrial Applications:**
1. Decorative chromium plating on automotive bumpers and two-wheeler parts.
2. Zinc plating (galvanizing) of fasteners and structural steel for corrosion resistance.
3. Gold plating on PCB edge connectors and switch contacts for low contact resistance.
4. Hard chromium plating on hydraulic cylinder rods for wear resistance.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for applications)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**
**Q: State Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis. Define current efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

**Faraday's First Law:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode during electrolysis is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.

$$m \propto Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$$

**Current Efficiency (η):** It is the ratio of actual mass of metal deposited to the theoretical mass predicted by Faraday's law, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%$$

*(3 marks — 1.5 each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the principle of electroplating with a neat labelled diagram of an electroplating cell. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Electroplating is based on the principle of **electrodeposition** — when a DC current passes through an electrolyte, the metal cations migrate to the cathode (negatively charged workpiece) and get reduced to the metallic form, forming a uniform adherent coating. The anode (positive electrode), typically made of the same metal being plated, dissolves into the solution to replenish the metal ions.

**Diagram (already covered in Section 4.1):**
A typical setup includes:
- **DC Rectifier Power Supply:** Converts AC mains to low-voltage, high-current DC.
- **Anode (+ve terminal):** Sacrificial bar of the plating metal (e.g., copper bar in acid copper bath).
- **Cathode (−ve terminal):** The workpiece to be plated (e.g., steel component).
- **Electrolyte Bath:** Aqueous solution of metal salt (e.g., CuSO₄ + H₂SO₄) with additives for brightness, leveling, and conductivity.
- **Heating & Agitation System:** Maintains temperature (25–60 °C) and uniform ion distribution.
- **Filtration Unit:** Removes particulates that cause roughness.
- **Exhaust Ventilation:** Removes acid mists and H₂ gas.

**Reactions:**
- At cathode: $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$
- At anode: $\text{Cu} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^-$

**(7 marks — 3 for diagram/labelling, 2 for principle, 2 for reactions)**

---

**(b)** A steel component of surface area **400 cm²** is to be copper-plated to a thickness of **30 μm**. If the current density is **2.5 A/dm²** and current efficiency is **95 %**, calculate the **time required** for plating. (Given: $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 8.96$ g/cm³, $A_{\text{Cu}} = 63.5$, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Area conversion:**
$$A_s = 400 \text{ cm}^2 = 4 \text{ dm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Thickness in cm:**
$$T = 30 \, \mu m = 30 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3 — Total current:**
$$I = CD \times A_s = 2.5 \times 4 = 10 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of copper required:**
$$m = T \cdot \rho \cdot A_s = (3 \times 10^{-3}) \times 8.96 \times 400 = 10.752 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5 — Using Faraday's law with efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m &= \eta \cdot \dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot t}{F} \\[6pt]
10.752 &= 0.95 \times \dfrac{(63.5/2) \times 10 \times t}{96{,}500} \\[6pt]
10.752 &= 0.95 \times \dfrac{317.5 \cdot t}{96{,}500} \\[6pt]
10.752 &= 0.95 \times 0.003290 \cdot t \\[6pt]
t &= \dfrac{10.752}{0.003126} \\[6pt]
\boxed{t &\approx 3440 \text{ seconds} \approx 57.33 \text{ minutes}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(7 marks — distributed across unit conversion, formula selection, substitution, and final answer)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**

**(a)** Discuss the major **industrial hazards and safety measures** associated with electroplating operations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Electroplating is classified as a **hazardous process** under the Factories Act, 1948 (India) and OSHA (USA) regulations. Major hazards and their controls are:

| Hazard | Source | Control Measure |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical burns** | Strong acids (H₂SO₄, HCl, HF, CrO₃) | PPE — acid-resistant gloves, aprons, face shields; emergency eye-wash and shower stations. |
| **Cyanide poisoning** | NaCN, KCN baths for Cu, Zn, Ag, Au, Cd plating | Strict pH control (alkaline bath); separate cyanide & acid storage (never mix — liberates HCN gas); cyanide detoxification unit (alkaline chlorination). |
| **Toxic fumes / Metal poisoning** | Cr⁶⁺, Ni, Cd, acid mists | Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV); wet scrubbers; mist suppressors; periodic air monitoring. |
| **Electrical hazards** | DC rectifiers (low V, high A); busbars | Lock-out tag-out (LOTO); insulated racks; proper earthing; double-pole isolation. |
| **Fire & Explosion** | H₂ evolution at cathode; flammable solvents | Flame-proof fittings; explosion-relief panels; inert gas blanketing for solvent cleaners; ATEX-rated equipment. |
| **Ergonomic hazards** | Manual rack loading, repetitive tasks | Mechanical hoists; job rotation; anti-fatigue mats. |
| **Environmental** | Heavy metal-laden wastewater | Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP) — precipitation, ion exchange, reverse osmosis; sludge as hazardous waste per Hazardous Waste Management Rules. |

**Regulatory Compliance:** Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) consent, IS 3682, OSHA 29 CFR 1910, Water Act 1974.

**(7 marks — 1 for classification, 5 for hazard-control pairs, 1 for regulations)**

---

**(b)** During a chromium plating operation, a current of **20 A** is passed for **1.5 hours**. The actual mass deposited is **6.2 g**. Calculate the **current efficiency** and **power consumed** if the bath voltage is **6 V**. (Given: $A_{\text{Cr}} = 52$, $n = 3$, $F = 96{,}500$ C/mol). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Theoretical mass using Faraday's law:**
$$t = 1.5 \times 3600 = 5400 \text{ s}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{theoretical}} &= \dfrac{A \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{52 \times 20 \times 5400}{3 \times 96{,}500} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{5{,}616{,}000}{289{,}500} \\[6pt]
&= 19.40 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Current efficiency:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{6.2}{19.40} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&\boxed{\eta \approx 31.96\%}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Power consumed:**
$$P = V \times I = 6 \times 20 = 120 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Energy consumed over 5400 s:**
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{energy}} &= P \times t = 120 \times 5400 = 648{,}000 \text{ J} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{648{,}000}{3.6 \times 10^6} \text{ kWh} \\[6pt]
\boxed{E_{\text{energy}} &= 0.18 \text{ kWh}}
\end{aligned}$$

**(7 marks — 3 for theoretical mass, 2 for efficiency, 2 for energy)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert μ→cm or cm²→dm²** — Causes a 10,000× error. Always write unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Using the wrong valency (n)** — Chromium is n=3, copper n=2, silver n=1, zinc n=2. Examiners deduct **2 full marks** for wrong valency.
> 3. **Ignoring current efficiency** — If the question gives η < 1, you MUST multiply in $m_{\text{theoretical}}$ or divide in $t_{\text{actual}}$. Board answer key explicitly tests this.
> 4. **Confusing Anode & Cathode reactions** — The cathode is where reduction (deposition) occurs; the anode is where oxidation (dissolution) occurs. Marking scheme often allocates 1 mark just for correctly identifying the polarities.
> 5. **In safety questions, missing PPE** — Examiners look for at least 3 specific PPE items and at least one engineering control (LEV/scrubber). Vague answers like "use safety equipment" earn 0 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Electrodeposition of metal onto cathode via DC current through electrolyte.
- ✅ **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ — memorize this value.
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $m = \dfrac{\eta \cdot E \cdot I \cdot t}{F}$ where $E = A/n$.
- ✅ **Thickness Formula:** $T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot E \cdot I \cdot t}{F \cdot \rho \cdot A_s}$ — keep $T$ in cm, $A_s$ in cm², $\rho$ in g/cm³.
- ✅ **Cathode reaction:** Reduction (M^n+ + ne⁻ → M).
- ✅ **Anode reaction:** Oxidation (M → M^n+ + ne⁻).
- ✅ **Side reaction:** $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ — fire/explosion hazard.
- ✅ **Current Efficiency** of chromium plating is notoriously low (~15–25%) due to massive H₂ evolution.
- ✅ **Cyanide baths:** Highly toxic; NEVER acidify; always maintain alkaline pH > 11.
- ✅ **Hygiene Safety:** Eating/drinking/smoking prohibited in plating shop (per IS 3682).
- ✅ **Effluent Rule:** Heavy metal rinse water must pass through ETP before discharge (CPCB norms).
- ✅ **PPE List:** Acid gloves, rubber apron, face shield, gumboots, respirator (for Cr baths).
- ✅ **Unit Conversions:** $1 \text{ dm}^2 = 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$; $1 \text{ A/dm}^2 = 100 \text{ A/m}^2$.
- ✅ **Common metals & valencies (must memorize):** Cu=2, Zn=2, Ni=2, Cr=3, Ag=1, Au=1 or 3, Cd=2, Sn=2 or 4.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Electroplating | Module 4 | OESFT725**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Pattern & Bloom's Taxonomy Levels (L1–L5).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Paint Shops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Paint Shops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Paint Shops

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Paint Shop** (also termed a *finishing shop*, *coating bay*, or *surface treatment facility*) is a designated industrial enclosure where metallic, polymer, or composite substrates are subjected to surface preparation, paint application, curing, and drying operations. According to **KTU 2024 Scheme (OESFT725)** and aligned with **NFPA 33 (Standard for Spray Application Using Flammable or Combustible Materials)** and the **Indian Standard IS 1989 (Painting, Varnishing, and Allied Finishes – Code of Practice)**, a paint shop is classified as a **Class I Division 1 hazardous location** due to the routine presence of flammable vapours, atomised mists, and combustible residues.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A paint shop is NOT merely a room with spray guns. It is an *integrated engineered system* comprising ventilation, fire suppression, electrical safety (explosion-proof fittings), ergonomic design, solvent management, and effluent treatment — all coordinated to control occupational and process hazards.

## 1.2 Types of Paint Shops (KTU Classification)

| Type | Application Method | Industry Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Wet Spray Booth** | Atomised liquid paint via compressed air | Automotive, furniture |
| **Electrostatic Spray** | Charged droplets attracted to grounded workpiece | Mass production lines |
| **Powder Coating Booth** | Dry polymer powder fused by heat | Appliances, architecture |
| **Dip Coating Tank** | Submersion of parts in paint | Small mass components |
| **Electrodeposition (E-Coat)** | Substrate immersed in paint tank, current applied | Automotive primers |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Kitchen on Fire Risk" Model**
> Imagine a commercial kitchen where chefs constantly spray cooking oil over an open flame. The oil mist catches fire instantly, so the kitchen MUST have powerful exhaust hoods, fire-suppression nozzles, non-sparking utensils, and restricted entry. A paint shop is *chemically* the same scenario: instead of edible oil, workers atomise **volatile organic compounds (VOCs)** near potential ignition sources (motors, static, hot work). The exhaust hood is your **ventilation system**, the suppression nozzle is your **CO₂/dry chemical system**, the non-sparking utensil is your **explosion-proof electrical fitting**, and the restricted entry is your **permit-to-work system**. Every "chef" (spray painter) needs personal protective equipment (PPE) — gloves, respirators, flame-retardant coveralls — analogous to a chef's apron and gloves.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **LEL (Lower Explosive Limit)** of typical solvents: **1.1 % to 1.4 % by volume** (e.g., toluene LEL = 1.1 %, acetone LEL = 2.5 %)
- **TLV-TWA (Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average)** for most aromatic solvents: **50 ppm** over 8 hours (ACGIH)
- **Air velocity at the booth face**: **0.5 m/s to 1.0 m/s** (NFPA 33 recommended range for cross-draft booths)
- **Flash point classification of paints**: **< 37.8 °C (100 °F)** → Class I flammable liquid
- **Minimum exhaust ventilation rate**: **0.5 m³/min per m² of booth floor area** (IS 1989)

## 1.5 Importance in KTU Curriculum

Paint shops represent a **convergence point** for nearly every module of *Safety in Engineering Industry* — fire engineering, industrial hygiene, electrical safety, ergonomics, and environmental protection. KTU examiners frequently use paint-shop case studies because they are **multi-hazard, high-incident, and regulatorily complex**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Hazard Inventory in a Paint Shop

A paint shop must be analysed under the **"Source–Path–Receiver" hazard model** (HSE, UK model adopted in IS 18294):

- **Source (Hazard)**: Flammable solvent vapours, atomised paint mist, isocyanate aerosols, chromate pigments, hardener catalysts.
- **Path (Mechanism)**: Inhalation, skin absorption, ignition by spark, electrostatic discharge, hot surfaces (> 200 °C auto-ignition of cellulose).
- **Receiver (Target)**: Worker, equipment, building, environment, neighbouring community.

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU 4M Hazard Framework applied to paint shops:**
> - **Man (Human)**: Operator error, failure to ground the workpiece, incorrect PPE
> - **Machine**: Spray gun malfunction, ventilation failure, static electricity
> - **Material**: Flammable solvents (Flash Point < 37.8 °C), toxic pigments (lead chromate, cadmium)
> - **Media (Environment)**: Poor ventilation, ignition sources nearby, ambient temperature affecting solvent evaporation

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Process Flow Inside a Spray Booth

1. **Pre-cleaning & masking** — substrate enters with grease, dust, or oil; wiped with **degreasing solvent** (typically white spirit or aqueous alkaline cleaner).
2. **Substrate grounding** — copper earthing clamp attached to ensure **resistance ≤ 1 MΩ** to ground (for electrostatic safety).
3. **Pre-treatment** — phosphating, chromating, or anodising forms conversion coating for adhesion.
4. **Spray application** — atomised paint sprayed at **2.5 to 4.0 bar** working pressure; transfer efficiency 30–70 % (conventional) vs 80–95 % (electrostatic).
5. **Flash-off zone** — wet film rests for 60–180 s allowing solvents to evaporate before entering the oven.
6. **Curing/drying oven** — typically **60 °C to 200 °C**; forced convection or infrared.
7. **Cool-down & inspection** — DFT (Dry Film Thickness) measured; typical range **20 µm to 80 µm**.

## 2.3 Ventilation Engineering Principles

Ventilation in paint shops follows the principle of **"Capture at Source, Dilute Below LEL, Discharge Safely"**.

- **Capture velocity** at the booth face: minimum **0.5 m/s** for cross-flow, **0.7 m/s** for down-draft booths.
- **Dilution ventilation formula** (used for solvent calculations):

$$ Q = \frac{100 \times G \times 24.45 \times 10^6}{MW \times LEL \times K} \quad \text{(ft}^3\text{/min, US)} $$

In SI units:

$$ Q = \frac{G \times 1000}{MW \times LEL} \times 0.0036 \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)} $$

where:
- $Q$ = required ventilation rate (m³/s)
- $G$ = solvent evaporation rate (g/s)
- $MW$ = molecular weight of solvent (g/mol)
- $LEL$ = lower explosive limit (volume %)

- **Discharge stack height**: minimum **3 m above the roof ridge** and **6 m from any air intake** (IS 1989 / CPCB norms).

## 2.4 Fire & Explosion Protection Hierarchy

Following the **Hierarchy of Controls (NIOSH)**:

1. **Elimination** — replace solvent-based paints with water-based or powder coatings.
2. **Substitution** — use high-solids or UV-cured coatings.
3. **Engineering Controls** — ventilation, explosion-relief panels, intrinsically safe (IS) electrical fittings.
4. **Administrative Controls** — permit-to-work, no-smoking zones, training.
5. **PPE** — respirators, chemical-splash goggles, flame-retardant overalls.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Threshold Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Booth face velocity (cross-draft) | $v_f$ | 0.5 – 1.0 | m/s | NFPA 33 |
| Lower Explosive Limit (toluene) | $LEL$ | 1.1 | % vol | NFPA 68 |
| Flash Point threshold for Class I | $T_{fp}$ | < 37.8 | °C | OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106 |
| TLV-TWA (xylene) | $C$ | 100 | ppm | ACGIH |
| STEL (xylene) | $C_{stel}$ | 150 | ppm | ACGIH |
| Minimum exhaust rate | $Q_{min}$ | 0.5 | m³/min per m² | IS 1989 |
| Earthing resistance (workpiece) | $R$ | ≤ 1 | MΩ | NFPA 33 |
| Explosion relief panel area | $A_{rel}$ | 0.022 × V_room | m² | NFPA 68 |
| Spray gun pressure (conventional) | $P$ | 2.5 – 4.0 | bar | OEM |
| DFT automotive topcoat | $t$ | 20 – 80 | µm | Industry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Students often confuse **LEL** and **Flash Point**. *Flash Point* is the temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapour to ignite with an open flame. *LEL* is the minimum concentration in air that can ignite. They are different physical concepts but **both must be controlled** in a paint shop.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Paint-shop safety principles are applied in:
- **Automotive OEMs** (Maruti, Tata, Hyundai) — robotic electrostatic lines
- **Aerospace** (HAL) — chromate-free primer booths
- **Heavy engineering** (L&T) — large structural paint halls
- **Defence** (ordnance factories) — explosion-proof ammunition coating bays
- **White goods** (LG, Samsung) — powder-coating lines

Understanding paint-shop safety qualifies a B.Tech graduate for roles in **EHS (Environment, Health & Safety)**, **fire safety auditing**, and **industrial hygiene**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations, Hazard Matrices & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Ventilation Rate Calculation (KTU Favourite)

> **Problem:** A paint shop uses **2.0 kg/hr** of toluene (MW = 92 g/mol, LEL = 1.1 % by volume) as a cleaning solvent. Calculate the **minimum dilution ventilation rate** in m³/s required to keep the vapour concentration below **25 % of LEL** (the NIOSH safety factor).

### Step 1 — Convert solvent mass rate to g/s

$$ G = 2.0 \ \text{kg/hr} = \frac{2000 \ \text{g}}{3600 \ \text{s}} = 0.5556 \ \text{g/s} $$

**Logic:** Air-volume calculations in SI use grams-per-second, so we must convert hourly usage.

### Step 2 — Apply the design safety factor

We must maintain the concentration at **25 % of LEL**, not 100 % of LEL. The effective LEL in the equation becomes:

$$ LEL_{design} = 0.25 \times 1.1 \% = 0.275 \ \% \ \text{by volume} $$

**Logic:** Industrial hygiene never designs up to the LEL; a 4× safety factor (i.e., 25 %) is the standard for flammable vapours per **NFPA 69** and **IS 18294**.

### Step 3 — Insert into the dilution ventilation formula

$$ Q = \frac{G \times 1000}{MW \times LEL_{design}} \times 0.0036 $$

$$ Q = \frac{0.5556 \times 1000}{92 \times 0.275} \times 0.0036 $$

$$ Q = \frac{555.6}{25.3} \times 0.0036 $$

$$ Q = 21.96 \times 0.0036 $$

$$ Q = 0.0791 \ \text{m}^3\text{/s} $$

### Step 4 — Express in standard engineering units

$$ Q = 0.0791 \ \text{m}^3\text{/s} = 4.74 \ \text{m}^3\text{/min} = 285 \ \text{m}^3\text{/hr} $$

### Final Answer

> **Required ventilation rate ≈ 4.74 m³/min** to keep toluene vapour at 25 % LEL. The exhaust fan selected should be rated **at least 1.25 × Q = 5.93 m³/min** to account for duct friction, filter loading, and fan degradation (industry standard derating).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Explosion Relief Panel Sizing

> **Problem:** A spray booth has internal volume **V = 6 m × 4 m × 3 m = 72 m³**. Calculate the **minimum area of explosion-relief panels** required per NFPA 68.

### Step 1 — Volume calculation

$$ V = L \times B \times H = 6 \times 4 \times 3 = 72 \ \text{m}^3 $$

**Logic:** The relief-panel area is proportional to the enclosure volume because the pressure wave energy scales with gas mass.

### Step 2 — Apply NFPA 68 empirical formula for low-strength enclosures

$$ A_{rel} = 0.022 \times V $$

$$ A_{rel} = 0.022 \times 72 = 1.584 \ \text{m}^2 $$

### Step 3 — Divide into multiple panels (good engineering practice)

Typically, panels are split into **4 identical panels** mounted on the rear and side walls:

$$ A_{panel} = \frac{1.584}{4} = 0.396 \ \text{m}^2 \ \text{per panel} $$

Dimensions: **0.63 m × 0.63 m** square panel each.

### Final Answer

> **Total relief area required = 1.584 m²**, distributed as 4 panels of 0.396 m² each.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Quantitative Risk Assessment Matrix (KTU Exam Favourite)

> **Problem:** In a paint shop, identify **5 typical hazards**, assign a **Severity (S)**, **Likelihood (L)**, and compute the **Risk Rating (R = S × L)** using the standard 5×5 matrix.

| Sl. No. | Hazard | Severity (S, 1–5) | Likelihood (L, 1–5) | Risk Rating R = S × L | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solvent flash fire | 5 | 3 | **15** | High |
| 2 | Isocyanate asthma | 4 | 4 | **16** | High |
| 3 | Static discharge ignition | 5 | 2 | **10** | Medium |
| 4 | Lead chromate poisoning | 4 | 2 | **8** | Medium |
| 5 | Musculoskeletal disorder (overhead spraying) | 3 | 4 | **12** | Medium |
| 6 | Dust explosion (powder booth) | 5 | 2 | **10** | Medium |
| 7 | Confined-space entry in oven | 5 | 1 | **5** | Low |

**Risk Classification Bands (Industry Standard):**
- **Low:** R = 1 – 4
- **Medium:** R = 5 – 12
- **High:** R = 13 – 25

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A risk score above 12 is classified as **"High"** and demands immediate corrective action per **ISO 45001 Clause 6.1.2**. Students must justify both the *severity* and *likelihood* scores using historical data, MSDS, and inspection records.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Paint Application Method

| Criterion | Conventional Air Spray | Airless Spray | Electrostatic | Powder Coating | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transfer Efficiency | 30 % | 50 % | 80 % | 95 % | 30 % |
| VOC Emission | High | High | Medium | **Zero** | 25 % |
| Fire Risk | High | High | Medium | **Low** | 20 % |
| Capital Cost | Low | Medium | High | **Highest** | 15 % |
| Finish Quality | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Good | 10 % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matrix matters for safety engineers:** A safety officer often participates in **process selection reviews**. Lowering VOC emission reduces chronic health hazards; higher transfer efficiency reduces both material waste AND operator exposure. Powder coating is therefore the **safest** process for the worker, though it requires higher capital investment.

---

## 3.5 Storage & Compatibility Matrix (KTU Favourite)

> **Problem:** Identify **incompatible chemical pairings** in a paint-shop storage room (must be segregated).

| Substance A | Substance B | Hazard |
|---|---|---|
| Nitrocellulose lacquer | Oxidising agents (e.g., hydrogen peroxide) | Spontaneous combustion |
| Solvent-based paints | Compressed gas cylinders (oxygen) | Explosion on leak |
| Epoxy hardeners (amines) | Isocyanate resins | Violent polymerisation, heat |
| Powder coating | Open flame / sparks | Dust explosion (Kst > 200 bar·m/s) |
| Chromate pigments | Reducing agents | Toxic chromyl chloride release |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Believing "all paints can be stored together". KTU examiners specifically test knowledge of **oxidiser–fuel segregation** as per the **Hazardous Chemicals Rules, 1989 (India)** and the **GHS classification system**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Paint Shop — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Substrate Entry and Pre-Cleaning] --> A2[Grounding Clamp Resistance less than 1 Megaohm]
    A2 --> A3[Pre-Treatment Phosphating Chromating]
    A3 --> A4[Spray Booth Atomisation 2.5 to 4.0 bar]
    A4 --> A5[Flash-Off Zone 60 to 180 seconds]
    A5 --> A6[Curing Oven 60 to 200 degrees Celsius]
    A6 --> A7[Cool-Down and DFT Inspection 20 to 80 micrometre]
    A7 --> A8[Finished Goods Dispatch]

    A4 -. Vapour extraction .-> B1[Axial Fan Exhaust]
    A4 -. Water curtain scrubber .-> B2[Emission Control Unit]
    A4 -. Flame detector .-> C1[Fire Suppression CO2 or Dry Chemical]
    A4 -. LEL sensor 25 percent LEL trip .-> C2[Explosion Relief Panels]
    A6 -. LEL sensor .-> C1
    A6 -. LEL sensor .-> C2

    A4 -. Operator .-> D1[PPE Respirator FR Overall Goggles]
    A4 -. Permit .-> D2[Hot Work Permit No Smoking]

    B2 --> E1[Stack Height greater than 3 m above roof]
    B2 --> E2[CPCB Emission Norm VOC less than 50 mg per Nm3]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show the *process flow of the workpiece*. Dotted arrows show *auxiliary safety subsystems* extracting from, monitoring, or protecting the spray booth and oven. This is the integrated engineering view KTU examiners expect.

---

## 4.2 Hazard Control Hierarchy in a Paint Shop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Eliminate Solvent Paints] --> H2[Substitute Water Based or Powder]
    H2 --> H3[Engineering Controls Ventilation Explosion Proof]
    H3 --> H4[Administrative Controls Permit Training Signage]
    H4 --> H5[PPE Respirator FR Suit Gloves Goggles]
    H5 --> H6[Worker Protected Residual Risk Documented]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept:** The hierarchy is *top-down* — you can never compensate for missing elimination with more PPE. KTU examiners will deduct marks if a student suggests "more respirators" as the *primary* control.

---

## 4.3 Spray Booth Cross-Sectional Airflow (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Paint Atomisation Zone] -->|0.5 to 1.0 m/s capture velocity| P2[Filters Mesh and Carbon]
    P2 -->|Ductwork| P3[Axial Exhaust Fan]
    P3 -->|Stack| P4[Atmosphere Discharge 3 m above roof]
    F1[Fresh Air Inlet Plenum] -->|Make-up air| P1
    P1 -. Cross-draft .-> W1[Operator Standing Outside Booth]
```

**Engineering reading:** Fresh air enters from the inlet plenum on one side, sweeps across the spray zone, captures overspray and vapour, and is exhausted through the opposite wall. The operator stands **outside the booth** to avoid inhalation — a critical KTU 2024 Scheme design rule.

---

## 4.4 Fire and Explosion Protection Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[LEL Sensor Set at 25 percent LEL] -->|Trip signal| S2[Emergency Vent Shutdown]
    S1 -->|Trip signal| S3[Sprinkler or CO2 Discharge]
    S1 -->|Trip signal| S4[Audible Alarm and Beacon]
    S5[Flame Detector IR UV] --> S3
    S5 --> S4
    S6[Manual Call Point Break Glass] --> S3
    S6 --> S4
    S7[Explosion Relief Panel Burst at 0.1 bar] --> S8[Pressure Wave Safely Vented Outside]
```

---

## 4.5 Process Selection Decision Tree for a Safety Engineer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is Flash Point less than 37.8 C?} -->|Yes| Q2{Is Daily Throughput greater than 500 parts?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Use Conventional Water Based Booth]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4{Is Operator Distance greater than 1 m?}
    Q2 -->|No| Q5[Use Manual HVLP Gun Booth]
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q6[Use Robotic Electrostatic Line]
    Q4 -->|No| Q7[Use Powder Coating if Substrate Heat Tolerant]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define *Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)* and explain why paint shops are designed to operate at 25 % of LEL and not at LEL.

**Model Answer:**
The Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) is the minimum concentration of a flammable vapour in air below which propagation of a flame does not occur on contact with a source of ignition. For example, the LEL of toluene is 1.1 % by volume. Paint shops are designed to maintain vapour concentration at **25 % of LEL** (i.e., 0.275 % for toluene) for three reasons: (i) instrumentation drift and sensor uncertainty may cause the actual LEL to be exceeded if the design is too close to the LEL threshold; (ii) transient releases (e.g., when an operator opens a paint container) can cause momentary spikes; (iii) regulatory codes such as **NFPA 69** mandate a minimum safety factor of 4× on flammable atmospheres. Thus, designing at 25 % LEL provides a robust engineering safety margin.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any **six personal protective equipment (PPE)** required for a spray painter and state the standard each conforms to.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Air-supplied respirator** (SAR) or half-face respirator with organic-vapour cartridge — **IS 14166 / EN 14387**
2. **Chemical-splash safety goggles** — **IS 5983 / ANSI Z87.1**
3. **Nitrile rubber gloves** (solvent-resistant) — **EN 374**
4. **Flame-retardant (FR) coveralls** (Nomex or Proban) — **NFPA 2112 / EN ISO 11612**
5. **Safety footwear with antistatic sole** — **IS 15298 / EN ISO 20345**
6. **Hearing protection** if booth noise > 85 dBA — **IS 6229 / EN 352**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **process flow of a typical automotive paint shop**. List the **six key hazardous substances** handled in each zone. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A spray booth of internal volume **120 m³** is fitted with a fan delivering **8 m³/min** of fresh air. Toluene (LEL = 1.1 %) evaporates at a rate of **3.5 g/s**. Determine whether the ventilation is adequate to keep the vapour concentration below 25 % of LEL. State the corrective action if it is not. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to A(a):**

The process flow of a typical automotive paint shop consists of the following stages:

1. **Pre-treatment (Degreasing → Phosphating → Rinsing → Drying)** — substrate is cleaned of oil/grease using alkaline solutions and a zinc/iron phosphate conversion coating is applied for corrosion resistance.
2. **E-Coat (Electrodeposition)** — the body is dipped in a primer tank; DC current deposits a uniform primer film of **18–25 µm**.
3. **Sealer & Underbody Coating** — PVC-based sealants applied in seams and wheel arches.
4. **Primer Surfacer Booth** — spray applied, then oven-cured at **140–160 °C**.
5. **Basecoat (Topcoat) Booth** — colour coat applied, **2.5–3.5 bar** spray pressure, transfer efficiency 65 %.
6. **Clearcoat Booth** — high-gloss transparent coat.
7. **Curing Oven** — **140–160 °C** for 20–30 min.
8. **Inspection (DFT, Gloss, Colour match, Orange Peel)**.

**Six Key Hazardous Substances:**
- Toluene, Xylene, MEK (solvents)
- Hexamethylene diisocyanate (HDI) — in clearcoat
- Lead chromate — historical pigment
- Zinc phosphate (irritant dust)
- Styrene — in body fillers
- Carbon black pigment

#### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:** V = 120 m³ (not directly needed), Q_fan = 8 m³/min = 0.1333 m³/s, G = 3.5 g/s, MW = 92 g/mol, LEL = 1.1 %.

**Design LEL** = 25 % × 1.1 = 0.275 %.

**Required ventilation rate:**

$$ Q_{req} = \frac{G \times 1000}{MW \times LEL_{design}} \times 0.0036 $$

$$ Q_{req} = \frac{3.5 \times 1000}{92 \times 0.275} \times 0.0036 $$

$$ Q_{req} = \frac{3500}{25.3} \times 0.0036 $$

$$ Q_{req} = 138.3 \times 0.0036 = 0.498 \ \text{m}^3\text{/s} $$

Convert to m³/min:

$$ Q_{req} = 0.498 \times 60 = 29.9 \ \text{m}^3\text{/min} $$

**Comparison:**

$$ Q_{fan} = 8 \ \text{m}^3\text{/min} < Q_{req} = 29.9 \ \text{m}^3\text{/min} $$

**Conclusion:** The ventilation is **grossly inadequate** — only 27 % of the required capacity.

**Corrective Actions:**
1. Replace the exhaust fan with one rated at least **30 m³/min** (with 25 % derating → 37.5 m³/min).
2. Reduce solvent consumption by switching to water-based cleaning.
3. Install a **local exhaust hood** at the source to capture vapours before they diffuse.
4. Add a **continuous LEL monitor** with an automatic shutdown at 25 % LEL.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Hierarchy of Controls** as applied to occupational health hazards in a paint shop. Give **two specific engineering controls** and **two administrative controls** relevant to spray painting. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** What is a **permit-to-work system**? Describe the **six essential elements** of a hot-work permit in a paint shop. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to B(a):**

The **Hierarchy of Controls** is a five-level framework (NIOSH) ranked from most effective to least effective:

1. **Elimination** — completely remove the hazard (e.g., eliminate hexavalent chromium from primers).
2. **Substitution** — replace with a less hazardous alternative (e.g., water-based paint instead of solvent-based).
3. **Engineering Controls** — physical modifications that reduce exposure (ventilation, interlocks, earthing).
4. **Administrative Controls** — changes in work procedures, training, and scheduling.
5. **PPE** — personal protective equipment as the last line of defence.

**Two Engineering Controls for Spray Painting:**
1. **Cross-draft ventilation booth** maintaining a face velocity of 0.5–1.0 m/s, ensuring vapours are captured before the operator inhales them.
2. **Intrinsically safe (IS) electrical fittings** conforming to IS/IEC 60079, eliminating ignition sources inside the booth.

**Two Administrative Controls for Spray Painting:**
1. **No-smoking and no-hot-work zone** within 6 m of the booth, demarcated by signage and a hot-work permit.
2. **Job rotation** to limit individual cumulative solvent exposure below the TLV-TWA.

#### **Model Solution to B(b):**

A **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** is a formal written document that authorises specific high-risk work to be carried out under controlled conditions, after hazards have been identified and precautions put in place.

**Six Essential Elements of a Hot-Work Permit in a Paint Shop:**
1. **Permit issuer's name and authorisation** (e.g., safety officer).
2. **Date, time, and duration** of the hot work.
3. **Exact location and description** of the job (e.g., welding bracket inside paint booth B-3).
4. **Hazard identification checklist** — flammable residues, vapour sources, adjacent activities.
5. **Precautionary measures** — LEL meter reading < 5 %, fire extinguisher within 3 m, fire watch posted, ventilation operational, gas test certificate.
6. **Sign-off signatures** of issuer, performer, and fire watch; **closure** of permit on completion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often forget to mention the **LEL gas test reading** as a precondition — this is the single most common cause of permit-related accidents globally (e.g., the 2005 BP Texas City fire).
> - Marks are deducted if the **fire watch** is omitted — a fire watch is a dedicated person whose sole duty is to monitor for ignition during and 30 minutes after the work.
> - Students list "PPE" under administrative controls in Q(a) — PPE belongs in the **fifth level**, not in administrative controls.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **paint shop** is a Class I Division 1 hazardous area due to flammable vapours.
- **LEL** = minimum concentration to ignite; **Flash Point** = temperature at which liquid emits ignitable vapour — both must be controlled.
- **Design target** for ventilation is **25 % of LEL** (4× safety factor), never LEL itself.
- **Booth face velocity** must be **0.5–1.0 m/s** (NFPA 33).
- **Earthing resistance** of the workpiece must be **≤ 1 MΩ** to dissipate static charge.
- **Explosion-relief area** ≈ **0.022 × enclosure volume** (NFPA 68).
