# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Distinguish between static balancing and dynamic balancing. Why is dynamic balancing essential for high-speed rotors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Static balancing** ensures that the center of mass of the rotor lies on the axis of rotation, eliminating the **net centrifugal force**. A statically balanced rotor will not have a preferred rest position.

**Dynamic balancing** is a more stringent condition that additionally requires the **resultant couple** of all centrifugal forces to be zero. This means the principal axis of inertia coincides with the axis of rotation.

| Aspect | Static | Dynamic |
|--------|--------|---------|
| Net force | Zero | Zero |
| Net couple | Not required | Must be zero |
| Speed suitability | Low-speed rotors | All speeds, especially high-speed |
| Test method | Place on knife edge | Spin and measure vibration |

For high-speed rotors, even a tiny couple unbalance produces large alternating forces at the bearing supports, causing **vibration**, **bearing failure**, and **fatigue cracking**. Hence dynamic balancing is non-negotiable above ~500 rpm.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark definition, 1 mark distinction, 1 mark for high-speed justification)*

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the terms "force polygon" and "couple polygon" in the context of dynamic balancing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **force polygon** is a closed vector polygon constructed by laying the $m_i r_i$ vectors (representing the centrifugal force contributions of each unbalanced mass, scaled by $\omega^2$) head-to-tail. Closure of the force polygon (ΣF = 0) ensures **no net unbalance force** acts on the shaft.

A **couple polygon** is similarly constructed using the vectors $m_i r_i z_i$ (where $z_i$ is the axial distance of each mass from a reference plane). Closure of the couple polygon (ΣM = 0) ensures **no net unbalance couple** acts on the shaft.

For complete dynamic balance, **both** polygons must close simultaneously. A rotor whose force polygon closes but couple polygon does not will exhibit **rocking vibration** (shaft whirls in a conical mode).

*(Mark distribution: 1.5 marks each definition + vector quantity identification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

> *A shaft carries four masses A, B, C, D of magnitudes 10 kg, 15 kg, 20 kg and 25 kg respectively. The corresponding radii of rotation are 200 mm, 150 mm, 100 mm and 250 mm, and the angular positions of the masses are 45°, 135°, 225° and 315° respectively, all measured from the same reference. The axial positions of the masses are: A at 0 mm (reference), B at 200 mm, C at 400 mm, and D at 600 mm. The shaft is to be dynamically balanced by adding two balance masses in planes E and F located at 800 mm and 1000 mm from the reference plane, with radii 300 mm and 250 mm respectively. Determine the magnitude and angular position of the balance masses.*

#### Part (a) — **Force Balance** [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute $m_i r_i$ values:**

$$m_A r_A = 10 \times 200 = 2000 \text{ kg·mm}, \quad \theta_A = 45°$$
$$m_B r_B = 15 \times 150 = 2250 \text{ kg·mm}, \quad \theta_B = 135°$$
$$m_C r_C = 20 \times 100 = 2000 \text{ kg·mm}, \quad \theta_C = 225°$$
$$m_D r_D = 25 \times 250 = 6250 \text{ kg·mm}, \quad \theta_D = 315°$$

**Step 2 — Resolve into X and Y components:**

$$X_A = 2000 \cos 45° = 2000 \times 0.7071 = 1414.21$$
$$Y_A = 2000 \sin 45° = 2000 \times 0.7071 = 1414.21$$

$$X_B = 2250 \cos 135° = 2250 \times (-0.7071) = -1590.99$$
$$Y_B = 2250 \sin 135° = 2250 \times 0.7071 = 1590.99$$

$$X_C = 2000 \cos 225° = 2000 \times (-0.7071) = -1414.21$$
$$Y_C = 2000 \sin 225° = 2000 \times (-0.7071) = -1414.21$$

$$X_D = 6250 \cos 315° = 6250 \times 0.7071 = 4419.42$$
$$Y_D = 6250 \sin 315° = 6250 \times (-0.7071) = -4419.42$$

**Step 3 — Sum the unbalanced force components:**

$$\Sigma X = 1414.21 - 1590.99 - 1414.21 + 4419.42 = 2828.43 \text{ kg·mm}$$
$$\Sigma Y = 1414.21 + 1590.99 - 1414.21 - 4419.42 = -2828.43 \text{ kg·mm}$$

**Step 4 — Required balance force (negative of unbalanced):**

$$R_X = -2828.43, \quad R_Y = 2828.43 \text{ kg·mm}$$

#### Part (b) — **Couple Balance & Balance Mass Calculation** [7 Marks]

**Step 5 — Take moments about plane E (at 800 mm):**

$$M_X^{(E)} = \sum_{i=A}^{D} (L_E - L_i) \cdot X_i$$
$$M_X^{(E)} = (800-0)(1414.21) + (800-200)(-1590.99) + (800-400)(-1414.21) + (800-600)(4419.42)$$
$$M_X^{(E)} = 800 \times 1414.21 + 600 \times (-1590.99) + 400 \times (-1414.21) + 200 \times 4419.42$$
$$M_X^{(E)} = 1131368 - 954594 - 565684 + 883884 = 494974 \text{ kg·mm} \cdot \text{mm}$$

$$M_Y^{(E)} = (800)(1414.21) + (600)(1590.99) + (400)(-1414.21) + (200)(-4419.42)$$
$$M_Y^{(E)} = 1131368 + 954594 - 565684 - 883884 = 636394 \text{ kg·mm} \cdot \text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $m_F r_F$ (moment about E eliminates $m_E r_E$):**

Since plane E is the moment reference, $m_E r_E$ has zero arm. So:
$$m_F r_F \angle \theta_F = -\frac{M_X^{(E)} + j M_Y^{(E)}}{L_F - L_E}$$

$$L_F - L_E = 1000 - 800 = 200 \text{ mm}$$

$$m_F r_F \text{ (X)} = -\frac{494974}{200} = -2474.87 \text{ kg·mm}$$
$$m_F r_F \text{ (Y)} = -\frac{636394}{200} = -3181.97 \text{ kg·mm}$$

$$|m_F r_F| = \sqrt{(-2474.87)^2 + (-3181.97)^2} = \sqrt{6125402 + 10124812} = \sqrt{16250214} = 4031.16 \text{ kg·mm}$$

$$\theta_F = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-3181.97}{-2474.87}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.2857) \approx 52.12° \text{ (in 3rd quadrant)}$$
$$\theta_F = 180° + 52.12° = 232.12°$$

$$m_F = \frac{4031.16}{250} = 16.12 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $m_E r_E$ using force balance:**

$$m_E r_E \text{ (X)} = -(\Sigma X + m_F r_F \cos\theta_F) = -(2828.43 + 4031.16 \cos 232.12°)$$

$\cos 232.12° = -0.6143$, $\sin 232.12° = -0.7891$

$$m_E r_E \text{ (X)} = -(2828.43 + 4031.16 \times (-0.6143)) = -(2828.43 - 2476.34) = -352.09 \text{ kg·mm}$$
$$m_E r_E \text{ (Y)} = -(-2828.43 + 4031.16 \times (-0.7891)) = -(-2828.43 - 3181.97) = 6010.40 \text{ kg·mm}$$

$$|m_E r_E| = \sqrt{(-352.09)^2 + (6010.40)^2} = \sqrt{123967 + 36124910} = \sqrt{36248877} = 6020.70 \text{ kg·mm}$$

$$\theta_E = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{6010.40}{-352.09}\right) \approx \text{(2nd quadrant, near 90°)} = 180° - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{6010.40}{352.09}\right) = 180° - 86.65° = 93.35°$$

$$m_E = \frac{6020.70}{300} = 20.07 \text{ kg}$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Mass at plane E:** $m_E \approx 20.07$ kg at $\theta_E \approx 93.35°$, radius 300 mm
- **Mass at plane F:** $m_F \approx 16.12$ kg at $\theta_F \approx 232.12°$, radius 250 mm

### **Question B-B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

> *A four-stroke single-cylinder engine has a crank, a connecting rod, and a reciprocating piston. The crank rotates at 1500 rpm with a crank radius of 75 mm. The reciprocating mass is 2.5 kg. Show that the engine requires a balance mass in a single plane and determine the magnitude and position of this mass. Take the balance mass radius as 100 mm. State the limitations of this single-plane balancing approach.*

#### Part (a) — **Forces in Single-Cylinder Engine** [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Identify the inertia forces:**

For a single-cylinder reciprocating engine, the primary inertia force of the piston is:
$$F_p = m_r \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \cos\theta$$

where $m_r$ is reciprocating mass, $r$ is crank radius, $\omega$ is angular velocity, and $\theta$ is crank angle.

**Step 2 — Calculate $\omega$:**
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum unbalanced force (at $\theta = 0°$):**
$$F_{max} = m_r \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 = 2.5 \times 0.075 \times (157.08)^2$$
$$F_{max} = 0.1875 \times 24674.01 = 4626.38 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Required centrifugal balance force:**

The balance mass rotating at radius $r_b$ must produce an equal and opposite centrifugal force:
$$F_b = m_b \cdot r_b \cdot \omega^2 = F_{max}$$
$$m_b = \frac{F_{max}}{r_b \cdot \omega^2} = \frac{m_r \cdot r}{r_b} = \frac{2.5 \times 75}{100} = 1.875 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Position of balance mass:**

The balance mass must be placed **180° out of phase** with the crank, i.e., on the opposite side of the crank throw, **along the line of stroke** (cylinder axis). This ensures the balance force opposes the maximum piston inertia force.

#### Part (b) — **Limitations of Single-Plane Balancing** [7 Marks]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often overlook the following limitations and lose 2-3 marks. State them **explicitly with examples** in your answer.

1. **Only primary inertia force is balanced.** The secondary inertia force ($F_s = m_r \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \cos 2\theta$, magnitude ~25% of primary for typical engines) remains unbalanced, causing vertical vibration.

2. **Couple unbalance is not addressed.** Since all masses (piston, balance mass) lie in different axial planes (cylinder plane vs. crank plane), an uncompensated **couple** is generated along the engine length.

3. **Rocking couple:** The piston and balance mass form a couple about the engine CG, causing the engine to rock on its mounts. This is most severe at resonance.

4. **Incomplete for multi-cylinder engines:** Cannot handle 4-cylinder, 6-cylinder, V-engines etc., where multiple reciprocating masses interact.

5. **Practical solution:** Use **multi-plane balancing** (e.g., 4 masses for 4-cylinder inline engine, with primary and secondary force & couple polygon closure for both primary and secondary orders).

6. **In a 4-cylinder inline engine**, primary forces are inherently balanced, but **primary couples** and **secondary forces** require multi-plane balance shafts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks on Dynamic Balancing:**
>
> 1. **Confusing static and dynamic balance** (lose 2 marks in definitions)
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s** (lose 1 mark per numerical)
> 3. **Ignoring sign/direction in vector sums** — KTU expects component-wise work, not just magnitudes
> 4. **Skipping the moment equation** — the second polygon (couple) is what differentiates dynamic from static balancing
> 5. **Not stating the safety implication** — link every solution to bearing life, fatigue, and vibration safety
> 6. **Using $N$ instead of $\omega$** in $F = m r \omega^2$ — a classic 1-mark trap

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this before the exam!**

- [ ] **Dynamic Balance = Force Polygon Closed AND Couple Polygon Closed**
- [ ] **Centrifugal Force** = $m r \omega^2$ where $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$
- [ ] **Force polygon vectors**: Use $m_i r_i$ (mass-radius product)
- [ ] **Couple polygon vectors**: Use $m_i r_i z_i$ (mass-radius-axial distance)
- [ ] **Reference plane**: Choose any convenient plane; the moment sum must be zero about *every* plane for a balanced rotor
- [ ] **Static balance is a subset of dynamic balance** — every dynamically balanced rotor is also statically balanced, but not vice versa
- [ ] **For n unbalance masses in n planes, we need n balance masses** (typically placed at the ends to maximize lever arm)
- [ ] **Single-plane balancing works only when all masses can be transferred to one plane** (i.e., when the rotor is short relative to its diameter)
- [ ] **Practical application order**: 1) Compute $m_i r_i$ products → 2) Reduce to common radius if needed → 3) Resolve components → 4) Apply force closure → 5) Apply moment closure → 6) Compute balance masses
- [ ] **Safety link**: Unbalance → vibration at 1× running frequency → bearing wear → fatigue failure → catastrophic rotor failure. Always state this chain in KTU answers to score full CO5 marks
- [ ] **ISO 1940-1** balance grades: G 6.3 for pumps, G 2.5 for machine tool drives, G 1 for grinding wheels, G 0.4 for gyroscopes
- [ ] **Multi-plane = multi-mesh equation system**: Use systematic elimination or graphical polygon method
- [ ] **Graphical method fallback**: For exams without calculators, vector polygons drawn to scale give partial marks (5/14) even if numerical answers are slightly off

---

**End of Notes — Module 4: Dynamic Balancing**
*Course: OESFT725 — Safety in Engineering Industry | KTU 2024 Scheme*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydro Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hydro Testing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HYDRO TESTING (HYDROSTATIC TEST)** > *Hydro Testing is a non-destructive pressure integrity test in which a pressure vessel, piping system, storage tank, boiler, or process equipment is completely filled with a liquid medium (almost always water) and then pressurized to a predetermined test pressure — typically **1.3 to 1.5 times the Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** — for a specified hold duration, in order to validate structural strength, leak-tightness, weld integrity, and overall fitness-for-service before commissioning, recommissioning, or after any modification or repair.* **Governing Standard (Industrial Reference):** - **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII** – Rules for Construction of Pressure Vessels - **ASME B31.3** – Process Piping - **API 510** – Pressure Vessel Inspection Code - **IS 2825** – Unfired Pressure Vessels (Bureau of Indian Standards) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Balloon Under the Tap" > [!NOTE] > **Think of Hydro Testing like this — Imagine blowing up a balloon to check if it leaks before giving it to a child.** > > You inflate the balloon (pressurize the vessel) with WATER (not air) because water is **incompressible**. If the balloon bursts, it makes a loud bang (dangerous) and releases stored energy violently. But if you fill it with water first and then apply gentle pressure, even if it bursts, the energy release is **slow and survivable**. The water also fills every tiny crack, seam, and weld, and **sweats out** from any leak, making the defect easy to find with your eyes. > > The vessel, just like the balloon, is "asked the question": *Can you handle a pressure slightly higher than what you'll ever see in real life?* If yes — it is certified safe. If no — it fails, but safely (because the medium is incompressible water, not explosive compressed air or gas). ## 1.3 Why Hydro Testing is the **Industry Default** | Property | Water (Hydro Test) | Air/Gas (Pneumatic Test) | |---|---|---| | **Compressibility** | Negligible (~0.5% by volume) | High (~1000x expansion potential) | | **Stored Energy on Rupture** | Very Low | Extremely High (Explosive) | | **Leak Detection** | Easy (visible seepage) | Difficult (whistling, soap bubble) | | **Energy Released on Failure** | Non-fragmenting | Fragmenting, lethal | | **Hazard Class** | Moderate | **Severe (often prohibited above 1.5× MAWP)** | | **Cost & Availability** | Cheap, abundant | Expensive, requires compression | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common answer students give is "pneumatic test is safer because no water spillage." The opposite is **academically correct**. Pneumatic testing is the **MOST DANGEROUS** form of pressure testing because compressed gas stores massive elastic energy. **Water saves lives.** ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Hydro Testing - **Density of water (ρ)** ≈ **1000 kg/m³** (or **1 g/cm³**) - **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** ≈ **9.81 m/s²** - **Standard test pressure multiplier (k)** = **1.3** (hydrostatic) or **1.5** (pneumatic/hydraulic combined stress) - **Minimum hold time** = **30 minutes** (small vessels) up to **8 hours** (large, thick-walled) - **Test temperature** should be **above the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** and ideally **above 16°C** to prevent brittle fracture. - **Acceptable pressure drop** during hold = **None** (any drop = leakage = FAIL). - **Hydrostatic head correction**: P_head = **ρgh** (Pa), where h is the vertical height of water column above the gauge. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" — Objectives of Hydro Testing (Mapped to KTU Learning Outcomes) A hydrostatic test is performed to answer **five critical safety questions** in an engineering plant: 1. **Strength Verification:** Can the vessel withstand a pressure 1.3× its design limit without yielding, rupturing, or permanently deforming? 2. **Leak-Tightness Verification:** Are all welded joints, flanges, threaded connections, and gasketed surfaces leak-free at the test pressure? 3. **Weld Integrity Verification:** Do fabrication welds contain hidden defects (porosity, lack of fusion, undercuts) that would propagate into leaks at operating pressure? 4. **Fitness-for-Service Recertification:** After years of service, has corrosion, erosion, or fatigue weakened the vessel below acceptable limits? 5. **Code Compliance:** Does the equipment satisfy the mandatory regulatory pre-commissioning and post-modification testing requirements? ## 2.2 The "How" — Step-by-Step Hydro Test Procedure (Industrial Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Step 1: Pre-Test Preparation (Most Safety-Critical Phase)** > - **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** of all energy sources (electrical, hydraulic, thermal) to the vessel. > - **Complete isolation** from process piping using blinds (spectacle blinds or slip blinds) on all connecting lines. > - **Removal of internal internals** that could be damaged or could hold trapped air. > - **Cleaning & flushing** to remove scale, debris, oil, grease (these affect leak visibility and can be pyrophoric). > - **Low-pressure air/soap test** to detect gross leaks at low pressure BEFORE filling with water (saves time). > - **Calibration of test gauges** — must be **calibrated within the last 30 days**, accuracy ±0.5% of full scale, range ≈ 1.5× to 3× the test pressure. Two gauges required (primary + witness). > - **Establish Exclusion Zone:** Typically **1.5× to 2× the vessel's diameter or height** (whichever is greater), rope barricaded, signed "DANGER — HYDRO TEST IN PROGRESS". > - **PPE Verification:** Hard hat, safety shoes, full-body harness (for vertical vessels), face shield, hearing protection (for pneumatic fallback only). > [!NOTE] > **Step 2: Filling the Vessel (Slow, Controlled)** > - Fill from the **lowest point** of the vessel; vent air from the **highest point** to ensure 100% liquid fill. **Trapped air is the #1 cause of hydro test accidents** — it creates a "pneumatic bomb" inside a hydro test. > - Filling rate is controlled — typically 1 m/s liquid velocity maximum in piping to prevent surge. > - Continue filling until water overflows the vent — wait 5–10 minutes for all air to purge. > [!NOTE] > **Step 3: Pressurization (Staged Pressure Increase)** > - **Stage 1:** Raise pressure to **0.5 × Test Pressure (TP)**. Hold for inspection of gaskets, joints. > - **Stage 2:** Raise pressure to **0.8 × TP**. Hold and inspect. > - **Stage 3:** Raise pressure gradually to **1.0 × TP** (full test pressure). Never overshoot. > - Pressurization rate must be **slow** — typically not exceeding **0.35 bar/second** or as per code. > [!NOTE] > **Step 4: Hold & Inspection** > - Maintain test pressure for the **specified hold period** (commonly **30 minutes to 4 hours**). > - **No pressure drop is permitted** during the hold (compensate for ambient temperature changes using a dead-weight tester or compare to a reference vessel — pressure drop > 1% = leak). > - Inspector walks the entire accessible surface, looking for: - **Sweating** (moisture beading on welds) → leak - **Dripping streams** → gross leak - **Permanent deformation / bulging** → structural failure - **Unusual sounds** → metal yielding - **Gauge drift** → leak > [!NOTE] > **Step 5: Depressurization & Draining** > - Reduce pressure **slowly and evenly** to atmospheric. > - Drain through the bottom drain valve, with the vent open. > - Remove blinds, restore LOTO tags only after gauge reads 0 bar. > - **Document** the test report: vessel tag number, test pressure achieved, hold time, inspector name, calibration certificate numbers, photographs of any leak points, and final disposition (PASS/FAIL). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THESE 7 FORMULAS — THEY COVER ~85% OF NUMERICAL QUESTIONS** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Description of Variables | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Test Pressure (ASME)** | $P_{test} = 1.3 \times MAWP$ | $MAWP$ = Max Allowable Working Pressure | bar, psi, MPa | | 2 | **Hydrostatic Head Pressure** | $P_{head} = \rho g h$ | $\rho$ = density, $g$ = gravity, $h$ = vertical column height | Pa, bar | | 3 | **Hoop (Circumferential) Stress — Thin Wall** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$ | $P$ = internal pressure, $D$ = diameter, $t$ = wall thickness | MPa, psi | | 4 | **Longitudinal (Axial) Stress — Thin Wall** | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ | Same as above | MPa, psi | | 5 | **Thin-Wall Validity Check** | $\dfrac{D}{t} \geq 20$ | If true, thin-wall theory applies | Dimensionless | | 6 | **Required Wall Thickness (Cylindrical)** | $t = \dfrac{PR}{SE - 0.6P} + C$ | $R$ = radius, $S$ = allowable stress, $E$ = joint efficiency, $C$ = corrosion allowance | mm, in | | 7 | **Leak Rate from Pressure Drop** | $\Delta V \approx V \times \dfrac{\Delta P}{P_{test}}$ | $\Delta V$ = leaked water volume, $V$ = vessel volume | m³, L | > [!WARNING] > **PITFALL — DO NOT use `|` for absolute value inside tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid`.** This is a markdown rendering issue. KTU paper markers WILL deduct if they can't parse your math. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Oil & Gas:** Every new pipeline (e.g., cross-country crude pipeline) is hydrotested at 1.25–1.5× design pressure section-by-section ("line break test"). - **Nuclear Power:** Steam generator tubes, primary loops, and containment structures undergo periodic hydro tests. - **Fire Protection:** Fire hydrant lines and sprinkler systems are hydrotested at 200 psi (13.8 bar) for 2 hours. - **Ammonia & Refrigeration:** Pressure vessels in cold storage plants (NH₃) require 10-year hydro test recertification. - **Aerospace:** Hydraulic accumulators, fuel tanks (with water-displaced leak test), and rocket motor casings. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Head Pressure (P = ρgh) This is the foundational equation that explains *why* vessels that are very tall have non-uniform pressure during hydro testing. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Setup:** Consider a vertical column of water of height $h$ and cross-sectional area $A$ inside a tall pressure vessel. The pressure at the bottom of the column must support the weight of the water above it. **Step 1 — Identify the forces acting on the water column.** - The weight of the water column acts downward due to gravity. - The pressure at the bottom of the column acts upward on the base area $A$. **Step 2 — Express the weight of the water column.** The volume of the water column is: $$ V = A \times h $$ The mass of the water column is: $$ m = \rho \times V = \rho \times A \times h $$ The weight (gravitational force) of the water column is: $$ W = m \times g = \rho \times A \times h \times g $$ **Step 3 — Express the force due to pressure at the bottom.** By definition of pressure, the force exerted by the fluid pressure $P_{head}$ on the base area $A$ is: $$ F = P_{head} \times A $$ **Step 4 — Apply equilibrium (force balance).** In static equilibrium, the pressure force at the bottom exactly balances the weight of the water above: $$ P_{head} \times A = \rho \times A \times h \times g $$ **Step 5 — Solve for $P_{head}$.** The area $A$ cancels from both sides (this is why pressure is independent of column cross-section — a key conceptual insight): $$ P_{head} = \rho \times g \times h $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{P_{head} = \rho g h} $$ **Numerical Evaluation Example:** For a vessel with $h = 10$ m water column, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²: $$ P_{head} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 10 = 98100 \text{ Pa} = 0.981 \text{ bar} $$ This means the **top** of a 10 m tall vessel reads 0.981 bar **less pressure** than the **bottom** on the same gauge line. This is critical when setting test pressure — you must measure at the **highest point** to ensure the entire vessel meets the test pressure. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Vessel This derivation explains *why* cylindrical vessels split **lengthwise** under excessive pressure, not circumferentially — and the answer is hidden in the math. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Setup:** Consider a thin-walled cylinder of internal diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, internal pressure $P$, cut longitudinally into two halves along its length $L$. **Step 1 — Identify the force trying to split the cylinder.** The internal pressure $P$ acts on the **projected area** of the cut cross-section. The projected area onto which the pressure pushes is: $$ A_{projected} = D \times L $$ **Step 2 — Calculate the bursting force.** The total force trying to push the two halves apart: $$ F_{burst} = P \times A_{projected} = P \times D \times L $$ **Step 3 — Identify the resisting force.** The two halves are held together by the **two walls** of metal (left wall and right wall), each of cross-sectional area: $$ A_{wall} = t \times L $$ Total resisting area (both walls combined): $$ A_{resist} = 2 \times t \times L $$ **Step 4 — Equate the bursting force to the resisting force for equilibrium at the design point.** $$ P \times D \times L = \sigma_h \times 2 \times t \times L $$ **Step 5 — Solve for the hoop stress $\sigma_h$.** The length $L$ cancels from both sides: $$ P \times D = 2 \sigma_h t $$ Rearranging: $$ \sigma_h = \frac{P \times D}{2 t} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{PD}{2t}} $$ **Physical Interpretation — Why cylindrical vessels split lengthwise:** The hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress (which we will derive next). The vessel fails along the **line of maximum stress**, which is the circumferential direction. So a pressurized gas cylinder (like an LPG tank) ruptures with a long axial split — not a clean end-cap blowout. This is a key KTU board exam question. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Longitudinal Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Vessel > [!NOTE] > **Physical Setup:** Now consider the *same* cylinder, but cut **transversely** (perpendicular to the axis) into two pieces, exposing the end-cap area. **Step 1 — Identify the force trying to push the end-cap off.** The internal pressure $P$ acts on the **internal cross-sectional area** of the cylinder (the end-cap area). The internal cross-sectional area is: $$ A_{end} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate the axial force pushing the end off:** $$ F_{axial} = P \times A_{end} = P \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ **Step 3 — Identify the resisting force (the cylindrical wall in hoop cross-section).** The annular wall cross-section (the "ring" of the cylinder) has area: $$ A_{ring} = \pi \times D \times t $$ **Step 4 — Equate forces and solve:** $$ P \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma_l \times \pi \times D \times t $$ The $\pi$ and $D$ terms cancel: $$ \frac{P \times D}{4} = \sigma_l \times t $$ Rearranging: $$ \sigma_l = \frac{PD}{4t} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{PD}{4t}} $$ **Comparison Summary:** $$ \sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{PD}{2t} = 2 \times \frac{PD}{4t} $$ This 2:1 ratio is a **fundamental result** in pressure vessel engineering. Long, thin pipelines (where D/t is large) are **hoop-stress critical** — they fail lengthwise first. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 2.0 m, wall thickness of 20 mm, and a design pressure of 10 bar. The vessel is to be hydrostatically tested. > > **Calculate:** > (a) The hydrostatic test pressure as per ASME Section VIII. > (b) The hoop and longitudinal stresses at the test pressure. > (c) The required wall thickness to keep the hoop stress below 140 MPa at the test pressure, with a corrosion allowance of 2 mm. ### Solution: **Given Data:** - $D = 2.0$ m = 2000 mm - $t = 20$ mm - $P_{design} = 10$ bar = 1.0 MPa = 1.0 N/mm² - $S_{allow} = 140$ MPa - $C = 2$ mm **Part (a): Hydrostatic Test Pressure** $$ P_{test} = 1.3 \times P_{design} = 1.3 \times 10 = 13 \text{ bar} = 1.3 \text{ MPa} $$ **Part (b): Stresses at Test Pressure** Hoop stress: $$ \sigma_h = \frac{P_{test} \times D}{2 t} = \frac{1.3 \times 2000}{2 \times 20} = \frac{2600}{40} = 65 \text{ MPa} $$ Longitudinal stress: $$ \sigma_l = \frac{P_{test} \times D}{4 t} = \frac{1.3 \times 2000}{4 \times 20} = \frac{2600}{80} = 32.5 \text{ MPa} $$ **Part (c): Required Wall Thickness** For hoop stress to be ≤ 140 MPa at test pressure: $$ \sigma_h = \frac{P_{test} \times D}{2 (t_{required} - C)} \leq S_{allow} $$ Solving for $t_{required}$: $$ t_{required} = \frac{P_{test} \times D}{2 S_{allow}} + C $$ $$ t_{required} = \frac{1.3 \times 2000}{2 \times 140} + 2 = \frac{2600}{280} + 2 = 9.286 + 2 = 11.29 \text{ mm} $$ $$ \boxed{t_{required} \approx 11.3 \text{ mm}} $$ The existing 20 mm thickness is more than adequate — a safety margin of $(20 - 11.3)/11.3 \times 100\% \approx 77\%$ exists. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Hydro Test Selection Decision Tree | Vessel/Service Condition | Test Type | Test Pressure Multiplier | Safety Hazard Level | |---|---|---|---| | Standard atmospheric storage tank | Hydro | 1.3 × design | Low | | New pressure vessel fabrication | Hydro | 1.3 × MAWP | Moderate | | New pressure vessel (gas service) | Pneumatic permitted if hydro impractical | 1.5 × MAWP | **High** | | In-service revalidation (10-year) | Hydro | 1.3 × MAWP (or calculated lower) | Moderate | | Repaired vessel | Hydro | 1.3 × MAWP | Moderate-High | | Lined vessel (glass, rubber) | Hydro only (no pneumatic) | 1.3 × MAWP (but ≤ 110% of lining rating) | High | | Catastrophically toxic service (chlorine) | Hydro only | 1.3 × MAWP | **Severe** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hydro Test Setup ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Water Source
Tank or Main] --> B[Suction Strainer] B --> C[Test Pump
Electric Driven] C --> D[Pressure Relief Valve
Set at 1.1x Test Pressure] D --> E[Primary Test Gauge
Calibrated] E --> F[Witness Test Gauge
Independent] F --> G[Pressure Vessel Under Test] G --> H[High Point Vent Valve] G --> I[Low Point Drain Valve] G --> J[Spectacle Blinds
on all process connections] K[Exclusion Zone
Barricaded] -.-> G L[Safety Officer
and Inspector] -.-> G M[Calibration Certificate
Reference] -.-> E N[Data Logger
Pressure vs Time] --> G ``` **Figure 4.1 — Functional Block Architecture of a Standard Hydrostatic Test Setup** ## 4.2 Hydro Test Procedure Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: Vessel Ready for Test] --> S2[Pre-Test
LOTO and Isolation] S2 --> S3[Install Blinds
on All Connections] S3 --> S4[Calibrate Gauges
Check Valves] S4 --> S5[Establish Exclusion Zone
Brief All Personnel] S5 --> S6[Fill Vessel from Bottom
Vent Air from Top] S6 --> S7{100% Filled?
No Trapped Air} S7 -->|No| S6 S7 -->|Yes| S8[Stage 1 Pressurize
to 0.5x Test Pressure] S8 --> S9{Visual Leak Check
at 0.5x} S9 -->|Leak Found| S10[Depressurize and
Mark for Repair] S9 -->|No Leak| S11[Stage 2 Pressurize
to 0.8x Test Pressure] S11 --> S12{Visual and Gauge
Check at 0.8x} S12 -->|Leak| S10 S12 -->|No Leak| S13[Stage 3 Pressurize to
Full Test Pressure] S13 --> S14[Hold for Required
Duration] S14 --> S15{Any Pressure Drop
or Visible Leak} S15 -->|Yes| S10 S15 -->|No| S16[Slow Depressurize
to Atmospheric] S16 --> S17[Drain Water
Remove Blinds] S17 --> S18[Document and Sign
Test Report] S18 --> S19[End: Vessel Passed
and Certified] S10 --> S20[Mark FAIL on Tag
Send to Workshop] ``` **Figure 4.2 — Hydrostatic Test Workflow (Sequential Decision Flow)** ## 4.3 Failure Mode Comparison — Hydro vs. Pneumatic Failure ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Pressure Test
Event] --> B{Test Medium} B -->|Water
Incompressible| C[Energy = 1/2 PV
Very Small] B -->|Air/Gas
Compressible| D[Energy = PV/(k-1)
Very Large] C --> E[Result: Slow Tear
Visible Leak
No Fragmentation] D --> F[Result: Explosive
Fragmentation
Shrapnel and Shockwave] E --> G[Safety Outcome:
Repairable Damage] F --> H[Safety Outcome:
Fatal Possible
Catastrophic Loss] ``` **Figure 4.3 — Why Water is Always Preferred Over Air** ## 4.4 Pressure Distribution Along Vessel Height (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph V[Vertical Cylindrical Vessel] V1[Top of Vessel
P = P_test - rho*g*h] V2[Middle of Vessel
P = P_test - rho*g*h/2] V3[Bottom of Vessel
P = P_test] end V1 --> V2 V2 --> V3 PG[Pressure Gauge
Location: HIGHEST POINT] -.-> V1 PG --> V1 ``` **Figure 4.4 — Pressure Gauge Must Always Be at the Highest Point to Ensure Minimum Pressure Reaches Test Value Everywhere** # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define hydrostatic testing. Why is water preferred over air as the test medium?** **Model Answer (3 marks — 10 key points, expand each):** Hydrostatic testing is a non-destructive pressure integrity test in which a pressure vessel or piping system is filled with water and pressurized to a value typically **1.3 times the Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** to verify strength and leak-tightness. Water is preferred over air because **(1)** water is essentially incompressible, so it stores negligible elastic strain energy — even if the vessel ruptures, the energy release is small and non-fragmenting; **(2)** compressed air/gas, being highly compressible, stores enormous energy which on release causes explosive fragmentation (a pressure vessel fragment can travel hundreds of meters); **(3)** water makes leaks easy to detect via visible seepage, sweating, or dripping from weld defects; **(4)** water is cheap, readily available, non-flammable, and non-toxic; **(5)** the high density of water prevents pressure surges and provides stable test conditions. Hence, hydro testing with water is the universally mandated default per **ASME Section VIII** and **IS 2825**. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any six safety precautions to be observed during hydro testing of a pressure vessel.** **Model Answer (3 marks — list any 6 of the following 9):** 1. **Complete LOTO** of all energy sources feeding the vessel (electrical, thermal, hydraulic, pneumatic). 2. **Install spectacle blinds** on all process line connections to ensure positive isolation. 3. **Calibrate the test pressure gauge** (and a witness gauge) within the last 30 days; accuracy ±0.5% of full scale. 4. **Establish an exclusion zone** of at least 1.5× to 2× the vessel's height or diameter (whichever is greater) and barricade with red rope and warning signs. 5. **Ensure 100% water filling** with no trapped air by venting at the highest point until water overflows. 6. **Pressurize in stages** (0.5×, 0.8×, 1.0× test pressure) — never go directly to full test pressure. 7. **Wear full PPE** — hard hat, safety boots, face shield, gloves, full-body harness for vertical vessels. 8. **Fit a pressure relief valve** set at 1.1× test pressure on the pressurizing line to prevent accidental over-pressurization. 9. **Ensure the test pump has a slow, controlled delivery rate** (typically < 0.35 bar/second) to prevent water hammer. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **7 + 7 marks** in sub-parts (a) and (b). You are expected to write approximately **3 to 4 pages** of structured answer with diagrams, formulas, and case-specific detail. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the step-by-step procedure for hydrostatic testing of a newly fabricated pressure vessel. Include all safety precautions to be observed. **Model Answer (Part a — 7 marks):** The hydrostatic test of a newly fabricated pressure vessel is a critical pre-commissioning activity governed by **ASME Section VIII** and **IS 2825**. The procedure is executed in the following phases: **Phase 1 — Pre-Test Preparation (Safety Foundation):** - Verify the vessel is **mechanically complete**, all fabrication welds have passed radiographic or ultrasonic NDT, and the post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) is complete if specified. - Apply **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** on all utilities and process connections. Install **spectacle blinds** on every nozzle to positively isolate the vessel. - **Calibrate the pressure gauges** (primary + witness) — accuracy ±0.5% of full scale, with a calibration certificate not older than 30 days. The gauge range should be 1.5× to 3× the test pressure for optimal readability. - Conduct a **pre-test low-pressure air leak test** (typically 0.2 bar) using soap solution to catch gross leaks early. - **Establish the exclusion zone** — 1.5× to 2× the vessel's largest dimension, rope-barricaded, with "DANGER – HYDRO TEST IN PROGRESS" signage. Brief all personnel on emergency procedures. - Position the test pump, pressure relief valve (set at 1.1× TP), and data logger near the lowest point of the vessel. **Phase 2 — Filling and Venting (Eliminating Trapped Air):** - Fill the vessel with **clean, potable water** from the **lowest point** to ensure air is pushed upward and out through the top vent. - Continue filling until water **overflows the high-point vent** for at least 5 minutes. Trapped air is the primary cause of catastrophic hydro test accidents (air becomes a pneumatic bomb). - Close the vent valve once steady water flow is confirmed. **Phase 3 — Staged Pressurization:** - **Stage 1:** Pressurize to **0.5 × Test Pressure**. Hold for 5 minutes. Inspector walks the vessel perimeter, checking all gaskets, flanges, and welds for seepage. - **Stage 2:** Pressurize to **0.8 × Test Pressure**. Hold for 5 minutes. Repeat visual inspection. - **Stage 3:** Raise pressure **slowly and gradually** (≤ 0.35 bar/sec) to the full **Test Pressure = 1.3 × MAWP**. Do not overshoot. **Phase 4 — Hold and Inspection:** - Maintain test pressure for the **specified hold period** (30 minutes to 4 hours, depending on vessel size and code). - Monitor both gauges; any pressure drop > 1% is a leak. - Document all observations — leaks (location, size, rate), permanent deformation, gauge readings, ambient temperature. **Phase 5 — Depressurization and Restoration:** - Reduce pressure **slowly** to atmospheric through the test pump's bypass or a vent valve. - Drain water from the bottom drain valve. - Remove spectacle blinds, restore LOTO devices, and tag the vessel as "HYDROSTATIC TESTED AND CERTIFIED". **Phase 6 — Documentation:** - Issue a **Hydrostatic Test Report** with: vessel tag no., design pressure, test pressure achieved, hold time, gauge calibration certs, NDT references, inspector signature, photographs of any defect points, and final disposition (PASS/FAIL). **Safety Precautions (Integrated Throughout):** - PPE: hard hat, safety shoes, face shield, gloves. - Emergency depressurization valve accessible to the safety officer. - Fire-fighting equipment standby. - Communication protocol: walkie-talkies between pump operator, inspector, and safety officer. - No personnel inside the exclusion zone during pressurization above 0.5× TP. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 1500 mm, design pressure 8 bar, and is fabricated from SA-516 Grade 70 carbon steel plate (allowable stress = 138 MPa) with a joint efficiency of 0.85 and corrosion allowance of 2 mm. Calculate: (i) the hydrostatic test pressure; (ii) the required wall thickness as per ASME code formula; (iii) the hoop and longitudinal stresses at the test pressure for the calculated thickness. **Model Answer (Part b — 7 marks):** **Given Data:** - Internal diameter $D = 1500$ mm - Design pressure $P_{design} = 8$ bar = 0.8 MPa = 0.8 N/mm² - Allowable stress $S = 138$ MPa = 138 N/mm² - Joint efficiency $E = 0.85$ - Corrosion allowance $C = 2$ mm **Step 1 — Hydrostatic Test Pressure:** $$ P_{test} = 1.3 \times P_{design} = 1.3 \times 8 = 10.4 \text{ bar} = 1.04 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 2 — Required Wall Thickness (ASME Section VIII, Div 1, UG-27):** $$ t = \frac{P_{test} \times R}{S E - 0.6 \times P_{test}} + C $$ where $R = D/2 = 1500/2 = 750$ mm. $$ t = \frac{1.04 \times 750}{138 \times 0.85 - 0.6 \times 1.04} + 2 $$ $$ t = \frac{780}{117.3 - 0.624} + 2 = \frac{780}{116.676} + 2 $$ $$ t = 6.686 + 2 = 8.686 \text{ mm} $$ Rounding up to standard plate thickness (next available): $$ \boxed{t_{required} = 10 \text{ mm (standard plate)}} $$ **Step 3 — Stresses at Test Pressure (for $t = 10$ mm):** Hoop stress: $$ \sigma_h = \frac{P_{test} \times D}{2 t} = \frac{1.04 \times 1500}{2 \times 10} = \frac{1560}{20} = 78 \text{ MPa} $$ Longitudinal stress: $$ \sigma_l = \frac{P_{test} \times D}{4 t} = \frac{1.04 \times 1500}{4 \times 10} = \frac{1560}{40} = 39 \text{ MPa} $$ **Verification of adequacy:** $$ \sigma_h = 78 \text{ MPa} < S = 138 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ Safe} $$ $$ \sigma_l = 39 \text{ MPa} < S = 138 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ Safe} $$ The vessel is safe at test pressure with a 10 mm wall. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between hydrostatic and pneumatic pressure testing. Under what conditions is pneumatic testing permitted, and what additional safety measures are required? **Model Answer (Part a — 7 marks):** | Parameter | Hydrostatic Testing | Pneumatic Testing | |---|---|---| | **Test Medium** | Water (incompressible) | Air or Nitrogen (compressible) | | **Compressibility** | ~0.5% | ~100% by volume | | **Stored Energy on Rupture** | Very low | Extremely high (10–1000× greater) | | **Failure Mode** | Slow tear, leak | Explosive fragmentation | | **Leak Detection** | Visible water seepage | Soap bubble, hissing, ultrasonic | | **Hazard Level** | Moderate | **Severe, life-threatening** | | **Pressure Multiplier** | 1.3 × MAWP | 1.5 × MAWP (or 1.1× for some codes) | | **Cost** | Low | High (compressor, gas) | | **Code Default** | **Yes (preferred)** | Only if hydro is impractical | **Conditions When Pneumatic Testing is Permitted (per ASME Section V, Article 10):** 1. The vessel is so large or heavy that filling with water would cause excessive foundation loading (e.g., large atmospheric storage tanks on soft soil, offshore platforms). 2. The vessel's design does not permit complete draining and drying (water would cause corrosion during service — e.g., aluminum alloy vessels, aerospace components). 3. The vessel operates under conditions where residual water would be harmful (e.g., cryogenic LNG vessels, chlorine gas lines). 4. The vessel is of a design unsuitable for hydro (e.g., thin diaphragms, bellows, non-metallic liners). 5. The vessel has been in-service and water filling may damage internal linings or catalyst beds. **Mandatory Additional Safety Measures for Pneumatic Testing:** 1. **Pre-test risk assessment** and method statement approved by a competent authority. 2. **Reduced test pressure** during initial pressurization (typically 0.17 × TP) to confirm integrity before going to full pressure. 3. **Two independent pressure relief devices** on the test line, both set at ≤ 1.1 × TP. 4. **Exclusion zone of 3× to 5×** the vessel's largest dimension (significantly larger than hydro zone). 5. **No personnel in the exclusion zone** during pressurization — all observations done remotely. 6. **Use of N₂ or air only — never O₂** (eliminates explosion risk from combustible atmospheres). 7. **Hood-mounted or remote gauges** with mechanical isolation to protect observers from fragments. 8. **Acoustic emission monitoring** to detect incipient failure signatures. 9. **Staged pressurization with longer hold times** at each stage to allow stress redistribution. 10. **Written authorization** from a senior safety officer before commencing. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A vertical cylindrical storage tank is 8 m tall with an internal diameter of 4 m. It is being hydrostatically tested. If the test pressure gauge is mounted 1.5 m above the tank base and reads 12 bar, calculate: (i) the pressure at the top of the tank; (ii) the pressure at the bottom of the tank; (iii) the maximum hoop stress in the tank wall, assuming a wall thickness of 14 mm. **Model Answer (Part b — 7 marks):** **Given Data:** - Tank height $H = 8$ m - Internal diameter $D = 4$ m - Wall thickness $t = 14$ mm = 0.014 m - Gauge location: $h_{gauge} = 1.5$ m above base - Gauge reading: $P_{gauge} = 12$ bar - $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1 — Convert gauge pressure to Pa:** $$ P_{gauge} = 12 \text{ bar} = 12 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.2 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 2 — Pressure at the top of the tank:** The top of the tank is at height $H - h_{gauge} = 8 - 1.5 = 6.5$ m **above** the gauge. So the pressure at the top is **less** than the gauge reading by the hydrostatic head of 6.5 m. $$ P_{head, top} = \rho g h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 6.5 = 63765 \text{ Pa} = 0.638 \text{ bar} $$ $$ P_{top} = P_{gauge} - P_{head, top} = 12 - 0.638 = 11.36 \text{ bar} $$ **Step 3 — Pressure at the bottom of the tank:** The bottom of the tank is at $h_{gauge} = 1.5$ m **below** the gauge. The pressure at the bottom is **higher** than the gauge reading. $$ P_{head, bottom} = \rho g h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 = 14715 \text{ Pa} = 0.147 \text{ bar} $$ $$ P_{bottom} = P_{gauge} + P_{head, bottom} = 12 + 0.147 = 12.147 \text{ bar} $$ **Step 4 — Maximum Hoop Stress:** The maximum pressure is at the bottom of the tank, so the maximum hoop stress occurs there: $$ \sigma_{h, max} = \frac{P_{bottom} \times D}{2 t} $$ Convert $P_{bottom} = 12.147$ bar = $12.147 \times 10^5$ Pa = $1.2147$ MPa = $1.2147$ N/mm². Convert $D = 4$ m = 4000 mm; $t = 14$ mm. $$ \sigma_{h, max} = \frac{1.2147 \times 4000}{2 \times 14} = \frac{4858.8}{28} = 173.5 \text{ MPa} $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_{h, max} \approx 173.5 \text{ MPa}} $$ **Note on thin-wall validity:** $$ \frac{D}{t} = \frac{4000}{14} = 285.7 \geq 20 \quad \checkmark \text{ Thin-wall formula is valid.} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > > 1. **Forgetting the head correction in tall vessels** — 5–7 marks lost. Always compute $\rho g h$ for any vessel height > 3 m. > 2. **Using design pressure instead of test pressure** in stress calculations — The code requires stress check at TEST pressure, not design pressure. > 3. **Forgetting the corrosion allowance $C$** in the thickness formula — This is added OUTSIDE the denominator, not inside. > 4. **Mixing units** — Pa vs. MPa vs. bar vs. N/mm² are all pressure units but with different conversion factors. Convert to a single unit BEFORE substituting. > 5. **Using 1.5× instead of 1.3× for test pressure** — This is for PNEUMATIC testing, not hydro. Hydro = 1.3×. > 6. **Not validating the thin-wall assumption** — Code deducts 1 mark if $D/t < 20$ but you used the thin-wall formula. > 7. **Skipping the gauge placement rule** — Pressure gauge must be at the HIGHEST point to ensure minimum pressure is verified. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HYDRO TESTING — FINAL REVISION BULLET LIST** - **Definition:** Non-destructive test using **water** at **1.3 × MAWP** to verify strength and leak-tightness of pressure equipment. - **Why water?** **Incompressible** → low stored energy → **safe failure mode** (no fragmentation). - **Why not air (pneumatic)?** **Compressible** → high stored energy → **explosive, fragmenting failure** — extremely dangerous. - **Test pressure formula:** $P_{test} = 1.3 \times P_{design}$ (hydro); $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design}$ (pneumatic). - **Hydrostatic head formula:** $P_{head} = \rho g h$ — critical for tall vessels; gauge must be at **highest point**. - **Hoop stress formula:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$ — this is the **dominant stress** in cylindrical vessels. - **Longitudinal stress formula:** $\sigma_l = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ — exactly **half** the hoop stress. - **Thin-wall validity:** $\dfrac{D}{t} \geq 20$ for thin-wall theory to apply. - **Wall thickness formula (ASME):** $t = \dfrac{PR}{SE - 0.6P} + C$ — note the **0.6P** term (NOT 0.5P); include $C$ separately. - **Procedure:** LOTO → blinds → calibrate gauges → exclusion zone → fill from bottom → vent air from top → **staged pressurization** (0.5×, 0.8×, 1.0×) → hold → inspect → **slow depressurize** → drain → document. - **Critical safety controls:** Trapped air elimination, exclusion zone (1.5–2× dimension), calibrated gauges (max 30 days old), pressure relief valve (set at 1.1× TP), staged pressurization (< 0.35 bar/s), full PPE, no personnel in zone during high-pressure phases. - **Governing codes:** ASME Section VIII (vessels), ASME B31.3 (piping), API 510 (in-service inspection), IS 2825 (Indian standard). - **Acceptance criteria:** No visible leak, no pressure drop during hold, no permanent deformation, no unusual sounds. - **Pneumatic testing allowed only when:** Hydro is impractical (very large tanks, vessels where water residue is harmful, cryogenic equipment, lined vessels with low lining pressure rating). - **Maximum hoop stress location in a tall tank:** At the **bottom** of the tank (highest pressure due to hydrostatic head). - **Why cylindrical vessels split lengthwise:** Hoop stress (2:1) is greater than longitudinal stress, so the vessel fails along its **length**. - **Always measure pressure at the HIGHEST point** to confirm that the **minimum** test pressure is achieved everywhere.

topics.title: Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **valve** is a mechanical device designed to control, regulate, or isolate the flow of fluids (liquids, gases, slurries, or vapors) within a piping system by opening, closing, or partially obstructing the passage. In the context of industrial safety engineering, valves are classified as **Safety-Critical Elements (SCEs)** because they perform barrier functions that prevent the release of hazardous materials, mitigate overpressure scenarios, and enable safe isolation of process equipment during emergencies.

From a functional standpoint, valves are governed by the following international standards:
- **API 6D** – Pipeline valves (specification)
- **ASME B16.34** – Valve design, materials, and testing
- **API 520 / API 521** – Sizing and selection of pressure-relieving devices
- **API 600** – Bolted bonnet gate valves
- **ISA 75** – Control valve standards
- **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511** – Functional safety of valve systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 4 of Safety in Engineering Industry, valves are studied as **active engineered barriers** that convert a *control signal* (manual, pneumatic, hydraulic, or electrical) into a *physical change in flow area*, thereby serving protection functions like **isolation, overpressure relief, and emergency shutdown (ESD)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **highway toll booth** controlling traffic onto a bridge:
- When the gate is **open**, traffic flows freely.
- When the gate is **closed**, flow is completely blocked.
- When partially raised, only a limited number of vehicles can pass.

A valve behaves exactly the same way for fluids inside a pipe. The "gate" (called the *disc*, *ball*, *plug*, or *gate*, depending on valve type) sits inside the flow path. When it moves, it changes how much fluid can pass.

Now extend the analogy to **safety**:
- If the bridge detects too many vehicles, the toll booth operator **automatically closes** the gate to prevent collapse. → This is exactly what a **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)** does when system pressure exceeds safe limits.
- If a fire breaks out near the bridge, the operator **remotely closes** entry points to prevent people from entering. → This is what an **Emergency Shutdown Valve (ESDV)** does.

Thus, valves are not just "on/off devices" — in safety engineering, they are **lifeline components** that act on their own (automatic) or on command (remote/manual) to keep a plant and its people safe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Safety Metric:** In Safety Integrity Level (SIL) studies under **IEC 61511**, a valve's probability of failure on demand (**PFDavg**) must be quantified. The standard requires **PFDavg ≤ 10⁻² for SIL 1**, **≤ 10⁻³ for SIL 2**, and **≤ 10⁻⁴ for SIL 3**.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Metrics of a Valve

The three most important physical parameters of any valve are:

1. **Nominal Diameter (DN or NPS)** – standardized size (e.g., DN 50, NPS 2").
2. **Pressure Rating (PN or Class)** – maximum allowable working pressure (e.g., PN 40, Class 300 = **5.0 MPa**).
3. **Flow Coefficient (Cv or Kv)** – the metric that expresses how much fluid passes through the valve at a given pressure drop.

$$C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{SG}{\Delta P}}$$

Where:
- $C_v$ = flow coefficient (US gallons/min at 1 psi drop)
- $Q$ = flow rate (US GPM)
- $SG$ = specific gravity of the fluid (water = 1.0)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across the valve (psi)

> [!NOTE]
> In SI units, the equivalent is the **Kv value**, representing flow rate in m³/h of water at a pressure drop of **1 bar**.

### 1.4 Primary Safety Functions of a Valve

| Safety Function | Description | Example Valve Type |
|-----------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Isolation** | Completely block flow during maintenance or emergency | Gate valve, Ball valve, Plug valve |
| **Overpressure Protection** | Relieve excess pressure to prevent vessel rupture | Pressure Safety Valve (PSV), Rupture Disc |
| **Emergency Shutdown** | Rapidly stop flow in hazardous situations | ESD Ball valve, ESD Butterfly valve |
| **Reverse Flow Prevention** | Stop backflow that could damage equipment | Check valve, Non-return valve |
| **Flow Regulation** | Maintain precise flow/pressure/temperature | Control valve (globe type) |
| **Sampling/Draining** | Take samples or drain condensate | Needle valve, Drain valve |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification of Valves (Operational Theory)

Valves are classified in two primary ways: **by motion of the closure element** and **by actuation method**.

#### 2.1.1 Classification by Motion Type

**A) Linear Motion Valves** – the closure member moves in a straight line to obstruct flow.

- **Gate Valve**: A wedge-shaped gate slides perpendicular to flow. Used for full open/close service; not for throttling.
  - *Why*: When partially open, vibration (called *gate chatter*) erodes the seating surfaces.
  - *Safety use*: Mainline isolation in oil & gas pipelines.

- **Globe Valve**: A disc moves parallel to the seat, forcing fluid to change direction (Z-pattern). Ideal for throttling.
  - *Why*: The tortuous flow path gives precise flow control but creates high pressure drop.
  - *Safety use*: Level control in boilers, pressure regulation.

- **Diaphragm Valve**: A flexible elastomeric diaphragm presses against a weir. Used for corrosive fluids and sterile applications.
  - *Safety use*: Chemical dosing lines, pharmaceutical pipelines.

- **Needle Valve**: A tapered needle provides extremely fine flow control. Used for instrumentation and sampling.

- **Pinch Valve**: A rubber sleeve is pinched to close flow. Used for slurries and abrasive media.

**B) Rotary Motion Valves** – the closure member rotates about a shaft.

- **Ball Valve**: A spherical ball with a bore rotates 90° between open and closed. Provides bubble-tight shutoff.
  - *Why*: Quarter-turn action enables fast closing — critical for ESD applications.
  - *Safety use*: ESD valves in offshore platforms, LNG terminals.

- **Butterfly Valve**: A disc rotates 90° on a central shaft. Compact and lightweight.
  - *Why*: Lower cost than ball valve, suitable for large diameters (>DN 300).
  - *Safety use*: Firewater lines, tank farm isolation.

- **Plug Valve**: A cylindrical or conical plug with a bore rotates to control flow.

**C) Self-Actuated / Special Safety Valves** – operate without external power.

- **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)**: Spring-loaded; opens automatically when upstream pressure exceeds set pressure.
- **Rupture Disc**: A thin metal disc that bursts at a calibrated pressure. Single-use, non-reclosing.
- **Check Valve**: Allows flow in only one direction; closes automatically on flow reversal.
- **Safety Relief Valve (SRV)**: For liquid service; opens proportionally to overpressure.

#### 2.1.2 Classification by Actuation Method

| Actuation Type | Symbol | Speed | Reliability | Typical Use |
|----------------|--------|-------|-------------|-------------|
| **Manual (handwheel/lever)** | ✋ | Slow | Depends on operator | Non-critical isolation |
| **Pneumatic** | 💨 | Fast (1–3 s) | High (gas supply failure → fail-safe) | ESD, control valves |
| **Hydraulic** | 🛢️ | Very fast (<1 s) | Very high | Subsea ESDV, HIPPS |
| **Electric (motorized)** | ⚡ | Slow (10–30 s) | Moderate | Remote isolation, large valves |
| **Self-actuated (spring)** | 🔄 | Instant | Highest | PSV, check valve |

### 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|------------------|------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Flow Coefficient (US)** | $C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{SG / \Delta P}$ | Q in GPM, ΔP in psi | Sizing control valves |
| 2 | **Flow Coefficient (SI)** | $K_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{SG / \Delta P}$ | Q in m³/h, ΔP in bar | Sizing control valves (metric) |
| 3 | **Cv → Kv Conversion** | $K_v = 0.865 \cdot C_v$ | dimensionless factor | Unit conversion |
| 4 | **Orifice Area (PSV)** | $A = \dfrac{W}{C_d \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot \sqrt{P_1 \cdot M / Z \cdot T_1}}$ | A in mm², W in kg/h | API 520 PSV sizing |
| 5 | **Set Pressure Rule (PSV)** | $P_{set} \leq 1.10 \cdot P_{MAWP}$ | pressure in barg | ASME Sec VIII UG-125 |
| 6 | **Blowdown** | $\%BD = \dfrac{P_{set} - P_{reseat}}{P_{set}} \times 100$ | typically 2–7% | Defines reseat pressure |
| 7 | **Relieving Capacity (Steam)** | $W = 51.5 \cdot A \cdot P_1$ | W in kg/h, A in mm², P in barg | API 520 Eq. 4 |
| 8 | **Critical Pressure Ratio** | $\eta_c = \dfrac{P_c}{P_1}$ | dimensionless | Choked flow condition |
| 9 | **Leakage Class (API 598)** | Class I → VI | bubbles/min | Defines shutoff tightness |
| 10 | **Failure Probability (SIL)** | $PFD_{avg} \leq 10^{-n}$ | n = 1, 2, 3, 4 | IEC 61511 SIL rating |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, note that for **leakage classes** of soft-seated ball valves, **Class VI** (also called *bubble-tight*) permits **zero visible leakage** — the standard used for **hydrocarbon ESD service**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The valve system theory is applied in:
- **Oil & Gas** – Christmas trees, manifold isolation, subsea ESD systems.
- **Power Plants** – Boiler safety valves (ASME Section I), turbine bypass, feedwater control.
- **Chemical Process** – Reactor pressure relief, batch transfer isolation, reactor dump valves.
- **Pharmaceutical** – Sterile diaphragm valves for batch reactors, CIP (clean-in-place) systems.
- **Aerospace & Cryogenics** – LOX/LH₂ valves with extreme leak-tightness requirements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) Orifice Area Sizing

The following is the **API 520 / API 521 standard derivation** for sizing the discharge area of a Pressure Safety Valve used for **gas/vapor service**.

#### Step 1 – Define the Mass Flow Equation for Compressible Fluid

The general compressible mass flow equation through an orifice is:

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T_1}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot k}{k-1} \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{2/k} - \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{(k+1)/k} \right]}
$$

**Explanation of terms:**
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (typically **0.9–0.97**)
- $A$ = effective discharge area (m²)
- $P_1$ = upstream relieving pressure (Pa absolute)
- $P_2$ = downstream/back pressure (Pa absolute)
- $M$ = molecular weight of gas (kg/kmol)
- $Z$ = compressibility factor
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**
- $T_1$ = inlet temperature (K)
- $k$ = specific heat ratio (Cp/Cv)

#### Step 2 – Recognize the Critical (Choked) Flow Condition

When the downstream pressure drops below a critical ratio, flow becomes **choked** (sonic at the throat) and downstream pressure no longer affects mass flow. For an ideal gas:

$$
\eta_c = \left( \dfrac{2}{k+1} \right)^{k/(k-1)}
$$

For air with **k = 1.4**, this gives the famous result:

$$
\eta_c = \left( \dfrac{2}{2.4} \right)^{1.4/0.4} = 0.528
$$

**Interpretation**: When **P₂/P₁ < 0.528** (for air), the flow is choked. This is why **API 520** simplifies the equation for choked flow.

#### Step 3 – Apply the API 520 Simplified Formula for Critical Flow

Under choked conditions, the equation reduces to:

$$
\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot T_1}} \cdot \sqrt{k \left( \dfrac{2}{k+1} \right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}
$$

Regrouping constants into **K_d** (effective coefficient of discharge) and introducing the **capacity correction factor K_b** (for back pressure) and **K_c** (for combination of rupture disc + PSV):

$$
W = \dfrac{A \cdot C_d \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{M}}{\sqrt{Z \cdot T_1}}
$$

This is the **engineering equation** used in API 520. Solving for **A**:

$$
A = \dfrac{W}{C_d \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{Z \cdot T_1}{M}}
$$

#### Step 4 – Numerical Example: Sizing a PSV for a Steam Boiler

**Given:**
- Relieving load W = **5000 kg/h**
- Set pressure = **10 barg** (≈ 11 barg relieving at 10% accumulation)
- Superheated steam at **250 °C** (T₁ = 523 K)
- M = 18 kg/kmol, Z ≈ 1.0
- k = 1.3 (superheated steam)

**Step 4a** – Apply the API 520 short-form for steam (for steam service, API 520 provides Eq. 4):

$$
W = 51.5 \cdot A \cdot P_1 \quad \text{[W in kg/h, A in mm², P in barg]}
$$

**Step 4b** – Solve for A:

$$
A = \dfrac{W}{51.5 \cdot P_1} = \dfrac{5000}{51.5 \times 10} = \dfrac{5000}{515}
$$

$$
A = 9.71 \; \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 4c** – Convert to standard orifice designation:

API 520 standard orifice designations: D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T
- Orifice **D** = 71 mm²
- Orifice **E** = 126 mm²

Since 9.71 mm² < 71 mm², the next available orifice is **D**.

**Step 4d** – Calculate actual relieving capacity:

$$
W_{actual} = 51.5 \cdot 71 \cdot 10 = 36{,}565 \; \text{kg/h} \gg 5000 \; \text{kg/h} \; \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion**: An **API 520 Orifice D** valve is selected — providing a large margin (over-design factor ≈ 7.3), which is normal practice for PSV selection.

#### Step 5 – Summary of Analytical Matrix

| Step | What is calculated | Formula used | Output |
|------|-------------------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | Mass flow (general) | Compressible orifice equation | $\dot{m}$ |
| 2 | Choked flow criterion | $\eta_c = (2/(k+1))^{k/(k-1)}$ | Decide choked vs subsonic |
| 3 | Required orifice area | $A = W / (51.5 \cdot P_1)$ | A in mm² |
| 4 | Select standard orifice | API 520 Table | Orifice letter |
| 5 | Verify capacity | $W_{actual} = 51.5 \cdot A \cdot P_1$ | Check margin |

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Valve Types for Safety Service

| Criterion | Gate Valve | Globe Valve | Ball Valve | Butterfly Valve | PSV |
|-----------|------------|-------------|------------|-----------------|-----|
| **Shutoff tightness** | Good | Good | Excellent | Moderate | N/A (opens) |
| **Flow capacity (Cv)** | High | Low | High | High | N/A |
| **Pressure drop** | Low | High | Low | Low | N/A |
| **Throttling capability** | Poor | Excellent | Moderate | Moderate | N/A |
| **Speed of operation** | Slow | Slow | Fast (¼ turn) | Fast (¼ turn) | Instant |
| **Fire-safe rating** | Yes | Yes | Yes (API 607) | Yes (API 607) | Yes |
| **Best safety use** | Mainline isolation | Level/pressure control | ESD isolation | Large-bore ESD, firewater | Overpressure protection |
| **Cost (relative)** | Low | Medium | Medium-High | Low | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **hydrocarbon ESD service**, the preferred valve per **API 6D / API 6DSS** is a **soft-seated ball valve with API 607 fire-safe certification** and **double block & bleed (DBB)** capability.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Valve Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[VALVES]:::root --> B[Linear Motion]
    A --> C[Rotary Motion]
    A --> D[Self-Actuated Safety]
    A --> E[Special Purpose]

    B --> B1[Gate Valve]
    B --> B2[Globe Valve]
    B --> B3[Diaphragm Valve]
    B --> B4[Needle Valve]
    B --> B5[Pinch Valve]

    C --> C1[Ball Valve]
    C --> C2[Butterfly Valve]
    C --> C3[Plug Valve]

    D --> D1[Pressure Safety Valve PSV]
    D --> D2[Rupture Disc]
    D --> D3[Check Valve]
    D --> D4[Safety Relief Valve SRV]

    E --> E1[Control Valve with Actuator]
    E --> E2[Emergency Shutdown Valve ESDV]
    E --> E3[Sampling Valve]

    B1 --> F1[Use: Mainline Isolation]
    B2 --> F2[Use: Flow Regulation]
    C1 --> F3[Use: Rapid ESD]
    C2 --> F4[Use: Large Bore Firewater]
    D1 --> F5[Use: Overpressure Protection]
    D3 --> F6[Use: Backflow Prevention]
    E1 --> F7[Use: Process Control]
    E2 --> F8[Use: Plant Emergency Isolation]

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#fff,stroke:#0b1d33,stroke-width:3px
    classDef branch fill:#dbe9f4,color:#1f3a5f,stroke:#1f3a5f,stroke-width:1px
    class A root
    class B,C,D,E,B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,C1,C2,C3,D1,D2,D3,D4,E1,E2,E3,F1,F2,F3,F4,F5,F6,F7,F8 branch
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Schematic: Pressure Safety Valve Operation (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    V[Vessel Under Pressure]:::input --> P1[Inlet Pressure P1]
    P1 --> SPRING[Spring Loaded Disc Assembly]:::core
    SPRING --> SENSOR{P1 vs Set Pressure}:::decision
    SENSOR -- P1 below set --> CLOSED[Valve CLOSED - Normal Operation]:::safe
    SENSOR -- P1 reaches set --> OPENS[Disc Lifts - Flow Begins]:::warn
    SENSOR -- P1 exceeds set+10% --> FULL[Full Lift - Max Discharge]:::danger
    FULL --> VENT[Discharge to Safe Location Atmosphere or Flare]
    VENT --> P2[Reaches Reseat Pressure]
    P2 --> CLOSED

    classDef input fill:#b8d4ed,color:#0b1d33,stroke:#1f3a5f,stroke-width:2px
    classDef core fill:#ffe082,color:#3e2723,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#fff59d,color:#3e2723,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px
    classDef safe fill:#a5d6a7,color:#1b5e20,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    classDef warn fill:#ffcc80,color:#e65100,stroke:#ef6c00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef danger fill:#ef9a9a,color:#b71c1c,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px
    class V,P1,P2 input
    class SPRING core
    class SENSOR decision
    class CLOSED safe
    class OPENS warn
    class FULL,VENT danger
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: ESD (Emergency Shutdown) Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    INIT[Process Running] --> MON[Continuous Sensor Monitoring Pressure Temperature Level Flow]:::monitor
    MON --> DETECT{Abnormal Condition Detected}:::decision
    DETECT -- Normal --> INIT
    DETECT -- Abnormal --> ALARM[Alarm Raised in Control Room]:::warn
    ALARM --> AUTO{SIS Logic IEC 61511}:::decision
    AUTO -- SIL 2 or 3 --> CLOSE[Close ESD Ball Valves within 1 to 3 seconds]:::danger
    AUTO -- Manual Override --> OPERATOR[Operator Activates ESD Pushbutton]:::warn
    OPERATOR --> CLOSE
    CLOSE --> ISO[Process Isolated from Source]
    ISO --> VENT[Vessels Vented or Depressurized Safely]
    VENT --> SAFE[Plant Reaches Safe State]

    classDef monitor fill:#bbdefb,color:#0d47a1,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#fff59d,color:#3e2723,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px
    classDef warn fill:#ffcc80,color:#e65100,stroke:#ef6c00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef danger fill:#ef9a9a,color:#b71c1c,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px
    classDef safe fill:#a5d6a7,color:#1b5e20,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    class MON monitor
    class DETECT,AUTO decision
    class ALARM,OPERATOR warn
    class CLOSE,ISO,VENT danger
    class INIT,SAFE safe
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Functional Comparison of a Manual vs Automatic Safety Valve

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MANUAL[Manual Valve System]
        H[Handwheel] --> G1[Gate or Ball Disc]
        G1 --> F1[Flow Control]
    end

    subgraph AUTOMATIC[Automatic Safety Valve System]
        P[Process Variable] --> S[Sensor Transmitter]
        S --> SIG[Signal 4-20 mA or Pneumatic 3-15 psi]
        SIG --> C[SIS Logic Solver]
        C --> ACT[Actuator Pneumatic Hydraulic Solenoid]
        ACT --> V[Ball or Butterfly Disc]
        V --> F2[Flow Blocked or Relieved]
    end

    classDef manual fill:#c8e6c9,color:#1b5e20,stroke:#388e3c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef auto fill:#ffcdd2,color:#b71c1c,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px

    class P,S,SIG,C,ACT,V,F2 auto
```
