<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** 
*Define a Pressure Safety Valve (PSV). State any two functions of a PSV in process industries.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)** is an automatic, spring-loaded pressure-relieving device that opens when the inlet static pressure reaches a predetermined set value, and recloses when the pressure falls below the reseat pressure. It is the **last line of defense** against overpressure in boilers, pressure vessels, and piping systems, conforming to **ASME Section VIII (UG-125 to UG-140)** for unfired pressure vessels and **ASME Section I** for power boilers.

**Two functions:**
1. **Overpressure Protection** – Prevents catastrophic rupture of pressure vessels by relieving excess fluid to a safe location (atmosphere, flare, or scrubber).
2. **Process Safeguarding** – Maintains the system pressure below the **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** during abnormal transients such as blocked discharge, fire exposure, or thermal expansion.

*(Distribution: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for the two functions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
*List any four types of valves used in process industries and state one specific application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Valve Type | Application |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Gate Valve** | Mainline isolation in crude oil pipelines |
| 2 | **Globe Valve** | Feedwater regulation in boiler steam drum |
| 3 | **Ball Valve** | Emergency Shutdown (ESD) on offshore platform |
| 4 | **Butterfly Valve** | Large-bore firewater mains (>DN 300) |
| 5 | **Check Valve** | Prevention of backflow in compressor discharge |

*(Any four with their applications — ½ mark for the valve type, ¼ mark for the application.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks each) — With Internal Choice

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024]**

**Q(a)** Classify valves used in process industries. Explain in detail the construction, working principle, and safety applications of any **two** types of valves. **(7 marks)**

**Q(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)**. Discuss the terms *set pressure*, *blowdown*, and *overpressure accumulation*. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Solution to Q(a) — 7 Marks

**Classification of Valves:**

Valves are classified based on:
1. **Motion of closure member** – Linear (gate, globe, diaphragm) and Rotary (ball, butterfly, plug).
2. **Function** – Isolation, regulation, safety relief, non-return.
3. **Actuation method** – Manual, pneumatic, hydraulic, electric, self-actuated.

**Detailed explanation of two valves:**

**(1) Ball Valve (3.5 marks)**

- **Construction**: A spherical disc (the "ball") with a cylindrical bore through its centre is mounted inside the valve body. The ball has a stem connected to a handle or actuator on top. Seat rings (typically PTFE or metal) provide sealing on both upstream and downstream sides.
- **Working**: When the bore aligns with the pipe, fluid flows freely (open position). Rotating the ball by **90°** (quarter-turn) brings the solid part of the ball across the flow path, blocking it completely (closed position).
- **Safety applications**: 
  - **ESD valves** in offshore platforms (API 6DSS, API 6A qualified).
  - **Subsea isolation** valves on Christmas trees.
  - **Double block & bleed (DBB)** configurations for hydrocarbon service.

**(2) Globe Valve (3.5 marks)**

- **Construction**: The body has an internal Z-shaped or Y-shaped partition. A disc is mounted on a vertical stem; when the stem is lowered, the disc seats against a horizontal seat ring, blocking flow.
- **Working**: Linear motion of the stem moves the disc up/down. The tortuous flow path causes the fluid to change direction twice, creating significant pressure drop but providing excellent throttling capability.
- **Safety applications**:
  - Boiler **feedwater level control**.
  - **Steam pressure reducing** stations.
  - **Reactor temperature control** via cooling water regulation.

---

##### Solution to Q(b) — 7 Marks

**Construction of a PSV (3 marks):**

A typical spring-loaded PSV consists of:
- **Valve body** with inlet and outlet nozzles.
- **Disc** that lifts to allow discharge.
- **Spring** that pushes the disc against the seat (closing force).
- **Spring adjuster** that sets the spring compression (and hence the set pressure).
- **Bonnet** housing the spring mechanism.
- **Lift lever** (optional) for manual testing.
- **Nozzle** (discharge outlet) directed to a safe location.

**Working (2 marks):**

When the inlet pressure $P_1$ exceeds the **set pressure** (which equals the spring force divided by the disc area), the disc lifts and the valve opens. Fluid discharges through the outlet. As pressure drops below the **reseat pressure**, the spring forces the disc back onto the seat, closing the valve. This is called a *pop-action* valve.

**Key Terminology (2 marks):**

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Set Pressure** | Inlet pressure at which the valve begins to open | 100% of design |
| **Blowdown** | Difference between set and reseat pressure, expressed as % of set | 2–7% |
| **Overpressure Accumulation** | Pressure rise above set pressure during discharge (nozzle load) | ≤ 10% for fire case (ASME) |
| **Reseat Pressure** | Pressure at which the disc fully closes again | Set pressure − Blowdown |

$$\text{Blowdown \%} = \dfrac{P_{set} - P_{reseat}}{P_{set}} \times 100$$

Per **ASME Section VIII UG-125**, the **accumulation** must not exceed **10%** above MAWP for fire exposure and **16%** for other cases (with multiple valves).

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024]**

**Q(a)** Explain the classification of valves based on **motion type** with diagrams. Describe the construction and working of a **butterfly valve** in detail, and discuss its **fire-water service** application. **(7 marks)**

**Q(b)** A pressure vessel is designed for a **MAWP of 8 barg**. The vessel is protected by a single PSV handling saturated steam at **200 °C**. The required relieving capacity is **4000 kg/h**. Using the API 520 short-form equation, determine the **required orifice area** and select the **standard API 520 orifice**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Solution to Q(a) — 7 Marks

**Classification based on motion (3 marks):**

- **Linear motion valves**: closure element moves in a straight line (gate, globe, diaphragm, needle, pinch).
- **Rotary motion valves**: closure element rotates about an axis (ball, butterfly, plug).
- **Self-actuated valves**: operate using process energy (PSV, check valve, rupture disc).

**Butterfly Valve – Construction (2 marks):**

A butterfly valve consists of:
- A circular **disc** mounted on a central shaft that passes through the disc's diameter.
- The disc rotates 90° between fully open (parallel to flow) and fully closed (perpendicular to flow).
- Soft (elastomeric) or metal seats provide sealing.
- The shaft is connected to a **lever, gear, or actuator** outside the body.

**Butterfly Valve – Working (1 mark):**

When the disc is parallel to the pipe axis, the valve is open with minimum obstruction. Rotating the disc by 90° brings it perpendicular to flow, blocking the passage. The valve can also be positioned at intermediate angles for throttling (though not recommended for long-duration throttling).

**Fire-water service application (1 mark):**

Butterfly valves are widely used in **fire-water mains** of refineries, petrochemical plants, and offshore platforms because:
- They are **lightweight and compact**, suitable for large diameters (DN 300 to DN 2000).
- **Fast quarter-turn operation** enables rapid isolation of fire zones.
- When **API 607 fire-safe** certified, they maintain integrity even after direct fire exposure for 30 minutes.
- Lower cost compared to ball valves of equivalent size.

---

##### Solution to Q(b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- MAWP = 8 barg
- Saturated steam at 200 °C (T₁ = 473 K)
- Required relieving capacity W = 4000 kg/h
- Single PSV, set pressure Pset ≤ 1.10 × MAWP = 8.8 barg

**Step 1 – Determine Relieving Pressure** (1 mark)

For saturated steam, use accumulation at set pressure + 10%:

$$
P_1 = P_{set} \times 1.10 = 8.0 \times 1.10 = 8.8 \; \text{barg}
$$

(Assuming set pressure = MAWP = 8 barg.)

**Step 2 – Apply the API 520 Steam Equation** (2 marks)

API 520 Eq. 4 for steam service:

$$
W = 51.5 \cdot A \cdot P_1
$$

Solving for A:

$$
A = \dfrac{W}{51.5 \cdot P_1}
$$

**Step 3 – Numerical Substitution** (2 marks)

$$
A = \dfrac{4000}{51.5 \times 8.8} = \dfrac{4000}{453.2}
$$

$$
A = 8.83 \; \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 – Select Standard Orifice** (1.5 marks)

API 520 standard orifice designations and areas:

| Orifice | Area (mm²) |
|---------|------------|
| D | 71 |
| E | 126 |
| F | 198 |

Since 8.83 mm² < 71 mm², the next available orifice is **Orifice D (71 mm²)**.

**Step 5 – Verify Capacity** (0.5 marks)

$$
W_{actual} = 51.5 \times 71 \times 8.8 = 32{,}177 \; \text{kg/h} \gg 4000 \; \text{kg/h} \; \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** An **API 520 Orifice D** PSV is selected, providing a relieving capacity of approximately **32,177 kg/h** — well above the required 4000 kg/h, satisfying the safety margin requirement.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units Confusion** – Students often mix `bar`, `barg`, `bara`, and `MPa`. The API 520 steam equation requires **P in barg** (gauge). Using absolute pressure gives wrong answers.
> 2. **Set Pressure vs Relieving Pressure** – Set pressure is when the valve *just opens*. Relieving pressure = set + 10% accumulation. Many students forget this and get smaller orifice areas.
> 3. **Cv vs Kv Confusion** – $C_v$ uses US gallons, $K_v$ uses m³/h. Conversion is $K_v = 0.865 \cdot C_v$. Mixing them costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping Diagrams** – KTU valuations give **1–2 marks** for neat labelled diagrams of gate/globe/ball/butterfly valves. Always draw.
> 5. **Forgetting Fire-Safe Standards** – For ESD valve answers, students forget to mention **API 607 / API 6FA fire-safe certification**, which is a key differentiator in marking.
> 6. **PSV Standards Mix-up** – For unfired pressure vessels, cite **ASME Section VIII UG-125**; for power boilers, cite **ASME Section I**. Examiners expect this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **valve** is a Safety-Critical Element (SCE) that performs **isolation, regulation, overpressure relief, and emergency shutdown** functions.
- Valves are classified into **linear motion** (gate, globe), **rotary motion** (ball, butterfly), and **self-actuated** (PSV, check).
- **Ball valves** are preferred for **ESD service** due to fast quarter-turn action and bubble-tight shutoff (API 598 Class VI).
- **Butterfly valves** are preferred for **large-bore fire-water mains** due to compactness and cost.
- **Gate valves** should *never* be used for throttling — only full open or full close — to prevent seat erosion.
- A **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)** must be sized such that **Pset ≤ 1.10 × MAWP** for the fire case (ASME Sec VIII).
- The **API 520 short-form steam equation** is $W = 51.5 \cdot A \cdot P_1$, where W is in kg/h, A in mm², and P in barg.
- **Blowdown** is the difference between set and reseat pressure (typically 2–7%); high blowdown wastes material, low blowdown causes *chattering*.
- **Accumulation** is the overpressure above set pressure during valve discharge — limited to **10%** (fire) or **16%** (non-fire) per ASME.
- **Fire-safe valves** must comply with **API 607 / API 6FA** for hydrocarbon ESD applications.
- **Check valves** prevent reverse flow automatically — critical for **compressor and pump discharge** lines.
- **ESD valves** operate within **1–3 seconds** and must meet **SIL 1/2/3** requirements per **IEC 61511**.
- **Rupture discs** are single-use, non-reclosing devices used either alone or in combination with PSVs (K_c factor).
- **Cv (US)** and **Kv (SI)** are flow coefficients; conversion is $K_v = 0.865 \cdot C_v$.
- The **critical pressure ratio** for choked flow in air is **0.528**; below this ratio, downstream pressure does not affect flow.
- For KTU exams, **always** include a **labelled diagram** of the valve under discussion — this typically fetches 1–2 bonus marks.
- Use the correct **ASME section** (Sec I for boilers, Sec VIII for unfired vessels) when answering PSV-related questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boiler Drums and Headers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Boiler Drums and Headers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Boiler Drum — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> A **Boiler Drum** (also called a *steam-and-water drum*, *steam drum*, or *mud drum*) is a large, cylindrical, pressure-vessel component forming the upper or lower extremity of a water-tube boiler's circulatory system. It functions as the primary phase-separation chamber, feed-water reservoir, and chemical-treatment distribution point, operating under saturated conditions corresponding to the boiler's working pressure.

In KTU board parlance, a boiler drum is classified strictly by its **thermodynamic role**:

| Drum Type | Location | Function | Phase Held |
|-----------|----------|----------|------------|
| **Steam Drum** | Top of boiler | Phase separation, steam purification | Steam + Water |
| **Mud Drum** | Bottom of boiler | Sludge/sediment collection | Water + Solids |
| **Water Drum** | Side of boiler (banked tubes) | Downcomer termination, circulation header | Saturated water |

## 1.2 Boiler Header — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> A **Boiler Header** is a smaller-diameter, thick-walled pressure vessel (cylindrical or box-type) that collects steam from, or distributes water to, multiple boiler tubes. Headers serve as the *interconnection nodes* between the boiler's generating bank and its external piping, including superheaters, economizers, downcomers, and the steam drum.

Two principal header categories exist in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Steam Header** — Collects superheated/saturated steam from multiple tube-banks and routes it to the steam drum or turbine stop valve.
2. **Water Header** — Distributes feed water from the downcomers or economizer outlet to the riser tubes.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Student-Friendly)**
> Imagine a high-rise apartment building's **plumbing and HVAC system**:
> - The **Steam Drum** is the *rooftop water tank* — it stores water, supplies it to "tenants" (tubes), and collects the "steam" (vapor) that bubbles up.
> - The **Mud Drum** is the *basement sump pit* — it catches the heavy sludge and sediment that sinks down.
> - The **Headers** are the *horizontal pipe manifolds* in the basement ceiling that distribute water to all vertical riser pipes (the boiler tubes) feeding the upper floors.
> - The **Downcomers** are the *return risers* carrying used water back down to be reheated.
>
> Just as the apartment's roof tank must withstand hydrostatic pressure, weather, and corrosion — the boiler drum must withstand internal pressure (often **>100 bar**), thermal cycling, and caustic corrosion.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Safety Engineering

> [!WARNING]
> **Boiler Drum & Header Failures are Catastrophic (Historical KTU Reference)**
> - The **2009 Karnal Sugar Mill Boiler Explosion (India)** was traced to a ruptured steam drum.
> - The **Bhopal–style incidents** in sugar/paper industries have all been linked to drum/header weld-joint failures.
> - Per the **Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR) 1950**, drums and headers are classified as **"Class I" pressure parts** requiring mandatory third-party inspection and hydrostatic testing at **1.5× the Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)**.

**Standard Operating Metrics (KTU 2024 Highlights):**

- Typical steam drum operating temperature: **260 °C – 345 °C**
- Typical working pressure range: **40 bar – 250 bar**
- Standard drum length-to-diameter ratio: **L/D ≈ 2.5 to 4**
- Drum material specification: **SA-302 Grade B** (low-alloy steel) for sub-critical, **SA-299** for lower pressures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Steam Drum — Operational Anatomy

A steam drum performs **four critical functions simultaneously**:

1. **Phase Separation** — Mechanical separation of steam from water using cyclones, baffles, and dry pipes.
2. **Water Reserve** — Maintains a minimum water level (NWL — Normal Water Level) for safe heat-transfer surface coverage.
3. **Chemical Treatment** — Allows dosing of phosphates, hydrazine, and amines for internal water treatment.
4. **Circulation Hub** — Receives downcomer water and distributes riser steam/water mixture.

### 2.1.1 Internal Components of a Steam Drum

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory KTU Board Diagram Vocabulary (Know these terms verbatim)**
> - **Cyclone Separators** — Centrifugal devices that throw water droplets outward by tangential entry; remove **>95%** of entrained moisture.
> - **Splash Plates / Chevron Dryers** — Secondary-stage moisture removal; reduce carryover to **<0.1% moisture**.
> - **Scrubbers (Corrugated Plates)** — Final polishing stage for steam purity.
> - **Chemical Feed Pipe** — Distributes phosphate/hydrazine into the drum water.
> - **Continuous Blowdown Pipe** — Removes concentrated TDS to maintain water quality.
> - **Dry Pipe (Perforated Manifold)** — Collects dry steam from the top of the drum for exit to superheater.

## 2.2 Mud Drum — Operational Analysis

The mud drum is the **sediment collection point**. Its design features:

- **Tapered/Conical Bottom** — Promotes sludge slide-off (typically **slope ≥ 1:12**).
- **Blowdown Connection** — Periodic bottom blowdown to remove settled solids.
- **Inspection Handholes** — For periodic internal cleaning and visual inspection.
- **Internal Baffle Plates** — Direct flow and prevent short-circuiting of water to downcomers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept: Caustic Embrittlement**
> Mud drums are particularly vulnerable to **caustic embrittlement** — stress-corrosion cracking at riveted/welded seams caused by concentration of NaOH in crevices. Mitigation: use of **Na₂SO₄** (sodium sulfate) as a neutralizing additive (the **"Sulfate-Alkalinity Ratio"** must be maintained **>2.5**).

## 2.3 Boiler Headers — Detailed Classification

### 2.3.1 By Shape

| Header Shape | Application | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|--------------|-------------|-----------|--------------|
| **Cylindrical (Round)** | Most modern boilers | Uniform stress distribution | Limited tube-pitch flexibility |
| **Box-Type (Rectangular)** | Older, low-pressure boilers | Easy tube insertion | Stress concentrations at corners |
| **Oval/Streamlined** | High-pressure, high-cyclic service | Reduced thermal stress | Complex fabrication |

### 2.3.2 By Function

- **Inlet Header (Downcomer Termination)** — Receives sub-cooled water from downcomers.
- **Intermediate Header** — Cross-ties between parallel tube banks.
- **Outlet Header (Riser Termination)** — Collects steam-water mixture heading to steam drum.
- **Superheater Header** — Inlet (saturated steam) and outlet (superheated steam) manifolds.

## 2.4 Design Pressure & Temperature Considerations

The **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** of a drum or header is calculated using the **ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code, Section I** (for boilers) formula:

$$P_{MAWP} = \frac{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot t}{D + 1.2 \cdot t} - \text{(corrosion allowance)}$$

Where:
- $P_{MAWP}$ = Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MPa)
- $S$ = Maximum allowable stress at design temperature (MPa)
- $E$ = Joint efficiency (for welded drums, typically **0.85** for radiographed, **0.70** for non-radiographed)
- $t$ = Shell thickness (mm)
- $D$ = Inside diameter (mm)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield — memorize for the 14-mark questions.**

| # | Formula / Rule | Variables | Units | Used For |
|---|----------------|-----------|-------|----------|
| 1 | $P_{MAWP} = \dfrac{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot t}{D + 1.2 \cdot t}$ | S, E, t, D | MPa | Drum shell pressure rating |
| 2 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + c$ | P=pressure, R=radius, c=corrosion | mm | Minimum thickness |
| 3 | $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | Hoop stress | MPa | Circumferential stress |
| 4 | $\sigma_{long} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | Longitudinal stress | MPa | Axial stress |
| 5 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{T_{sat} - T_{feed}}{T_{sat} - T_{air}}$ | η = efficiency | % | Economizer effectiveness |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{blowdown} = \dfrac{C_f \cdot \dot{m}_{steam}}{TDS_{drum} - TDS_{feed}}$ | Mass flow rate | kg/hr | Continuous blowdown rate |
| 7 | $\text{Sulfate Ratio} = \dfrac{[SO_4^{2-}]}{[OH^-] + [CO_3^{2-}]}$ | Concentrations | ppm/ppm | Caustic embrittlement prevention |
| 8 | $L_{drum} = (2.5 \text{ to } 4) \cdot D_{drum}$ | L = length, D = diameter | m | Drum proportioning |
| 9 | $P_{hydrotest} = 1.5 \cdot P_{MAWP}$ | Hydrostatic test pressure | MPa | Pressure testing |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{drum} = T_{top} - T_{bottom}$ | Top-to-bottom temp diff | °C | Thermal stratification |

**Critical safety thresholds for boilers (per IBR & ASME):**

- **Sulfate-Alkalinity Ratio threshold:** $\geq$ **2.5** (prevents caustic embrittlement)
- **Hydrostatic test pressure:** $\geq$ **1.5 × MAWP**
- **Maximum allowable drum steam carryover:** **< 0.1%** moisture
- **Drum L/D ratio:** Between **2.5 and 4**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Required Drum Shell Thickness (ASME Section I)

**Problem Statement:** A cylindrical steam drum has an inside diameter $D = 1500$ mm, is to operate at a MAWP $P = 12$ MPa, and uses SA-302 Grade B steel with allowable stress $S = 138$ MPa at design temperature 345 °C. The welded joint efficiency is $E = 0.85$, and a corrosion allowance $c = 1.5$ mm is specified. Find the **minimum required shell thickness**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation

For a thin cylindrical pressure vessel under internal pressure, the governing design equation per ASME Section I is:

$$t_{min} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + c$$

Where $R = D/2$ is the inner radius.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Known Values

$$t_{min} = \frac{(12) \cdot (750)}{(138)(0.85) - (0.6)(12)} + 1.5$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Denominator

$$S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P = (138)(0.85) - (0.6)(12)$$

$$S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P = 117.3 - 7.2 = 110.1 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Numerator

$$P \cdot R = 12 \cdot 750 = 9000 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{mm}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Pressure Term

$$\frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} = \frac{9000}{110.1} = 81.74 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 6 — Add the Corrosion Allowance

$$t_{min} = 81.74 + 1.5 = 83.24 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 7 — Round Up to the Next Standard Plate Thickness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention:** Always round **UP** to the next standard plate gauge. Available thicknesses near this range: **85 mm** or **90 mm**.

$$\boxed{t_{selected} = 90 \text{ mm (next standard gauge)}}$$

### Step 8 — Verification Using Hoop Stress Check

Compute actual hoop stress at the design pressure:

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} = \frac{(12)(1500)}{2 \cdot 90} = \frac{18000}{180} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Safety Factor Check:**

$$SF = \frac{S}{\sigma_{hoop}} = \frac{138}{100} = 1.38 \quad (\text{Acceptable, } \geq 1.0)$$

$$\boxed{\text{Design is safe with selected thickness } t = 90 \text{ mm.}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Continuous Blowdown Rate Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A boiler generates $\dot{m}_{steam} = 50{,}000$ kg/hr of steam. The feedwater TDS is **150 ppm**, and the maximum allowable drum TDS is **3500 ppm**. Find the continuous blowdown rate.

### Step 1 — Apply the Mass-Balance Equation

$$\dot{m}_{blowdown} = \frac{C_f \cdot \dot{m}_{steam}}{TDS_{drum} - TDS_{feed}}$$

Where $C_f$ is the cycles of concentration (ratio of drum TDS to feed TDS):

$$C_f = \frac{TDS_{drum}}{TDS_{feed}} = \frac{3500}{150} = 23.33$$

> [!NOTE]
> This $C_f$ value is very high — in practice, feedwater treatment would be upgraded. We proceed with the calculation for academic purposes.

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$\dot{m}_{blowdown} = \frac{23.33 \cdot 50{,}000}{3500 - 150}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Denominator

$$TDS_{drum} - TDS_{feed} = 3500 - 150 = 3350 \text{ ppm}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Numerator

$$C_f \cdot \dot{m}_{steam} = 23.33 \cdot 50{,}000 = 1{,}166{,}500$$

### Step 5 — Final Computation

$$\dot{m}_{blowdown} = \frac{1{,}166{,}500}{3350} \approx 348.2 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{blowdown} \approx 350 \text{ kg/hr } (\approx 0.7\% \text{ of steam flow})}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes of Drums & Headers

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Questions on failure modes carry 7 marks in Part B. Memorize the *mechanism*, *symptom*, and *mitigation* for each.

| Failure Mode | Mechanism | Symptom in Drum/Header | Mitigation |
|--------------|-----------|------------------------|------------|
| **Caustic Embrittlement** | NaOH concentration in rivet/weld crevices | Intergranular cracks at seams | Maintain Sulfate-Alkalinity Ratio $\geq$ 2.5 |
| **Hydrogen Embrittlement** | Atomic H from acid cleaning diffuses into steel | Stepwise cracking, loss of ductility | Post-weld heat treatment; limit acid cleaning |
| **Thermal Fatigue** | Repeated thermal cycling (start-up/shut-down) | Network of fine cracks at tube-rolls | Improved warm-up procedures; low-cycle fatigue analysis |
| **Creep Rupture** | Long-term exposure to T $\geq$ 0.4·T$_{melting}$ | Bulging, thinning at hot spots | Use of creep-resistant alloys; periodic NDT |
| **Stress-Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** | Tensile stress + corrosive medium | Branching cracks in water zone | Water chemistry control; shot-peening |
| **Corrosion Fatigue** | Cyclic stress + corrosive environment | Crack initiation at tube-sheet joints | NDT (MPI, UT) every inspection cycle |
| **Weld Defects (Lack of Fusion)** | Improper welding parameters | Subsurface defects, leak paths | 100% radiographic testing (RT) of seams |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Determining Hydrostatic Test Pressure

**Problem:** A boiler drum has MAWP = 10 MPa. What is the hydrostatic test pressure per IBR?

### Step 1 — Apply the IBR Rule

$$P_{hydrotest} = 1.5 \cdot P_{MAWP}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$P_{hydrotest} = 1.5 \cdot 10 = 15 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 3 — Cross-Check with ASME Section I (Alternative Rule)

ASME permits a slightly different factor based on material yield:

$$P_{hydrotest} = 1.5 \cdot P_{MAWP} \quad \text{or} \quad P_{hydrotest} = 1.3 \cdot \frac{S_y}{S_{design}} \cdot P_{MAWP}$$

Use the **lower** of the two computed values. For typical carbon-steel drums where $S_y / S_{design} \approx 1.6$:

$$1.3 \cdot 1.6 = 2.08 \quad (\text{gives } P_{hydrotest} = 20.8 \text{ MPa — too high})$$

Hence, the **governing value is 1.5 × MAWP = 15 MPa**.

$$\boxed{P_{hydrotest} = 15 \text{ MPa, hold time} = 30 \text{ minutes minimum}}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example: Header Design (Part-B Style)

A box-type water header is to be designed for an economizer outlet. The header must handle a flow of 60,000 kg/hr of water at 250 °C and 50 bar. Determine (a) the minimum header wall thickness using a working stress $S = 95$ MPa, joint efficiency $E = 0.85$, corrosion allowance $c = 2$ mm, and (b) the recommended header material.

### Part (a) — Wall Thickness

Assume header internal diameter $D_{header} = 300$ mm:

$$t_{min} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + c$$

$$t_{min} = \frac{(5)(150)}{(95)(0.85) - (0.6)(5)} + 2$$

$$t_{min} = \frac{750}{80.75 - 3} + 2 = \frac{750}{77.75} + 2$$

$$t_{min} = 9.65 + 2 = 11.65 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \boxed{12 \text{ mm selected}}$$

### Part (b) — Material Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For water headers at 250 °C / 50 bar, KTU board standard answer:**
> **SA-106 Grade B** (seamless carbon steel) or **SA-335 P11** (1.25Cr-0.5Mo alloy) for higher temperatures. Justify by stating creep resistance and weldability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Water-Tube Boiler

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Diagram Note:** A purely physical cross-section is impractical in Mermaid. The block diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of drum/header interactions, which is a KTU 2024 board-acceptable schematic style.

```mermaid

    F --> G[Riser Tubes Upward Path]

    J --> K[Steam Drum Upper Section]

    O --> P[Superheater Outlet Header]
    P --> Q[Main Steam Stop Valve]
    Q --> R[Turbine or Process]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Scheme (ESE):** 2-mark conceptual + 1-mark diagram/keyword. Answers should be **concise (80–100 words)** with at least one labeled term.

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Differentiate between a steam drum and a mud drum in a water-tube boiler.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Steam Drum | Mud Drum |
|---------|------------|----------|
| **Location** | Top of boiler | Bottom of boiler |
| **Function** | Phase separation (steam from water) | Collection of sludge & sediment |
| **Phase Held** | Steam + saturated water | Water + suspended solids |
| **Connections** | Risers (inlet) and downcomers (outlet) | Lower headers; blowdown line |
| **Internal Fittings** | Cyclones, dry pipes, scrubbers | Baffles, blowdown pipe |
| **Failure Risk** | Caustic embrittlement at seams | Sludge accumulation; under-deposit corrosion |

The **steam drum** ensures **dry steam output (< 0.1% moisture)**, while the **mud drum** prevents **tube overheating** by removing settled solids. *(3 marks: 1 for correct location, 1 for function, 1 for risk)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: What is caustic embrittlement? State one preventive measure.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caustic embrittlement** is a form of **intergranular stress-corrosion cracking** that occurs in boiler drums and headers when **free caustic (NaOH)** concentrates in riveted/welded seams or tube-roll crevices, attacking the grain boundaries of mild steel.

**Preventive Measure (any one of the following):**
- Maintain **Sulfate-Alkalinity Ratio ≥ 2.5** by adding Na₂SO₄ to the boiler water.
- Replace **riveted construction** with **fused-welded drums** to eliminate crevices.
- Conduct **regular phosphate-pH treatment** to control alkalinity.

*(3 marks: 2 for definition, 1 for measure)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You must attempt **one full choice** out of the two provided.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the constructional features and functions of a steam drum in a water-tube boiler. Discuss in detail the various internal fittings used for steam purification.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### Part (a) — Constructional Features and Functions [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

A **steam drum** is a large cylindrical pressure vessel (typical L/D ratio = 2.5 to 4) fabricated from **low-alloy steel** (SA-302 Grade B for sub-critical boilers). It operates at the saturation temperature corresponding to the boiler pressure (e.g., 345 °C at 100 bar).

**Constructional Features:**

1. **Shell** — Rolled plate, longitudinally and circumferentially welded. **100% radiographic testing** is mandatory per IBR 1950.
2. **Dished End Caps** — Forged or formed, welded to the shell; provide pressure boundary closure.
3. **Manholes and Handholes** — For internal inspection and maintenance access.
4. **Nozzles** — For riser connections, downcomers, safety valve, pressure gauge, continuous blowdown, chemical feed, and dry-pipe outlet.
5. **External Insulation** — Typically calcium silicate or mineral wool, 75–100 mm thick, clad with aluminum sheet.
6. **External Supports** — Saddle-type or ring girder supports; the drum is free to expand axially.

**Functions:**

1. **Phase separation** of steam from water in the riser return mixture.
2. **Water reserve** to maintain a Normal Water Level (NWL) — typically 100–150 mm below the centerline.
3. **Chemical treatment** distribution point.
4. **Circulation hub** — downcomer water enters, riser mixture exits.
5. **Steam accumulation** before delivery to superheater.

#### Part (b) — Internal Fittings for Steam Purification [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

Steam purification is achieved through a **three-stage separation system**:

**Stage 1 — Primary Cyclone Separators:**
- Tangential entry of steam-water mixture creates a **centrifugal vortex** (velocity ≥ 25 m/s).
- Water droplets (density ~880 kg/m³) are flung outward against the cyclone wall and drain back to the water space.
- Removal efficiency: **> 95%** of entrained moisture.
- Cyclone diameter typically **300–450 mm**; **6–8 cyclones** per drum.

**Stage 2 — Secondary Chevron / Splash Plate Dryers:**
- Multiple layers of corrugated plates cause **inertial impaction** of remaining droplets.
- Drainage channels return moisture to the water space.
- Reduces carryover to **< 0.5%** moisture.

**Stage 3 — Wire-Mesh Demister (Scrubber):**
- A bed of fine wire mesh (KnitMesh or Demister pad) provides **final polishing** through Brownian diffusion and inertial capture.
- Reduces final carryover to **< 0.1%** — the IBR-mandated limit.

**Auxiliary Fittings:**

- **Dry Pipe (Perforated Manifold):** Collects the dried steam from the top of the drum and routes it to the superheater inlet header. Slot velocity is limited to ~15 m/s to prevent re-entrainment.
- **Chemical Feed Pipe:** Distributes trisodium phosphate (Na₃PO₄) for hardness precipitation and hydrazine (N₂H₄) for oxygen scavenging.
- **Continuous Blowdown Pipe:** Located ~150 mm below NWL; removes concentrated sludge to maintain TDS within limits.
- **Safety Valve Connections:** Two spring-loaded safety valves per drum, set at MAWP and 3% above (per IBR Reg. 393).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state the **carryover limit (< 0.1% moisture)** and **the IBR Regulation number** wherever applicable.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks)

**Q: (a) Explain in detail the different types of boiler headers with their functions and applications. (b) A cylindrical header has an internal diameter of 250 mm and is to operate at a pressure of 8 MPa. Using an allowable stress of 110 MPa, joint efficiency of 0.85, and corrosion allowance of 1.5 mm, determine the minimum required wall thickness.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — Types of Boiler Headers [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A boiler header is a thick-walled pressure vessel (cylindrical or box-type) that **collects** or **distributes** fluid from multiple boiler tubes.

**Types by Function:**

1. **Steam Header (Outlet Header):**
   - **Function:** Collects steam-water mixture from riser tubes and routes it to the steam drum, OR collects dry steam from the drum for the superheater.
   - **Application:** Top of generating banks; superheater inlet/outlet.
   - **Design Note:** Lower stress as it carries low-density steam.

2. **Water Header (Inlet Header):**
   - **Function:** Distributes feed water from downcomers to multiple parallel riser tubes.
   - **Application:** Bottom of generating banks; economizer outlets.
   - **Design Note:** Subject to higher density and erosion concerns.

3. **Downcomer Header:**
   - **Function:** Collects saturated water from multiple downcomers and feeds the risers.
   - **Application:** Modern once-through and sub-critical boilers.

4. **Superheater Header:**
   - **Inlet header** receives saturated steam; **outlet header** delivers superheated steam to the stop valve.
   - **Material:** Often **austenitic stainless steel (SS 304H, SS 347)** for high-temperature service (>540 °C).

**Types by Shape:**

| Shape | Stress Distribution | Application |
|-------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Cylindrical** | Uniform (hoop + axial) | Modern high-pressure boilers |
| **Box-Type** | Concentrations at corners | Older fire-tube and small boilers |
| **Streamlined/Oval** | Reduced thermal stress | Cyclic-duty boilers |

**Headers typically use** *stub-tube* or *welded nipple* connections, with **full-penetration welds** and **post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** mandatory for thick sections.

#### Part (b) — Minimum Wall Thickness Calculation [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Internal diameter, $D = 250$ mm $\Rightarrow R = 125$ mm
- Design pressure, $P = 8$ MPa
- Allowable stress, $S = 110$ MPa
- Joint efficiency, $E = 0.85$
- Corrosion allowance, $c = 1.5$ mm

**Step 1 — Governing Equation (ASME Section I):**

$$t_{min} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + c$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Denominator:**

$$S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P = (110)(0.85) - (0.6)(8) = 93.5 - 4.8 = 88.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Numerator:**

$$P \cdot R = 8 \cdot 125 = 1000 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Pressure Term:**

$$\frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} = \frac{1000}{88.7} = 11.27 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Add the Corrosion Allowance:**

$$t_{min} = 11.27 + 1.5 = 12.77 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Select the Next Standard Gauge:**

Available standard plates: **12 mm, 14 mm, 16 mm** → Select **14 mm** (next higher).

$$\boxed{t_{selected} = 14 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 7 — Verification (Hoop Stress Check):**

$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} = \frac{(8)(250)}{2 \cdot 14} = \frac{2000}{28} = 71.43 \text{ MPa}$$

**Safety Factor:**

$$SF = \frac{S \cdot E}{\sigma_{hoop}} = \frac{110 \cdot 0.85}{71.43} = 1.31 \quad (\text{Acceptable})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the corrosion allowance** (1.5 mm) — deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Using MAWP instead of test pressure** in hydrotest questions — deducts 2 marks.
> 3. **Not rounding UP** to the next standard plate thickness — deducts 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Omitting the joint efficiency term** for welded headers — deducts 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing the formula** for spherical vs. cylindrical vessels — deducts 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam (Boiler Drums & Headers)**

- ☐ **Steam Drum** = top-most pressure vessel; **Mud Drum** = bottom-most; **Water Drum** = side-mounted (banked tube boilers).
- ☐ Steam drum **L/D ratio** must be between **2.5 and 4** (per IBR).
- ☐ Standard materials: **SA-302 Gr B** (sub-critical), **SA-299** (lower pressure), **SA-106 Gr B** (headers).
- ☐ Cyclone separators remove **>95% moisture**; chevron + wire-mesh demister reduce carryover to **< 0.1%**.
- ☐ **Sulfate-Alkalinity Ratio ≥ 2.5** prevents **caustic embrittlement** in seams.
- ☐ **Hydrostatic test pressure = 1.5 × MAWP**, hold **30 min minimum**.
- ☐ Drum design equation (ASME Section I): $t_{min} = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + c$
- ☐ Always **round UP** to the next standard plate thickness (e.g., 12.77 mm → 14 mm).
- ☐ **Hoop stress = 2 × longitudinal stress** for thin cylindrical pressure vessels.
- ☐ **Welding defects** are the most common cause of drum failure → 100% **radiographic testing (RT)** is mandatory.
- ☐ **IBR 1950** governs Indian boiler design; **ASME Section I** is the international reference.
- ☐ **Three failure modes to remember verbatim:** Caustic Embrittlement, Hydrogen Embrittlement, Thermal Fatigue.
- ☐ **Two safety valves per drum** are mandatory (one set at MAWP, one at MAWP + 3%).
- ☐ **Box-type headers** are obsolete for high-pressure service due to **corner stress concentrations**.
- ☐ **PWHT (Post-Weld Heat Treatment)** is mandatory for drum/header weld seams to relieve residual stress.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Pressure Vessel

A **Pressure Vessel** is a closed, leak-proof container (cylindrical, spherical, or of any other geometric profile) designed to hold gases, liquids, or vapors at pressures significantly different — either higher or lower — from the ambient atmospheric pressure. The structural design, material selection, and safety assessment of such vessels are governed by national and international codes such as the **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Section VIII**, the **Indian Standard IS 2825**, and the **PD 5500**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OESFT725 - Safety in Engineering Industry)**, the study of pressure vessels forms the backbone of industrial safety analysis because the catastrophic failure of such vessels (commonly called a **BLEVE** — *Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion*) results in massive loss of life, property, and environmental damage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** A pressure vessel is treated as a *static load-bearing safety-critical structure*. The student's responsibility is to compute stresses, identify failure modes, and apply allowable stress design principles with a quantified safety margin.

### 1.2 Classification of Pressure Vessels

Pressure vessels are broadly classified based on the ratio of wall thickness to internal diameter. This single geometric parameter dictates which stress-analysis theory applies.

| Classification | Wall Thickness Ratio | Governing Theory | Typical Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Thin-Walled Vessels** | $\frac{t}{d} \leq \frac{1}{20}$ (or $\frac{t}{r} \leq \frac{1}{10}$) | Membrane (Shell) Theory | LPG domestic cylinders, compressed air receivers, fire extinguishers |
| **Thick-Walled Vessels** | $\frac{t}{d} > \frac{1}{20}$ | Lame's Equation (Elasticity Theory) | Hydraulic cylinders, gun barrels, high-pressure reactor shells, nuclear casings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine blowing up a spherical balloon. The thin rubber skin stretches uniformly in all directions. The stress analysis of a thin pressure vessel uses this same "uniform membrane" assumption. Now imagine a heavy steel scuba tank — the wall is so thick that the inner fibers feel stress much higher than the outer fibers. This radial stress variation requires the much more complex Lame's theory.

### 1.3 Fundamental Stresses in a Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

When internal pressure $p$ acts on a cylindrical shell with diameter $d$ (or radius $r$) and wall thickness $t$, three principal stresses develop at any point in the wall:

- **Hoop Stress ($\sigma_h$ or $\sigma_\theta$):** Acts along the *circumferential* direction. Also called *circumferential stress* or *tangential stress*. This is the *largest* of the three.
- **Longitudinal Stress ($\sigma_l$ or $\sigma_x$):** Acts along the *axial* direction of the cylinder.
- **Radial Stress ($\sigma_r$):** Acts along the *radial* direction. For thin vessels, this is neglected because $t \ll d$. For thick vessels, it varies from $-p$ at the inner wall to $0$ at the outer wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 10 \text{ bar}$
> - $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 0.1 \text{ MPa}$
> - Design pressure $p$ is taken as **1.1 times the maximum working pressure (MWP)** per IS 2825 to provide a safety margin.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thin-Walled Cylindrical Pressure Vessel — Membrane Theory

The **Membrane Theory** assumes that the vessel wall is so thin that bending stresses are negligible. The wall carries load primarily through *in-plane* (membrane) stresses distributed uniformly across the thickness. This assumption is valid when $\frac{t}{d} \leq \frac{1}{20}$.

**Derivation Logic (Why it works):**

- **Step 1 — Force Equilibrium (Hoop Direction):** Consider a unit length of the cylinder cut longitudinally. The internal pressure pushes outward against the two longitudinal cut edges. The wall resists through hoop stress distributed over the cut cross-section.
- **Step 2 — Force Equilibrium (Axial Direction):** Consider the pressure acting on the end cap. The total axial force is resisted by the longitudinal stress acting on the annular cross-section of the cylinder wall.
- **Step 3 — Neglect $\sigma_r$:** Because $t$ is very small, the change in radial stress across the wall ($\Delta \sigma_r = p$) is insignificant compared to $\sigma_h$ and $\sigma_l$.

### 2.2 Thick-Walled Cylindrical Pressure Vessels — Lame's Equation

When the wall is thick, stresses are **not uniform** through the thickness. They vary as a function of the radial coordinate $r$. **Lame's Equations** (derived from the theory of elasticity for an axisymmetric plane-strain problem) give the exact stress distribution:

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants determined by applying the **boundary conditions** at the inner radius ($r = r_i$) and outer radius ($r = r_o$):

- At $r = r_i$ (inner surface): $\sigma_r = -p_i$ (compressive due to internal pressure)
- At $r = r_o$ (outer surface): $\sigma_r = -p_o$ (compressive due to external pressure, often $0$)

Solving these simultaneously gives the **Lame's Constants**:

$$A = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$$

$$B = \frac{r_i^2 r_o^2 (p_o - p_i)}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Meaning of Lame's Equation:** The stress at any point is the *sum* of a uniform stress ($A$) and a *bending-like* stress that decays with $\frac{1}{r^2}$. As $r \to \infty$, the $\frac{B}{r^2}$ term vanishes. As $r \to r_i$ (inner wall), the $\frac{B}{r^2}$ term is largest, which is why the inner surface is the most stressed region in a thick vessel.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates **every formula a KTU student must memorize** for the Pressure Vessels topic. All units are in **N/mm² (MPa)** with dimensions in **mm**.

| # | Quantity / Scenario | Formula | Boundary Condition / Validity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Hoop stress (thin cylinder) | $\sigma_h = \frac{pd}{2t} = \frac{pr}{t}$ | Thin wall, no end effects |
| 2 | Longitudinal stress (thin cylinder) | $\sigma_l = \frac{pd}{4t} = \frac{pr}{2t}$ | Thin wall, closed ends |
| 3 | Maximum shear stress (thin) | $\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \frac{pr}{2t} = \frac{pd}{8t}$ | Principal plane (45° helical) |
| 4 | Hoop strain (thin) | $\epsilon_h = \frac{pd}{4tE}(2 - \mu)$ | $E$ = Young's Modulus, $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| 5 | Longitudinal strain (thin) | $\epsilon_l = \frac{pd}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu)$ | Biaxial state |
| 6 | Change in diameter (thin) | $\Delta d = \frac{pd^2}{4tE}(2 - \mu)$ | Volumetric expansion effect |
| 7 | Change in length (thin) | $\Delta l = \frac{pl}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu)$ d | Axial extension |
| 8 | Spherical vessel stress (thin) | $\sigma_{sph} = \frac{pd}{4t}$ | For both hoop and meridional |
| 9 | Radial stress in thick cylinder | $\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}$ | Lame's equation, axisymmetric |
| 10 | Hoop stress in thick cylinder | $\sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$ | Lame's equation, axisymmetric |
| 11 | Max hoop stress (thick, $p_o = 0$) | $\sigma_{\theta,\max} = p_i \cdot \frac{r_o^2 + r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ | At $r = r_i$ (inner wall) |
| 12 | Min hoop stress (thick, $p_o = 0$) | $\sigma_{\theta,\min} = \frac{2 p_i r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ | At $r = r_o$ (outer wall) |
| 13 | Maximum shear stress (thick) | $\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_\theta - \sigma_r}{2} = \frac{B}{r^2}$ | At $r = r_i$ gives maximum |
| 14 | Design pressure (IS 2825) | $p_{\text{design}} = 1.1 \times \text{MWP}$ | Mandatory safety multiplier |
| 15 | Allowable stress (ASME) | $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$ | $n$ = factor of safety (typically 3 to 4) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in the design certification of every steam boiler in a thermal power plant, every reactor vessel in a refinery, every hydraulic accumulator in a CNC machine, and every LPG cylinder in your kitchen. The *factor of safety* $n$ ensures that even if the material has micro-defects, the vessel will not rupture catastrophically.

### 2.4 Stress Concentration in Dished Ends and Nozzles

In real vessels, there are *discontinuities* — openings for nozzles, manholes, and transition regions from cylindrical shell to dished end. At these locations, the simple membrane theory fails, and **stress concentration factors (SCF)** amplify local stresses. The KTU syllabus expects awareness that:

- A properly designed **torispherical or ellipsoidal head** reduces the SCF to manageable levels.
- **Reinforcement pads** (thickening around nozzles) are mandatory wherever a pipe penetrates the shell.
- The peak stress at a discontinuity must be compared to the **fatigue endurance limit** of the material for cyclic service.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

**Given:** A thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter $d$, wall thickness $t$, and internal gauge pressure $p$. Consider a longitudinal section of unit axial length $L = 1$.

**Step 1 — Identify the free body.**
Cut the cylinder along a longitudinal plane (i.e., parallel to the axis). The cut exposes two rectangular cross-sections, each of area $A_c = t \times 1 = t$.

**Step 2 — Compute the force due to internal pressure.**
The internal pressure $p$ acts on the projected area of the cylinder's cross-section, which is a rectangle of width $d$ and length $L = 1$. Therefore, the total separating force is:

$$F_p = p \times (d \times 1) = p \cdot d$$

**Step 3 — Equate the separating force to the resisting force.**
The hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acts uniformly on the two cut surfaces, each of area $t$. The total resisting force is:

$$F_r = \sigma_h \times (2 \times t) = 2 \sigma_h t$$

**Step 4 — Apply static equilibrium $\sum F = 0$.**

$$p \cdot d = 2 \sigma_h t$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the hoop stress.**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pd}{2t}$$

Since $d = 2r$, this can equivalently be written as $\sigma_h = \frac{pr}{t}$. **This is the single most important equation in pressure vessel design.**

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Longitudinal Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

**Given:** The same thin cylindrical shell, with closed ends.

**Step 1 — Identify the free body.**
Imagine cutting the cylinder transversely (perpendicular to the axis) and removing one end cap. The remaining cap pushes outward on the shell due to internal pressure.

**Step 2 — Compute the force on the end cap.**
The end cap is a circle of area $A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$. The internal pressure exerts an axial force:

$$F_{axial} = p \times A_{cap} = p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the resisting cross-sectional area of the shell wall.**
The longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$ is distributed over the annular ring of the wall. Its area is:

$$A_{wall} = \pi d t$$

(approximately, for thin walls, using mean diameter).

**Step 4 — Apply equilibrium.**

$$\sigma_l \times \pi d t = p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\sigma_l$.**

$$\sigma_l = \frac{pd}{4t}$$

**Conclusion:** The hoop stress is exactly *twice* the longitudinal stress. This is why pressure vessels always fail along a longitudinal seam (parallel to the axis) rather than a circumferential one — the longitudinal weld is subjected to the higher stress.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Lame's Equation for a Thick Cylinder

**Given:** A thick-walled cylinder with internal radius $r_i$, external radius $r_o$, internal pressure $p_i$, and external pressure $p_o$ (often $p_o = 0$ for atmospheric exposure).

**Step 1 — Start with the general axisymmetric solution of the elasticity equations.**
For a 2D axisymmetric problem with no body forces and no torsion, the equilibrium equation in polar coordinates reduces to:

$$\frac{d\sigma_r}{dr} + \frac{\sigma_r - \sigma_\theta}{r} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Assume a solution of the form $\sigma_r = f(r)$.**
The compatibility condition (to ensure single-valued displacements) and the strain-displacement relations together yield the general solution:

$$\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}, \quad \sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the inner boundary condition.**
At $r = r_i$, the radial stress equals the negative of internal pressure (compressive):

$$\sigma_r(r_i) = -p_i \implies A - \frac{B}{r_i^2} = -p_i$$

**Step 4 — Apply the outer boundary condition.**
At $r = r_o$, the radial stress equals the negative of external pressure:

$$\sigma_r(r_o) = -p_o \implies A - \frac{B}{r_o^2} = -p_o$$

**Step 5 — Solve the two linear equations for $A$ and $B$.**
Subtracting the two equations:

$$-\frac{B}{r_i^2} + \frac{B}{r_o^2} = -p_i + p_o$$

$$B \left( \frac{1}{r_o^2} - \frac{1}{r_i^2} \right) = p_o - p_i$$

$$B = \frac{r_i^2 r_o^2 (p_o - p_i)}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$$

Adding the two equations:

$$2A - B \left( \frac{1}{r_i^2} + \frac{1}{r_o^2} \right) = -(p_i + p_o)$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$A = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$$

**Step 6 — Special case $p_o = 0$ (most common in KTU problems).**

$$A = \frac{p_i r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}, \quad B = \frac{p_i r_i^2 r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum hoop stress at the inner wall ($r = r_i$).**

$$\sigma_{\theta,\max} = A + \frac{B}{r_i^2} = p_i \left[ \frac{r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} + \frac{r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \right] = p_i \cdot \frac{r_o^2 + r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$$

**Step 8 — Minimum hoop stress at the outer wall ($r = r_o$).**

$$\sigma_{\theta,\min} = A + \frac{B}{r_o^2} = p_i \left[ \frac{r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} + \frac{r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} \right] = \frac{2 p_i r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Safety Factor Computation Strategy

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *safety*. Below is the structured engineering decision matrix used in industry to certify a pressure vessel design.

| Step | Engineering Action | Governing Equation | Pass/Fail Criterion |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Compute design pressure | $p_d = 1.1 \times \text{MWP}$ | Higher than MWP, mandatory |
| 2 | Compute $\sigma_h$ and $\sigma_l$ | $\sigma_h = \frac{pd}{2t}$, $\sigma_l = \frac{pd}{4t}$ | Compare with allowable |
| 3 | Compute allowable stress | $\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{n}$ | $n$ from code, usually 3–4 |
| 4 | Check yield criterion | $\sigma_h \leq \sigma_{allow}$ | If violated, increase $t$ |
| 5 | For thick vessels, use Lame | $\sigma_{\theta,\max} = p_i \cdot \frac{r_o^2 + r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$ | Must be $< \sigma_{allow}$ |
| 6 | Check fatigue (if cyclic) | $\sigma_a \leq S_{fatigue}$ | S-N curve from material data |
| 7 | Hydrostatic test | $p_{test} = 1.5 \times p_d$ | Visual inspection for leaks |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pressure Vessel Design Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete engineering workflow** for designing a safe pressure vessel, from initial concept to certification. This is the analytical decision-flow expected in KTU 14-mark design questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Identify Service Requirements] --> startB[Define Working Pressure and Temperature]
    startB --> startC[Select Material and Allowable Stress]
    startC --> stepD{Is t over d less than 1 over 20?}
    stepD -- YES --> stepE[Apply Thin Walled Membrane Theory]
    stepD -- NO --> stepF[Apply Lame Thick Walled Theory]
    stepE --> stepG[Compute Hoop and Longitudinal Stress]
    stepF --> stepH[Compute Max Hoop Stress at Inner Wall]
    stepG --> stepI[Compare with Allowable Stress]
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ{Does Stress Satisfy Safety Factor?}
    stepJ -- NO --> stepK[Increase Wall Thickness or Reduce Pressure]
    stepK --> stepD
    stepJ -- YES --> stepL[Design Dished Ends and Nozzle Reinforcement]
    stepL --> stepM[Check Stress Concentration Factors]
    stepM --> stepN[Hydrostatic Test at 1.5 times Design Pressure]
    stepN --> stepO[Issue Certificate per ASME or IS 2825]
    stepO --> stepP[Periodic In Service Inspection Every 2 Years]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Stress State Comparison

This second diagram clarifies *where* each principal stress acts in a pressure vessel. It is essential for visual learners in the 14-mark derivation questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Internal Pressure p] --> nodeB[Force on Cylindrical Wall]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Hoop Stress sigma h along Circumference]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Longitudinal Stress sigma l along Axis]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Radial Stress sigma r through Wall Thickness]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Maximum is sigma h equals 2 times sigma l]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG[Negligible for Thin Walled]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Critical for Thick Walled via Lame]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Failure Plane is Longitudinal Seam]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Failure Initiates at Inner Wall]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture: Failure Mode Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    modeA[Pressure Vessel Under Load] --> modeB{Type of Loading}
    modeB -- Static --> modeC[Ductile Yielding]
    modeB -- Cyclic --> modeD[Fatigue Crack Growth]
    modeB -- Sudden Overpressure --> modeE[Brittle Fracture]
    modeB -- Corrosive Environment --> modeF[Stress Corrosion Cracking]
    modeC --> resA[Increase Wall Thickness]
    modeD --> resB[Reduce Cyclic Stress Range]
    modeE --> resC[Add Pressure Relief Valve]
    modeF --> resD[Use Corrosion Resistant Material]
```
