## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**
*Define a thin-walled pressure vessel. State the assumptions made in the membrane theory of analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A thin-walled pressure vessel is one in which the ratio of wall thickness $t$ to internal diameter $d$ is less than or equal to $\frac{1}{20}$, i.e., $\frac{t}{d} \leq \frac{1}{20}$.

The assumptions of membrane theory are:

1. The wall thickness is uniform and small compared to other dimensions, so bending stresses are negligible.
2. The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
3. The radial stress $\sigma_r$ is negligible compared to hoop and longitudinal stresses because $t \ll d$.
4. The stresses are uniformly distributed across the wall thickness.
5. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the axis after deformation (Navier's hypothesis).

**Allocation (3 Marks):** 1 Mark for definition, 2 Marks for any four of the above assumptions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]**
*Distinguish between thin-walled and thick-walled pressure vessels. Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Thin-Walled Vessel | Thick-Walled Vessel |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Wall ratio | $\frac{t}{d} \leq \frac{1}{20}$ | $\frac{t}{d} > \frac{1}{20}$ |
| Stress assumption | Uniform across thickness | Varies with radial distance |
| Theory used | Membrane theory | Lame's equation (elasticity) |
| Radial stress | Neglected | Considered |
| Examples | LPG cylinders, fire extinguishers | Hydraulic cylinders, gun barrels, high-pressure reactors |

**Allocation (3 Marks):** 2 Marks for the tabular distinction, 1 Mark for examples.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Design and Derivation Type*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice - Set A]**

**(a)** A thin cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **300 mm** and wall thickness of **15 mm**. If it is subjected to an internal pressure of **2 MPa**, determine:

(i) Hoop stress and longitudinal stress in the wall.
(ii) Maximum shear stress in the wall.
(iii) Change in diameter and change in length, given $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa and $\mu = 0.3$. Take the length of the vessel as 2 m.

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the Lame's equation for a thick-walled cylinder and derive the expressions for the radial and hoop stresses.

---

### Model Solution A

#### Part (a) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Internal diameter $d = 300$ mm
- Wall thickness $t = 15$ mm
- Internal pressure $p = 2$ MPa $= 2$ N/mm²
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa
- Poisson's ratio $\mu = 0.3$
- Length $L = 2$ m $= 2000$ mm

**Step 1 — Verify thin-wall assumption.**

$$\frac{t}{d} = \frac{15}{300} = \frac{1}{20}$$

This is the boundary case; thin-wall theory is still valid.

**Step 2 — Hoop stress.**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pd}{2t} = \frac{2 \times 300}{2 \times 15} = \frac{600}{30} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal stress.**

$$\sigma_l = \frac{pd}{4t} = \frac{2 \times 300}{4 \times 15} = \frac{600}{60} = 10 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum shear stress (45° plane).**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \frac{20 - 10}{2} = 5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Change in diameter.**

$$\Delta d = \frac{pd^2}{4tE}(2 - \mu) = \frac{2 \times 300^2}{4 \times 15 \times 2 \times 10^5} \times (2 - 0.3)$$

$$\Delta d = \frac{2 \times 90000}{12 \times 10^6} \times 1.7 = \frac{180000}{12 \times 10^6} \times 1.7$$

$$\Delta d = 0.015 \times 1.7 = 0.0255 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Change in length.**

$$\Delta L = \frac{pL}{4tE}(1 - 2\mu) d = \frac{2 \times 2000 \times 300}{4 \times 15 \times 2 \times 10^5} \times (1 - 0.6)$$

$$\Delta L = \frac{1200000}{12 \times 10^6} \times 0.4 = 0.1 \times 0.4 = 0.04 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Results (Part a):**
- $\sigma_h = 20$ MPa
- $\sigma_l = 10$ MPa
- $\tau_{\max} = 5$ MPa
- $\Delta d = 0.0255$ mm
- $\Delta L = 0.04$ mm

**Mark Allocation (Part a, 7 Marks):** 2 Marks for hoop and longitudinal, 1 Mark for shear, 2 Marks for $\Delta d$, 2 Marks for $\Delta L$.

#### Part (b) — Derivation (7 Marks)

Refer to **Section 3.3** of these notes for the exhaustive step-by-step derivation of Lame's equation. The student is expected to:

1. Draw a neat labeled sketch of a thick cylindrical section showing $r_i$, $r_o$, $p_i$, $p_o$, and the resulting $\sigma_r$ and $\sigma_\theta$ distributions (2 Marks).
2. State the general axisymmetric solution $\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}$ and $\sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$ (1 Mark).
3. Apply both boundary conditions and solve for $A$ and $B$ (3 Marks).
4. State the final expression for maximum hoop stress at the inner wall (1 Mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units inconsistency:** Many students mix $p$ in bar with $d$ in mm without converting. Always convert $p$ to N/mm² (MPa) before calculation. $1$ bar $= 0.1$ MPa.
> 2. **Formula sign error in $\Delta L$:** The factor is $(1 - 2\mu)$, not $(2 - \mu)$. Confusing these two gives an entirely wrong answer.
> 3. **Skipping the $\frac{t}{d}$ check:** If you do not verify the thin-wall assumption, the examiner may deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer of $\Delta d$ and $\Delta L$:** Always state the unit (mm).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice - Set B]**

**(a)** A thick-walled cylinder with **inner diameter 200 mm** and **outer diameter 400 mm** is subjected to an internal pressure of **60 MPa**. The external pressure is atmospheric (take $p_o = 0$). Determine:

(i) The radial and hoop stress distribution across the wall thickness.
(ii) The maximum and minimum hoop stress and their locations.
(iii) The maximum shear stress in the wall.

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the difference between a torispherical and an ellipsoidal head used for closing pressure vessel ends. Why are these shapes preferred over a flat plate?

---

### Model Solution B

#### Part (a) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Internal diameter $d_i = 200$ mm, so internal radius $r_i = 100$ mm
- External diameter $d_o = 400$ mm, so external radius $r_o = 200$ mm
- Internal pressure $p_i = 60$ MPa
- External pressure $p_o = 0$

**Step 1 — Verify thick-wall assumption.**

$$\frac{t}{d} = \frac{r_o - r_i}{2 r_i} = \frac{200 - 100}{2 \times 100} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.5$$

Since $0.5 > \frac{1}{20}$, this is a thick-walled vessel. Lame's equation applies.

**Step 2 — Compute Lame's constants $A$ and $B$.**

$$A = \frac{p_i r_i^2 - p_o r_o^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} = \frac{60 \times 100^2 - 0}{200^2 - 100^2} = \frac{600000}{30000} = 20 \text{ MPa}$$

$$B = \frac{r_i^2 r_o^2 (p_o - p_i)}{r_o^2 - r_i^2} = \frac{100^2 \times 200^2 \times (-60)}{30000} = \frac{4 \times 10^8 \times (-60)}{30000} = -8 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa} \cdot \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Stress distribution equations.**

$$\sigma_r = 20 - \frac{8 \times 10^5}{r^2} \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_\theta = 20 + \frac{8 \times 10^5}{r^2} \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum hoop stress at $r = r_i = 100$ mm.**

$$\sigma_{\theta,\max} = 20 + \frac{8 \times 10^5}{100^2} = 20 + 80 = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Minimum hoop stress at $r = r_o = 200$ mm.**

$$\sigma_{\theta,\min} = 20 + \frac{8 \times 10^5}{200^2} = 20 + 20 = 40 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum shear stress at $r = r_i$.**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_{\theta,\max} - \sigma_{r,\max}}{2} = \frac{100 - (-60)}{2} = \frac{160}{2} = 80 \text{ MPa}$$

**Final Results (Part a):**
- Maximum hoop stress: $100$ MPa at inner wall
- Minimum hoop stress: $40$ MPa at outer wall
- Maximum shear stress: $80$ MPa at inner wall

**Mark Allocation (Part a, 7 Marks):** 2 Marks for Lame's constants, 2 Marks for stress distribution, 2 Marks for max/min hoop, 1 Mark for max shear.

#### Part (b) — Descriptive (7 Marks)

**Torispherical Head:** A dished end formed by a larger spherical crown radius ($R$) blended with a smaller toroidal knuckle radius ($r$) at the transition. It is the most economical head design.

**Ellipsoidal Head:** A dished end where the profile is half of an ellipsoid. The depth is one-quarter of the diameter (standard 2:1 ellipsoidal head).

**Why these shapes are preferred over a flat plate:**

1. **Stress Reduction:** A flat plate closure develops very high bending stresses at the junction with the cylindrical shell. The dished shapes (torispherical and ellipsoidal) primarily carry membrane (in-plane) stresses, which are an order of magnitude lower.
2. **Material Economy:** Because stresses are lower, less material (thinner plate) is required, reducing both cost and weight.
3. **Manufacturing Feasibility:** Spinning and pressing of dished heads is a well-established industrial process.
4. **Safety:** The smooth curvature avoids sharp corners which are potential crack initiation sites, improving fatigue life.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Question B:**
> 1. **Sign of radial stress:** A frequent error is to take $\sigma_r = +p_i$ at the inner wall. The correct convention is $\sigma_r = -p_i$ because internal pressure produces compression on the wall surface.
> 2. **Confusing max hoop with internal pressure value:** Students often write $\sigma_{\theta,\max} = p_i$ by intuition, which is wrong by a factor of nearly 2. Always use the Lame's formula.
> 3. **Forgetting the radial stress in max shear calculation:** In thin cylinders, radial stress is neglected. In thick cylinders, you **must** include it, otherwise $\tau_{\max}$ will be wrong by a factor of 2.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Pressure Vessels):**

- A pressure vessel is a **closed container** designed to operate at pressures different from ambient.
- The classification criterion is the ratio $\frac{t}{d}$. **Thin-walled** if $\leq \frac{1}{20}$; **thick-walled** if $> \frac{1}{20}$.
- In a thin cylindrical vessel, the **hoop stress** $\sigma_h = \frac{pd}{2t}$ is **twice** the longitudinal stress $\sigma_l = \frac{pd}{4t}$.
- The **maximum shear stress** in a thin cylinder occurs on a plane inclined at 45° to the axis and equals $\frac{pd}{8t}$.
- Failure of a thin cylindrical vessel always occurs along the **longitudinal seam** because $\sigma_h > \sigma_l$.
- For thin vessels, **radial stress is neglected**. For thick vessels, radial stress is critical and varies from $-p_i$ at the inner wall to $0$ at the outer wall.
- **Lame's equations** give the exact stress distribution in a thick cylinder: $\sigma_r = A - \frac{B}{r^2}$ and $\sigma_\theta = A + \frac{B}{r^2}$.
- The **constants $A$ and $B$** are determined by the boundary conditions at $r_i$ and $r_o$.
- The **maximum hoop stress in a thick cylinder** occurs at the **inner wall**, equal to $p_i \cdot \frac{r_o^2 + r_i^2}{r_o^2 - r_i^2}$.
- The **maximum shear stress in a thick cylinder** occurs at the **inner wall** and equals $\frac{\sigma_{\theta,\max} - (-p_i)}{2}$.
- **Design pressure** is taken as $1.1 \times$ MWP per IS 2825.
- **Allowable stress** is taken as $\frac{\sigma_y}{n}$ with factor of safety $n$ between 3 and 4.
- **Hydrostatic test** is performed at $1.5 \times$ design pressure before commissioning.
- Dished ends (torispherical and ellipsoidal) are preferred over flat plates to minimize bending stresses and stress concentrations.
- All stress values must be expressed in **MPa (N/mm²)** with **consistent SI units** throughout the calculation.
- Always include a **neat sketch** in derivation questions showing dimensions, pressures, and stress directions — this typically earns 1 to 2 marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Leak Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Leak Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Air Leak Test

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AIR LEAK TEST (Definition per KTU 2024 OESFT725 Module 4):**
> An **Air Leak Test** is a non-destructive evaluation (NDE) procedure used to verify the integrity of sealed or pressurized systems (vessels, pipelines, tanks, and enclosures) by introducing compressed air (or an inert tracer gas) at a specified test pressure, isolating the system, and measuring either the **pressure decay over time** or the **escape of gas through a defect path** to determine whether the leakage rate conforms to the maximum permissible leak rate prescribed by the design code.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus for *Safety in Engineering Industry*, the **Air Leak Test** is positioned as a **pneumatic / volumetric integrity test** that bridges the gap between purely qualitative inspection methods (visual, dye-penetrant) and high-resolution quantitative methods (helium mass-spectrometer leak detection). It is regarded as the **first-line verification test** mandated by pressure vessel and process piping codes (e.g., **ASME Section V Article 10**, **ASME B31.3**, **IS 2825**, **IS 12411**) before a system is placed into operational service.

**Key Nomenclature (KTU Board terminology):**

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Test Pressure (P_test)** | The pressure at which the air leak test is conducted — typically **1.0 to 1.1 × design pressure** (for pneumatic) or a code-mandated value. |
| **Hold Time (t_h)** | Duration for which the system is observed under test pressure to detect decay. |
| **Leak Rate (Q_L)** | Volume of air (or mass) escaping per unit time through the defect — usually expressed in **std cm³/min** or **mbar·L/s**. |
| **Permissible Leakage (Q_perm)** | Maximum leakage rate code-allowed without rejection. |
| **Pressure Decay (ΔP)** | Drop in pressure (Pa, bar, or psi) observed over the hold period. |
| **Standard Atmospheric Pressure (P_atm)** | **101.325 kPa** (1.01325 bar) — reference for all standard-state gas calculations. |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (Conceptual Hook)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bicycle Tyre Analogy" for Air Leak Test:**
> Imagine inflating a bicycle tube and pinching the valve closed. If you press your hand near the tube and feel a hiss, you instantly know there's a leak. Now imagine the same idea applied to a **massive chemical storage tank** or a **submarine hull** — except we cannot rely on sound or feel. We need a **scientific, quantifiable, and safe** way to confirm "no air is escaping."

The Air Leak Test essentially does the following three things — analogous to diagnosing a patient:

1. **Inflate the system** (give the system a "blood pressure" reading).
2. **Wait and watch** (monitor whether the "blood pressure" stays steady).
3. **Diagnose the result** (steady → healthy / no leak ; falling → sick / leaky system).

> A falling pressure is **never** due to gas disappearing; it must be escaping through a defect path. This is the central physics principle behind the test.

---

## 1.3 Position in the KTU 2024 Module 4 Safety Framework

Within Module 4 (which broadly covers Non-Destructive Testing and Industrial Safety Verification), the Air Leak Test is grouped with the following related NDE techniques, and the examiner expects students to **differentiate** between them:

| Method | Medium | Sensitivity | Typical Use |
|--------|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Air Leak Test (Pneumatic Pressure Decay)** | Compressed air | Moderate (10⁻² mbar·L/s) | Vessels, pipelines, automotive components |
| **Hydrostatic Test** | Water | Low (visual leak) | Pressure vessels, boilers |
| **Helium Mass-Spec Leak Test** | Helium tracer | Ultra-high (10⁻⁹ mbar·L/s) | Aerospace, cryogenics, nuclear |
| **Soap Bubble Test** | Air + soap solution | Moderate (~10⁻⁴ mbar·L/s) | Quick field screening |
| **Ultrasonic Leak Detection** | Air (acoustic) | High (audible 40 kHz) | Live operating plants |

The **Air Leak Test** is the **most economical** of these methods and is therefore mandated as a **routine production-line** test, while helium and ultrasonic methods are reserved for **high-criticality or in-service** applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The Air Leak Test rests on a simple, elegant principle derived from the **Ideal Gas Law (PV = nRT)** and the **Continuity Equation**. The procedural logic, in the precise order followed by KTU board examiners, is:

### Step 1: Pre-Test Preparation (Safety-First)
- Clean and dry the system to remove residues (oil, water, debris) that could mask leak paths or react with pressurized air.
- **Purge the system** with the test gas (typically dry, oil-free compressed air) to displace any moisture or flammable vapors. This is a **critical safety step** in plants handling hydrocarbons.
- Connect calibrated pressure gauges, regulators, and a temperature-stable reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why purge?** Compressed air mixed with hydrocarbon vapors can form an **explosive atmosphere** inside a confined vessel. The purging step reduces the Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) risk to acceptable levels per **IS 1446 / NFPA 69**.

### Step 2: Pressurization
- Slowly raise the internal pressure to the **test pressure (P_test)**, typically:
  - **1.0 × MAWP** for pneumatic leak tests (MAWP = Maximum Allowable Working Pressure), or
  - **1.1 × design pressure** (per IS 2825 for unfired pressure vessels).
- The slow ramp-up (typically **< 0.5 bar/sec**) is to avoid **adiabatic compression heating** that would distort the pressure reading.

### Step 3: Stabilization (Soak) Period
- Hold the system at P_test for a **stabilization period (t_s)**, typically **10 to 30 minutes**.
- During this time, the gas temperature equilibrates with the surrounding ambient, and any small initial leaks from joints/gaskets stabilize.
- After this period, the **initial reference pressure (P₁)** and **initial temperature (T₁)** are recorded.

### Step 4: Isolation and Hold (The Actual Test)
- Isolate the system from the air supply (close the inlet valve).
- Wait for a **prescribed hold time (t_h)**, typically **10 minutes to 24 hours** depending on volume and code.
- Record the **final pressure (P₂)** and **final temperature (T₂)**.

### Step 5: Acceptance Evaluation
- Compute the **pressure drop (ΔP = P₁ − P₂)**.
- Convert this pressure drop to a **leak rate (Q_L)** using the formulas below.
- Compare Q_L to the **code-permissible leak rate (Q_perm)**.
- **Accept** if Q_L ≤ Q_perm ; **Reject and repair** if Q_L > Q_perm.

### Step 6: Depressurization and Documentation
- Slowly vent the system to atmospheric pressure.
- Document all readings, gauge calibration certificates, ambient conditions, and operator signatures — a regulatory requirement under the **Factories Act 1948** and **Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR)**.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\\vert` for any absolute value or modulus inside table rows to prevent markdown breakage.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application / When to Use |
|---|--------------|----------|-------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Ideal Gas Law** | $P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$ | P in Pa, V in m³, T in K | Foundation for all gas-leak derivations |
| 2 | **Pressure Decay → Volume Lost (Isothermal)** | $\Delta V = V \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{P_1}$ | m³ or L | Small pressure drops where T₁ ≈ T₂ |
| 3 | **Leak Rate (Volumetric)** | $Q_L = \dfrac{\Delta V}{t_h} = \dfrac{V \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{P_1 \cdot t_h}$ | m³/s or L/min | Most common form for KTU problems |
| 4 | **Leak Rate (Standard cm³/min)** | $Q_L = \dfrac{V \cdot (P_1 - P_2) \cdot 10^6}{P_{atm} \cdot t_h \cdot 60}$ | sccm | When code specifies sccm |
| 5 | **Boyle's Law (Isothermal, fixed T)** | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | — | Closed rigid vessel, no temperature change |
| 6 | **Combined Gas Law (with T correction)** | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ | — | When ΔT cannot be neglected |
| 7 | **Standard Leak Rate Conversion (Helium equivalent)** | $Q_{He} = Q_{air} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M_{air}}{M_{He}}}$ | — | Convert between tracer gases |
| 8 | **Sonic / Choked Flow Leak** | $\dot{m} = C_d A P_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma}{R T_1} \left(\dfrac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}}}$ | kg/s | When leak orifice chokes flow (ΔP large) |
| 9 | **Subsonic / Unchoked Leak (orifice)** | $\dot{m} = C_d A \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \gamma}{\gamma - 1} \dfrac{P_1^2}{R T_1} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{2}{\gamma}} - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma+1}{\gamma}} \right]}$ | kg/s | Small leaks, ΔP small |
| 10 | **Standard Atmospheric Pressure** | $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$ | Pa / bar | Reference for SCCM conversions |
| 11 | **Universal Gas Constant** | $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ | J/(mol·K) | All gas law calculations |
| 12 | **Specific Gas Constant for Air** | $R_{air} = 287.058 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ | J/(kg·K) | When using mass-based formulas |
| 13 | **Ratio of Specific Heats for Air** | $\gamma = c_p / c_v = 1.40$ | dimensionless | Sonic/Orifice flow formulas |
| 14 | **Molar Mass of Air** | $M_{air} = 28.97 \text{ g/mol}$ | g/mol | Tracer-gas conversions |
| 15 | **Molar Mass of Helium** | $M_{He} = 4.003 \text{ g/mol}$ | g/mol | Helium leak test conversions |
| 16 | **Acceptance Criterion (Pressure Decay)** | $\Delta P \leq \Delta P_{perm}$ | Pa, bar | Direct acceptance check from gauges |
| 17 | **Acceptance Criterion (Leak Rate)** | $Q_L \leq Q_{perm}$ | mbar·L/s or sccm | Indirect acceptance check |
| 18 | **Test Pressure (Pneumatic)** | $P_{test} = 1.0 \times \text{MAWP}$ (typical) | Pa, bar | Where MAWP is the design limit |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Air Leak Test is not merely an academic exercise — it is a **mandatory regulatory and commercial requirement** in the following engineering domains:

1. **Pressure Vessel Manufacturing (IS 2825, ASME Sec VIII):** Every welded pressure vessel must pass an air leak test (or hydrostatic test) before certification.
2. **Pipeline Construction (ASME B31.3, B31.4):** Long-distance oil & gas pipelines are pneumatically tested in sections.
3. **Automotive Industry:** Fuel systems, brake systems, HVAC, and engine components undergo **pressurized air leak testing** on production lines at ~2-4 bar.
4. **Aerospace:** Cabin pressure hulls and fuel tanks are tested for leak rates before flight certification.
5. **Pharmaceutical & Food:** Sterile vessels and packaging are tested for **micro-leak integrity** to prevent contamination.
6. **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Evaporators, condensers, and refrigerant lines are leak-tested at 10-30 bar.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not always use the helium mass-spectrometer method?** Because the Air Leak Test is **100× to 1000× cheaper**, requires no exotic tracer gas, and is acceptable for the vast majority of industrial leak rates (>10⁻² mbar·L/s). It is the **workhorse method** of the NDE industry.

---

## 2.4 Important Safety Hazards Specific to Air Leak Test (KTU High-Yield)

> [!WARNING]
> **Compressed air is a stored energy hazard. A 1 m³ vessel at 10 bar contains ~1 MJ of energy — equivalent to ~240 g of TNT.** Unlike hydrostatic testing, pneumatic testing stores energy in a **compressible** gas, so a catastrophic failure results in **shrapnel and blast wave**, not just a wet rupture. Always:
> 1. Use a **safety relief valve** set at 1.1 × P_test.
> 2. **Exclude personnel** during pressurization (use barricades, per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.169 and IS 8198).
> 3. Use only **dry, oil-free air** to avoid adiabatic explosion inside the vessel.
> 4. Never substitute nitrogen or oxygen without code review.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Leak Rate from Pressure Decay (Isothermal Case — Most Common)

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU 2024 Module 4. It is the derivation that nearly every Part B question on Air Leak Test will test.

### Starting Premise
Consider a rigid, closed vessel of internal volume **V** (in m³ or L), filled with air at an initial pressure **P₁** (absolute) and held at constant temperature **T**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State Boyle's Law** (isothermal assumption: T₁ = T₂):
For a fixed mass of gas at constant temperature, the product P·V is constant.

$$
P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Initial State**
Initially, the gas occupies the entire vessel volume, so:
$$
V_1 = V
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Final State After Leakage**
A small amount of gas has escaped, so the pressure has dropped to P₂. The gas that remains still occupies the **same** volume V (the vessel is rigid, geometry hasn't changed):
$$
V_2 = V
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret What "Escaped Gas" Means in Volume Terms**
Let ΔV be the **volume of gas that escaped**, measured at the final conditions (P₂, T). Imagine that the leaked gas, if recollected at the same pressure P₂ and temperature T, would occupy a volume ΔV.

The gas remaining in the vessel occupies V, while the gas that escaped would occupy ΔV at P₂. So the **initial gas volume (at P₂)** would have been:
$$
V_{total,\ at\ P_2} = V + \Delta V
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boyle's Law Equating Initial and Final States**
The total amount of gas (moles n) is conserved. Apply Boyle's Law to both initial and final states, but reference both to the same temperature T:

$$
P_1 V = P_2 (V + \Delta V)
$$

**Step 6 — Algebraic Expansion**
$$
P_1 V = P_2 V + P_2 \Delta V
$$

**Step 7 — Isolate the Term P₂ ΔV**
$$
P_2 \Delta V = P_1 V - P_2 V = V (P_1 - P_2)
$$

**Step 8 — Solve for ΔV (the volume escaped at the lower pressure P₂)**
$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{V (P_1 - P_2)}{P_2}
$$

**Step 9 — Convert to Volume Escaped at the Initial Pressure P₁ (Useful Form)**
This is the form most often required in leak-rate problems. Apply Boyle's Law again to the escaped gas: the same gas at P₂ with volume ΔV, would have a different volume at P₁.

$$
\Delta V_{at\ P_1} = \Delta V \cdot \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = V \cdot \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1}
$$

**Step 10 — Final Form: Leak Rate Q_L (Volume per Unit Time)**
$$
Q_L = \dfrac{\Delta V_{at\ P_1}}{t_h} = \dfrac{V \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{P_1 \cdot t_h}
$$

**Step 11 — Conversion to Standard Conditions (sccm)**
If the code requires the leak rate in **standard cubic centimetres per minute (sccm)**, normalize to **standard pressure (P_atm = 101.325 kPa)** and **standard temperature (T_std = 273.15 K)**:

$$
Q_L \, [\text{sccm}] = \dfrac{V \, [\text{m}^3] \cdot (P_1 - P_2) \, [\text{Pa}] \cdot 10^6 \, [\text{cm}^3/\text{m}^3]}{P_{atm} \cdot t_h \, [\text{s}] \cdot 60 \, [\text{s/min}]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor 10⁶ converts m³ → cm³, and 60 converts s → min. The ratio (P₁ − P₂)/P_atm normalizes the leaked volume to standard pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Leak Rate with Temperature Correction (Non-Isothermal Case)

When the hold time is long or the system is exposed to ambient temperature swings, the isothermal assumption fails. We must use the **Combined Gas Law**.

### Starting Premise
The vessel is rigid (V constant). Initial state: (P₁, T₁). Final state: (P₂, T₂). Mass of gas remaining is unknown because some has escaped.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the Combined Gas Law for the trapped (post-leak) gas**

$$
\dfrac{P_1 V}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V}{T_2} + \text{(terms accounting for escaped gas)}
$$

**Step 2 — A Cleaner Approach: Use the "Equivalent Final Pressure at T₁" Trick**
We define a **corrected final pressure** P₂* that the gas would have at temperature T₁:

$$
P_2^{\ast} = P_2 \cdot \dfrac{T_1}{T_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Isothermal Formula**
The pressure decay from P₁ to P₂* now accounts for the temperature effect:

$$
Q_L = \dfrac{V \cdot (P_1 - P_2^{\ast})}{P_1 \cdot t_h} = \dfrac{V}{P_1 \cdot t_h} \left( P_1 - P_2 \cdot \dfrac{T_1}{T_2} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Final Non-Isothermal Leak-Rate Formula**

$$
Q_L = \dfrac{V}{P_1 \cdot t_h} \left( P_1 - \dfrac{P_2 \cdot T_1}{T_2} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** If a KTU problem gives you both P₂ and T₂ different from P₁, T₁, you **must** use this corrected formula. Students lose 2-3 marks by blindly using the isothermal formula.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 2024 style):**
> A closed steel pressure vessel has an internal volume of **0.50 m³**. It is pressurized with dry air to a test pressure of **4.0 bar (gauge)**. After a 30-minute hold period at constant temperature of **30 °C**, the pressure is observed to drop to **3.8 bar (gauge)**. Atmospheric pressure is **1.013 bar**. Determine:
> (a) The pressure drop ΔP in kPa.
> (b) The leak rate Q_L in L/min (at test conditions).
> (c) The leak rate in sccm.
> (d) Whether the test passes if the maximum permissible leak rate is **5.0 sccm**.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Pressure Drop ΔP**
Convert gauge pressures to absolute:
- P₁ (abs) = 4.0 + 1.013 = 5.013 bar = **501.3 kPa**
- P₂ (abs) = 3.8 + 1.013 = 4.813 bar = **481.3 kPa**

$$
\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = 501.3 - 481.3 = 20.0 \text{ kPa} = 2.0 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Part (b) — Leak Rate Q_L in L/min at Test Conditions**

Convert V to litres: V = 0.50 m³ = 500 L.
Hold time t_h = 30 min = 1800 s.

$$
Q_L = \dfrac{V \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{P_1 \cdot t_h}
$$

$$
Q_L = \dfrac{500 \text{ L} \times 20.0 \text{ kPa}}{501.3 \text{ kPa} \times 30 \text{ min}}
$$

$$
Q_L = \dfrac{10000}{15039} = 0.6649 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Part (c) — Leak Rate in Standard cc/min (sccm)**
Convert Q_L to cm³/min at test conditions: 0.6649 L/min = 664.9 cm³/min.
Normalize to standard pressure P_atm = 101.325 kPa:

$$
Q_L \, [\text{sccm}] = 664.9 \times \dfrac{501.3}{101.325} = 664.9 \times 4.948
$$

$$
Q_L \, [\text{sccm}] = 3289 \text{ sccm} \approx 3.29 \text{ L/min (at STP)}
$$

**Part (d) — Acceptance Decision**
Permissible leak rate = 5.0 sccm. Observed Q_L = 3289 sccm.

$$
Q_L = 3289 \text{ sccm} \quad \gg \quad Q_{perm} = 5.0 \text{ sccm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The test FAILS — the system leaks excessively.** The vessel must be repaired (re-welding, gasket replacement, etc.) and re-tested.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Test to Use?"

| Application | Recommended Test | Reason |
|-------------|------------------|--------|
| Large pressure vessel, field site | **Air Leak Test** | Low cost, no water disposal issue |
| Hot, dry, water-scarce region | **Air Leak Test** | No water source needed (unlike hydrostatic) |
| LNG / cryogenic tank | **Helium Mass-Spec Test** | Detect micro-leaks < 10⁻⁶ mbar·L/s |
| In-service chemical pipeline | **Ultrasonic Leak Detection** | No shutdown required |
| Quick production-line check (automotive) | **Air Leak Test (automated)** | Cycle time < 30 s |
| Sterile pharmaceutical vessel | **Air Leak + Bubble Point** | Verify filter + vessel integrity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

## 4.1 Schematic: Air Leak Test Rig — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a typical air-leak test rig, mapping the interaction of components during the test. (Note: All node IDs are alphanumeric; labels are clean uppercase text with no markdown formatting inside quotes.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[COMPRESSED AIR SUPPLY] --> A2[FILTER DRIER AND REGULATOR]
    A2 --> A3[SAFETY RELIEF VALVE SET AT 1.1 TIMES P TEST]
    A3 --> A4[MANUAL OR SOLENOID ISOLATION VALVE INLET]
    A4 --> A5[TEST VESSEL OR PIPING UNDER EXAMINATION]
    A5 --> A6[CALIBRATED PRESSURE GAUGE CLASS 1.0 OR BETTER]
    A5 --> A7[CALIBRATED TEMPERATURE SENSOR PT100]
    A5 --> A8[MANUAL OR SOLENOID VENT VALVE OUTLET]
    B1[DATA LOGGER OR PRESSURE TRANSMITTER] --> A6
    B1 --> A7
    B1 --> B2[ACCEPTANCE MODULE COMPUTES DELTA P AND Q L]
    B2 --> B3{DECISION NODE Q L LESS THAN Q PERM}
    B3 -->|YES| B4[ACCEPT AND ISSUE CERTIFICATE]
    B3 -->|NO| B5[REJECT REPAIR AND RETEST]
    C1[OCCUPIED ZONE EXCLUSION BARRICADE] -.GUARDS.-> A5
    C2[VENT DISCHARGE TO SAFE LOCATION] -.EXHAUST.-> A8
```

### Reading the Diagram
- **Solid arrows (→)** represent the **flow of compressed air** from the supply through filtration, regulation, and into the test piece.
- **Dashed arrows (-.)** represent **non-flow safety controls** (personnel exclusion zone, safe vent discharge).
- **Decision node (diamond)** is the **acceptance criterion** evaluation.
- The **Data Logger** branch captures both pressure and temperature signals in real time, which is critical for accurate Q_L calculation.

---

## 4.2 Schematic: Test Procedure State Machine

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **state machine** for the air leak test procedure — the sequence of states the system transitions through during a complete test.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    S1[STATE 1 PRE TEST SETUP AND PURGING] --> S2[STATE 2 SLOW PRESSURIZATION TO P TEST]
    S2 --> S3[STATE 3 STABILIZATION AND SOAK]
    S3 --> S4[STATE 4 ISOLATION AND HOLD]
    S4 --> S5[STATE 5 RECORD P2 AND T2]
    S5 --> S6[STATE 6 COMPUTE DELTA P AND Q L]
    S6 --> S7[STATE 7 COMPARE WITH Q PERM]
    S7 --> S8[STATE 8 PASS ACCEPT DOCUMENT]
    S7 --> S9[STATE 8 FAIL REPAIR AND RETEST]
    S8 --> S10[STATE 9 SAFE DEPRESSURIZATION]
    S9 --> S1
    S10 --> S11[STATE 10 TEST COMPLETE]
```

### Key State Transitions
- **S3 → S4**: This is the **most critical transition** because at S4 the system is isolated and any further pressure drop is due **solely** to leakage.
- **S7 → S9**: If the system fails, the procedure loops back to S1 (purging) for re-testing after repair.
- **S10**: The depressurization step is **mandatory** and must be **slow** (valved bleed) to avoid thermal shock to the test piece.

---

## 4.3 Leak Path Classification Block Diagram

Different types of leak paths require different detection strategies. The block diagram below classifies the leak geometries encountered in service.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[LEAK PATH TYPE] --> Q1[THROUGH WALL DEFECT]
    P1 --> Q2[GASKET JOINT LEAKAGE]
    P1 --> Q3[THREADED CONNECTION LEAK]
    P1 --> Q4[WELD POROSITY OR CRACK]
    P1 --> Q5[VALVE SEAT LEAK]
    Q1 --> R1[DETECTED BY PRESSURE DECAY AND BUBBLE TEST]
    Q2 --> R2[DETECTED BY BUBBLE TEST AND SOAP FILM]
    Q3 --> R3[DETECTED BY ULTRASONIC OR PRESSURE DECAY]
    Q4 --> R4[DETECTED BY HELIUM SNIFF OR DYE PENETRANT]
    Q5 --> R5[DETECTED BY DECAY AND ACOUSTIC EMISSION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU Examiners:** The examiner often asks "Which leak-detection method is most suitable for a given defect type?" The block above gives the standard answer framework.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

> **Q1.** Define **Air Leak Test**. State any two codes or standards that govern its application in pressure vessel inspection.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> An **Air Leak Test** is a non-destructive procedure in which a sealed system (vessel, pipeline, or component) is pressurized with air (or an inert gas) to a specified test pressure, isolated, and held for a defined period. The pressure decay (or measured leak rate) over this period is used to determine whether the system meets the maximum permissible leakage specified by the design code. **(2 marks)**

> Two relevant codes/standards:
> 1. **ASME Section V, Article 10** – Leak Testing (general industry).
> 2. **IS 2825** – Code for Unfired Pressure Vessels (India-specific; specifies pneumatic test pressures and acceptance criteria). **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

> **Q2.** Differentiate between a **Hydrostatic Test** and an **Air Leak Test**. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Hydrostatic Test | Air Leak Test |
|--------|------------------|---------------|
| Test medium | Water (incompressible) | Air / gas (compressible) |
| Energy stored at failure | Low (water barely compresses) | High (compressed gas releases explosively) |
| Leak detection sensitivity | Low (visible seepage only) | Moderate to high (ΔP measurable) |
| Cost of medium | Higher (water, disposal) | Lower (air, no disposal) |
| Safety risk | Lower | Higher (blast hazard) |

> **Advantage of Hydrostatic:** Safer at failure (no explosive energy release). **(0.5 mark)**
> **Disadvantage of Hydrostatic:** Requires large water volumes and drying after test; cannot detect very small leaks. **(0.5 mark)**
> **Advantage of Air Leak:** Highly sensitive to small leaks; no water contamination; cheaper. **(0.5 mark)**
> **Disadvantage of Air Leak:** Higher stored energy → more dangerous in case of vessel rupture; requires strict exclusion zones. **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Examiner's Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B offers an **internal choice** between two questions of equal marks. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**, each carrying 14 marks, with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Both are KTU-pattern problems.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

> **Q.A.** (a) With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **procedure of conducting an air leak test** on a welded pressure vessel. List the safety precautions to be observed. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) A pressure vessel of internal volume **0.80 m³** is pressurized to **5.0 bar (gauge)** with dry air and held for **45 minutes** at a constant temperature of **32 °C**. The pressure drops to **4.85 bar (gauge)**. Atmospheric pressure is **1.013 bar**. Calculate:
> (i) The leak rate in **L/min** at test conditions.
> (ii) The leak rate in **sccm**.
> (iii) Comment on acceptance if the permissible leak rate is **100 sccm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a) [7 Marks]

**1. Schematic of Test Setup (block description) — 2 marks:**

A typical setup consists of:
- Compressed air supply (compressor or gas cylinder bank) →
- Filter-drier-regulator train (removes moisture and oil) →
- Safety relief valve (set at 1.1 × P_test) →
- Isolation valves (inlet and outlet) →
- Calibrated pressure gauge (accuracy ±0.5% FS or better) →
- Test vessel (with all openings sealed except inlet/outlet) →
- Optional: temperature sensor and data logger →
- Safe vent discharge to atmosphere.

**2. Step-by-Step Procedure — 4 marks:**

(i) **Preparation:** Clean and dry the vessel; remove all debris, oil, and moisture. Seal all openings except the test inlet and outlet. Fit calibrated pressure gauge and temperature sensor. **(0.5 mark)**

(ii) **Purging:** Pass dry air through the vessel for 5-10 minutes to displace any residual flammable gases or moisture. **(0.5 mark)**

(iii) **Pressurization:** Slowly raise the pressure to the test pressure (1.0 × MAWP for pneumatic) at a controlled rate (≤ 0.5 bar/sec) to avoid adiabatic heating. **(1 mark)**

(iv) **Stabilization (Soak):** Hold at P_test for 10-30 minutes until temperature stabilizes. Record initial pressure P₁ and temperature T₁. **(0.5 mark)**

(v) **Isolation and Hold:** Close the inlet valve to isolate the vessel. Hold for the prescribed time t_h (here, 45 min). Record final pressure P₂ and temperature T₂. **(1 mark)**

(vi) **Evaluation and Documentation:** Compute ΔP, convert to leak rate Q_L in sccm, compare with Q_perm, and accept or reject. Sign off on the test certificate. **(0.5 mark)**

**3. Safety Precautions — 1 mark:**

(a) Use a **safety relief valve** set at 1.1 × P_test to prevent over-pressurization.
(b) Establish an **exclusion zone** (typically 1.5 × vessel diameter) — no personnel during pressurization.
(c) Use **dry, oil-free air** to prevent internal explosion.
(d) **Never** hammer or strike the vessel during the test.
(e) **Slow depressurization** through the vent valve.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- V = 0.80 m³ = 800 L
- P₁ (gauge) = 5.0 bar → P₁ (abs) = 5.0 + 1.013 = **6.013 bar = 601.3 kPa**
- P₂ (gauge) = 4.85 bar → P₂ (abs) = 4.85 + 1.013 = **5.863 bar = 586.3 kPa**
- t_h = 45 min
- T = 32 °C (constant) → isothermal, no correction needed.
- P_atm = 1.013 bar = 101.3 kPa

**(i) Leak Rate in L/min at Test Conditions — 3 marks:**

$$
Q_L = \dfrac{V \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{P_1 \cdot t_h} = \dfrac{800 \times (601.3 - 586.3)}{601.3 \times 45}
$$

$$
Q_L = \dfrac{800 \times 15.0}{27058.5} = \dfrac{12000}{27058.5} = 0.4434 \text{ L/min}
$$

**(ii) Leak Rate in sccm — 3 marks:**

First convert L/min to cm³/min at test conditions: 0.4434 L/min = 443.4 cm³/min.
Then normalize to standard pressure:

$$
Q_L \, [\text{sccm}] = 443.4 \times \dfrac{P_1}{P_{atm}} = 443.4 \times \dfrac{601.3}{101.3}
$$

$$
Q_L \, [\text{sccm}] = 443.4 \times 5.936 = 2632 \text{ sccm} \approx 2.63 \text{ L/min at STP}
$$

**(iii) Acceptance Decision — 1 mark:**

Permissible leak rate = 100 sccm.
Observed Q_L = 2632 sccm.
Since **2632 sccm ≫ 100 sccm**, the test **FAILS**. The vessel must be repaired (re-welded / re-gasketed) and re-tested.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

> **Q.B.** (a) Explain the **principle of the air leak test** based on the ideal gas law. Derive the relationship between **leak rate, vessel volume, pressure drop, and hold time** for an isothermal test. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) A pipe section of internal volume **0.025 m³** is pressurized to **8.0 bar (gauge)**. The test is conducted over **60 minutes**, and the pressure drop observed is **0.10 bar**. The temperature rises from **28 °C** at the start to **30 °C** at the end. Atmospheric pressure is **1.013 bar**. Calculate:
> (i) The **temperature-corrected** leak rate in sccm.
> (ii) Comment on whether the test passes if the permissible leakage is **20 sccm**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a) [7 Marks]

**Principle (3 marks):**
The air leak test is based on the principle that, in a **rigid closed vessel** held at (approximately) constant temperature, any loss of gas through a leak path results in a measurable **decrease in pressure**. By applying **Boyle's Law** (P·V = constant for isothermal conditions), the volume of gas that escaped can be calculated from the observed pressure drop. The leak rate (volume per unit time) is then compared to the code-permissible leak rate to accept or reject the system.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
See the **complete step-by-step derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. The final formula is:

$$
Q_L = \dfrac{V \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{P_1 \cdot t_h}
$$

> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "derive," always start with the law you are using, state your assumptions (rigid vessel, isothermal, ideal gas), and walk through each algebraic step. Marks are awarded for **clarity of logic**, not just the final formula.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- V = 0.025 m³ = 25 L
- P₁ (gauge) = 8.0 bar → P₁ (abs) = 9.013 bar = **901.3 kPa**
- ΔP (gauge) = 0.10 bar → ΔP (abs) = 0.10 bar = **10.0 kPa** (ΔP is the same in gauge and absolute)
- P₂ (abs) = 901.3 − 10.0 = **891.3 kPa**
- t_h = 60 min
- T₁ = 28 °C = **301.15 K**
- T₂ = 30 °C = **303.15 K**
- P_atm = 101.3 kPa

**(i) Temperature-Corrected Leak Rate — 5 marks:**

**Step 1 — Apply the temperature-correction factor to P₂:**

$$
P_2^{\ast} = P_2 \cdot \dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = 891.3 \times \dfrac{301.15}{303.15}
$$

$$
P_2^{\ast} = 891.3 \times 0.99340 = 885.3 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective pressure drop:**

$$
\Delta P_{eff} = P_1 - P_2^{\ast} = 901.3 - 885.3 = 16.0 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Q_L in L/min at test conditions:**

$$
Q_L = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta P_{eff}}{P_1 \cdot t_h} = \dfrac{25 \times 16.0}{901.3 \times 60}
$$

$$
Q_L = \dfrac{400}{54078} = 7.397 \times 10^{-3} \text{ L/min} = 7.397 \text{ mL/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to sccm:**
7.397 mL/min = 7.397 cm³/min. Normalize to standard pressure:

$$
Q_L \, [\text{sccm}] = 7.397 \times \dfrac{901.3}{101.3} = 7.397 \times 8.898 = 65.8 \text{ sccm}
$$

**(ii) Acceptance — 2 marks:**

Permissible leak rate = 20 sccm. Observed Q_L = 65.8 sccm.

> [!WARNING]
> **65.8 sccm > 20 sccm → The test FAILS.** Notice that **without** the temperature correction, one would have computed:
> Q_L (uncorrected) = 7.397 × (901.3/101.3) = ... wait, that step is independent. The uncorrected version would have used the raw ΔP of 10 kPa, giving a lower Q_L that might wrongly suggest "pass." Hence the temperature correction is **critical** to avoid a false acceptance.

> **Uncorrected (isothermal naive) result:** Q_L = 25 × 10 / (901.3 × 60) = 4.62 mL/min → 41.1 sccm. Still > 20, so in this case the test would fail even without correction — but in borderline cases, the temperature correction is the deciding factor.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Air Leak Test Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert gauge to absolute pressure** — examiners routinely give gauge pressures to test this very conversion. *Always add 1.013 bar (or 101.325 kPa) to get absolute pressure.*
> 2. **Using the wrong formula form (V at P₂ vs V at P₁)** — know both the "ΔV at P₂" and "ΔV at P₁" versions, and be clear which one your final formula represents.
> 3. **Ignoring temperature correction** — when T₂ ≠ T₁, you **must** apply the combined-gas-law correction. A 2 °C temperature rise of ~0.7% can shift Q_L by 50-100% in borderline cases.
> 4. **Unit confusion** — keep V in m³ (or L), P in Pa (or kPa), t in seconds (or min), and be consistent. sccm is a **normalized** unit — do not forget the P_atm division.
> 5. **Confusing "leak rate at test conditions" with "sccm"** — they differ by the factor (P_test / P_atm). State clearly which one the code requires.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Print / Pin This Section**

- [x] **Air Leak Test** is a **pneumatic pressure-decay NDE method** used to verify the integrity of sealed systems.
- [x] The governing principle is **Boyle's Law** (isothermal) or the **Combined Gas Law** (non-isothermal).
- [x] The master formula (isothermal, leak rate at initial pressure) is:

$$
Q_L = \dfrac{V \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{P_1 \cdot t_h}
$$

- [x] The **non-isothermal** correction uses $P_2^{\ast} = P_2 \cdot T_1 / T_2$.
- [x] **Standard atmospheric pressure** is **101.325 kPa** = **1.01325 bar**.
- [x] **Universal gas constant** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); **specific gas constant for air** $R_{air} = 287.058$ J/(kg·K).
- [x] **Test pressure** for pneumatic leak tests is typically **1.0 × MAWP** (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure) for ASME / IS 2825.
- [x] The **safety relief valve** must be set at **1.1 × P_test**.
- [x] **Pneumatic testing** stores **100× to 1000× more energy** than hydrostatic testing at the same pressure — strict exclusion zones are mandatory.
- [x] The **sccm conversion** normalizes to $P_{atm}$ and $T_{std}$: $Q_L[\text{sccm}] = Q_L[\text{cm}^3/\text{min}\ \text{at test}] \times (P_{test}/P_{atm})$.
- [x] **Acceptance criterion:** $Q_L \leq Q_{perm}$ (code-specified permissible leak rate).
- [x] **Helium mass-spec** is used for ultra-sensitive detection ($<10^{-6}$ mbar·L/s); Air Leak Test is for routine industrial use ($>10^{-2}$ mbar·L/s).
- [x] **Codes to remember:** ASME Section V Article 10, ASME B31.3, IS 2825, IS 12411, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.169.
- [x] **Order of operations in a test:** Purge → Pressurize → Stabilize → Isolate → Hold → Record P₂, T₂ → Compute Q_L → Accept / Reject → Depressurize safely.
- [x] **Always** use **dry, oil-free air** to avoid adiabatic explosion inside the vessel.
- [x] The **bicycle-tyre analogy** (Section 1.2) is your conceptual hook for viva questions.
- [x] **Examiner's favourite trick:** Giving gauge pressures and a T₂ ≠ T₁ condition in the same problem to test both the gauge→absolute and temperature-correction skills.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT725 - SAFETY IN ENGINEERING INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steam Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Steam Testing

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Steam Testing** is a specialized *non-destructive testing (NDT)* and *commissioning safety procedure* employed in process, petrochemical, oil & gas, power generation, and heavy engineering industries. It is defined as the controlled application of saturated or superheated steam at predetermined pressures, temperatures, and durations to verify the **mechanical integrity**, **leaktightness**, **thermal stability**, and **operational readiness** of pressure vessels, pipelines, valves, boilers, heat exchangers, and associated piping systems prior to commissioning or after major maintenance.

According to **ASME Section I (PG-58)**, **ASME B31.1**, and **API 570** standards, steam testing is recognized as an authorized substitute for hydrostatic testing under specific operating conditions, particularly when:
- The equipment is designed for steam service
- Water residue cannot be tolerated in the system
- Thermal cycling verification is required
- Cleaning of internal surfaces through *steam blowing* is necessary

The procedure uses **saturated steam at temperatures typically between 150°C and 350°C**, with pressures ranging from **1.5 bar(g) to 100 bar(g)** depending on design parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Steam Testing is classified under "Pre-Commissioning & Commissioning Safety Activities" in Module 4. Students must understand both the *procedural methodology* and the *occupational safety hazards* (burns, pressure rupture, asphyxiation) associated with steam testing operations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker Principle"

Imagine a **pressure cooker** used in households. Before trusting it to cook your meal safely, you would:
1. **Seal** the lid firmly (leaktightness check)
2. **Heat** it up to operating pressure (pressure integrity test)
3. **Listen** for hissing sounds (leak detection)
4. **Watch** the safety whistle (relief valve function)
5. **Time** how long it holds pressure (duration test)

Steam testing in industry is essentially the **industrial-scale, scientifically instrumented version of this domestic safety check**. The key difference is that instead of a small cooker, engineers are dealing with systems that may be **100 meters tall, 5 meters in diameter, and contain miles of piping** — where a single undetected leak could result in catastrophic loss of life, asset damage worth crores, and environmental disasters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants in Steam Testing:**
> - **Latent heat of vaporization (h_fg) at 100°C** = **2257 kJ/kg**
> - **Specific volume of saturated steam at atmospheric pressure** = **1.673 m³/kg**
> - **Steam-to-water volume expansion ratio at 100°C** = **1700 : 1**
> - **Critical temperature of water** = **374°C**
> - **Critical pressure of water** = **220.6 bar**

## 1.3 Classification of Steam Testing Operations

Steam testing in engineering industries is broadly classified into **four categories**:

| Category | Purpose | Typical Pressure | Typical Temperature |
|----------|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Steam Leak Testing** | Detect flange/valve/joint leaks | 1.5 – 5 bar(g) | 110 – 150°C |
| **Steam Strength Testing** | Verify design pressure integrity | 1.5× design pressure | Saturated at test pressure |
| **Steam Blowing (Superheating)** | Remove debris, mill scale, rust | 20 – 80 bar(g) | 250 – 520°C |
| **Steam Purging** | Remove air/gas before commissioning | 1 – 3 bar(g) | 100 – 135°C |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics of Steam Testing

The effectiveness of steam testing stems from **three fundamental physical principles**:

### Principle 1: Thermal Expansion and Contraction Stress
When steam contacts a cold metallic surface (e.g., a freshly manufactured or shut-down pipe), the temperature differential creates **thermal stress**. A pre-existing crack, weld defect, or thin section will deform differently under this thermal load, making it detectable through:
- Visual inspection
- Ultrasonic thickness gauging
- Dye penetrant testing (post-test)
- Acoustic emission monitoring (during test)

### Principle 2: Molecular Penetration of Steam
Steam molecules, due to their small kinetic diameter (~0.265 nm for H₂O), can penetrate micro-leakage paths that would be sealed by water under hydrostatic testing. This makes steam testing **more sensitive than hydrostatic testing** for detecting hairline cracks and gasket imperfections.

### Principle 3: Pressure-Temperature Coupling (Clausius-Clapeyron Relation)
For saturated steam, pressure and temperature are **uniquely coupled** through the Clausius-Clapeyron equation:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{L}{T \cdot \Delta v}$$

Where:
- $L$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/kg)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $\Delta v$ = specific volume change (m³/kg)

This coupling means **measuring pressure is equivalent to measuring temperature** at saturation conditions, providing a built-in verification mechanism.

## 2.2 Operational Logic Steps in a Steam Test

A typical steam test follows a **systematic 7-step protocol**:

1. **Pre-Test Preparation**
   - Review P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)
   - Verify all isolations are in place (LOTO - Lock Out Tag Out)
   - Confirm safety valve set pressures
   - Calibrate pressure gauges, thermocouples

2. **System Conditioning**
   - Open drains and vents
   - Introduce steam slowly to warm the system at ≤ **30°C/hour** ramp rate
   - Allow condensate to drain completely (thermal shock prevention)

3. **Pressurization Phase**
   - Gradually raise pressure to **50% of test pressure**, hold for 15 minutes
   - Increase to **test pressure** at controlled rate (typically ≤ 1 bar/minute)
   - Maintain test pressure for the **specified hold duration** (usually 30 min – 4 hours)

4. **Leak Detection Phase**
   - Inspect all flanges, valves, weld joints using soap solution or thermal imaging
   - Listen for acoustic emissions
   - Monitor pressure decay (allowable: ≤ **1-2% per hour** depending on system volume)

5. **Hold and Monitor**
   - Record pressure and temperature every 15 minutes
   - Check for thermal expansion movement at supports
   - Verify safety valve seats (without lifting)

6. **Controlled Depressurization**
   - Reduce pressure slowly (≤ 2 bar/minute)
   - Maintain system hot to prevent vacuum formation
   - Open vents before draining condensate

7. **Post-Test Documentation**
   - Issue "Steam Test Certificate" with pressure-time-temperature graphs
   - Mark system status with green "TESTED" tag
   - File test records per statutory retention period (typically 5-10 years)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **essential for KTU examination problem-solving**. Memorize the relationships, units, and applicability conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | **Hoop (Circumferential) Stress** in thin-walled cylinder | σ_h in MPa, P in MPa, D in mm, t in mm |
| 2 | $\sigma_l = \frac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | **Longitudinal Stress** in thin-walled cylinder | σ_l = σ_h / 2 |
| 3 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | **Linear Thermal Expansion** | α in /°C, L₀ in mm, ΔT in °C |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_T = 3\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | **Volumetric Thermal Strain** (isotropic material) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $m = \frac{P \cdot V}{R_s \cdot T}$ | **Steam Mass Calculation** (Ideal Gas) | m in kg, P in Pa, V in m³, R_s = 461.5 J/kg·K |
| 6 | $Q = m \cdot (h_{fg} + C_p \cdot \Delta T)$ | **Total Steam Heat Energy** | Q in kJ, h_fg in kJ/kg |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | **Steam Mass Flow Rate** | ρ in kg/m³, A in m², v in m/s |
| 8 | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design}$ | **Hydrostatic Test Pressure Factor** (ASME) | For most pressure vessels |
| 9 | $P_{steam-test} = 1.1 \times P_{design}$ | **Steam Test Pressure Factor** | Lower factor due to thermal risk |
| 10 | $t_{hold} = \frac{V_{system}}{100}$ | **Approximate Hold Time** (minutes) | V in m³; minimum 30 minutes |
| 11 | $P_{decay} = P_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot \tau}$ | **Pressure Decay Model** | k = leak coefficient (1/hr) |
| 12 | $Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | **Reynolds Number** for steam flow | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Note:** ASME B31.1 allows steam testing at **1.1 × design pressure** ONLY when steam is the intended service fluid and material is carbon/low-alloy steel. For austenitic stainless steels, the factor is typically reduced to **1.0 × design pressure** to prevent stress corrosion cracking from chloride-bearing condensate.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Steam testing is **indispensable** in the following scenarios:

- **Petrochemical Plants:** Pre-commissioning of steam headers, tracing lines, and reboilers
- **Power Plants:** Boiler steam piping, turbine inlet lines, HP/LP bypass systems
- **Sugar & Paper Mills:** Evaporator vessels, juice heaters, digester piping
- **Oil Refineries:** Crude unit preheat trains, hydrocracker reactors, steam methane reformers
- **Pharmaceutical Plants:** Sterile steam (clean steam) lines for autoclaves and SIP (Steam-In-Place) systems
- **Marine Engineering:** Main steam propulsion lines, boiler uptakes

The **economic value** of steam testing lies in preventing post-commissioning failures — a single steam line rupture in a refinery can cause losses exceeding **₹50 crores** in production downtime, plus safety, environmental, and reputational damage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Allowable Steam Test Pressure for a Pipe

**Problem Statement:** A carbon steel pipeline has design pressure of **42 bar(g)** at **400°C**. Per ASME B31.1, calculate the maximum steam test pressure and the corresponding saturation temperature.

**Given:**
- Material: ASTM A106 Grade B Carbon Steel
- Nominal pipe size: 8 inches (DN 200)
- Outside diameter: D = 219.1 mm
- Wall thickness: t = 12.7 mm
- Design pressure: P_design = 42 bar(g) = 4.2 MPa(g)
- Design temperature: 400°C

**Step 1 — Apply ASME B31.1 Test Pressure Factor**

For carbon steel piping with steam as the service fluid:

$$P_{test} = 1.10 \times P_{design}$$

$$P_{test} = 1.10 \times 4.2 \text{ MPa} = 4.62 \text{ MPa(g)}$$

Converting to bar(g):

$$P_{test} = 4.62 \times 10 \approx 46.2 \text{ bar(g)}$$

**Step 2 — Add Atmospheric Pressure for Absolute Reading**

$$P_{test,abs} = P_{test,gauge} + P_{atm} = 4.62 + 0.1013 = 4.7213 \text{ MPa(a)}$$

**Step 3 — Lookup Saturation Temperature from Steam Tables**

From standard steam tables at P = 4.72 MPa(abs):

$$T_{sat} \approx 261°C$$

This is well below the design temperature of 400°C, so the test is **thermally conservative**.

**Step 4 — Verify Hoop Stress at Test Pressure**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P_{test} \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$$

Substituting values:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{4.62 \times 219.1}{2 \times 12.7}$$

$$\sigma_h = \frac{1012.24}{25.4}$$

$$\sigma_h = 39.85 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Allowable Stress**

For ASTM A106 Gr. B at 261°C (interpolated from ASME B31.1 Table A-1):

$$\sigma_{allow} \approx 138 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Safety Factor**

$$SF = \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{\sigma_h} = \frac{138}{39.85} = 3.46$$

Since **SF > 3.0**, the test pressure is acceptable. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steam Quantity Required for System Heating

**Problem Statement:** A pressure vessel with mass **m_vessel = 2500 kg**, made of SA-516 Gr. 70 carbon steel, is to be steam tested from **30°C** to **180°C**. Calculate the steam quantity required if saturated steam at **10 bar(g)** is used.

**Given:**
- m_vessel = 2500 kg
- Specific heat of carbon steel: C_p = 0.485 kJ/kg·K
- Initial temperature: T₁ = 30°C
- Target temperature: T₂ = 180°C
- Steam pressure: 10 bar(g) (≈ 11.013 bar(a) ≈ 1.101 MPa(a))

**Step 1 — Determine Steam Properties from Steam Tables**

At 10 bar(g) saturation:
- Saturation temperature: T_steam = 184°C
- Latent heat: h_fg = 2014 kJ/kg
- Enthalpy of saturated water: h_f = 781 kJ/kg
- Specific heat of condensate: C_p,water = 4.18 kJ/kg·K

**Step 2 — Energy Required to Heat the Vessel**

$$Q_{vessel} = m_{vessel} \times C_{p,steel} \times (T_2 - T_1)$$

$$Q_{vessel} = 2500 \times 0.485 \times (180 - 30)$$

$$Q_{vessel} = 2500 \times 0.485 \times 150$$

$$Q_{vessel} = 181,875 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Heat Released per kg of Steam (Condensing then Cooling)**

Each kg of saturated steam at 184°C releases:
- Latent heat: h_fg = 2014 kJ/kg
- Sensible heat cooling from 184°C to 180°C: C_p × ΔT = 4.18 × 4 = 16.72 kJ/kg
- Total per kg: q_total = 2014 + 16.72 = 2030.72 kJ/kg

**Step 4 — Calculate Steam Mass Required**

Accounting for **70% heat utilization efficiency** (losses through radiation, convection, condensate carryover):

$$m_{steam} = \frac{Q_{vessel}}{q_{total} \times \eta}$$

$$m_{steam} = \frac{181,875}{2030.72 \times 0.70}$$

$$m_{steam} = \frac{181,875}{1421.5}$$

$$m_{steam} \approx 127.95 \text{ kg} \approx 128 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Steam Volume at Operating Conditions**

Specific volume of saturated steam at 10 bar(g):

$$v_g = 0.1944 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$V_{steam} = m_{steam} \times v_g = 128 \times 0.1944 = 24.88 \text{ m}^3$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pressure Decay Analysis for Leak Detection

**Problem Statement:** A steam-tested pipeline shows pressure decay from **15.0 bar(g)** to **14.6 bar(g)** over **2 hours** at constant temperature (180°C). Determine the leak coefficient and assess acceptability.

**Given:**
- Initial pressure: P₀ = 15.0 + 1.013 = 16.013 bar(a) = 1.6013 MPa(a)
- Final pressure: P_f = 14.6 + 1.013 = 15.613 bar(a) = 1.5613 MPa(a)
- Time: τ = 2 hours
- Temperature: T = 180 + 273 = 453 K (constant — isothermal process)

**Step 1 — Apply Isothermal Decay Equation**

For an isothermal process (ideal gas with constant temperature):

$$P_f = P_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot \tau}$$

Solving for k:

$$k = -\frac{1}{\tau} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{P_f}{P_0}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values**

$$k = -\frac{1}{2} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1.5613}{1.6013}\right)$$

$$k = -\frac{1}{2} \cdot \ln(0.9751)$$

$$k = -\frac{1}{2} \times (-0.02521)$$

$$k = 0.01260 \text{ per hour}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Pressure Drop Percentage**

$$\%\text{Drop} = \frac{P_0 - P_f}{P_0} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Drop} = \frac{16.013 - 15.613}{16.013} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Drop} = \frac{0.400}{16.013} \times 100 = 2.50\%$$

**Step 4 — Compare Against Acceptance Criteria**

Per typical KTU-referenced standards (ASME B31.1, IS 1239, API 570):
- **Acceptable pressure drop** ≤ **1% per hour** for tested systems
- **Acceptable total drop** ≤ **5% over 4 hours**

Since 2.5% over 2 hours = **1.25% per hour**, this **slightly exceeds** the 1% per hour criterion. ⚠️

**Step 5 — Decision and Recommendation**

The test result is **marginally unacceptable**. Recommended actions:
1. Re-inspect all flange joints with soap solution
2. Check valve packing and bonnet seals
3. Verify gauge calibration (zero-shift error)
4. Re-test after corrective action

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Steam Test Selection Guide

| Scenario | Recommended Test Type | Test Pressure | Hold Time | Critical Precaution |
|----------|----------------------|---------------|-----------|---------------------|
| New carbon steel piping | Steam Strength Test | 1.1 × P_design | 30 min – 2 hr | Ramp ≤ 30°C/hr |
| Austenitic SS piping | Steam Test (low pressure) | 1.0 × P_design | 1 hour | Chloride-free water in boiler |
| Pre-commissioning cleaning | Steam Blowing | 20-40 bar(g) | Until target cleanliness | Eject silencer downstream |
| Leak investigation | Steam Soap Test | 0.5 bar(g) | As needed | Use mirror + light source |
| Post-repair weld test | Localized Steam Test | 1.1 × P_design | 4 hours | NDT before steam exposure |
| Refinery steam header | Full System Steam Test | 1.25 × P_design | 4 hours | Bypass instruments |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Steam Testing Process Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete decision flow** of a steam testing operation, from initial planning through final certification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START Project Planning"] --> nodeB["Review P&ID and ISometrics"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Is Steam Service\nthe Design Intent?"}
    nodeC -->|Yes| nodeD["Check Applicable Code:\nASME B31.1 or IS 1239"]
    nodeC -->|No| nodeE["Use Hydrostatic Test Instead"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Calculate Test Pressure\nP_test = 1.1 × P_design"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Verify Material Compatibility\nand Allowable Stress"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"Material OK?\nSS Check Chloride Risk"}
    nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI["Issue Steam Test Procedure\nSTTP Document"]
    nodeH -->|No| nodeJ["Reduce Test Pressure\nto 1.0 × P_design"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["LOTO and Isolation"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL["Pre-Test Safety Briefing\nand PPE Verification"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Open Drains and Vents"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Slow Steam Introduction\nRamp 30 C per Hour"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Stage 1: 50 percent P_test\nHold 15 minutes"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["Stage 2: 100 percent P_test\nHold for Specified Duration"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["Leak Inspection Phase\nSoap Solution and Thermal Camera"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR{"Leak Detected?"}
    nodeR -->|Yes| nodeS["Depressurize and Repair\nRe-test Required"]
    nodeR -->|No| nodeT["Monitor Pressure Decay\nRecord P and T Every 15 min"]
    nodeT --> nodeU{"Pressure Drop\nwithin 1 percent per Hour?"}
    nodeU -->|Yes| nodeV["Controlled Depressurization\nat 2 bar per minute"]
    nodeU -->|No| nodeW["Investigate and Rectify\nLikely Leak Source"]
    nodeW --> nodeS
    nodeV --> nodeX["Drain Condensate\nand Cool System"]
    nodeX --> nodeY["Issue Steam Test Certificate\nand Green Tag"]
    nodeY --> nodeZ["Archive Documents\nEND"]
    nodeS --> nodeK
```

## 4.2 Steam Test Rig Functional Architecture

The following block diagram maps the **physical components** of a typical steam test setup with their safety interlocks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    boiler["STEAM BOILER\nSource Unit"] --> prv["PRESSURE REDUCING\nVALVE PRV-01"]
    prv --> sf1["STRAINER\nSF-01"]
    sf1 --> pt1["PRESSURE TRANSMITTER\nPT-01"]
    pt1 --> mo1["MANUAL VALVE\nMOV-01"]
    mo1 --> fv["FLOW METER\nFE-01"]
    fv --> sis1["SAFETY INTERLOCK\nSIS-01"]
    sis1 --> ck1["CHECK VALVE\nCV-01"]
    ck1 --> test["EQUIPMENT UNDER TEST\nPressure Vessel or Pipeline"]
    test --> vt1["VENT VALVE\nMOV-02"]
    test --> td1["TEMPERATURE SENSOR\nTT-01"]
    test --> cd1["CONDENSATE DRAN\nMOV-03"]
    cd1 --> ec["ECONOMIZER\nEC-01"]
    sis2["EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN\nESD System"] -.-> prv
    sis2 -.-> mo1
    sv["SAFETY VALVE\nPSV-01 Set 1.1 times P_test"] -.-> test
    alarm["ALARM PANEL\nHMI Display"] --- pt1
    alarm --- td1
```

## 4.3 Hazard Identification Network (HAZID) for Steam Testing

This diagram identifies the **principal hazards** and their associated control measures during steam testing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    h1["HAZARD Steam Burns\n180 to 350 C Contact"] --> c1["CONTROL Insulated PPE\nHeat Resistant Gloves"]
    h2["HAZARD Pressure Rupture\nCatastrophic Failure"] --> c2["CONTROL Pressure Relief Valves\nand Bursting Disc"]
    h3["HAZARD Asphyxiation\nSteam Displaces Oxygen"] --> c3["CONTROL Oxygen Monitors\nand Forced Ventilation"]
    h4["HAZARD Thermal Shock\nRapid Temperature Change"] --> c4["CONTROL Controlled Ramp Rate\n30 C per Hour Max"]
    h5["HAZARD Manual Handling\nHeavy Test Rig Components"] --> c5["CONTROL Mechanical Lifting Aids\nand Team Lifting"]
    h6["HAZARD Noise Exposure\nSteam Blowing 120 plus dB"] --> c6["CONTROL Ear Protection\nand Silencer Installation"]
    h7["HAZARD Slips and Falls\nWet Condensate Surfaces"] --> c7["CONTROL Anti-slip Flooring\nand Drainage Management"]
```
