# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Engineering Controls and Administrative Controls. Why are engineering controls considered more reliable than administrative controls?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Controls** are physical modifications to equipment, processes, or facilities (e.g., LEV, machine guards, interlocks) that remove or contain hazards *at the source*, independent of worker behavior.
>
> **Administrative Controls** are procedural or policy-based measures (e.g., SOPs, training, job rotation, permit-to-work) that change *how* people work to reduce exposure.
>
> **Reliability reason:** Engineering controls are **built into the physical system** and operate continuously regardless of the worker's attention, fatigue, or compliance. Administrative controls rely on **human compliance** with rules — which is inherently variable, error-prone, and subject to fatigue or deliberate violation.

*(Expected length: 8-10 lines. Award full 3 marks only if both definitions and reliability comparison are given.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six examples of engineering controls used in manufacturing industries.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** — captures dust/fumes at source.
2. **Substitution** — replacing toxic solvents (e.g., benzene → toluene-free).
3. **Enclosure / Isolation** — soundproof enclosures for noise-generating equipment.
4. **Machine Guarding** — fixed and interlocked guards on moving parts.
5. **Wet Methods** — using water sprays to suppress dust in grinding.
6. **Interlocks and Sensors** — pressure/gas sensors linked to automatic shutdown.
7. **Process Automation** — robotic welding replacing manual welding.

*(6 items × 0.5 marks each = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer with KTU Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`  *(Module Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Describe the **Hierarchy of Controls** as applied in industrial safety. Explain why engineering controls rank above administrative controls and PPE. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the components and working of a **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q(a) — Hierarchy of Controls (7 marks)

The **Hierarchy of Controls** is a systematic framework used in occupational safety to select the most effective hazard control measure. From most to least effective:

| Rank | Control | Type | Reliability |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Elimination** | Physically remove the hazard | Highest — hazard no longer exists |
| 2 | **Substitution** | Replace with a less hazardous option | Very high — reduces hazard severity |
| 3 | **Engineering Controls** | Built-in physical changes (LEV, guards) | High — works regardless of worker behavior |
| 4 | **Administrative Controls** | SOPs, training, scheduling | Moderate — depends on human compliance |
| 5 | **PPE** | Personal protective equipment | Lowest — depends on correct wear/use |

**Why engineering > administrative > PPE:**
- **Engineering controls** are *inherent* to the equipment/process. They operate 24/7, irrespective of who is operating the machine or whether the operator is attentive.
- **Administrative controls** depend on the worker remembering, understanding, and willingly following procedures — vulnerable to human error, fatigue, complacency, or intent.
- **PPE** is the weakest because it only protects the wearer, can fail (e.g., glove tear), and is uncomfortable, leading to non-use.

**Reference to KTU: Indian Factories Act 1948, Section 14** mandates ventilation and dust suppression (engineering) before relying on respirators (PPE).

*(Award 7 marks: 3 for diagram/text of hierarchy, 4 for justification.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q(b) — LEV System (7 marks)

A **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** system captures contaminants *at or near their point of generation*, preventing them from entering the worker's breathing zone.

**Components (refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram):**

1. **Hood** — flanged, booth-type, or canopy hood positioned to capture contaminant at source. Hood entry loss coefficient $\zeta$ depends on hood geometry.
2. **Duct System** — transports contaminated air to the air cleaner. Velocity $V_d$ must exceed **minimum transport velocity** (typically $15-25 \, m/s$ for dusts).
3. **Air Cleaner** — cyclone, fabric filter, wet scrubber, or electrostatic precipitator.
4. **Fan** — provides motive force; must overcome total system pressure drop (friction + entry + exit + equipment losses).
5. **Discharge Stack** — releases cleaned air safely, away from intakes.

**Working principle:** The fan creates suction at the hood. Contaminants are captured (capture velocity $V_c$ determined by contaminant type), entrained in duct flow ($Q = V_d \cdot A_d$), cleaned, and discharged.

**Design steps:**
- Determine $V_c$ from contaminant type (Table in Section 2.3).
- Calculate $Q = V_c \cdot A_h$.
- Size duct: $D_d = \sqrt{4Q/(\pi V_d)}$ with $V_d$ > minimum transport velocity.
- Select fan based on total pressure $TP = SP + VP + \sum \Delta P$.

*(Award 7 marks: 3 for components, 2 for working principle, 2 for design steps.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  *(Alternative Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **administrative controls** used to manage occupational hazards in industries. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industry releases a contaminant at $G = 0.0008 \, m^3/s$. The Permissible Exposure Limit is $C_{max} = 0.02$ (vol. fraction). Determine the **dilution ventilation airflow rate** required. If a circular duct of diameter $0.25 \, m$ is used, calculate the **duct velocity** and **velocity pressure**. Take $\rho = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q(a) — Administrative Controls (7 marks)

Administrative controls alter *work procedures and human behavior* to minimize exposure. Detailed classification:

1. **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** — written step-by-step instructions ensuring consistent, safe work practices. Example: Lockout/Tagout procedure for maintenance.
2. **Permit-to-Work (PTW)** — formal authorization documents for high-risk tasks (hot work, confined space entry, electrical isolation, height work). Ensures supervisor approval and controls risk.
3. **Training and Induction** — competency-building through safety inductions, refresher courses, toolbox talks.
4. **Job Rotation** — limits cumulative exposure by alternating workers across tasks (e.g., rotating workers in noise zones to comply with occupational noise exposure limits).
5. **Work-Rest Cycles** — mandated rest periods to combat fatigue.
6. **Housekeeping / 5S** — Sort, Set in order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain. Reduces fire, slip, and trip hazards.
7. **Signage, Color Coding, and Warning Systems** — visual communication (e.g., red = fire equipment, yellow = caution).
8. **Supervision and Monitoring** — safety audits, CCTV, supervisor presence.

**Limitation:** Effectiveness depends entirely on **human compliance**, making administrative controls less reliable than engineering controls.

*(Award 7 marks: ~1 per control, plus 1 for limitations.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
$$G = 0.0008 \, m^3/s, \quad C_{max} = 0.02, \quad D_d = 0.25 \, m, \quad \rho = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$$

**Step 1: Dilution Ventilation Flow Rate**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{dilution} &= \dfrac{G}{C_{max}} \\[6pt]
             &= \dfrac{0.0008}{0.02} \\[6pt]
             &= 0.040 \, m^3/s
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Duct Cross-Sectional Area**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_d &= \dfrac{\pi D_d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.25)^2}{4} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{\pi \times 0.0625}{4} = 0.04909 \, m^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Duct Velocity**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \dfrac{Q_{dilution}}{A_d} = \dfrac{0.040}{0.04909} \\[6pt]
    &\approx 0.815 \, m/s
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Velocity Pressure**

$$\begin{aligned}
VP &= \dfrac{\rho V_d^2}{2} = \dfrac{1.2 \times (0.815)^2}{2} \\[6pt]
   &= \dfrac{1.2 \times 0.664}{2} \\[6pt]
   &\approx 0.399 \, Pa
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Dilution flow rate $Q$ | $\mathbf{0.040 \, m^3/s}$ |
| Duct velocity $V_d$ | $\mathbf{\approx 0.815 \, m/s}$ |
| Velocity pressure $VP$ | $\mathbf{\approx 0.399 \, Pa}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (1 mark lost)**: Students often use $C_{max}$ in $\% \cdot 100$ instead of decimal form. Always convert $ppm$ or percentages to fractional form before dividing.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (1 mark lost)**: Forgetting units or not stating $\rho = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$ explicitly. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing assumptions.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (2 marks lost)**: Using duct diameter instead of radius in area formula, or mixing up $V_d$ with $V_c$ in the $VP$ equation.
> - **Engineering reality check**: $V_d = 0.815 \, m/s$ is **far below** the minimum transport velocity for dusts ($15-25 \, m/s$). In a real design, the duct would need to be **smaller** to maintain transport velocity, OR LEV should be used instead of dilution. State this insight for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering & Administrative Controls — Key Takeaways**

- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls**: Elimination → Substitution → **Engineering** → Administrative → PPE. Effectiveness *decreases* as we descend.
- ✅ **Engineering Controls** are *physical, built-in, behavior-independent* — therefore more reliable. Examples: LEV, guarding, interlocks, isolation, substitution.
- ✅ **Administrative Controls** are *procedural, behavior-dependent* — less reliable. Examples: SOPs, PTW, training, job rotation, signage.
- ✅ **LEV Components**: Hood → Duct → Air cleaner → Fan → Stack. Always size duct for **minimum transport velocity** to prevent dust settling.
- ✅ **Capture Velocity $V_c$** depends on contaminant type ($0.25-5.0 \, m/s$); higher for toxic/buoyant/hot releases.
- ✅ **Core Equations** (memorize these):
  - $Q = V_c \cdot A_h$
  - $D_d = \sqrt{4Q/(\pi V_d)}$
  - $VP = \rho V^2/2$
  - $Q_{dilution} = G/C_{max}$
- ✅ **Factories Act 1948, Section 14** mandates effective ventilation as a primary engineering control in Indian factories.
- ✅ **Examiner Tip**: Always mention *why* engineering > administrative in your answers — this is a high-frequency 2-3 mark differentiation question.
- ✅ **Numerical Tip**: Always convert $C_{max}$ to decimal fraction; always state $\rho = 1.2 \, kg/m^3$ for air at NTP.
